rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: My personal opinion is that the UK should require employers to implement madatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees because this will help all employees to have a better understanding of their co workers and will reduce the scope for conflict and misunderstandings in the workplace environment, so improving productivity and output. Additionally mandatory diversity and inclusion training will help employees to have a better understanding of people and groups of people they meet or encounter in their wider world such as their local community, ie. outside work, so helping foster a more tolerant and understanding workfore and communites.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text:
So on some certain levels i see this act as a very good means to foster a cummunity that feels free from bias, were everyone feels respected without discrimination and unethical mal treatment.
I think that kind of environment would generally just thrive better and communication would be quite easy in that kind of setting as well.. i genuinely think its a good way to improve training for all employees . In the society at large today a face showing diversity is something that strikes relevance in so many organisations and companies and shunning away from these could really have some very scary or bad effects. it shows that an organisation is very respectful and responsible and that is very attractive for any establishment

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: The UK, depending more on the area, still has prevalent racism, sexism and ableism. Through mandatory diversity and inclusion training at work you can "water down" those views and make people more accepting, understanding and tolerant of other's race, sex or disability. This would also extend into their personal lives and may dampen the fires of any prejudice they hold. Additionally, workplaces that have good harmony and are free from abuse/toxicity would naturally allow for increased productivity whilst lowering staff turnaround and increasing staff happiness. Overall, the mandatory diversity and inclusion training would be better not only for the workplace but for day to day life in the UK as well.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

Because even though the idea is good, to get the maximum out of it, we really need the employees who participate to udnerstand the rationale behind it and to take away the important messages. at the end of the day, it should not be another tick box exercise to them.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

I'm not 100% sure it is needed in this country though, I think most people are decent and know how to behave themselves. It is a good idea to make sure everyone knows how not to be insensitive, but I can't quite decide if everyone needs to learn this, maybe just people who have had problems with others. rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: Diversity in the work place and inclusiveness are important, but for many jobs it is not relevant. Many people work alone and don't engage with others and so have no need for training in this area. Employers have many costs and making this training compulsory adds to their costs unnecessarily. On the other hand, these issues are important, but maybe the government should take the lead in raising awareness, rather than expecting employers to provide this. Where it is relevant, then I feel that employers should be providing some training, but no doubt they would do so as part of general training without being mandated to do so.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: I think mandatory diversity and inclusion training doesn't really work. Just attending a session doesn't mean someone will change how they think or act. If people are already set in their ways they might not even take the training seriously. It can feel like something they have to do just to follow the rules, not something they learn from. This means that training will probably not lead to real change in the workplace. Also, these training programmes can take up a lot of time and money. Companies have to pat for trainers and time away from normal work. This would be a bigger issues for small companies.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text:
The argument that we need more diversity in the workplace has been taken to the extreme where it started to take more importance that focusing on hiring the best people which is all that matters.  Recruitment should not be about making up the numbers of different groups of people in order to meet some superficial requirements. It should always focus on hiring the best person for a job in terms of their skills and abilities since that is what they will be using if they get the job.

Many companies in the US have already started to push back on diversity, particularly since President Trump was elected and I think this trend will continue worldwide as there is no evidence that diversity resulted in better hires being made during recruitment. I personally don't mind who get hired as long as it the person is skilled at what they do.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: I think this is overall a negative thing. I think there is too much focus on diversity and inclusivity nowadays. By making this training mandatory, the company could be forcing certain ideologies onto their employees, and making them follow certain beliefs. For example, trans rights activism tells people that trans women or women and that if you belief in human biology then you're transphobic. Employees shouldn't be forced into adopting these beliefs. Diversity training will be expensive and will take up a lot of time, as all new employees will need to be trained. There are more important things for companies to spend their time on. The only positive I can see is that it can prevent workplaces from getting sued, which is something that people from certain minorities are very keen on doing.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: Employers would be complying with the law, the Equality Act 2010 and can demonstrate they've step steps to prevent discrimination. If everyone were required to do diversity and inclusion training, there would be increased awareness creating a culture shift. Training would help prevent discrimination, harassment and other harmful behaviours reducing legal risks for the employers. Inclusive workplaces can lead to greater employee satisfaction, engagement and retention. Also, diverse and inclusive teams are shown to be innovative and profitable making diversity and inclusion training a good investment. Programmes should be well-designed, relevant, and effectively integrated to create benefits instead of simply just being enforced.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

Historically there has and still is a lot of discrimination in businesses and during hiring processes.  I think it is a good thing and should be mandatory for businesses to train all staff on the importance of diversity and inlcusiveness.  Important to understand other ethnicities, religions and religious holidays/celebrations.  As to not unneccessarily offending others.  We should all be open minded to others beliefs and cultures.

I think especially during the hiring process making sure that all candidates are considered regardless of things like gender, ethicity etc is really important as there historically has been a real problem.  But i also think it is important to hire the right candidate and not hire to hit diversity targets

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: For a company to be successful they need diversity in terms of staff, this includes race, age, sex etc. All the employees, should have the right to exactly the same training programmes, that way nobody can claim favouritism etc. It is vital for a successful company to have competent staff members, this way everybody is working the same way and giving the same level of customer experience. It will also cut out any petty squabbling amongst staff where they can claim favouritism. it will also make equal opportunities for everybody which makes for a successful company and a happy working environment.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

If people have a better understanding of an appropriate way to behave within the workplace it may lead to a more harmonious and accepting workplace. Further to this a better understanding of other cultures that could come from this may lead to not only an improved workplace but an improved society. People may be able to communicate more effectively if they have a better understanding of where the other person is coming from. This level of knowledge also may be a foundation to build meaningful relationships where it may not have been possible.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: D&I initiatives mean that individuals from underrepresented and marginalised communities can have more opportunities. This makes workplaces more innovative and successful. Making the learning mandatory gives every employee an opportunity to learn and further educate themselves- it creates a baseline of awareness and knowledge. It also ensures that every employee understands the policies on D&I and cannot plead ignorance if conflicts or incidents occur. Health and safety is usually mandatory and D&I training comes under the umbrella of psychological safety, which is everyone's responsibility at work (as physical H&S is).  When people have basic training they can better relax and contribute their ideas.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: Diversity and inclusion training has become controversial but it doesn't need to be. The reasoning behind it is to make employers better at what they do; an 'included' employee is a productive, loyal, and content contributor to the success of an organisation. Empathy for colleagues and for clients or customers who are of diverse backgrounds can lead to excellent business practices founded on such things as the building of inclusive facilities, the retention of increasingly diverse staff, and a better understanding of how to meet the needs of internal and external clients. It is such a positive social progression to have diversity and inclusion training that making it mandatory could have positive effects on the whole of the UK's culture and society.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: Mandatory training can help employees understand unconscious bias, systemic inequality and the value of inclusion.  This can lead to more equitable workplaces in industry.  Inclusive workplaces have been shown as part of studies to be more productive and innovative.  Training can build empathy and cooperation across different team dynamics.  The UK Equality Act 2010 protects against discrimination on various grounds such as race, gender, age etc.  Proactive training helps ensures that employers and employees are aware of their legal obligations and reduces costly legal claims.  Companies seen as inclusive are also seen as being more attractive employers and will encourage better growth.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text:
Mandatory diversity and inclusion training helps teams work more effectively by creating an environment of mutual respect and collaboration. When employees understand and appreciate different perspectives, they communicate more openly and solve problems more creatively. Diverse teams, supported by inclusive practices, have been shown to perform better because they can draw on a wider range of experiences, skills, and ideas.

Requiring all employees to participate in diversity and inclusion training promotes fairness in the workplace. It raises awareness about the systemic barriers that certain groups may face and encourages practices that ensure hiring, promotions, and evaluations are based on merit rather than bias. By educating staff on issues such as unconscious bias and privilege, this training fosters a culture where everyone has an equal opportunity to succeed, regardless of their race, gender, age, disability, religion, or background.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees to create fairer, happier and more respectful workplaces. Such training helps individuals recognise unconscious bias, challenge stereotypes, and understand the value of diverse perspectives. This not only reduces discrimination but also strengthens teamwork and communication. A more inclusive environment allows employees from all backgrounds to feel values and supported, leading to improved morale and retention. Furthermore, diverse teams are proven to be more innovative and productive. By making this training mandatory, the UK can promote equality while also boosting organisational performance and long-term economic growth.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text:
Diversity & Inclusion training done sensitively has the potential for excellent outcomes. Any unconscious bias can be addressed promoting empathy amongst colleagues. Any discrimination and harassment can be recognised for what it is and can be nipped in the bud. Inclusive and empathetic teams work better together and all staff can feel a sense of belonging.  This in turn could lead to higher morale and better cooperation amongst staff .On a practical level, this kind of training can limit the employer's risk of legal issues related to discrimination

Overall this type of training can promote a more respectful, fair and productive workplace

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: I'm not sure I was always so in favour of mandatory diversity and inclusion training, feeling that you were going to be told what to think - and possibly insulting if you were already minded to think that way. However, I've come to see it as a very useful and positive approach, requiring people to be aware of different perspectives and experiences. This heightened awareness that some people may experience discrimination or bias because of a protected characteristic can only benefit workplaces, improving relationships between employees and improving that environment. I think it also has wider benefits for our society generally. The more people who are encouraged to think like this, the better. This is especially true at a time when there is a push back against what's described as 'wokeness', and when more people seem to feel empowered to discriminate unthinkingly. I would push back harder against any call to drop these approaches. The longer it continues and the more widespread it becomes, hopefully the more normal it will seem within our society to occassionally be asked to stop and think in this way.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: I think in theory is is a great thing but I do wonder if it would lead to any actual difference in the employees views, and would also obviously cost time and money. Anyone who has unsavoury views in regards to diversity and inclusion will most likely not change their opinion due to mandatory workplace training. It could however help to provide the tools needed for employees to raise concerns in regards to diversity and inclusion. It's also good PR for the company and helps promote a good message and while it may come across as insincere, that mostly depends on how the company operates beyond the training.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: Diversity and inclusion has been a growing topic for quite a few years now. When I stated working back in 1994 diversity and inclusion wasn't a phrase you heard. This wasn't necessarily right, as I remember the executive board of the company I worked for then all being males!  But to me I think it's gone too far the other way - that there is a danger that we are recruiting people just to meet certain diversity and inclusion quotas, not necessarily based on merit. We need to try and strike a balance with this, and formulate a system that both encompasses ensuring all are given an equal opportunity, but that we also recruit/promote based on who is most deserving, regardless of any characteristics. I appreciate this is not an easy balance to achieve.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text:
The reason for carrying out mandatory diversity training is to help cultures learn about each other, learn about other people from different walks of lives, be it disablity, to religious and cultural differences. The goal is to build compassion and understanding between different people in hopes it helps create unity. You cannot do that by legally mandating someone to be compassionate. This leads me onto my second point(technically third point), all it does is create resentment between groups rather than allowing for a nurturing understanding bteween them. In order to have compassion, and understanding you have to nurture it with love and positive emotions, the concept of a forced and mandatory diversity training and inclusion is akin to saying "you will love me after you do this really annoying thing" rather than have a good impact it has a negative one instead. 

Lastly, people will and do treat it as a simple checkbox exercise, that they need to complete, people don't really pay attention and just want to get it over with. So alongside it building resentment subsconsciously, it also doesn't actually build any understanding. Using positive examples is much better, for example, when Salah played for Liverpool FC, racism dropped significantly towards asians and middle easterns, and especially towards people of Muslim faith.

rows.proposition: The UK should require employers to implement mandatory diversity and inclusion training for all employees.
rows.text: I believe that diversity and inclusion are very important, and should be encouraged throughout daily life, at home, work, school and generally in society. As for the most effective way of achieving this, I think it is vital for people who have influences, so parents, teachers, bosses, etc. to lead by example, and model the behaviour they would like to see from others. Desirable attitudes and behaviours should be encouraged in a positive way, rather than forced onto people - people like to make their own minds up. Demonstrating the positives of diversity and inclusion may be better than forcing training onto people. Often training can feel like it is just something that needs to be done, like a box-ticking exercise, but diversity and inclusion should be part of everyday life. However, training might be a step in the right direction, and probably not a bad thing.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: I strongly agree that uk should implement policies to protect and promote religious freedom for muslim communities. Religious freedom is a basic human right, and safeguarding it is essential for a fair and just society. Unfortunately, muslim society in the UK has faced discrimination and Islamophobia, which can lead to social exclusion and unequal treatment. By actively supporting religious rights and cultural inclusion. In the UK, it can foster mutual respect, reduce prejudice and strengthen community bonds. These policies would not only ensure quality but also enhance the sense of belonging and participation among Muslin citizens, contributing to a more unified and diverse society.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: As muslim, and people from other faith groups expand within the UK they should be given greater support. This is for a few reasons, firstly because as they are still a minority group they will sometimes recieve negative treatment, this could be from the general population, or being scapegoated in the media. There is a growing population of muslims within the UK and their faith should be recognised and supported by the UK people and the government, as we are a multicultural society we just do more to uphold traditional values of all our people, and as Muslims make up a good portion of this, we need to give them greater support.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: All people in this country should be free to practice the religion of their choice.  Legislation is probably necessary to ensure this.  Religious freedom applies equally to Muslims as to Christians, Jews etc.  We cannot discriminate against any one group.  Religious tolerance is essential to ensure that we don't treat any groups less favourably, purely based on their religion.  If existing laws are not felt to be strong enough to ensure this equal treatment for Muslims, then it is necessary, in the interests of fairness, and to ensure social cohesion, to implement policies which strengthen this position, whilst not discriminating against other groups.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text:
I have little time for any religion. Religion has three purposes:
1) To try and explain things people didn't understand in the past, before there were rational and scientific explanations. Almost all religious beliefs in explaining the world around us have now been disproven so it's irrational to cling onto these outdated beliefs.
2) Religion is used to instill a set of moral values/codes in the population. e.g. the carrot and stick of the reward of heaven or the threat of hell after you die. Some of these are fine such as it's wrong to kill or steal, etc. Others are outdated and medieval and no longer appropriate in modern society especially those that treat women as second class citizens.
3) Religion has always been used by those in power as a means to control the general population.

Whilst I believe adults should be free to believe whatever they wish, even if it's utter nonsense like religion I am strongly against the indoctrination of children by their parents and other adults. e.g forcing them to attend religious services, etc, although giving them some understanding of ALL religions and there key beliefs in school is fine.

Some religious beliefs encourage views which may conflict with UK law especially equality of the sexes. Religious beliefs are irrational and can often fly in the face of clear scientific evidence. In a minority of cases it can lead to fanatical beliefs that may be forced on to others or put them in danger, so it's dangerous and irrational to encourage any religion.

Lastly there is no need for this law as religious freedom is already part of UK law. We should NEVER promote religion which is irrational nonsense anymore than promoting other irrational beliefs like astrology, the power of crystals, homeopathy, etc.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text:
This country already seems to promote policies to protect Muslim communities. The government should no be promoting them. The promotion should be done by the Muslim communities themselves. I am fairly widely travelled in the UK and Muslim segregation (driven by their own choice) is prevalent. A greater effort should be made by those to encourage integration with the wider communities around.

Any pro-Muslim policy at a time when we see hundreds of illegal immigrants enter our country would surely be detrimental and encourage right wing doctrine. The government needs to actually institute policies that endorse integration and limit 'Muslim' areas.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text:
The protection of religious freedom has been traditional, if not explicit in common law.  No particular religion should have more protection than any other.  A law that specifically names a religion for protection is immediately discriminatory against all other religions including non-religious beliefs and is obviously absurd.  It has the same logic as a law that says everybody with the surname Smith will be protected.

Our state nay have an official religion, but it should not promote it in any way.  Again, it is a matter of fairness, in order not to bestow particular benefits or advantages  on one religion as it would automatically discriminate against everybody else.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: Firstly, the UK is a country that offers a lot of religious freedom, freedom to practice, freedom of speech, freedom to congregate, freedom to recruit, freedom to condemn other religions if they wish. There is no need to implement more policies when all religions already have these freedoms in place. It is also unnecessary to implement policies specifically for Muslims. All religions are protected in the same way, Muslims do not require added protection. Secondly, the promotion of religious freedoms suggests that the Muslim community is unaware of their freedoms, or that the rest of the country are unaware there is religious freedom for Muslims specifically. I would need some evidence that the general public are unaware that the UK is a safe place for anyone to follow whatever religion they wish. I do not believe this is the case, and I do not believe it is necessary for the government to educate the populace on something they already know.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: I disagree that the UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities. In my opinion, in the UK, Islam, Judaism, Christianity, Catholicism, Hinduism, Sikhism and other religions and communities have plenty of freedom. There are mosques, temples, synagogues and churches already, there are Halal and Kosher foods available in supermarkets and some specialty shops. They already are protected in my opinion and giving special treatment to one particular religion is meritless as it only causes a divide. Additionally, pedestaling one religion could cause them to feel entitled to ask for more and more liberties and perks and is generally undesirable.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: The UK should implement policies to ensure that UK citizens know and understand the Muslim way of life.  This policy would ensure that there are no misconceptions about Islam and that Muslims ideals and beliefs are respected.  I would expect the UK to enshrine the rights of a Muslim to be able to pray at the appropriate times in a quiet appropriate room into employment legislation.  This policy should make it clear that Muslim women are not required to remove their hijabs.  A promise to increase the number of police on the streets to ensure that nobody is unfairly targeted for their faith, this would ensure protection for all communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: I agree that there should be protection of religious freedom for Muslims in the UK. Indeed, I agree that freedom of religion should be protected for all people, but I think that this is most notably absent when it comes to Muslim communities, which is why I would be in favour of specific policies aimed at addressing the disparity. I think that by protecting Muslim people's rights, including right to freedom of religion, we can create a more tolerant and understanding society, which would be a good outcome. However, the UK is a secular state and should remain so - I wouldn't want to see any religion (Islam or any other) prioritised over freedom not to be religious.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: religous freedom should be protected because everyone deserves the right to do what they want in a religous way without the fear of being hurt or made fun of. it helps everyone live together in a nice and peaceful way, this makes the whole environment nicer and less stressful for everyone resulting in a better well being. Freedom should be protected because if one freedom is taken away then more could also be taken away in the future, it is about principals and standing up for yourself as a human being, and not being taken advantage of. It is important.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: Every community in the UK should feel safe and secure and if that means that the UK needs to implement policies that not only protect muslims but also allow freedom to practice their beliefs should be a given right. this is a basic human rights that the UK should adhere to and protect minorities such as muslims to feel more welcomed and part of the UK. muslims contribute to the society especially in the healthcare sector and as a result the UK should lead by example and protect its community to enhance cohesion and tolerance within our communities. UK media has enhance hatred to muslim communities and thus the government should protect them.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities as this is a policy enshrined in parliamentary law, under the human rights act 1998, and I believe this is an important attribute for British people. Further we take great pride in this country for freedom of expression and allowing people to express their religious believes is an important aspect that makes Britain the way it is. No one should live in fear due to having a different religious upbringing and/or belief. If we do not protect these core freedoms for our minorities, how can we believe that we would not have our own freedoms taken away down the line?

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.
rows.text: The UK should implement such policies as freedom of religion is a protected right under the Human Rights Act. Every individual has the right to practice their religion without the fear of discrimination or persecution. In the UK, Muslim communities face high levels of Islamophobia including and not limited to hate crimes, negative portrayals in the media and unequal treatment in the public. Targeted policies can help to address these injustices and help promote a more inclusive accepting society. Protecting religious freedom can also support social integration by promoting mutual respect helping Muslim individuals to feel accepted and values as part of the wider community. However, some critics may argue creating policies specific to one group may be perceived as offering special treatment potentially leading to division or resentment if not handled carefully.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

I do not subscribe to the belief that we are a predominantly Christian country and therefore our laws should only protect and promote Christianity. The world is getting smaller and smaller and we should be open and respectful to all.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement policies to ensure the protection and promotion of religious freedoms for Muslim communities.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

It may make political change less likely as people who are only voting because they have been forced to do so may be more likely to just vote for a mainstream party without putting any real thought into it. People may feel their only option to exercise free will is to vote at random rather than making an informed choice.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: I think voting should be a choice not something people have to do. If voting is imposed it takes away the freedom to decide if you want to vote or not. rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: Any sort of mandatory vote would be incredibly difficult to implement and enforce. In a democratic society with a voting system, people should be free to vote for whoever they choose AND be free to not vote if they wish. It could lead to abuse of the voting system with bogus votes being cast affecting the result of elections. The increase in cost for this would also not be popular as inevitably it would have to be funded by the tax payer. The legal system would probably also struggle to process prosecutions or fines if that was the punishment, and if imprisonment was the highest punishment, then the current system would be unable to cope with extra inmates.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: I strongly disagree with the statement of making voting mandatory in the UK. I think that voting should be a political right, not a duty, and that people should be able to choose whether or not they do so. Forcing voting does not ensure that people will be informed or involved in the issues, and it may even cause people to vote at random in order to avoid any penalties. The quality of election outcomes may really suffer as a result. In my opinion, people shouldn't be forced to vote only because they feel that no party reflects their opinions. Voting should be a meaningful activity rather than a chore.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: Universal suffrage - the right for all citizens to exercise their vote in the democratic process - is a fundamental right. However, those who do not wish to exercise this power are free to do so. It is an individual human right to have choice, so forcing citizens to partake in elections would be onerous and, in my view, would also be counterproductive. The role of democracy, in a free and fair society, is for parties to argue their respective policies, in an effort to convince the populace. Those who are unengaged in politics would simply turn up and vote arbitrarily, or spoil their ballots, upon pain of (presumably) financial sanction from the state. This is neither fair nor democratic.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text:
Elections are supposed to represent the will and beliefs of a nation in regard to how a country should be run. however, we often see that this is not the case and in the clearest examples, we see that an increase in voter turnout has repeatedly shown to change elections, showing some smaller elections are dubiously representative. 

voter disenfranchisement is an underhanded tool often used in politics to prevent another party's ability to acquire votes in certain areas, this may be due to a lack of access to voting booths or not having time to vote. mandatory, easy-access voting would prevent this as everyone would be required to show up and either vote or spoil their ballot.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: I do not particularly agree that a mandatory vote is a good idea.  Although I am not very well versed on the statistics and so would need to gain more familiarity with the subject.  I think this should remain a personal choice whether to vote and not be forced into it  There is possibly a lot of very valid reasons why a person may decide to vote, eg, there may not be any good candidates and so effectively no voting is a vote against the current offerings. rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: At this current time there is great voter apathy with people not believing they should vote, as it doesn't make a difference. However, forcing mandatory voting ensures that ALL people will get a voice and a say in who is running the country. It would encourage more engagement generally with the political process, even for those who do not normally have an interest. Lower classes and the underprivileged would have more of a say as typically the higher educated and more affluent in society generally are more likely to vote and force their opinions generally. Although, it seems dictatorial it would probably encourage greater democracy.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: Mandatory voting ensure that election results better reflect the will the entire population, not just the politically engaged. Higher turnout gives elected governments a stronger democratic mandate, increasing their legitimacy. Voluntary voting often leads to underrepresentation of young people, low-income groups, and ethnic minorities. Turnout tends to be skewed toward older, wealthier, and more educated voters. This can create an imbalance in political representation. Mandatory voting helps ensure all voices are heard equally, reducing systemic bias in political outcomes, leading to better funding for public services in disadvantaged communities and stronger protection for workers. It can create a more united community.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: There is a tendency for individuals to complain about political decisions, often blaming the party in power as not the one they would have chosen. If everyone is made to vote then the political party in power would have been chosen by the majority of the public and therefore a fair choice. There would therefore be no complaints in decisions as it reflects those of the nation. I think as well when you have only a turnout of 40% at the polls, it is hard to believe and get behind the party that wins power as you can claim that the remaining 60% of the population who chose not to vote could have voted elsewhere.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

-If every citizen had to vote, parliament would have a better, and more diverse representation. Extremist parties would find it difficult to spout populist views when they have a voice in parliament.  Citizens would better engage with politics if they feel that their vote actually matters. Longer term there would be better MPs elected.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text:
For transparency; elections in the UK must be done in an open way so that the people may see and believe in the choice they have made.

For democratic justification; this is to ensure that democracy is being practised to show that it is the government of the people, by the people and for the people.

To ensure it is free and fair; elections must be transparent for the people to take part. If elections are not conducted in a free and fair manner, citizens will lose interest in the elections.

To engage the masses; elections having all the above qualities are the best way to engage people to participate in choosing a government for themselves.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: I would say I am very unsure about this one! rows.proposition: The UK should implement mandatory voting in all major elections.
rows.text: I slightly agree with idea of implementing mandatory voting in the UK, mainly because it could strengthen democratic legitimacy by ensuring broader representation in election results. When more people vote, the governments are more likely to reflect the will of the people as a whole and not just the political active. It also encourages citizens to stay informed and engaged with current affairs of the country. However, there are concerns about person freedom because some people may feel being forced to vote violates their right to abstain. Additionally, mandatory voting may cause disengaged individuals casting random or ill-informed votes, which can dilute the quality of  democratic decisions.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text:
It's about time something positive happened for the working class, who work in awful places (sometimes) for very low pay. 
It puts a great strain on people who have to work for a living so if they could get the same pay for less hours/days they would feel so much happier and more appreciated. They would have more time for recreational activities and more importantly, they would not feel so tired and weary and worn down. When you think of all the NHS workers, who work such long hours, doing shifts around the clock. How great would it be for them, and they would be able to do an even better job if they weren't so tired.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: I support this because it is evidence based and indicates that this approach support employee well-being and economic growth - a win-win situation. A four day week also has other advantages, reducing workplace commute (CO2 emissions) and reducing workplace energy consumtion (electric, gas etc..) A four day week is long overdue, the workforce should be judged on how well it performs and not how many hours employees put in. If you can get better performance but working only four days then this is a winner for both the employee and the employer. Overall,this will lead to a healthier, and happier society.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: Basically a 4-day week would allow for greater flexibility for both employees and employers.  This flexibility would enable better productivity as a result of employees taking less days off sick, and from efficient work produced by happier employees.  Mental well being is the key advantage, I believe, to a shorter working week.  Employees and employers could benefit from this through less days spent being ineffective due to illness, and more days being spent working in a focused and efficient manner.  This, in turn, would increase productivity, and prevent employers from experiencing a high turnover, thus savings being made all round.  For the community as a whole, happier people will mean less pressure on our National Health Service and Social Services.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: First of all having a reduced work week has been trialed and tested and has lead to more free time to relax and unwind. This improves the persons wellbeing significantly. The next is that with a four day work week they will be more productive. Since the weeks are shorter they have four days to finish work. Lastly most companies don’t take advantage of a four day work week so a company that does this in order to support they’re staff will gather the employees locality as the company is providing benefits that most do not. This coincides with productivity as they are more likely to work better in a company they like.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: Working five days per week and only having two days off is not a good balance. Returning from maternity leave, I reduced to four per week and have taken a large pay cut to be able to do so. Many of my friends have done this too, and it only widens the gender pay gap between males and females as it's still predominantly women who have to pick up the 'slack' here. Since reducing my hours, the amount of comments I have had (from mostly men!) about being a 'part time' worker, is quite frankly disappointing. If everyone worked 4 days, it would increase the amount of opportunities women would get as I feel I have been overlooked several times due to how working 1 less day is perceived. I also think employees would be more productive, have a greater work life balance and would be overall happier working less.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: Studies have shown that 4-day work weeks increase rather than decrease employee productivity. Less time is wasted and more quality time is spent focused on the tasks that need to be completed. A 4-day work week vastly improves the home / life balance of employees as they are able to spend more time with their families, exercising, or other activities that are important to them. A 4-day work week also reduces stress, which in turn leads to employees having better mental and physical health. This results in employees taking less days off work due to illness or being signed off for stress.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: Implementing a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees has alot of merits. One of the merits include good work-life balance. This is essential for stress management as studies show that the primary reason for employee burnout is increase duration of working hours (at least 50hours/week). Employee burnout will eventually lead to less productivity. Also, overworked employees are at risk of various health issues like increase blood pressure. These health issues can put a strain on healthcare, causing the NHS millions of pounds to cater for the affected ones. Indeed, implementing a 4-day workweek has both occupation health, public health and clinical health benefits.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text:
I think many people can feel demotivated working full time over 5 days. Work can become monotonous and staff can lack   motivation.  By having 3 days break over a week would allow people time to focus on family, hobbies and their home. I consider that a job should not be your life! Working 4 days would also save many people on commuting costs/childcare.
Working the same job over a condensed 4 days would necessitate better time management,  working more effectively to get the job done.  I know many jobs, including ones I have had in the past, that a lot of time is wasted on non work and clock watching. 4 day weeks would not only benefit employees but employers too.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text:
If the UK would implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees that would immediately improve people's mood and how they see work.
Eventually people would have less health issues related to stress or injuries related to extensive work, as they have more time to recharge and relax after a full week of work.
With more time to rest and enjoy with their families, people would be overall happier, improving the life of the general population. This could mean an improvement in productivity, as people can focus more on the 4 days of work instead of wasting time on some days as it's usually common.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: Retaining the top talents because the days are less compared to the normal 5-day workweek. Especially for the younger generation which prioritizes on a work-life balance. The environment will benefit due to less gas emissions. A four-day workweek will reduce the country's carbon footprint. The employees working for only four days a week will lea to them being active during the working hours. Contrary to the concerns that reducing the working hours will affect the productivity, this could be the opposite because the employees will not be tired. This leads to them being more careful and working more efficiently to bring out good results.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: I think shortening the working week to 4 days would be beneficial to the UK workforce. A 4 day working week would mean longer days, 7-6 for example. This would mean people get more done in the same amount of hours, just with less days to work them in. It would give people time to follow their passions, enjoy life, and to get more out of their spare time. It would also help smaller business as people would have more time with an extra day to visit them and spend money at them. Pubs and restaurants would certainly benefit from this.

Parents of pre-school children would be able to look after their kids at home instead of paying for childcare or relying on other family members to care for their children. There would also be a reduction of traffic as less people would be commuting every day.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.

I'm also not convinced that this would have any positive impact as it restricts the rights of those workers happy to work 5 or more days in a week. It would almost certainly have a negative economic impact as if there were any productivity or cost saving efficiencies to be made by such a program, I suspect the private sector would have already adopted such a scheme.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.

Another major problem with such an approach is pay, because unless people are going to receive the same amount of money for a four day week as they would have done for a five day week, and therefore work the same number of hours in longer days, people will be worse off and may not be able to pay basics such as a mortgage or rent. The labour market definitely needs reforming, however this approach is not the way forward.

Going to festivals and music concerts - going away on holiday - I think people would definitely spend more money buying things and experiences if they had more time to enjoy doing these things. More money would be spent and the economy would get a boost. 

- Studies have shown that people can get the same amount of work done in 4 days as they can do in 5, especially if they work longer hours. Lots of office workers sit twiddling their thumbs looking for something to do throughout the day - we could get all that work done in 4 days. rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: In favour of this policy, I believe enforcing this 4-day workweek allows full-time employees to create a better work-life balance that will in turn enhance productivity during work. When it comes to full-time employees, it is due to the amount of hours overworked that there is such an increase in depression, anxiety and even suicides; it is important to have a work-life balance that allows people to rejuvenate whilst off work. With more time off to rest and recharge, they will be able to return to work with more efficiency and more energy; people who like their work and have the energy to work will always produce the best results. However, in disagreement with this policy, there is a possibility that due to a 4-day workweek, there could be an increase in working hours leading to 12hour workdays to get more done during those days.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: The statement seems to assume that "all" full-time workers carry out their duties over a standard 5 day working week. This shows a lack of understanding as to how employment is structured and that many jobs span 7 days and can be worked using varying shift patterns. Some people like to work over a longer period of time and this often also suits the companies for which they work. A blanket statement such as the above one is not very useful and would probably lead to a number of foreseeable and unknown negative consequences. Rather than aiming for efficiency, a happier and more contented workforce should be the goal.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a 4-day workweek for all full-time employees.
rows.text: I oppose the idea of requiring all full-time workers in the UK to work four days a week. Despite its allure, it may lower productivity in industries like manufacturing, retail, and healthcare where constant output or service is necessary. These sectors would find it difficult to sustain the same level of performance with fewer working days because they depend on steady staffing. Furthermore, cramming five workdays into four could put more strain on staff members, resulting in lengthier workdays and elevated stress levels that could have a detrimental impact on their wellbeing and job satisfaction. Finally, because they may need to hire more employees or pay overtime to ensure coverage, firms may incur greater costs as a result of this shift.

rows.proposition: The UK should loosen regulations on the import and distribution of international pharmaceuticals.
rows.text:
From my limited knowledge of the UK MHRA's processes for drug approvals, I am confident that the UK applies robust standards to domestic and foreign pharmaceutical producers and distributors already, by means of e.g. demanding a registered contact in the UK for demanding technical information, peer-reviewed studies, and standardised testing procedures. I also believe that the standardised prescription cost set through the NHS generally ensures fairness and access to medicines for patients.
I do not believe there would be any significant benefit to loosening regulations in this instance, as the presumed benefits would merely be potential reductions in costs of procurement and greater availability of supply.
A preferrable alternative to loosening regulations on international pharmaceuticals could be to encourage the expansion of domestic generics production to promote stable supplies and healthy competition within a well-regulated market.

There would also be the risk that other, illegal drugs, would end up entering the country disguised as medication.

rows.proposition: The UK should loosen regulations on the import and distribution of international pharmaceuticals.
rows.text: I believe there are both advantages and risks to loosening regulations on the import and distribution of international pharmaceuticals. On one hand, it could allow patients to access cutting-edge or urgently needed treatments that might not yet be available under current UK regulations. This could be especially important for rare diseases or in cases of medicine shortages. On the other hand, loosening regulations might increase the risk of substandard or counterfeit drugs entering the market, which could compromise patient safety. Rigorous checks and standards exist for a reason: to maintain trust in the healthcare system and ensure effectiveness. In my view, any policy change should be carefully balanced, prioritising both accessibility and patient protection.

rows.proposition: The UK should loosen regulations on the import and distribution of international pharmaceuticals.

Given that such products are very often used for health reasons, this then becomes of particular concern- as human health may then be adversely affected.
On the other hand, it may improve trade in these products if the regulations were looser. This may bring down the prices of such products- which is of benefit to the hard pressed consumer- on limited budgets.
However, overall, I think the risks outweigh potential benefits.

rows.proposition: The UK should loosen regulations on the import and distribution of international pharmaceuticals.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the teaching of creationism alongside evolution in all public schools.
rows.text: In terms of school education, the goal of teaching is to educate, not to dumb down. A historical analysis of creationism may be valuable in social sciences such as philosophy, psychology and religious studies where an investigation into the evolution of ideas has value in developing critical minds. However, the teaching methodology for the sciences is entirely different and is not based on subjectivism, but rather empiricism and rationalism. Mandating creation confuses a scientific approach with a social sciences one and requires the suspension of the obligation towards evidence. As such treating creationism as a worthy competitor theory would undermine the very basis on which scientific theories are tested and send out the message that there is no such thing as expertise or evidence.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the teaching of creationism alongside evolution in all public schools.
rows.text: Creationists, by definition, promulgate a singular view of what constitutes, for them, an acceptable religion. The suggestion that creationism should be taught alongside evolution is an very powerful and damaging idea that reduces scientific thought and progress to an equivalent dogma that can be accepted or rejected simply through a choice of religion. Science is not an alternative religion. While religion is based on unquestioning acceptance of the teachings of God, faith in an ideal or an all-powerful creator,  it is largely metaphorical and figurative. It is open to manipulation to suit the political ideals of the day, as we see in USA, among others. Contrarily, science is based on questioning convnentional wisdom, and on the idea that there is no universal truth, only a provisional explanation that fits our observations to date and is always open to question and debate. They are different ways of thinking.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the teaching of creationism alongside evolution in all public schools.
rows.text: Quite a lot of the people in the UK don't believe that Darwin's theory of evolution is the way life was created. A lot of religions, and there are scriptures and books that have the same information, is that life on Earth was created by one God alone for punishment of Adam and Eve for not following one instruction which was not to eat from the forbidden tree. This should be taught in public schools as it plays a huge importance in religion and also self-discipline. I do agree that methods can be changed and new things can be added to school curiculum.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the teaching of creationism alongside evolution in all public schools.
rows.text: Mandating the teaching of creationism alongside evolution in public schools allows students to explore different perspectives and form their own opinions more freely, rather than only being presented with a single and potentially biased viewpoint. It will aid them in understanding science and religion as two separate but influential ways of thinking, one based on evidence and the other on faith, without conflating the two. It opens the door to philosophical discussions about belief, existence and knowledge. By including multiple worldviews, this will create a more inclusive student environment where students from different backgrounds feel seen and heard; leading to more meaningful engagement. Overall, it will lean towards creating a more comfortable, respectful and open-minded learning environment that values diversity, dialogue and independent thought.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
I don't know much about privatising but history shows it doesn't always work. They sold off our water services and the system has major issues. I wouldn't want to see the same happen with the prison system where profits are put ahead of human welfare. If it would guarantee a overhaul and all the profits went into new prisons, rehabilitation opportunities and care outside of prison then I would support it. But private companies have shareholders and they tend to come first.
I also have reservations in how to monitor safety and efficiency. Not just for the prisoners but the local populations who live around the prison.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Current levels of places in our prisons have resulted in letting prisoners out early, some have gone straight back into re offending before they have completed their full term and just punishment. This puts the public more at risk. Some prisons are also in desperate need of updating, some are Victorian and really need replacing with more modern and suitable facilities. We have lost faith in prison as a deterrent and newly sentenced prisoners know they will get out a lot earlier than their original sentence and this isn't fair to their victims. Ten years should mean 10 years regardless.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
I think it is widely acknowledged that the prison system is underfunded, resulting in overcrowding, poor conditions for both prisoners and staff, low staff morale, and lack of investment in rehabilitation initiatives.

It may be that privatisation is the answer. If it is run as a business, then a competent business, with their business acumen, may be in a better position to run the prison system effectively. A business may be able to consider ways of raising funds in addition to payment from taxes. 

However, businesses are focused purely on profit. The detention of criminals, often violent criminals, is too important to be in the hands of a business who's focus is profit. There would be a risk that cost cutting will worsen conditions and lead to a greater risk to the safety of the staff and general public. 

I think it's undeniable that the prison system needs overhauling but privatisation is now the way to do it. I don't think I know enough about the subject to be sure though. I would want more information.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
The state in the UK operates a failing prison system.  Many prisoners reoffend shortly after release.  There are not enough government (public) funds to pay for rehabilitation and eduction of prisoners.  However it is far from clear that a privatised system would be better because the costs wield be as great but with the added pressure of needing to ensure that there is a decent return to shareholders.
There are a number of charities which help with the rehabilitation  of prisoners for example by running cafes open to the public which enables the prisoners to learn all sorts of skills which will be useful in the outside world.  These should be encouraged

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: It feels like there is a two tier system with the public private prisons. Maybe I'm wrong with that of course. The private sector recruit their own staff and vet them. They are a separate entity to the public jails. Don't get me wrong, if you can get good facilities that improve prisoner engagement that is obviously a good thing, but it should be the case you bring the public jails up to their standard. Wherever the private sector is involved, profit will always be in the minds of the management or the investors. I believe that you can get sacked from the public sector but still apply for a job in the private arena.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Privatising UK prisons could offer benefits like increased efficiency, cost savings and innovation and rehabilitation. Private firms may operate more flexibly and adopt new practices faster than the public sector. However, these advantages depend on strong regulation. Without proper oversight, privatisation can lead to cost cutting that compromises safety, staff conditions and inmate welfare. It also raises ethical concerns about profiting from incarceration and often lacks transparency. Service quality may vary and accountability can be weaker than in public prisons. All in all, I’m fairly ambivalent on the matter and can see pros and cons in both, although if thinking on it I probably lean against privatisation.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Anything which helps and resolves the prison issue needs attention and looking at. Privatisation could work as the current set up seems to be failing. From what I hear prisons are dangerously overcrowded which has a negative impact on the prisoners as well as the staff. Perhaps dish out more community service orders rather than locking people up. Also immigration seems to be wild at the minute - I wonder how many people in prison are immigrants...illegal or otherwise. The whole system needs a shakeup. In fact everything is crumbling in Britain. 14 years of Tory rule has pretty much ruined this country. Everything has a domino effect and the country needs a new lease of life.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.

It is only my opinion i can be wrong

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: While it is currently true that the prison system does, in my opinion, certainly need reforming, the privatisation of the system would not achieve the necessary changes. What the system needs is more of the Scandinavian model to focus on proper rehabilitation, which would reduce prison overcrowding too - and cyclically help the problem overall. Privatisation of prisons would lead to big companies who own the prisons intending to increase their profits rather than what prisons should be used for, deterring crime and preventing it in the future, which includes the punishment of harsh criminals and the rehabilitation of criminals with lesser sentences.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: There needs to be an overhaul of the sentencing and legal system which would improve the prison system, rather than patching up the existing system. In the above statement scenario, companies will be competing to win contracts and it will usually be the cheapest quotes that win. So therefore, the company will probably be cutting corners and there may be similar problems and comparable levels of corruption. With such a large institution, the state is best placed to run it but with modifications, such as better financial oversight and people management. The legal and sentencing system should be changed to reduce the prison populations and increase the likelihood of rehabilitation and reduce recidivism, rather than keeping the current status quo and looking at private versus state control.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
When someone commits a crime and is sent to prison, they are going to be spending a length of time behind bars and their care is handed over to the people running the prison. It is imperative that the people running the prison are answerable to a governing body. That the prison officers who are in direct contact with the prisoners receive training in the same vein as police officers and that every prison is ran the same to the same rules and guidelines. We cannot have prions being run as businesses where the owner/chairman is only interested in profits and making money. Prisons are not businesses as such, peoples lives are being managed and that means that it isn't about profit and loss, it's about keeping people safe and mentally and physically healthy whilst is the care of the prison.
Prisons need to be inspected in the same way that schools and doctors surgeries are inspected so that improvements can be made where necessary. If prisons were privatised then it is my belief that once a prisoner was admitted to prison and the door closed behind them, there would be no way of controlling what happened to them and that would be wrong. Prisoners are not livestock.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: As UK citizens, we have a right to know what is happening inside our prisons. Are prisoners treated well and fairly without being tortured? It also discourages young people from committing crimes because they have observed that prisoners have no freedom, and life there is not luxurious. This gives them a choice to make their lives better and avoid committing crimes. The government has not given us enough evidence that privatizing our prisons is an effective way to correct or contain people from committing a crime, or a way to reduce overpopulation in our prisons, thus reducing the cost of maintaining a prison.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Since the 1980's when privatisation became government policy, it has seemed clear that apart from a small percentage of people who invested, the idea has been a failure. Companies repeatedly failing to deliver while awarding their executives huge bonuses even if they have failed in their jobs. Currently, previously privatised companies in areas such as provision of water and sewage services and rail transport are failing and are starting to be taken back into public ownership which ssuggests to me that the experiment has been a failure. Even though government run entities tend to have lots of waste, I feel that they could not be as badly run as many of the privatised companies at present in operation.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Making the UK prision system private could lead to all shorts of  risks, including profit motivation.  private companies may prioritise cost cutting for example, which can lead to a breakdown in prison services, meaning fewer people can seek help in prison, which can lead to a poorer prison enviroment for staff and inmates. Secondly, privatley owned prision systems leads to lack of transparencey and accountabillity. Without proper oversights, it will start to become extremley difficult to ensure high standards of safteu  and care, which can lead to misuse of public spending money. These issues are the reason i think government should keep in control of these public institutions.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: I believe that public services are best run by public servants who prioritise the effectiveness of services against notions of public value rather than wider concerns of making profits. In this context it means that vital elements of work to deliver rehabilitation of prisoners and to secure the safety of the public and others who are incarcerated are not sacrificed to save money to increase profits. This could be, for example, changing shift patterns of prison officers to cut corners or the number of times a day prisoners have access to exercise or educational programmes or facilities. Some straightforward elements of delivery could, however, be undertaken by private contractors where they don't endanger overall delivery of the public service if there are problems.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: I think the prison system should not be privatised as I feel it is important for the government to retain control of this service and how it operates. I feel it would be at risk of mis-management if it was to be made private and given how reliant we are on the service to enforce the law and keep individuals safe from harm, we need to ensure it works as it should. It is an expensive service though and privatisation would reduce costs for the taxpayer, however, I have concerns about how it would be funded and where a private body would find the money to fund it. Overall, I think it is safer to not privatise this service.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
Many of the prisons in this country are old prisons that do not meet the current needs.  With investment from private companies new prisons could be built that meet modern standards and would also increase the number of spaces available.  Overcrowding in prisons has led to the shortening of sentences and early release - much to the chagrin of many victims.  
A problem with many institutions run by the government is the massive inefficiency and consequent wasting of money that stems from it.  My knowledge here is based primarily on the NHS but I can only imagine it being the same in other government run organisations.  I think that this could be set up as a test case one prison at a time and see if it is any better.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
Prison systems are at breaking point.  They are full, to the point that criminals are being released early.  Some of these prisoners i hear have very quickly reoffended - so clearly should never have been released early! Additionally, some criminals are avoiding sentences due to there being no space. Privatising could add more much needed money which in turn could build more prisons or expand existing ones.  
The care sector desperately needs more state financial help, by reducing state costs it could be directed to other much needed services.  Privatization of the prison services could give a much needed boost and reform the whole sector.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Most private sectors prioritise profit over public good. Private prisons would most likely choose to make profit more than check on the safety of people they put on the prisons. It is common behaviour for the private prisons to cut cost and provide substandard services to prisoners. Privatisation of prisons would also lead to the question of accountability as most private companies do not operate on the same oversight as government owned entities. With such lack of oversight from the government or governmental entities, private prisons will be left at the mercies of the people owning the prisons leading to lack of accountability on matters prisoners. This can even lead to public distrust if the prisons are not accountable to the government.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.

I feel that it would be wrong to allow any stage of the justice system to be run for the profit of private contractors and their share holders.
The state should be able to control every aspect of the justice system from the start, with the police, to the finish with the probation and rehabilitation services to bring offenders back into society.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.

I believe only one other country has more for profit prisons than we do in the UK. Serco and G4S have a poor record and if articles in the mainstream press are to be believed are not very good at delivering an acceptable service. Many years ago I used to work in prisons and from what I can gather now there is no improvement following the privatisation thus far. With privatisation it is all about the profit element and cost cutting and penny pinching will come in to play to deliver more for the shareholder not to mention the directors of the companies concerned.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.

The probation privatisation was ill advised and caused major disruptions to anotherwise efficient service, causing staff shortages and low staff morale. Privatising prisons is wrong. It needs specialised staff to operate effectively and efficiently and the government will choose G4S or Serco to run them, both of whom have failed dramatically when bidding for other areas of the CJS. Why would you waste billions of pounds of public money when they can't even operate the prisons they are currently contracted to run, it makes no sense, especially given their track records.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
