rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: This would be an awful idea. We have already started on this slippery slope of privatising our previously government run systems, and it has only ended poorly for those using the systems while making shareholders richer. our rail system was privatised and our trains are now the most expensive and least reliable across europe. America's prison system, which is privatised, essentially turns it's prisoners into slaves, paying them pennies an hour for hard labour, while keeping them imprisoned for as long as possible to create more profits. Because they cannot possibly be rehabilitated in such an eviroment, the reoffending rate of prisons in the USA is one of the highest in the world. Our increasing privatisation of previously government run systems, in sectors such as the NHS creates a profit incentive for the new shareholders to cut back the quality of the service to make as much money as possible.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Many American prisons are for profit prisons and there are often horror stories coming out about mistreatment of prisoners. Many stories of overcrowding and poor conditions, many of which are spurred on by cost cutting measures. Many prison companies are also publicly traded with exacerbates the problem as shareholders care about profits above all else. British culture is similar to American culture so i fear this would become a problem here with investors caring about profits above all else. this is also fundamentally a governmental responsibility. governments should be providing services such as justice and this falls under that remit in my opinion. i pay my taxes to the government, not a private entity.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Privatising of the UK prison system could lead to increased efficiency and cost savings. Private companies, driven by competition and the need for profit, often manage operations more efficiently than public nstitutions. This can reduce the financial burden on taxpayers. Additionally, private operators may be more rflexible, adaopting innovative rehabilitation programs and management practices that improve prisoner outcomes. Handling over operational duties to private firms also allows the goverment to focus on oversight and on policy, ensuring the standards are met without being bogged down by daily administration. Overall, privatization may offer a more effective and modern approach to incarceration.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Prisons should be privatised as it would allow competitive tendering to take place and contracts being awarded to ensure the best possible value for money is achieved for the taxpayer. Performance targets could be set and measured to ensure a minimum level of performance and value was being achieved and, if companies failed to meet targets then they could be replaced. Private companies often have better governance and are able to demonstrate a better grasp of finances than public bodies which often run over-budget and have little motivation to ensure staff are performing adequately. Prisons should be profitable and not another burden on the taxpayer's shoulders.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Firstly let me preface by saying I am pro - vaccine, I think vaccines work and should be taken. However I respect individual choices in situations like this and if someone doesn't want to take the vaccine that is their right. When the state oversteps that mark and legislates for vaccine mandates I worry about where that power could lead to, for example mandated assisted suicides to deal with an aging population. Do I think that will happen - NO but it leaves a space for it potentially to. Additionally we live in a time where people are chronically online and easily led into conspiracy theories, this plays right into the hands of those conspiracy theories and will push people more into a sinsister world. Finally lets be honest, the government cant build a railway without it costing hundreds of billions over budget and taking 20+ years, hwo do we expect them to roll this out to the entire populatio

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: People should have a choice to wither or not they want to have the COVID-19 vaccination.  A lot of people had side effects from taking the vaccine or even had long term side effects.  People should have a choice when it comes to these new vaccinations. They shouldn't be forced into having them done. What harm will be caused to those who struggle having them? COVID-19 hasn't been an issue for a few years now we have adapted to living with it. Forcing people to take the vaccine will make people question why as we've been dealing with it well since.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The benefits of covid vaccinations have been proven, and the gaining of herd immunity protects all of society, particularly those who cannot be vaccinated or are susceptible to the virus. The more people are vaccinated, the greater the potential for herd immunity. However, mandating it for any individual group undermines individual freedom of choice and autonomy over their own body. This could be the thin edge of the wedge. If vaccines are mandated, what else may then follow? Furthermore, there are better ways to encourage vaccination, making them easier to access at work and eliminating barriers to access, as well as supporting through public health campaigns.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Mandated vaccination is an extreme measure that should be reserved for very high risk outbreak. It may be appropriate for something that quickly affects a wide population and that has serious impact. Covid was initially in this category (perhaps) but is not any more. Hence it is reasonable to allow the individual to determine the level of risk they are prepared to take. A similar situation to flu vaccination, perhaps. It is not a disaster of their is widespread infection and the main sufferer is the person infected. In such circumstance is seems reasonable to leave the decision to the individual.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Mandating COVID-19 vaccinations cold play a crucial role in safeguarding public health by reducing transmission,protecting vulnerable groups ,and preventing futue outbreaks.When more people are vaccinated herd immunity becomes more attainable ,which benefits the entire population. However, such mandates can also have negative effcects if not handled carefully.People may feel their personal autonomy is being ignored,leading to feelings of resentment or defiance.This can weaken trust in healthcare providers and public institutions. If individuals feels coerced rather than informed ,they may become more skeptical of future health guidance ,making long -term public health effortd more difficult to implement.Striking a balance between strong public health messaging and respect for individual freedoms is essential for building lasting trustband cooperation.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I do not believe that the Government should make the COVID-19 vaccination mandatory as it should be an individual choice as to whether one has or doesn't have it.  However, I can see the benefits of the entire population being vaccinated.  The MMR vaccinations debacle is a case in point.  When scare tactics were made about this, many parents refused to let their children be vaccinated and as a result may children contracted either measles, mumps or rubella unnecessarily.  By not having the Covid vaccination, individuals are running the risk of contracting the virus and then passing it on to others.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The covid-19 vaccine was very new and made in record time, this possibly scared a lot of people because they were not aware of the side effects of the vaccine. On the other hand it is important that the government and healthcare system explains to people the importance of vaccination and how crucial it is to to protect against the spreading. However, people are the ones who ultimatly decide if they want to have it or not, if they have severe damages because of the vaccine who's the blame? How will they feel if the goverment mandate that and they die or get severely injured?

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The pandemic caused alot of loss to the country when it errupted therefore the idea of getting everyone vaccinated would be a good one to prevent us from going back to what was witnessed some moments back there.Therefore doing this will enable the country to do away with the thoughts of the virus coming back again.But also as this is considered,people also have right of expression and of doing whatever they want provided it's not agains the laws.This means citizens should not be forced to do anything they are not willing to do.Citizens only needs to be adviced well and go for the vaccine willingly and not forcefully.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I feel that although it would make us all safer if we made everyone take the covid-19 vaccination, we should not force people against their onw will to take it. It should be a choice to take it as we are a free democratic country and this would not allow people their own free will. We could force everyone to take it, but even thought it would make things safer it wouldn't stop the virus as other countries will not make the same step. So we should still allow people to choose if they want to take the vaccination rather than force people to take something they may not agree with taking.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Once upon a time i would have agreed that everyone should be made to have covid vaccinations. That was when people were dying in their millions and we had no idea where it would all lead. But now that things have settled down and our hospitals are no longer overflowing with people dying of covid, then we have to think differently. I know a few people who are still having their vaccinations and have very bad side effects which affect their lives 2 years later. I am not entitled to a covid vaccination as i dont qualify. But i have decided that, as im pretty healthy, that i would rather take the risk than suffer from these awful side effects

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The vaccines for COVID-19 didn't stop the virus spreading, so it therefore wasn't even a true vaccine. It might be in the future if there's another pandemic we get a more effective vaccine that does stop a virus in it's tracks. Then it might be worth looking again. In fact, those who took the vaccine were usually well enough (in my experience) to go out and about with the COVID virus, merrily spreading the virus and causing death and destruction to vulnerable people they give it to. If you haven't had the vaccine, which reduces symptoms and doesn't stop the spreading of the virus, you probably aren't going anywhere. You'll likely feel terribly ill and wish you'd had the vaccine. You aren't going to be out and about on the train, in the supermarket, at the theatre, spreading death and destruction with your virus, as the vaccinated did. It's also shown in court and inquests that in a very small number of cases the vaccine was responsible for death. Obviously so was the virus. Therefore it should very much be people's own choice whether to take it or not.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: It is well known in the UK that we have a right to choose whether we choose or not to accept medical treatment and that is our basic human right at present. To force people by mandating vaccinations would be a step towards a dictatorship by bypassing people's right to choose in a democratic way. It is a well known fact that Covid 19 vaccinations have had an adverse effect on a considerable number of individuals health making them very poorly in the short or long term and even an end result of fatalities occurring. Without unequivocal medical/scientific evidence to the contrary then the government would be going down a slippery slope and losing public support.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
I don't support the idea of mandating Covid-19 vaccinations for all eligble citizens. People should have the freedom to make informed decisions about their own health, including whether or not to get vacinnated. Forcing medical treatment can lead to mistrust in public health authorities and may acutally make hesistant individuals more resistant. 

Instead of mandating vaccines, efforts should focus on education, transparency and respectful dialogue to encourage voluntary uptake. It is also important to recognise that individuals have different medical needs and personal beliefts and a one size fits all approach doesn't necessarily reflect the values of a democratic society.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.

I would also say genuine consent requires one to decide freely and not be subjected to coercion; this should be observed to avoid legal battles.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Whilst I do agree that all eligible citizens should take the COVID-19 vaccination, I do not feel that the vaccination should be mandated. I feel that the choice should be left up to the citizen. Revoking people's power to make their own decision is likely to have devastating social impacts, with people likely to further lose trust in government. Furthermore, people will have a better understanding of their own personal circumstances and said circumstnaces might result in them not wanting to take the vaccine. Having this decided on their behalf would likely make these people feel uncomfortable and further result in negative effects.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I don't believe anyone should be forced into getting the covid vaccination. It should be a personal preference as there have been people who have had adverse reactions to the vaccinations and it is our own bodies so it should be our own choice. Also we don't know enough about the vaccines and the damage and side effects that it may cause people further down the line. It very much falls under the freedom of choice of the person as I believe everyone is entitled to be responsible for themselves and their own decisions in life without having anything forced upon them.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I value individual liberties very much, which makes me wary of vaccination mandates. An individual should have the right to choose whether or not they are vaccinated against COVID-19. This becomes especially pertinent when considering that, in some cases, COVID-19 vaccinations have led to adverse health effects for those who have taken them, particularly the elderly. I don't believe that wide-sweeping government mandates can suitably accommodate all edge cases such as this, rendering them government overreach which, in the worst case, fail at their core objective of ensuring public health. Therefore, I would be averse to supporting mandates for COVID-19 vaccines.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
1. There were many reports of myocarditis and other heart conditions cases after vaccination. Me  personally know people , friends who were absolutely healthy but after few vaccines they suffer from heart conditions that became as a chronic condition
2. In the beginnig on the news it was announced mrna vaccines are approved by some health agencies and said they are safe.  Now it has been revealed that is not the truth.
3. So many side effects. From miscarriages , heart attacks, even cancer and many others. I know people who were absolutely healthy and died after takin covid19 vaccines.  many people lost trust in Health institutions
4. The way vaccines were mandated created so much pressure .  Every body is unique and handle the vaccines differently. This wasnt taken into consideration. There were restrictions that in my opinion were cruel and not human. Losing jobs, no fllights, no eating out , no gym and on and on

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
Covid-19 is a very serious & potential deadly virus & any protection for me is worth having. Many people get long Covid which is debilitating & can lead to lifelong problems.
It's important to protect veneral people from getting the virus via contact with others. 
It's offered free to everyone in the UK so why not have it. Durin the Covid epedemic the Government spent many hours informing us of the dangers & advised to stay away form others.
We get regular text messages telling us when the vacine is raedy &  available locally in our doctors surgery & the pharmacy.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I think that people have a choice as to whether they receive the vaccinations or not.  I also think that there has to be more research into those who received the vaccinations and suffered serious side effects, and some led to death.  There are many "conspiracy theories" floating about, there was scaremongering carried out by government, there was rules for the "working class" and rules for the members of parliament etc.  I think COVID 19 is serious, however, moving forward, I think there's a lot of lessons to be learnt.  People say COVID 19 will end up like the flu - a common illness, where, in the early days people died (and some, unfortunately, still die today).  However, COVID 19 is at the early stage of illness and I think that if it ends up like the flu then treatments/understanding of symptoms will hopefully develop through time.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I believe that vaccines should be more mandated because it helps to protect vulnerable children and adults and eases pressures on the health care systems all around the world by reducing the severity of the virus and therefore eliminating a lot of need for hospital care. This helps save lives and prevents hospitals from being overwhelmed, allowing Doctors and nurses to provide their best care to people in hospital for covid 19 and other reasons. Mandating vaccines can contribute to herd immunity and that will slow down the transmission of the virus.  while i believe that vaccines are crucial i also believe that it is equally important to everyone's rights and to foster understanding through education and clear and factual communication

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.

By being vaccinated it could help slow down the infection numbers and protect people from catching Covid or lessen the symptons if they did catch it. The death rate from Covid would then fall and also cut down the pressure on the NHS. By being vaccinated you would not only protect yourself but also others around you. To not get vaccinated in my opinion is a selfish act and if the choice was taken away and it was mandatory to be vaccinated a lot of deaths and hospitalisation of people could of been avoided

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens as it will prevent another pandemic. Scientists worked for months to create a vaccine so why shouldnt it be mandatory. The vaccines protect the population and lessen the effect of the virus when transmissioned. It will protect the vulnerable populations as transmission will not be as severe. The side effects of the vaccine are lesser than the actual coronavirus. If it will not create issues then people should get vaccinated. It has been shown to be effective. By having everyone immunised, it prevents another pandemic from breaking out, as the virus can be contained.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: The UK should mandate COVID-9 vaccinations as this would help reduce the amount of people suffering the effects of COVID-19, with an emphasis on hospitalisations. The NHS is still recovering from the height of the pandemic and unrelated setbacks, showcasing the importance of vaccinations to relieve some stress. Other vaccinations are mandated in the UK already thus it is proven to be possible. This would not only help the individual receiving the vaccine, but also those around, especially those who cannot have the vaccine who already deal with issues of immune disorders. COVID-19 is not of the past but is still prevalent, especially during the winter months.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
With the jobs market as it is, demand far outstrips supply when it comes to job-hunting, which puts employers at a huge advantage. They know they can hire and fire at will, more or less. They also keep employees on temporary contracts, and take whatever the cheapest option is for them. Temping is one example. Why is it that in this country workers can be kept on as 'temps' for months, years, even? Temps who work through agencies don't have the same rights as company employees and this is why the company chooses to hire them this way. A union would probably have the powers to do something about this. In other countries for example, the employer can only keep soeone as a temp for about 3 months, then they either hire them, or don't 

One thing that people might not know about belonging to a union is that as well as protecting employees, it also has other perks like legal advice, or help with things like writing a will, etc.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I am very pro-union, as workers can better protect and fight for their rights as  a collective, which increases bargaining power. This has been shown through out history (both UK and worldwide), as many rights and protections we now take for granted (paid leave, maternity leave, the length of the working week) have been fought for by unions. Stronger unions are needed at the moment, with their status protected in the UK, as many International corporations have used union busting tools elsewhere, and can discriminate against unionising, led by billionaires and oligarchs. We mustn't let this happen in the UK, as it contrasts with the values of our society.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I believe the UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions, and I would rate my agreement of 90 out of 100. Stronger unions are essential for protecting workers rights, ensuring fair pay, safe working conditions, and reasonable working hours, especially in sectors where employees are more vulnerable to exploitation. By balancing between employees, unions help prevent abuses and promote more equitable treatment across the workforce. They also contribute to economic justice and come equality , and supporting collective bargaining that uplifts low and high earners voice in key decisions that affect their lively hood and well being.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
I think that by strengthening the rights and powers of labour unions it would be a positive move as workers rights have decreased in recent years and this now needs to reversed. 
Wages have also fallen in recent years for workers, while CEO's, company directors, managers and shareholders incomes have increased beyond what is reasonable and fair, and this imbalance needs to be corrected in a much more balanced way so that everyone can have a good standard of living for their contributions to society and peoples way of living. only labour unions can work to make these changes for everyone.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Strengthening labour unionjs rights and powers would bring significant benefits for workers and society. Unions play a crucial role in protecting employees from unfair treatment, securing better pay and working conditions, and giving a voice to those who might otherwise be unheard, especially in low wage or precarious jobs. Stronger unions can help create a healthier balance of power between workers and employers, encouraging fiarer workplaces and reducing inequality. At the same time, I think it's important that this strenghtening is done thoughtfully, with mechanisms in place to prevent excessive disruption to essential services or the wider economy. Ultimately, well suported unions can contribute to a more just, stable and productive society.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
Trade unions are important to protect the rights and the welfare of the employees that are members of such unions. They protect employees from unscrupulous employers, and employers willing to take advantage of their workforce.
However, on the other hand, we must be careful not to grant to them extra rights and powers. This is because of the recent history of trade unions. For example, in the late 1970s, the trade unions had much power, which it can be said- they abused. This resulted in the famous, "Winter of Discontent" in 1978, which brought Margaret Thatcher to power as PM. It paralysed the country, its public services and also the economy. More recently there have been numerous strikes in the civil service, customs, railways and health and education services- granting even more rights increases the odds of even more disruption.
Overall, the status quo is preferable.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: In the 1970s the strength of the unions was very much to the advantage of the employees but to the detriment of the industries. Many days of productivity were lost through strikes and businesses struggled to change structure to meet new requirements. To my mind there is value in union membership in many ways but  when the rights and powers are increased it can lead to problems in resolving issues. If we have a government who look to ensuring the job security and pay for employees the unions should need no increased rights and powers. I am more inclined to ensuring we have that level of government who will be answerable to the electorate.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I do not fully understand the current situation with labour unions in the UK, nor do I anticipate exactly what the implications of strengthening the labour unions with more rights and powers. I cannot think that it would be a bad thing to do this though. More rights and powers for employees in general could have a positive impact on the workplace. On the other hand, giving more power and rights to labour unions could complicate the structure of many companies, particularly the smaller ones. This could potentially lead to loss of profits due to fines and employee strikes which would not be good.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I am indifferent on the issue of strengthening the rights and powers of labours an unions. On one side of the argument the change could allow for workers and trade unions to have more power and this would help protect worker rights such as pay rates and working hours . The knock on impact of this could lead to a decline in the inequality in society as the working class are able to get necessary pay increases. However these changes could also have a negative impact as trade unions and workers could abuse the power and lead to strikes and other forms of protest against changes that are necessary for businesses to function.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
I feel indifferent to this statement primarily because I am not too familiar with labour unions and the work they do. I understand and appreciate the principle but I don't know what exactly they do and how this benefits people. It sounds like a good idea to have them (and give them rights and powers) in principle but I'm concerned about those who aren't part of one (and choose not to be). Are they afforded less rights as an employee? Who is representing them? 

I'm kind of hesitant about the idea of strengthening their powers, despite my lack of knowledge. It would be good to know what kind of powers specifically and the ramifications of them. There's need to be some mechanism to protect exploiting it.

Ultimately, I am undecided

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I don't really know enough about unions to have an informed opinion on this subject. I do however remember the 70s and 80s where unions seemed to have huge power and the issues of secondary picketing. I am not sure what further rights we are talking about. As far as I am aware everyone has the right to join a union and gets to vote on any action the union takes. This seems like a democratic way of doing things and I agree with the general principles. I have never been in a union so I am not sure what the further issues are.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
Unions traditionally represent the working class and are the spokesmen for working class people.  Unions have traditionally supported and organised their members where they may have problems representing themselves.  The working classes are generally in labour type roles and probably don't get together as often as white collar workers in order to discuss their wants and needs.  In losing a lot of our manufacturing we have lost a lot of our workforce in these roles and our working classes have somewhat diminished and have lost their voice to some degree.  

Memberships of unions have declined a lot over recent years, and therefore have lost their powers with loss of members.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
employee rights are great, however these must be leveled with the ability of the employer to agree and negotiate. An example could be that staff want a  pay rise of 50% however the company can only offered 10%. how is that balanced.

SO should there be an increase in rights, however coupled with training and advice lines for unions. so as to up skills union reps, and provide a resource for the company to discuss with.

Would union reps be able to explain the new rules, when they come into force and how they can be applied, and what happens if the rules are not followed.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I have mixed feelings about the idea of strengthening the rights and powers of labour unions and I can see both the pros and cons to doing so. On one hand, unions can be beneficial to employees by providing them legal protection and advice in the event that anything goes wrong in the workplace. However, they can also be responsible for employees feeling under pressure to conform to the union, such as taking strike action for example, even if they disagree. Overall there are arguments for and against. I would ultimately put the power with the individual rather than encouraging any formal strengthening beyond what already exists.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: There is more employees in Uk if they feel their rights are protected. More employee means more productivity and it is boosting the economy.I think UK s Labour Union were in weak position think about zero hours contracts, low sickness pay, bad working surroundings. Since workers rights were not protected lots of people left employment, more unemployment is problem for the government. Creating well paid vacancies encourage higher spending habits and economical growth. It is beneficial for employees mental , emotional and physical well being when they employed by a safe and secure working place where their rights are well represented

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: The UK should strengthen labour unions for a few different reasons - mainly the point that morale in the UK and especially with the working man is at an all time low so the government should be thinking of the importance to make the most fair decisions that benefit the majority of the country to bring back positivity and equality amongst all. With that being said the power should only be increased to the power of what other political unions currently possess so everyone is at an even playing field and there aren't comparisons of fairness and equality to be made between unions.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I think that people in this day of age feel extremely entitled. Everything seems to be an issue to everyone at the moment. I think that strengthening the rights and powers of labour unions is a slippery slope for many reasons. We need to work on increasing our sense of duty and responsibility; not providing more avenues for people to complain and play the system. The labour unions have enough power at present to provide fair rights to workers; what we don't want to do is tip that balance too much so that the feelings and opinions of workers outweigh the necessities of jobs and employment.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: unions already have a lot of power and regularly exercise that power in a way which is, in my opinion, overbearing. look at the RMT union, constantly pushing for higher wages and other things simply just because they can. Rail is already constantly coming to a near standstill because of this, if union rights are expanded, other sectors may come to a standstill too. Doctors are already constantly striking too, if they had more powers i believe this sector could come to a standstill too and this would not be a good thing. Who chooses which sectors get these strengthened powers? unions by design are not governmental agencies. so how does the government choose which unions get these excess powers? how is this legislated for?

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I think we should abolish the unions.  We pay a lot of money for them to do nothing.  The salaries they pay their staff are much higher than other industries.  They are supposed to work for the staff, but most of them work with the government and whilst this is not all unions, a lot of them are corrupt and either in certain clubs together or brown envelopes.  I have witnessed many things about unions, and one of my former staff members worked for a union.  I wish they did work like in the 1980s, but they don't seem to be worth a penny now.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: We need much greater protections for workers in this country . We used to have a lot more of a bargaining tool over big business in decades gone by but those have been steadily diminished over time . In terms of rights employees need to be able to band together as a unit and fight for better terms and conditions for their workforce , and for workers as a whole in our country. Companies cannot be allowed to trample over our rights as they see fit . Unions raise moral and bring a sense of community to workers ; who understand that other peers have their backs in times of dspute.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Strengthening the rights and powers of labour unions can significantly benefit workers by improoving their rights, workplace safety, and overall conditions. Unions serve as a collective voice, ensuring that employees have the ability to negotiate fairly with employers, which helps reduce exploitation and promote fair wages. Additionally, stronger unions often contribute to better job security and working conditions, leading to increased employee satisfaction and productivity. However, it is important to balance this with the needs of businesses, as excessive union power could reduce flexibility for employers to adapt quickly to changing economic conditions, potentially impacting innovation and growth. Overall, empowering unions thoughtfully can create a more equitable work environment without sacrificing economic vitality.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: currently there is many loopholes companies are using to abuse workers for example in zero hour contracts workers can randomly face 0 hours at work when being told they would regularly get 20 hours or be told that if they dont accept work they will not receive hours in the future which is abusive to the workers, the only way this could be fixed is with unions having more powers to stop such instances from happening. this would then help workers mental health as many workers face stress during these types of senarios causing low job satisfaction, affecting their mental wellbeing

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
I am part of a labour union, and feel that my voice is better represented by a union on topics that affect me eg pay, conditions of work. The union will have better bargaining powers than individuals on their own. They can negotiate better working conditions and pay deals. They can help represent you in disciplinary meetings. 
They also provide training which is relevant to my job and keep me updated with any changes in the law and scientific research. This is important, for me, to be working under current recommendations and within the law. They help to campaign for better government contracts for my job.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: As a general rule, I'm opposed to the prevalence of labour unions in society. This is particularly true under governments aligned with labour unions (e.g., Labour), as there is a very apparent line of normalised and institutionalised corruption, namely in that labour unions tend to fund parties which form these governments, followed by the government increasing the role and prominence of labour unions when in office. Strengthening the rights and powers of labour unions would simply perpetuate this problem. Furthermore, I do not believe that labour unions actually achieve much; they are mainly political entities which utilise the money from their members to achieve political goals (as aforementioned). While I would support the formation of groups which have a genuine interest in the well-being of those within a given industry or organisation (cf. medieval guilds), I do not believe that trade unions aptly fulfil this role.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: As I remarked in my bullet points I think it would be a huge mistake to strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions. Unions already have too much power which they tend to exercise in an overtly political way primarily by using strike action in key sectors of the economy and public sector to try to hold the government to ransom. We have seen examples of that with the strikes by doctors and nurses and by the rail unions. As most unions are affiliated to the Labour Party this tends to especially manifest itself when there is a Conservative govt. The prime example of this from history were the strikes of the early 1970s which effectively brought down the Heath government. Unions should not have the power to bring down a democratically elected government. Historically unions have also used their power to preserve restrictive practices and resist modernization. This has had a devastating effect on Britain's productivity and competitiveness over many decades. Granting unions more rights and powers would exacerbate these problems and be damaging to both democracy in the UK and to the UK economy.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: As paymasters to the Labour party, at the moment Government, they have them over a barrel when it comes to policy, they would never admit it.  The number of public sector pay rises in the last 11 months has been far greater due to this relationship.  The Unions make far more noise in representing public sector than in the private, it says it all.  Yes they are out of touch, the Train drivers strikes made no logical sense when they were paid higher than average, causing total disruption to people who use trains daily. Public transport should be categorised as an essential service and not, by law, allowed to strike. The leaders of the unions are all Labour party members and traditionally Labour are left wing, so they practice their ideology.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: the use of nuclear bombs would not be desirable because the result would be absolutely devastating for the population of the earth. Nuclear bombs are so destructive that we should hope and desire that they are never used. If nuclear bombs were used widely ( and the use of one bomb would more than likely set off a chain) the earth and it's population would never recover. I believe that they are so destructive that they act as a deterrent to greater conflicts and in that respect they are useful.  But to commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs would have the same effect as not actually having nuclear capabilities and therefore in some respects removes the deterrent for greater conflicts.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
Having read Hershey's Hiroshima, and learned about the human suffering caused by such a weapon, we should do as New Zealand do, and not have any or use any. These weapons should not be used by any country, anywhere, ever. They should all be disposed of, but they are not, because of fear and (legitimate) distrust of certain rogue states who'd gladly take advantage of the situation if they could. 
But there is an argument that a nuclear weapon (or several, as in our case) may act as a deterrent. I'm not sure this is the case, but it does kind of make sense at some level. But the number of countries in the world who have hundreds of nukes, not to mention all the so-called broken arrows that have ended up in the sea, is just ridiculous. Isn't there something like 50 tons of TNT per each person in the world?

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I feel that this is a subject that should never be needed to talk about but here we are. It scares me and know this is the route we are all going down. With so many countries cautious and having a just in case approach eventually it's going to end up in the wrong hands of someone that will cause disastrous events and a new war. When it comes to this kind of thing you can't be the only one without but then no good will ever come from this. The world is going mad and the less I know about this the better.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: There are some countries that would probably try and hit us if they thought they could get away with it, it is sad that this is true but nether the less it is true. There are certain countries for example Iran, hates the west, if they thought they could bomb UK with nuclear weapons and not get any comuppance then they would have done it already, the only thing stopping countries like Iran from blowing us to smithereens is the thought that we would get them back. It is like a means of protection, with no plan to use it but always have them with people that are not trustworthy about.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: To commit to never using nuclear bombs is a hard to do as we don't know what the future holds and what conflicts might arise. I'm sure nobody wants the UK to ever use a nuclear bomb but we need to retain the ability to do so as a deterrent and to put us on the same footing as other big powers. It would be a terrible decision to have to make and would mean 'sacrificing' our own natives and part of the country to save others. It's hard to imagine any sane person making that decision in a civilised society but by saying we won't ever then that makes the Uk vulnerable.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
- In some instances, retaliation is not the best policy. However, on the subject of nuclear power, a country has to be stopped in its tracks if it sadly chooses to go down that route. UK residents lives and the lives of their families are under threat. It is a time when action needs to be taken.
- More and more countries are 'dabbling' with nuclear energy and are idly throwing out the threat of using it on their enemies. All it needs is one hot head with power to press the button and a chain reaction will begin. One which , if started, will be very difficult to stop. Certainly, the consequences of such an action do not bear thinking about. It paints a very bleak picture globally.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: The case regarding nuclear weapons is a tricky one with no clear cut answer. The case will be made by nations that having nukes provides them with a safety net and a means of defence, which prevents them from being attacked by another nuclear armed nation. Those that do not have them may see the stance as hypocritical because ultimately, who should and should not have nuclear weapons? For example the US is the only nation to have actually used a nuclear bomb, yet have a leading voice, in deciding who else should and should not have them. There are indeed a handful of nations that have these weapons including the UK, but when you look at the track record of how many wars have been started by these nations, why should they have nukes? Other nations may see that as a threat. Living in the UK, i think it is better to have them than not, but in the event that they are actually used, then both nations will suffer unimaginable destruction. So is it not a better policy for all nuclear armed nations to disarm, therefore the threat of being attacked by nukes is zero?

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I am strongly opposed to nuclear warfare. There is no avoiding the certain deaths of innocent people here. It is mass murder, whatever the supposed reasoning is - targeting a specific person or group is impossible with a nuclear bomb. Further, the knock on effects of a nuclear bomb globally would be devastating. The aftereffects could lead to a nuclear winter, meaning that climate and sunlight worldwide would be affected, potentially affecting crops, which could lead to worldwide food shortages and famine. People who are exposed to the radiation of the bomb, even if not killed by it, could end up suffering from poisoning, cancers, disfigurement - and their future generations could also be affected.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
Firstly, the damage, destruction and loss of life is catastrophic! The impact of nuclear weapons will be felt not just in the targeted area, but for miles around. The loss of life will be absolutely terrible. and not a nice way to go if you are not killed instantly, radiation, cancer, slow death etc.
There is no need to go nuclear, there are other ways to deal with conflict if need be without it. Negotiations are the best way forward first and foremost, there is just no reason for any country to use nuclear, and it will open up the door for other countries to use them too, and that would be a disaster.
There is also the environmental impact caused by the radiation, so much wildlife habitat would be lost, it's not just humans who will lose out.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.

The only people not getting killed are the politicians who press the big red buttons. Nothing is worth a war over, not land, resources or money. I am a pacifist so there was only going to be one answer.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.

As for the UK making a policy to never use nuclear bombs I do agree with the sentiment but keep it to yourselves. Other geared up countries don't need to be aware that we wont do messed up things. rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I strongly agree that the UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances. These weapons cause unimaginable human suffering and environmental devastation, with consequences that last for generations. There is no scenario where their use could be morally justified. By adopting a no-use policy, the UK would demonstrate global leadership in the pursuit of peace and disarmament, reinforcing its commitment to international law and humanitarian values. The idea that nuclear weapons ensure security through deterrence is deeply flawed, as it relies on the constant threat of mass destruction and leaves room for deadly misjudgments or accidents. True security comes not from fear, but from diplomacy, cooperation, and investment in conventional defence strategies and conflict prevention. A firm stance against the use of nuclear weapons would help build a safer and more stable world

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.

If there is any issue it can be tackled using non violence. It should be the first option before a nuclear bomb can be used. It is not the right way of handling any issue.  There can be other methods too. It is not peaceful in any way.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: Any use of nuclear weapons would lead to humanitarian disaster. The impact in short term would be huge including massive casualties, widespread destruction and long term consequences such as environmental devastation and disruption to the climate. Also it could trigger a retaliatory strike, leading to a full scale nuclear exchange between nuclear armed countries/ states which could have catastrophic consequences for the whole world. Also there is a case for the nuclear weapons being immoral as they can kill innocent civilians and a commitment to not use them aligns with a higher moral standard and demonstrates a dedication to peace.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: We should work towards prevention of war through better and safer means rather than through mutually assured destruction, as the threat of nuclear fallout is far too great, it is an existential threat that cannot be treated lightly, or as some pawn in a political game. We should look towards making peace treaties with foreign enemies and allies and look toward protecting the common good and general safety of all the citizens of the world rather than of just the citizens of a given nation. We should work together to achieve an equitable society, not have to worry about nuclear fallout.

What we know from incidents like Chernobyl is that the after effects of a nuclear bomb  are  far reaching going though the infected DNA and down through the generations. The higher rate of birth defects. 

If the UK was to use a nuclear bomb then if we bombed a country who also had nuclear bombs then it is very likely they would retaliate with a nuclear bomb too. All of those innocent lives lost in 2 countries and countless more people suffering in the aftermath of the nuclear fallout. 

We should look for other ways to end conflicts and not resort to nuclear bombs.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.

It is sad to observe that the use of force is still used as a means of 'getting your own way' rather than negotiation and mutual respect that was the principals of the United Nations, as set up after the World War two. rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
I do believe using nuclear bombs should be avoided at all costs as they cause destruction and suffering which ultimately impacts civilians way more than military targets. The long term environmental damage and human casualties make it ethically indefensible under most circumstances. 
However, in an extremely rare emergency situation where the UK's population was under an immediate, severe threat, it may be appropriate to use a nuclear bomb to prevent an even greater loss of life. Any decision to use nuclear weapons should be made with extreme caution as this does not only affect the current citizens but future generations as well.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: Having grown up in the 80s the threat of nuclear war seemed very real to me with programmed like 'Threads'. However, if the UK were to commit to such a definitive position, I feel it could leave us exposed and therefore, vulnerable to attack from any other country that possesses nuclear weapons. Similarly, just having nuclear weapons is enough of a deterrent (particularly with mutually assured destruction) so I really don't see what value there would be in committing to such a definitive position as it would be seen as a weakness and I'm not convinced that we would stick to it when faced with an attack.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.

If we committed to never use nuclear bombs, it would mean Trident would be stopped, leaving the UK vulnerable to nuclear attack should another country go nuclear. The global risks to this country given the current circumstances in the Middle East and Ukraine highlight the importance of a deterrent. Nuclear bombs are a terrible reality and should only be the final option, but to say under any circumstances could have catastrophic consequences for the UK's defence.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I agree with the statement to a large extent.  However, we need to be prepared to defend ourselves.  If the world believes we are too 'soft' and will not use nuclear weapons under any circumstances, I believe we are 'sitting ducks' for attack.  I think using bombs is a very last resort.  However, if someone fires at us, should we just take it and die? I don't think so.  I believe, we have to stand tall and let the world know we are strong and will use the weapons only if we are fired at first.  We must have a defence deterrent

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: This ridiculous and ridiculously costly idea ignores all the benefits colonial civilising brought to the world outside the first world.  It is a kneejerk reaction from people who grieve the concept of slavery (first promulgated by many civilisations thousands of years before the UK and European powers got involved in it) and think there is money to turn the clock back, and be paid as recompense.  This is unaffordable, and unjustifiable.  The distance between the slavery trade and now has diminished the impact it had on the slaves, and the benefits of the slavers, and they should not be punished by ancestral actions.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I understand the need to make some sort of amends for colonisation and also understand the thought behind it, I also think it's going to be a minefield that could be a lose-lose either way. For a start, how will it be decided who gets reparations? If it's individual people, then what makes you eligible? I would say not many people alive today are currently negatively affected by past colonisation but for those who would say they are, how would you even measure and prove this? There's also the fact of how do we decide how much reparations someone is entitled to? Does everyone get a set amount of is it dependant on how much they've been affected? This is another factor that would probably cause huge amounts of uproar. We've also got to consider if Britain pays reparations for this then shouldn't other countries pay reparations for similar things? And in that case, where does it end? Plus, so many British citizens are struggling due to cost of living, it seems a bit rubbish if we were to pay reparations but we can't somehow decrease the amount of current British citizens currently living in poverty.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I don't agree that the UK should pay reparations for what happened during colonisation. These events took place many years ago now and most of the people involved either as perpetrators or victims, are not around anymore. Furthermore, people alive today would have to pay for something they are not responsible for. I also don't see how money would resolve the problem, it can't change history. It would be better to learn from the mistakes that were made and ensure certain things never happen again than to pay people who were not alive at the time at the expense of those who are alive today.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.

What about any support provided to date, would that be ofsett against reparations? Would we expect affected persons or countries to reset back to the date that problems began? rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I do not agree with providing reparations for colonialism, I am more willing to offer an apology. It is reasonable to point to the fact that some nations were colonies in the 1950-60's, but the real 'age' of colonialism had passed long before this. The last direct colonies were added I believe by League of Nations mandate in the wake of the first world war making even the oldest citizens alive children then. It does not seem fair to ask for people today to pay for the potential wrongdoing of their forebears. The question of reparations if opened wider would see a flurry of claim and counterclaim between nations; being the only nation to do the 'right thing' is not in the national interest. It is almost impossible to calculate the true figure, for instance should we make allowance for infrastructure that was left or for ending practices like sati.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.

I feel that apology for the country's colonial past might be welcome but essentially it would be an empty gesture. It would mean much more, perhaps, were it supported by financial investment in formerly colonised areas (ensuring independent growth, not ties to Britain), increase in overseas aid, disaster preparation, poverty relief, as well as educational initiatives (literacy, business skills etc), health-related areas, peace initiatives, and environmentally-oriented support.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I am bemused that someone has made this statement.  Colonisation brought benefits to the countries colonised.  Without the UK, they would have continued to have a poor standard of living. Should France, Portugal and Spain also apologise?  I think not!  There is no explanation over what this apology should cover and why one is due.  This is political correctness to an extreme! rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The proposal is an example of virtue-signalling nonsense that posits the moral and logical absurdity that people of today are responsible for the societies of yesterday and that those who have never been slave owners pay money to those who were never slaves. The conception is unilateral and ignores real history. Ethiopia did not abolish slavery until 1943, Mauretania 1981. The West African empire of Benin built its economy on slavery and practiced slave sacrifice and more black people were enslaved by Arabs than by white people. All Turks are colonisers, African countries still have ethnic inequalities. Yet none of these are asked to do any apologising. Britain was the first country to attempt to outlaw slavery abroad and thousands of sailors died ending the slavery of the Barbery Coast slavers. It's colonising was far less destructive than those of many other cultures.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The premise is a specious one. The UK was itself many times colonised; should the UK be seeking reparations from France, Germany, Norway and Italy for the colonising work of the Normans, Saxons, Vikings and Romans? How far back do we go? What about degrees of colonisation? Next how do we value the BENEFITS that colonisation historically brought and continues to bring? Benefits of technology, industrialisation, medicine and healthcare? Social reform? Might the colonies end up paying the UK for colonisation? Who qualifies and how? Does Hong Kong qualify - the country that gave China a port to 21st century industrialisation via import and export? Does this mean Hong Kong should pay the UK "inverse reparations"? There is nothing for the UK to apologise for - countries that pillaged cheap labour for slavery (leading to their own civil war and racial problems even today) faced intervention as the UK used it's vast navy to surpress the evils of slavery - should Africa pay reparations to the UK for doing this? What about Egypt, who kept both white and black slaves - should Egypt pay reparations? After all, the pyramids didn't build themselves! The premise of making apologies and paying reparations is a nonsense, promulgated by those who hate the UK.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: Events throughout history need to be understood in their full context and not judged solely on modern standards.  Whilsst colinisation involved exploitation and suffereing, it also contributed to the development of infrastructure, legal systems and global connection.  If we were to apologise, it would risk over simplifying history and promoting a sense of inherited guilt among people who were not even involved.  Efforts should focuss on meaningfull partnerships between one another, education , and shared progress.  A balanced understanding og history allow societies to acknowledge past injustices while also recognising the complexity of history and focusing on the future together

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The idea of the UK apologising and offering reparations for the impacts of colonisation comes from a place of wanting to acknowledge real historical harm, and that's completely understandable. Many countries and communities were affected by British rule , and recognising that suffering is important. However, formally apologising and offering reparations is a lot more complicated in practise. The people directly involved are long gone, and today's taxpayers weren't responsible for what happened centuries ago. There are also challenges in deciding who exactly should receive reparations and in what form.  It's crucial to teach the full history honestly, support affected communities today and promote fairness, but formal reparations might not be the most efficient way to do that

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: Even though colonisation had many negative effects on the world, the fact is that it is a past issue. I am not certain how a formal apology could help, or how reparations are making up for it. The effects are already in place and, for better or for worse, the world is as it is now, in part, because of it. Instead of looking towards the past, I believe we should look into the future and make sure past mistake are not repeated. Not only that, but the UK could and should try to help other countries maintain their freedom and identity, so that other countries cannot make the same mistake (or cause similar effects with other practices) as the UK did in the past.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text:
Whilst colonisation is part of history, it's part of our own history which we now have the opportunity to make amends for in some way, so I do feel that apologising is the least we can do.

However, our current and previous government don't seem to have enough of a handle on domestic issues and international threats, let alone be able to deal with reparations relating to historical issues. You could look at the Windrush scandal and see that there is a close-to-home issue which needs a huge apology and reparations, but not enough is being done to ensure this happens in the lifetime of those directly affected by it. 

It does feel that, as the UK is not alone in the whole historical act of colonisation, that perhaps this could be done at an international level, with apologies and reparation agreements in collaboration with other countries which also colonised parts of the world and have not yet taken responsibility for it. By its nature, this is a global issue and it needs to be managed in a way which ensures that groups of people are cared for and respected as the action takes place to make amends for the past, not further marginalised and disrespected by it.

If apology and reparation by the UK as a nation is agreed, then groups within the UK (such as the Church) should also be required to look at their own behaviour in the past to identify their own culpability. Missionary work was not always about bringing aid to stricken countries but often about 'saving souls' and progressing the reach of the church, essentially colonisation disguised under the banner of religion. This is an issue which there seems to be significant silence on and I feel it should be part of the same conversation.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text:
Acknowledging the harm caused:
The lasting negative impacts of colonialism on former colonies are undeniable, including economic exploitation, political instability, and social disruption in some parts of the world. This has not only caused damage but has also affected those nations in ways that are hard to recover from. The UK played a major role in creating exploitative systems that displaced local industries, suppressed indigenous cultures, and contributed to the widespread poverty and inequality in those regions. A formal apology and reparations would demonstrate a commitment to recognizing these historical injustices. while such actions cannot undo the past, they acknowledge the real suffering caused and signal a willingness to take moral responsibility.

Seeking reconciliations and healing
A sincere, formal apology from the UK would acknowledge the harm caused by colonialism and begin the reconciliation process. This act of recognition is important for the healing process. This will further increase the trust between the UK and the nations that were affected by colonization. Many descendants of those affected by colonization still feel the legacy of that era in social, political, and psychological ways, especially in regions like Africa. This has created distrust and resentment towards the UK. Public recognition of this pain can open the door to meaningful dialogue, healing, and reconciliation. Without acknowledgement, the wounds of colonialism remain unaddressed. Apologies are a necessary step toward building trust and acknowledging the dignity of those who suffered.

Addressing the issues created,
Reparation, though, may be hard to implement, but the UK can decide to invest in education, infrastructure, and support cultural preservation in these nations. And this may help address some of the chaos created by colonization. Many post-colonial nations still face inequities that are rooted in colonial policies, such as underdeveloped infrastructure and limited access to education. Reparations could help redress these long-term consequences and offer farmer colonies the tools to overcome systemic disadvantages. So many things were damaged, supporting efforts to rebuild what was damaged or taken, the UK would be contributing to global equity and fairness.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.

The UK should formally apologise to all countries that it has previously ruled and whose assets it plundered.  Indigenous people who were enslaved need to be compensated for the destruction of the lives of their ancestors.    Assets stolen from other country's during the colonization process need to be returned.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text:
Putin is apparently set on expansion of Russia into a greater entity resembling the old USSR, and is pursuing this through covert and hybrid means from disinformation and interference in foreign elections to cyber-crime and espionage as well as traditional hard force.  Where possible he influences regime change in eastern european countries towards more sympathetic leadership.  And as in Crimea, he will invade with unmarked combatants then suppress resistance and install puppet administrations.  The Western countries, lulled by decades of post-Cold War peace reacted supinely and the result was the invasion of Ukraine in 2022.  That failed but the eastern 20% of the country is now de-facto under Russian control.
Without adequate support from its European neighbours it will ultimately face the same fate.  This is morally wrong in consideration of its population, and of the next countries to be targeted.  The UK must face its responsibilities in a troubled world and do all it can to preserve Ukraine as an independent, sovereign and democratic country or murderous thuggery will have succeeded and the UK may be next.  NATO exists for a reason.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: UKRAINE'S SOVEREIGNTY IS UNDER DIRECT THREAT, AND SUPPORTING THEM HELPS DEFEND INTERNATIONAL LAWS THAT PROTECT INDEPENDENT NATIONS FROM BEING INVADED. IF COUNTRIES ALLOW SUCH AGGRESSION TO GO UNCHECKED, IT SENDS A DANGEROUS SIGNAL THAT BORDERS CAN BE VIOLATED WITHOUT CONSEQUENCES. STRONG MILITARY AID ALSO DISCOURAGES FURTHER ESCALATION BY RUSSIA OR SIMILAR ACTIONS FROM OTHER COUNTRIES IN THE FUTURE. BY PROVIDING SUPPORT, THE UK STRENGHTHENS ITS ALLIANCES, SHOWING COMMITMENT TO NATO AND GLOBAL PARTNERSHIPS, WHICH HELPS MAINTAIN COLLECTIVE SECURITY. ADDIOTIONALLY, ONGOING CONFLICT IN UKRAINE HARMS EUROPEAN STABILITY, AFFECTING ECONOMIES AND INTERNATIONAL RELATIONS. INCREASING MILITARY SUPPORT COULD HELP BRING THE WAR TO A QUICKER RESOLUTION, REDUCING SUFFERING AND LONG TERM INSTABILITY.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: While the UK government appears focussed on pandering to Reform voters on trivial issues such as immigration, the threat from Russia is one of the two great dangers (along with climate change) that require leadership and action to avert existential threats. If Ukraine falls, then Moldova and the Baltic states will be next, followed by Poland, and other western democracies. It is therefore crucial the Ukraine is either in a position to win this war, or at the very least to not lose it, thereby increasing Russian casualties and pushing back the date at which they would be in a position to advance on another flank. The urgency is greater because of the venal naivety of the US President, who is beholden to our enemy and insults our allies. The actual and potential shortfall caused by the dereliction of the US administration is within the capability of Europe to fill, and it must do so, with the UK playing a major part. If that means taxes rising, so be it.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: the uk should increase military support to ukrain in response to the 2022 russian invasion for both political and moral reasons. there has been a longstanding alliance and regardless of not  being a part of the EU anymore, so i believe there is still a major political responsibility to stand with your global allies. by showing and proving the support for ukraine it reinforces that the UK does not stand for political bullying and unprovoked warfare. not only does russia hold many more military advances in comparison to ukraine, they have needlessly and repeatedly provoked the ukraine with aggression and the impact of threat coming from russia in comparison to ukraine is incredibly imbalanced. with the uk providing more military assistance to ukraine, it represents solidarity, defends democratic values, and with more support it reiterates that ukranian lives & historical values are important.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: As a member of NATO and a democratic nation we have a duty to protect the democratic values and ideals which we uphold. We should help Ukraine to defend itself against an attack on democracy. Russia's unprovoked attack and attempt to seize Ukraine for expansionist purposes (resources, strategically important geographic location, objection to NATO) is something we should reject and defend against. It's important to ensure that Russia doesn't succeed to is incentivised to continue trying to expand it's borders. Russia is potentially a threat to European territory. Finland, Poland and many other democratic nations share borders with Russia. War anywhere in the world potentially disrupts trade but more so with nearby countries with whom we trade important commodities such as oil and gas.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

If we sit by and do nothing, then we should expect the same treatment if we were in a similar situation, which is not what we'd want if it were us. It comes down to basic human decency and doing the right thing. If we sit idly by we're complicit.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

Ukraine is important to all of Europe and Britain should continue to support Zelensky through supplies of weapons and training. rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

The people of Ukraine have fought valiantly and have succesfully stifled Putins plans to firstly blitzkreig ther land and to take over its governance. rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
