rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: The government proposes things like this as they believe it will force people to make healthier choices, but I honestly don't think that will be the outcome. Yes, people may buy cheaper products due to this and yes, that may result in them buying a healthier product as it's cheaper but often people will just suck It up and pay the higher price. People have ro want to get healthier, forcing them is not the way. With the current cost of living crisis the way it is and the high number of people living in poverty, this is just adding Insult to injury. Is this what the government should be focusing on right now? Or should it be focusing on financial equality and dealing with the cost-of-living crisis?

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: The UK government is already vastly bloated and interferes almost daily with our innate freedoms. Freedom of choice , freedom of speech, freedom of sexuality and almost everything else. I am a firm believer in small government and implementation of personal responsibility. Most of this stems from the family unit which inspires mutually respectful behaviour towards fellow citizens. Frankly it is my opinion that taxes on sugary drinks or anything else which is deemed bad for the populace, should be completely unnecessary. People should suffer the consequences of their own bad choices. The information is freely available on the internet.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: I gave my rating as I believe that simply taxing people more for unhealthy food is unlikely to make a significant impact. I am concerned that the tax is likely to hit low-income families hardest, as they tend to spend more on such products. Additionally, healthier food options are often more expensive than their lower nutritional alternatives, and I feel that's something which needs to be addressed. Furthermore, I feel there could be a growth in the black market for such products. I can see that the intention is well-meaning, but I'm not sure it will work in practical terms.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text:
The thought behind the tax is to help combat rising obesity rates, meaning the price makes it less likely that people will opt for that purchase. 
As we've seen since it's introduction, there have been a variety of additional alternative, low-calorie and sugar-free drink options, although I'm sure there will always be research that says that these are still bad for us. 
Some view it as a tax on fat people, when there are plenty of people that enjoy a sugary drink, perhaps even to help health issues such as diabetes, and so are now being charged.
As it is another tax, are we sure that the increased revenue is going towards promoting good health, or is it indeed just another revenue stream?

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

Health - Too much sugar is considered unhealthy for overall health. I cut refined sugar out of my diet and I feel so much better. You obtain natural sugar from fruit so your body doesnt actually need sugar although cakes etc taste delicious. 

Too expensive - People are not going to be able to afford to buy drinks which actually is a good thing.  However, the drinks industry will take a knock and then prices may ultimately increase further.  I dont think people will stop buying sugary drinks despite this and this will cause hardship for people having to find the extra money, although it may be a good deterrant.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

On the other hand, I think there are probably other places where taxes could be raised before sugary drinks are targeted. It might be good though, to make them more expensive so that people are deterred from buying them. From another perspective though, I wouldn't want sugary luxuries to become something that only richer people can afford.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

So it would be interesting to see if raising taxes will reduce the amount of sales and whether the general health of the people improves. I appreciate that sugar is not only included in sugary drinks and is in many other foods. Perhaps taxes should be raised on sweets also.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

The food industry in this country is a joke. We make healthy foods expensive and poor nutrition food dirt cheap, imo this has got to be the other way around, help food companies with producing nutritious foods, lower the taxes of food companies who produce quality whole foods to encourage them, and punish the companies who make ultra-processed foods. For me, this is simply the only way forward; however, nothing will change with the current government's focus on globalisation.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: Increasing tax on sugary drinks will will help to tackle the obesity crisis, which is putting a burden on the healthcare system. It will encourage people to buy healthier options. However, it does feel a like government overreach where the government acts like a nanny state and micromanages people's lives. If the government were to use the additional revenue to subsidise healthier food and drink options, it would further encourage healthier consumption and improve the health of the population overall, and reduce the burden on the healthcare system. Overall, I am in two minds about whether ensuring people eat and drink well should be the government's responsibility or whether it should be individual responsibility.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: I don't really agree with increasing taxes on sugary drinks - a sugar tax already exists (the Soft Drinks Industry Levy) which has successfully encouraged companies to reduce sugar content. Given the cost of living crisis, increasing these taxes further could be considered unfair, especially to low-income families who are already struggling with rising prices. There are better alternatives to increasing the tax - the government could invest more in education regarding nutrition or offer subsidies to make healthier food options more affordable. However, sugary drinks do contribute to rising levels of obesity  which puts extra pressure on the NHS so I understand why people may think this a reasonable first step to solving that problem

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

However, I also see it as unnecessary because if people want to buy and enjoy these drinks they will, regardless of the cost so I feel increasing taxes on other things is more important.

I have mixed views on this statement. I am not someone who drinks a lot of sugary drinks as I am able to control my intake, but if I want to enjoy one I will and don't want to pay more for a sugary drink over a non one.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

We should put more funding into solving the issue of child poverty which will in turn mean that parents can buy more healthy and more expensive flavorful meals and food and drink for their children. We should also look at ways to promote healthy eating in schools.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

It is our choice what we consume and if it is bad for us so be it. Adding more taxes to products we all use is wrong, our cost of living is rising at an alarming rate and there is no end in sight. The excuse of adding taxes to make us more healthy is wrong and in most cases a total waste of time.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: Raising taxes on sugary drinks isn't the way to go. It ends up hitting low-income families the hardest since they spend more of their income on more stuff like this. Plus, with the amount we're already taxed, adding another one just feels like too much.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

I feel that drinks containing sweeteners are more harmful than those with sugar so a tax on sugar is not helpful in terms of healthcare.  I think more research is needed and the root of the issue with obesity and health generally is needed.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

I don't believe it makes any difference and the Government should stop trying to nanny people.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: Raising tax on sugary drinks can be an easy, simple step to help people to make more healthy choices and can help to reduce issue like obesity and diabetes. If people buy less of these drinks it can lead to better health for people and reduce intervention by the health services. The money from the tax can be used to fund programes in healthy living and education. Increasing taxes could be an easy way to encourage healthier living and support helpful schemes all over the country. If people are not put off from buying less with the tax, then at least it can be used for education purposes.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: There is a well-documented correlation between consumption of sugary drinks and things like obesity, diabetes and dental decay. Applying a tax to sugary drinks would put pressure on producers to cut down on sugar and help us to re-educate our palate and accept healthier options instead. The revenue raised would, I hope, be used within the NHS and the whole exercise would relieve some of the pressure on the NHS having to deal with unnecessary problems associated with elevated consumption of sugar. Using plant based, natural sweeteners is quite an exciting thing to look forward to after the bland taste of sugary drinks of recent years.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: Increased taxes on sugary drinks will entice manufacturers to reduce sugar content in their products. At present they deliberately include sugar at high levels to enhance taste and make the products themselves more addictive. A reduction will have an immediate positive impact on the general health of consumers. Obesity levels, particularly among children, have increased over the years. A reduction in sugar consumption will lead to a reduction in obesity levels. Improved health will lead naturally to an easing of pressure on NHS services. and should free up NHS resources accordingly. Additionally there will be financial benefits due to reduced health spending on sugar/obesity related health issues.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

People should have the freedom to enjoy a drink of their choice, at a reasonable price, not be forced to choose cheaper alternatives which dont taste as good. A bit less sugar may be good, but taxing sugar is not good.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text:

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

One problem with this approach is that poor people will find their budgets even more stretched while rich people will be able to absorb a price increase without a problem. It will also hit hard those people who like to make their own wine or jam. I would prefer to see action taken on the sugar that is put in so many foodstuffs - often savoury ones - which are 'hidden' - people often consume a lot of sugar that way and their tastes are conditioned to expect foods to be sweeter than they are

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.
rows.text: I believe that the UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks because it would help improve public health and reduce the burden on the NHS. By making sugary drinks more expensive, people will be encouraged to reach for cheaper, more healthy alternatives such as water - leading to long-term improvements in diet and well-being. This would lower the prevalence of diet-related illnesses and reduce the costs sustained from them, saving money for other issues such as cancer and building more schools/ housing.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase taxes on sugary drinks.

However, poorer people are forced to buy the cheaper reformulated drinks and so take more artificial sweeteners into their diet. The effect on their future health is, as yet, unknown. It is more productive for the government to increase messaging about healthy eating and the benefits of including more fruit and vegetables into one's diet. They should also make sure that all children are taught how to cook using raw ingredients rather than them growing up relying on convenience foods.

Energy Security: Discouraging domestic oil production could increase the Uk's dependence on foreign energy sources, thus making the country  more vulnerable to global supply shocks.
High Inflation and consumer cost: Oil companies may pass higher tax cost onto consumers, increasing fuel and energy prices during a time when many households are already struggling with cost of living.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography.
rows.text: This is a difficult area to legislate effectively, and given the finite resources of legislator's time, one would need compelling evidence that it would avert substantial harms. Such evidence might - or might not - arise from either the rapid advances of A.I. which already provides, for instance, the means to create offensive material using photos of random individuals, but it is unclear whether existing legislation already covers such areas, or can easily be adjusted to do so. Alternatively, evidence from troubling instances such as the Pelicot case might merit consideration. That said, the list of material that governments and societies have banned - or considered banning - for indecency or moral harm over the centuries is very long indeed, including comics, literature etc. and legislators should be exceedingly wary of stepping into this area without clear evidence of a harm that can and should be prevented, and when parliamentary time and the credit balance of a government's approval rating are finite resources.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography.

Regulation of the production to ensure proper checks are carried out, age verification, health and wellbeing checks, maybe a route for anonymous whistleblowing, etc. would help protect the performers better.

Distribution is a bit of a tricky subject, with the vast array of sites on the internet, plus tools like VPNs, torrents, etc. it is almost impossible for any one government to restrict access to pornography in any meaningful way. rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography.
rows.text: Implementing stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography in the UK could offer important protections, especially for vulnerable groups such as minors. Such regulations would help prevent easy access to harmful or inappropriate content and combat the distribution of exploitative or non-consensual materials. Additionally, clearer standards could ensure that performers’ rights and safety are better safeguarded within the industry. However, it is important that these regulations strike a balance, as overly restrictive measures might infringe on adults' freedom of expression and access to legal content. Thoughtful regulation can protect individuals without unnecessarily limiting personal freedoms or creativity .

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography.
rows.text: I don't have a moral issue with the existence or production of pornography. Like moral panics about horror movies, heavy metal music and violent video games, I think the harms of pornography can sometimes be unnecessarily overstated. However, I'm still in favour of stricter regulation for two reasons. Firstly, pornography can be an exploitative business, especially for women working in the industry, and I would be in favour of anything that provides them better protections, working conditions, employment rights, etc. Second, I do think that pornography is too easily available these days, which means that people who are too young to responsibly consume it, including children who are sexually immature or insufficiently sexually educated, end up being confronted by ever more extreme porn.

lastly is protection of addictions. a porn addiction is real and is increasing in our environment due to it being so readily available. by implementing stricter regulations, it will have more hurdles to these addicts to reach what they want.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the production and distribution of pornography.
rows.text: I agree with the rationale for stricter regulation of pornography, as overconsumption and addiction is undoubtedly harmful and promotes unhealthy attitudes around sex and relationships. I would be in favour of greater regulation of pornography at the point of production, only. I would not feel comfortable supporting greater restrictions at the point of distribution, however, as this would only be possible through the erosion of online privacy. In order to access pornography, web users would have to sacrifice their anonymity to provide proof of age, banking information, etc. and I don't feel that this loss of privacy is worth it.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: My position on increasing the UK's spending to strengthen its role within NATO remains balanced. On the one hand, additional investment could help the UK meet its commitments and enhance its strategic influence globaly, particularly as new threats and geo political challengers emerge.  However, defence spending must be carefully considered in light of competing domestic priorities.  An increase in the military budget may limit the funding available for essential public services such as healthcare, education and environmental protection, which are critical to national wellbeing. There is no guarantee that higher spending leads to greater effectiveness making it important to choose what to prioritise .

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
With countries like Russia and Israel carrying out wars like we have not seen in decades it is important that the UK is able to defend itself and its allies in the event of these wars escalating. Should Russia attack and ally we need to ensure our forces and equipment are up to the job, along with modernising our technology for things like drones and other unmanned vehicles.
Our older equipment can be recycled and sold which will also reduce the expense of upgrading. 
While we have a relatively small armed forces, they need to be the best quality of troops and equipment we can produce.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: The world has become a more dangerous place. With Trump in the United States aligning more with Putin and showing that the media can be controlled with a fascist approach, it has emboldened other potential dictators and non democratic politicians to pursue their own agendas. This has made armed conflicts more likely, generally, but specifically concerning Russia, who seem to be able to influence President Trump. This has left a power vacuum, as in NATO it appears unlikely that the US would fulfil its role to protect other 'allies'. This vacuum needs to be filled, ideally with multiple nations, as there is less risk if many nations are all invested and can defend each other. If the UK increases its spending, that may lead by example and help to make the world a more secure place.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

I also think that relying on American weapon systems is very ricky and we should support a European arms industry including British companies.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

We also have so many other issues that need money and using money this way may take it away from other important issues

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

Basically, I do not think that a spending increase off hand is needed at this point, but if it is to maintain the UKs position then i would support it. This is why I am largely ambivalent to the proposal at this time, not because I think NATO is not important, just that I do not see the need to increase.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: It is probably very true that the threats to the UK has grown in recent years and the world is a more unstable place so it is a necessary evil that the UK should increase military spending to strengthen its role within NATO and to help strengthen NATO as a whole.  With the Trump Presidency, it is clear that the USA is a less reliable ally and the other countries in NATO need to increase their contributions as a consequence.  That all said, military spending is not a great use of finite resource and everything should be done to reduce the risk and flashpoints and therefore the need for more military spending.  And it is likely to be a false economy to increase military spending by reducing spending on overseas aid.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: It appears that over the past 50 years most wars as far as the UK are concerned have been proxy wars linked to oil and other resources. Conflicts in Europe surrounding the dissolution of the USSR and nation states separating into distinct entities have been bloody and led to many deaths but they have been relatively localised. The Russian invasion of Ukraine (although they denied they would take such action) has been fairly limited in terms of the death toll for the civilian population but the devastation to infrastructure will have consequences for many years. The Israeli conflict has had a much large impact in terms of the catastrophe facing Gaza and its inhabitants and this looks to be escalating and NATO will almost certainly have a major role to play alongside the UN.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
Its important that we join with nato to increase our military support both defensively and strategically to prepare for the possibility of war, but also ensure we can protect our infrastructure at home and the military bases we have abroad.
Since Trump took office  he has stated that NATO countries need to pay more and he has refused to offer to support to countries he has a beef with, for example he knew about the escalation between Israel and Iran but didnt tell the UK until after it began. Trumps policies seem to walk over other NATO members and exclude discusions with supposed allies in his mission to promote America First in all he does despite who else becomes collateral damage in his shoot from the hip descision making .

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: There are currently wars, such as in Ukraine, Iran, and Israel, which could leave the UK very vulnerable and a target for foreign attacks. Therefore it is important that the UK should strengthen its international relationships, solidify its role within NATO, and protect itself against potential attacks, in particular nuclear bombing. However, the downside to this is that the UK may alienate countries outside of NATO and give the impression that they are militarily preparing to defend itself against attack. While ethically I don't really agree with bolstering the UK military, I can see that it is an unfortunate necessity of our times.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
My personal opinion is that a universal decrease in military spending would be a powerful demonstration of faith in a global setting.

Such a collective action could foster an environment of trust and co operation , allowing resources currently allocated  to armaments to be redirected towards global challenges like climate change, poverty and injustice.

This approach, centred on diplomacy and mutual understanding could pave the way to a more peaceful stable world.

I believe the USA would not be positive to add funding to NATO and therefore would make the whole suggestion unproductive and a complete waste of time and money

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

The amount of government money that has been assigned to our armed forced,  has declined over the years. The world is extremely volatile hence the need to ensure that we prepare and protect our troops to the best of our ability. We should ensure that we are able to support NATO.
Potential combat is not the only reason for increasing the spending budget, also to ensure that the living conditions, training and medical support is fit for purpose for our enlisted personnel during peace.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: Ordinarily I would oppose increased spending on military in favour of prioritising healthcare and education. However, at present there seems to be no end to Russia's war on Ukraine in the foreseeable future and Israel's attacks on Palestine and now Iran too mean that the situation in the Middle East is more volatile than ever. With Donald Trump being volatile and unpredictable, I don't think that Europe or the UK could rely on the US for support in case of foreign aggression either. So I reluctantly feel that this is an important priority for the UK at the moment to protect ourselves and other nations from attacks from countries such as Russia. I also like to think this could go someway to improving our relationship with Europe following the Brexit vote.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: There is some doubt about the role of the United States in NATO and particularly with it's support to European nations in the threat it faces from Putin's Russia. I believe this is a real and imminent threat. European countries and indeed the UK should face this threat by increasing their defence spending, and showing a concerted front in deterring any the threats from dictatorial and expansionary nations such as Russia. There is however, a limit to how much a country like ours can increase their defence spending, without being detrimental to the health and wellbeing of the nation. The key thing is for there to be a concerted increase and agreement between all of the European NATO countries.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: The UK is too small and has insufficient military force to defend itself against aggression. By aligning with NATO we can help ourselves and also help others who are similarly weak. In order to contribute effectively to NATO, we need to increase our military spending. That would increase our defense strength individually  as well. Unless we have credible military strength, the other members of NATO will not be able to take us seriously and we run the risk of losing NATO support and perhaps even our NATO membership. That would clearly be highly serious and leave us vulnerable to attack.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
I think the UK should not increase it's spending on defence.  Our NHS is in crisis and spending the money on the NHS would directly benefit more people who are in need.  It would help to cut waiting lists and mean that more people could have the treatments that they need and get the operations they need quicker.  They could also have a recruitment drive as more and more doctors and nurses are leaving the NHS to join the private section.  

On the other hand it would create more jobs for more people, and strengthen out position in the event of the threat of attack.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: It is well known that the UK has been facing a cost of living crisis. Basic costs have steadily been increasing for years and most individuals are feeling the strain of this. Most services and infrastructure within the UK is in dire need of additional funding, such as the NHS, public transport, education. Morally I generally disagree with most military action anyway, but especially in these circumstances I believe the money that could be used for military spending should be spent on the previously mentioned things to benefit people on an individual level, especially those who are low income. Additionally, it is well known that the military target low income individuals, which is extra inappropriate in a social climate where money is a problem due to the failure of the government to budget and fund services appropriately.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: I personally think it is not a good idea for the UK to increase its military spending solely to strengthen its role within the NATO because the UK already holds a strong and respected position in the alliance, supported by its close ties with the United States and European allies. Further increasing military expenditure could be perceived negatively by other global powers particularly Russia, which view NATO expansion as a threat to its national interests especially amid the ongoing war in Ukraine. Instead of allocating more funds to defense, the UK should prioritise investing in the well-being of its own citizens addressing domestic challenges such as healthcare and the cost of living crisis.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

These are the main issues facing the country, government should focus on issues at hand before trying to stengthen an outside organisation. NATO should not pressure countries to give beyong what they can manage. Often NATO is worthless, especially when big war crimes are going on and they sit there taking no real action. I wonder if NATO are worthy of investing in and being part of. Focus should be on what actually will help this country inside and out. NATO is losing its powr and respect.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: I believe the UK should increase it's military spending as the current geopolitical status of the world is uncertain and conflicts and tension are happening in many places around the globe. War is currently in Europe and with the US loosening support for Ukraine and not focusing on Europe as much I think the UK needs to increase spending to have a leadership role in NATO but also for it's own benefit as it seeks to counter the threat of Russia and other potential adversaries. The UK has reduced defence spending consistently since the end of the cold war and is now unprepared and behind to what it should be so an increase of spending as the world gets more dangerous is definitely a necessity.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.

With the lessening of support for NATO by Trump, the responsibility for European security falls increasingly on the rest of the NATO allies, along with other countries in Europe that are not members. We are therefore left with no choice to increase our military spending to strengthen NATO. This should not be left to the UK alone, however. All European countries and NATO members (including the US) should be ramping up their contributions to combat the increasing threats from the East.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
I believe that the UK needs to increase its military spending as we're seeing escalating global security threats, from geopolitical instability to the resurgence of aggressive states and complex cyber warfare. To effectively protect our national interests, our citizens, and our sovereignty, it's rational that our defence capabilities must be robust and well-funded. This isn't about flexing muscles; it's about essential preparedness in an unpredictable international landscape.

NATO, without a doubt, remains an absolutely vital security agreement for the UK and the whole of Europe. It provides a collective defence frameworks that has underpinned peace and stability for decades. Our commitment to the alliance should be uphold. However, while our role within NATO is important, I think the primary driver for increased military spending should be addressing national security rather than standing out in the alliance.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
UK defense spending is currently a joke. We have a few units in the army, about 20 ships in the navy and 50 air planes or so in the air force. The nuclear deterrent has 4 submarines with only one submarine at any one time armed with ICBM's. This is intercontinental ballistic missiles. The last test of one of these missiles (with attached heavy monitoring equipment) was a complete failure off the USA east coast about one year ago.

NATO defence spending in Europe is about 2% of national wealth per country; totally insufficient. Only Finland, Poland and the Baltic states (Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia) take defence seriously, at about 5% GDP, mostly because Russia is close to or on their borders, and a major threat. Ukraine has found out to its cost in the last 3 years that Russia under Putin is a warmonger and criminal.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text:
Increasing the UKs spending would reinforce its leadership role within NATO by enhancing its ability to contribute to collective defence and deter growing threats from places such as Russia. As global security challenges grow,  strong military presence would help NATOs readiness and credibility, ensuring the alliance remains effective and unified. Greater investment would also support the UKs strategic influence, improve relations with allied forces and demonstrate a firm commitment to shared secruity responsibilities. 
It will also create jobs across the UK by boosting defence manufacturing, supporting research and development, expanding armed forces recruitment and stimulating local economies through supply chains and infrastructure projects.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: Increasing the budget for  defense in the UK is vital if the country wants to safeguard itself and honour NATO promises. More money will help the armed forces react quicker and do a better job when any crisis breaks out, whether that means a ground war or a sudden cyber raid. New threats demand fresh thinking and high-tech tools, so it is essential to replace old kit and put serious funds into cyber, intel, and space safeguards. With extra resources the UK can stay tough and flexible as the face of fighting changes. Spending more also underscores Britain's leading seat in NATO, proving allies it can be counted on and bolstering the blocs shared shield. In short, such investment defends national aims and helps keep the wider world calmer.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: The UK government should increase it`s spending to strengthen it`s role within NATO to a certain extent to ensure the safety of our own country . The government need to consider and get the balance of spending  just right , as there are many other areas that need funding . For example , the NHS is a mess and needs funding , as does education , welfare payments , transport , the list is endless . We send too much money to other other countries , so maybe cut back on overseas aid and put the money into our own needs.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase its military spending to strengthen its role within NATO.
rows.text: I am no expert on military spending and my bias is towards pacifism.  However, given the increasingly volatile world order,  it would seem prudent to align ourselves with our allies and to make sure that we are able to secure our country from external threats.  This goes beyond military spending, however, and it is important not to forget that our soft power is what has kept us on the world stage.  At this moment when America is 'withdrawing' from the world, particularly in terms of funding, there is a vacuum opening up.  This is an opportunity for the UK to step forward.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text: These days, there's a lot of trouble going on in the world, wars and arguments between countries. Places like Ukraine and the Middle East are really unstable. Other countries are spending more on their armies and technologies, so it makes sense for UK to do the same. If UK put more money into their military, they can stay ready for anything, keep themselves safe, and show others that there are not to be messed with. It is a good point to make sure that UK is protected. New technologies like drones, AI and cyber defences are changing the way wars are fought. It is important not to fall behind other countries.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text:
Given the increasing number of armed conflicts and tensions erupting across various parts of the world, its imperative that we increase investment in our military capabilities. These conflicts underscore the volatility of the current global security environment. If we don't invest in our military, our ability to respond to emerging threats could be severely compromised. 

Several nations are rapidly advancing their military strength through significant investments in cutting edge technologies. Countries like Russia and China are modernising their armed forces at a pace which threatens to surpass our own capabilities in critical areas such as cyber security and weapons. If we fail to match or exceed this progress, we run the risk of falling behind strategically, which will ultimately affect our ability to defend our country.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text: We have been under spending on our military for too long a time and not treating our service people with the respect they deserve as well.  We are living in a time when the world wide issues are spreading nearer and nearer to our own shores and we need the increases in spending to update our ability to protect our country.  The Russia/Ukraine conflict shows us that it once a conflict starts it can continue for years and the costs involved are unimaginable and even with help for other countries can bring a country to its knees.  Therefore we need to be as ready as possible to face any future conflicts.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.

They sleptwalked into severe underinvestment in defence in the last few decades and thus are very vunerable and weak to external threats. The global conflicts in many parts of the world mean we must act now and drastically increase defence spending, to protect ourselves and future generations. It will take years to catch up, to get to the level reuqired to properly defend ourselves, so there is no more time to wait. Act now UK or we are going to fall even further behind.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.

China too is becoming more belligerent, especially in the sea around the coast and with Taiwan.  They have grown their military significantly over the last decade and continue to so do.  Without increasing spending on defence, we become ever more vulnerable and with no guarantee that the USA would come to our aid in the event of conflict.  Our standing army has diminished in size and power, our military equipment is no longer a match for that of our enemies and our general standing in the world has diminished as a consequence

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text: I think currently the world is a very unstable place and one where countries who are militarily weak are more likely to be a target for power hungry countries as in the case of Russia invading Ukraine. I believe that to increase our spending and weapons is necessary as we should not rely on other countries to help us in the case of attack but should be able to defend ourselves which I believe will make other countries think twice about making an attack on us. I also feel we should go a step further and reinstate national service for all whether you are born here or move here as a migrant or refugee as a way to show allegiance to the country

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text:
- global threats - the UK & many other countries with shared values in Europe & democracies in South East Asia (South Korea, Japan etc) are all facing increased threat levels from an aggressive Russia & beligerent China. Both countries are engaged in hybrid warfare against democracies & looking to destabalise countries with democratic western values. WE therefore need to increase spending on defence to help counter these threats

- poor readiness - the UK military has declined in numbers & resource in recent years. The size of the army & navy is much reduced. We are too heavily reliant on an unreliable ally (USA under Trump). We need  to increase military spending to counter the real threats we are facing.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text:
Although there seem to be plenty of people who seem sanguine and complacent about the threat of Russian expansionism (this seems prevalent amongst the younger generations if my sons and their friends are anything to go by) I think the peace in Europe since WW2 has shown that deterrence has worked. However Europe, including the UK, has dropped its guard with the post 1989 peace dividend. Increasing defence spending may result in decreases in other parts of government spending but I think we need to regard this as a necessary sacrifice to reduce the chances of the greater sacrifices if Britain had to protect herself or our fellow Europeans.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.
rows.text: I believe the Uk should cautious about significantly increasing its military spending. A large increase in funding could divert resources from essential public services like healthcare, education and social care which also contribute to national resillience and well being. Addionally many modern threats such as cyber attacks and global instability may not be addressed solely through tradional military means. Investing in cyber defense, intellegence and diplomatic efforts can be just as important in maintaining security. Therefore rather than significantly boosting military spending, I think a more balanced and strategic approach that considers both military and non military needs would be more effective and reporsible.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its military spending.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text:
In terms of disadvantage these are numerous but taking for example the ability to holiday in the EU. The cost to individuals in terms of new passport rules (validity, etc.), entry forms, queues in terms of money and time is just a huge inconvenience. Individuals and businesses are paying more and the governments are dealing with more administration and costs for arguably little gain. Illegal immigration to the UK has increased since we left the EU so that was hardly  a good reason for leaving. If we did rejoin the EU things would be a lot easier, cheaper and less time consuming.
Studying and working abroad was fun and challenging and such a beneficial experience and I feel so lucky to have been able to do this. I also know it helped my employment prospects in the UK and the value I have delivered to companies I have worked for in the UK. I feel sad that this is not easy or normal for generations at school now. I fear that the physical world is less accessible to people growing up now. I also think that retirement options are more limited to me personally too, it's not as easy to move to the EU for a few years and come and go as you please. Being in the EU there were more opportunities for everyone.
When you have a block of countries with whom you trade the most and are physically close to it makes sense to have no barriers to this trade. Free movement of goods and people doesn't mean a loss of national identity. Free movement makes it simple and cheap to trade amongst you. It offers flexibility and makes companies more competitive and encourages collaboration rather than hostility. There are sufficient resources and I believe working together people usually make things better.
When we were in the EU is was not all perfect but I genuinely believe that working together brings better results and protection for individuals. Many of the EU standards and processes of the state and law are still used in the UK. So being outside of the EU hasn't changed some of these fundamental institutions or standards, for better or worse, there have been no cost savings. Yet, we are apart from the EU and I feel that way, even though we are actually more alike than different. I don't want the UK to be going down a tribalism path and inflaming tensions in everyday life.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text: Yes, the UK should absolutely rejoin the European Union! Being part of a larger group for local and global decisions is beneficial as there is strength in numbers. Equally we get various practical benefits. Trade is easier and cheaper, currency value is likely higher, people can move around and work more easily and we can travel more easily with fewer queues at passport control. One big thing taken away by leaving the EU is the ability for younger people to go on exchange schemes allowing them tio study and learn abroad. Anything which reduces our ability to experience more is inherently a bad thing.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text: We should never have left the EU. Since the referendum, many people have regretted the way they voted as they've now realized the implications of leaving the EU. Our costs have increased massively for importing goods. Prices in the shops have risen. We are dependent on many goods being imported and the cost to do this has increased a great deal. If the referendum was held again now, there's no way we'd be leaving the EU knowing what we now know.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text: The UK should rejoin as the benefits of leaving were exaggerated and a number of years on these have just not been realised. In fact the UK economy is smaller as a result of leaving the EU.  In modern times, the UK cannot be inward looking but needs to form strong bonds with other nations, to be able to compete for business with larger countries in the world, such as the US, India and China.  Joining the EU again would also strengthen UK security as it would be more closely integrated with EU countries and this would help against current rogue states.  Being part of the EU would also mean the UK is part of common standards for consumer goods, which would benefit consumers.  The UK would be joining a large consensus on best practice in this area, rather than make decisions on its own.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text: I feel that since we have left the European Union we are doing well on our own. rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.

We should leave the EU entirely and stop having to abide by anything whatsoever they say. Leaving their control would allow us to protect our borders and look after our citizens better, Many industries in the UK suffer as a result of deals the EU have brokered which allow trade imports to destroy our own manufacturing industries.

rows.proposition: The UK should seek to rejoin the European Union.
rows.text: During the referendum debate the campaign ran by Leave was I believe built on falsehoods. Claims such as there would be a reduction in red tape and no financial impact to leaving - if fact Europe would be looking to make a deal with the UK especially German car manufacturers. The reality has been somewhat different and the impact on the UK economy has been profound with estimates being that GDP has been hit by ~4%. The consequence of which can be seen on the ability of Government to provide enough funding for services. There is also a change in the demographic with many younger voters looking enviously at the opportunities denied to then by the decision to leave the EU taken by older voters.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the sale of violent video games to minors.
rows.text:
Since at least the 1950s there has  been a long history of moral panics around the activities enjoyed by children and teenagers. These have ranged from satanic panics around rock music / heavy metal / role playing games to sexualisation through pop music and to fear of temptation into criminal activity and violence from hip-hop / grime / comics etc.

In many cases individual specific acts have been generalised into a wider issue. In the case of video games there have long been claims that the violence represented in the games leads to violence carried out by the users and games players. The majority of formal research into this suggests that this is not the case so that would not be a valid reason to toughen regulation

There might however be a case for stronger regulation to protect younger children from material they might come across unawares and find disturbing

rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the sale of violent video games to minors.

I think it is primarily up to parents or other responsible adults to police thier children's online and video activities and decide what is suitable. Children vary and some are immature. The games already have classifications similar to cinema and, in my experience, they were enforced in shops. rows.proposition: The UK should implement stricter regulations on the sale of violent video games to minors.
rows.text: Although there is insufficient evidence linking violent video games to violent behaviour, there is definitely a causal link between playing violent games and mental health. Minors will not have developed the capacity to process the violence they observe in violent video games, and they risk normalising (and accepting) this behaviour themselves when they see it. As a society, we know what is bad for minors (outside of video games) and we tightly regulate this as a result - tobacco, alcohol, drugs, pornography etc. So regulating mentally harmful content like violent video games would be consistent with this approach. Once they are old enough to process what's going on, they should be allowed to purchase violent video games as they know this is entertainment and not instruction!

Personally, for  me,  it would work quite well because I have a reasonable amount of other income. But If it were set, for example, at the state pension level then it just would be inadequate for many people.  I can see the point though that by paying an income to everyone it would cut the cost of means testing everything. Way less bureaucracy.
So for these reasons I'm thinking such a scheme is just unworkable and too many people would be outraged by it all.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text: There are now so many people in the UK of working age who are no longer actively looking for work that I suspect UBI would simply make matters worse. Why bother to work when you are guaranteed an income irrespective of your own efforts. UBI might also fuel the burgeoning "black economy" if people wish to "top up" their UBI without declaring earnings. Most of all, tax payers will need to fund such a scheme. It just seems wrong to reward persons who are just lazy and can't be bothered to contribute to society. UBI would also reduce the complexity of the current benefits system which would cause job losses in the civil service and local authories who currently administer the existing schemes.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text:
The UK country has a large national debt. I don't know what the exact figure is, but I know that a large amount of interest is paid per year to service that debt. Therefore, I wonder exactly how the UK could afford to implement such a scheme without adding to country's large debt figures.

There is also the potential that such a scheme could disenfranchise people from working. What steps would be implemented with such a proposal to ensure that the initiative is not more attractive to people than working a full-time job?

It is worth pointing out that AI is going to have a huge impact on the job market in the near future. Therefore, such a scheme is certainly worth discussing. I wouldn't be surprised it such a scheme were to be implemented in the future (say 50 years time). However, I don't think we've reached that point yet both in terms of the potential costs and the need for such a scheme.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text: The UK implementing a Universal Basic Income (UBI) has both strong support and valid concerns. Supporters argue UBI could reduce poverty, improve mental health, and offer security in the face of job automation. It may simplify the welfare system and empower people to pursue education, caregiving, or entrepreneurship. However, critics highlight the high cost of such a program and worry it could discourage work, particularly in low-income jobs. There are also fears of inflation and reduced targeted support for the most vulnerable. While UBI has potential benefits, careful planning and testing are essential to determine if it is sustainable and effective for the UK’s economic and social context.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.

I have heard arguments against this universal basic income, I think it may give the government more control over people, which is not a good thing. I think that instead, there should be a minimum income for people on benefits, sick pay and pensioners, as these are the people likely to be in poverty.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.

I do not believe most would. Many people would do little for society as now, but would also not attend their current jobs which help society function. I do not believe that the system would work, at least in a large society such as the UK. There would not be enough income generated overall for the government to then pay a basic income to all that would be anywhere near at a liveable level.  The country is already running out of money in providing a minimum income guarantee for pensioners, plus benefits to all who claim to be sick or need help. Basically nice idea, maybe once we have World peace and harmony (so never).

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text: The UK is struggling with low productivity and not enough taxes being raised to cover our costs.  The deficit is currently running at £150 billion a year.  Our debt is about £3 Trillion.   Our interest on that debt is about £100 billion year.  We are struggling with not enough growth especially since this Labour government has raised taxes on employment.    Worse still is out growth per Capita - which has been falling for a few years now.  What we need is more people to do more work not less.   Introducing universal basic income will allow too many people to step out of employment altogether.   The cost would be in the £100 billions of pounds which would add to the debt and would probably mean that the interest we pay of that debt goes up due to loss of confidence in the UK economy.    As far as I know - wherever it's been tried, its had to be cancelled due to it being a disaster.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text: If the government offered a universal income, a lot of people would take this and not work for a living. This could lead to labour shortages, especially in lower paid areas, as the incentive to work diminishes. It would also be a drain on the UK economy as taxes would need to increase to make up for the money everyone receives, placing more pressure on businesses and workers whilst potentially shrinking the overall tax base. It would also cause some unrest as certain people would be working for a living and they'd see people doing nothing but still receiving money.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text:
I feel that providing a basic income to all citizens, regardless of wealth/social status is not a good idea. I believe it would discourage people from finding work, especially in lower skilled sectors. I believe it would drive inflation as companies would need to raise salaries to entice people into work and thus increase the cost of the goods they sell, rendering the universal basic income as pointless.
I prefer the idea of targeted, means tested support which benefits the groups of people who need it most (such as the elderly, disabled and carers etc). If implemented correctly, this should provide all the benefits of a universal basic income (such as poverty reduction and reduced inequality) without the financial wastage caused by providing this money to every system. If coupled with strong incentives that encourage working, I believe this would be a far superior option to a Universal Basic Income.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a universal basic income for all citizens.
rows.text: I think it is a slippery slope. We already have a benefits system and a minimum wage for jobs. To introduce this policy would have similar effects to the existing systems in terms of people's financial security, but would introduce further challenges. This might include social challenges, such as children not being motivated to work hard and get jobs, and increased crime rates, as people without jobs are having to spend their time elsewhere, e.g. being antisocial. I think we should be encouraging that people work hard instead. I agree that the benefits system is important for individuals in some cases, but I think it also contributes towards laziness in some instances, and the current policy might have an even worse effect.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text: Firstly, the automation of who becomes the head of state through the monarchy is undemocratic. Like other democratic countries, the UK should abolish the monarchy system to allow its citizens to vote and elect leaders of their choice. Secondly, the monarchy is expensive to maintain which adds financial burdens to the taxpayers. Maintaining the royal families expenditures which includes big family residences, loads of staff ranging from house managers, security, and drivers costs quite a big share of public funds which could otherwise be used for beneficial public services. Lastly, the monarchy promotes inequality in power acquisition. This is because people are born into power leading to hereditary privilege which hinders people from outside the royal family from getting into the head of state position.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.

Removing this institution would support a fairer merit based society where public roles are earned not inherited. Additionally abolishing the monarchy would help UK move towards a more modern and secular system of government one that reflects the values of a contemporary democracy. It would ensure that political power and public funds are used transparently and with full accountability to the people of the nation.

It is also deeply embedded in British history and identity, contributing to national pride and global recognition. Furthermore, the Royal Family draws millions in tourism revenue and international attention. often exceeding the public cost of its maintenance. With consistent public support and its ability to remain above party politics, the monarchy continues to serve as a valuable and stabilising force in the UK we must keep it.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text: I can see both sides of the argument for and against abolishing the monarchy. On the one hand, the monarchy is deeply embedded in the UK's traditions, providing a sense of continuity, national identity, and a figurehead that stands apart from political divisons. The royal family also contributes to diplomacy, charity, and the economy, particularly through tourism and soft power. On the other hand, in a modern, democratic society, the idea of a hereditary head of state raises valid concerns about fairness, privildge, and accountability. While I understand and respect the republican argument, I think meaningful reform of the monarchy might be a more balanced solution than outright abolition, allowing the UK to modernise the insitution without losing its cultural and historical value.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.

The traditions and values are outdated and i feel like they are irrelevant now. On top of this, the tax payer is paying towards the royal family and the upkeep of them including the security costs. The public funding is a major part of my opinion, we are paying for an unelected person and we are a democratic country. This is why i agree with the statement.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text:
I personally find there is some comfort in the relative stability and continuity of the monarchy as representatives of our Country. As Head of State, entertaining other heads of state and representing the years of history that have shaped the UK, the monarchy form a consistent, a-political symbol of the UK. I found this mindset easier to hold whilst the Queen was alive, as she brought a certain gravitas to the position that is clearly not so present with King Charles.
The world is full of inequality, and the new tech billionaires will create dynasties the same as the Rothschilds and the like did in the past - I don't find the inherited wealth and privilege of the monarchy difficult to accept, it's not as if they have any real power in our current system, so I find the republican argument to be mostly driven by small minded jealousy.
A far bigger concern is the total mediocrity of the politicians - it's not an argument for the monarchy get any actual power, but we do need to fix the huge issue of populists and intellectual lightweights gaining power by standing in safe seats

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text: While the UK monarchy is usually characterised as being largely ceremonial and symbolic, they still hold a considerable amount of power and influence. King Charles III, when he was Prince of Wales, is known to have quietly influenced government decisions. In a true democracy, there should not be any person whose power comes solely from their lineage, as even with the best will in the world they can never truly represent the people of that nation. Add to that the monarchy holds huge amounts of wealth and land (the crown estates covers something 200,000 acres), and makes money from it all. People who argue that the monarchy costs the country very little, and even is a profit because of tourism, fail to recognise that we can keep the palaces and royal parks even if the monarchy itself is removed.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text: I feel somewhat ambivalent about abolishing the monarchy. On one hand, the royal family represents tradition, continuity, and a symbol of national identity that many people in the UK value. The monarchy often brings attention to charitable causes and helps promote British culture globally. On the other hand, in a modern democratic society, it feels inconsistent to have a head of state chose by birth rather than by merit or election. The financial costs associated with maintaining the monarchy also raise fair questions about priorities in public spending. While I see reasons for reform, I think any move toward abolition would need to be gradual and carefully planned to avoid constitutional instability.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: Climate change poses a severe threat to the country. We have seen many more extreme weather events in recent years, as have all other countries on the planet. Whilst acknowledging that this problem is multi-faceted, it is clear to one and all that we need to ensure that industries are complying with the latest guidelines in terms of their emissions. For some time now we have tried persuasion and "appealing to their better nature" To no avail. It is time to bring in legal requirements, with real potential punishments, so that companies have no choice but to reduce their emissions. Our future (and the futures of our children) depend on this.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: As Great Britain attempts to reduce its carbon output and reach net zero, it must be firm but fair on the culprits who emit the most carbon emissions. Industries take up a large proportion of this, and it should be capped. This would encourage industries to limit their output and reduce fossil fuels. More should also be encouraged to take up renewable heat incentives. Although the Non domestic renewable heat incentive is closed, there are several other schemes that could be introduced by the government to tackle this issue. More needs to be done by the government and this would be an effective start.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
They should introduce a mandatory carbon emissions cap because carbon emissions are the main contributor of global warming. Limiting emissions is essential to keeping the global temperate from rising and causing irreversible damage. 
High emissions lead to a poor air quality which can obviously affect the health of the public, particularly in regards to respiratory diseases. It is the governments responsibility to look after the health and wellbeing of its citizens and do what they can to protect future generations from the effects of global warming. By introducing a cap they can actually measure progress and the positive impact it's having

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: As an initial statement, I agree with this based on the effects of climate change. However, I do feel that this is ultimately not a huge solution, and so really my viewpoint is relatively ambivalent. It's too late; humanity is charging into a global extinction event that's already unfolding and cannot be stopped. Expecting the highest "earners" (See also: wage thieving psychopaths) in any industry to adhere to mandated policies such as this one is... immature, at best. Having said this, I suppose whilst we have 10-25 years of reasonable existence left, this sort of policy may have a minor positive effect of some sort, and I, of course, could be wrong. Sadly, I'm not.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
I chose a midway point on the scale for these reasons: I am sceptical of most 'green' measures, suspecting that they are often another way of shunting money into the pockets of our 'great leaders', while taxing the rest of us ever more. Governments of all colours show us that this is true over and over again. I would go so far as to say, greenwashing is the new religion, in that it is a way of distracting and guilt-tripping us, the masses, while the elite fill their boots.

However, in answer to the basic, innocent form of the question of whether companies should be belching toxic waste into the atmosphere, well of course they should not. Therefore, I chose what I chose to reflect the complications of this.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: This is nonsense. The UK is responsible for about 1% of global emissions, so even if you believe it's a good idea, it would make no actual difference. All this would do is increase costs for businesses which are passed onto the consumer. What about where demand exceeds supply? That's right, it would be bought in from abroad, thus making the whole exercise a complete waste of time. The correct method is to stop believing the climate change "emergency" cranks and do what is best for UK. Be efficient, yes, use minimal resources, of course, but don't try and tax your way to oblivion.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: This is a silly idea as we produce such a tiny amount of the overall global total of Carbon emissions that by placing a cap on UK companies we would be doing nothing except causing us to be less competitive with foreign competitors. This would lead to job losses in the UK and also to "brain drain" as young British talent would choose to work overseas with our competitors who could afford to offer higher salaries. Without action being taken by the main polluter countries (e.g. China, India, USA) anything the UK does would be a drop in the ocean and make no practical difference to Carbon emissions globally.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: I dont think that all industries would be able to stay in the cap set by the government. It would most likely result in job losses as the company attempts to cover the costs of the cap. I don't see how it would be beneficial if it causes problems for certain companies and results in job losses in the industries involved. I would look into other ways to reduce carbon emissions in the industries than choose this option of a cap. Maybe there are better alternatives to reduce the emissions in the industries targeted by this cap on emissions from the government.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
What are the alternatives.  When we experienced strong winds it wrecked the solar panels fields and the wind farms had to be shut down - if these are out of action how can we obtain energy.
I appreciate that we are experiencing global warming, climate change - but to push hard to eliminate fossil fuels could damage the economy, I'm not very confident that the government has fully explored other routes   to go down.
If an emissions  cap was implemented it would have a very negative impact on industries which are already struggling to survive.  If they go under then unemployment increases. A very gloomy outlook.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: Hi - I am broadly against this statement.    An insistence on a carbon emissions cap will indirectly penalise traditional manufacturing industries and further deplete UK industry's ability to compete internationally with nations that do not have these caps.   An insistence on a cap will mean that some industry has to reduce capacity leading to supply chain shortages.  This will be filled by increased inportation often from countries that have even higher carbon footprints in their country.  For an example, the UK's ban on fossil fuel mining results in importation of products, such as coking coal, from overseas.  This has a much higher carbon footprint than if UK industry were allowed to operate without a cap.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: The reason why I think there shouldn't be one is because at first I think it would just stop progress and development, in order for society to grow, progress must be made, but if firms are capped, then its hard to say what they will do. I also think it will stop competition because if everyone is hitting that ceiling cap, then whats the point continuing and trying to do better. In order for industries to do better and improve products, they need competition and by limiting business with such caps, this type of competition in my opinion will not exist or thrive.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: setting a limit on carbon emissions for all industries would help fight climate change by reducing pollution and slowing down global warming. if businesses are required to cut their emissions, they will be encouraged to invest in cleaner energy and more efficient technology, making their operations better for the planet. a mandatory cap also makes sure that every company playsby the same rules, so no one gets an unfair advantage by polluting more than others. plus, lower emissions mean cleaner sir, which helps prevent health problems like asthma and lung diseases. while it is important to protect the environment, these rules should be carefully planned so businesses do not struggle too much. finding the right balance between helping the planet and keeping industries running smoothly is key to making a policy like this work

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
We need to accept the existence of this community in our society to avoid conflict and prejudice against homosexual people. In a fair society all forms of opinion are allowed even the minority opinions. Furthermore this practice is lawful in this country and children should grow up respecting that right from an early age.
Educating children in an academic environment will likely follow an established pattern using books which have been scrutinised to be unbiased and not derogatory. This education may offset opposing views exposed to the children elsewhere such as friends, parents etc. It is up to the children to decide for themselves.
Educating children in their formative years will most probably make them fair minded in the future. Teaching children about this subject will not make them gay but it will make them less ignorant.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: An education system's primary responsibility is to shape and inform young people in a comprehensive, appropriate and fullsome manner. This includes ensuring that all that is taught includes a well-rounded understanding of society, current norms and also fosters inclusivity. As long as what is taught is age-appropriate and suitable for the young people and their developmental stage, incorporating education about the LGBTQ+ community is entirely correct and appropriate. Inclusion like this supports empathy, respect and social awareness, helping students to better understand and live within our world. By embracing inclusivity in education schools can pay a vital role in this endeavour.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I support the factual explanation of the LGBT+ community to primary school children as a way to explain we live in a free and tolerant society; and to prevent any discrimination against the community because they might be perceived as a minority who are different from historically heterosexual relationships. Kids can be cruel when they lack education and I would want my child to grow up knowing there was no reason to discriminate, fear or ridicule any member of this community. Explaining this simply to primary school kids is a low impact and gentle introduction to the concept of different doesn't mean better or worse and I would see this as a similar issue to that of racism or disability education. I would however not support any promotion of this community as in any way superior to other forms of relationship. Inform and educate is 2/3 of the BBC motto and I would apply this to this issue.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I feel that primary school children should, and need, to be taught about the LGBT+ community in primary schools. This should include information on biology, physiology, societal, and psychological aspects, in a way that's accessible to the children, along with other life skills. Regrettably, there is still a lot of misinformation, prejudice, and stereotyping surrounding the community as a whole and as well as its individual members. Children should be able to access objective, fact-based information, and have a good insight into the diversity of the community. This would help the children function in a multifaceted society as they grow up and make their own choices, unencumbered (or less influenced by) traditional, misinformed, or misguided family/religious/societal norms.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
I think we should in the UK teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school because the communities that these children live in will have people from that community living in it. We need to educate these primary school children and teach them about inclusivity, teaching them about creating an environment where everyone feels valued, respected and has a essential belonging regardless of the differences.

Explaining what LGBTQ+ is will be the first step, being open and honest about peoples sexuality takes away any taboo from the start.
