Once that explanation has been taught to the children, we can then teach them about treating people in an inclusive way and not looking down on people because of their sexuality, et cetera.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I do think that children should be taught about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school as this community is now more open and accepted. These children can then grow up knowing to accept the LGBTQ+ communty from a younger age and there will be a lot less hate for these people for coming out and showing their true selves. Every person should be accepted and protected whatever they choose to do or be in life even if it is against what someone else believes or feels and its not hurting anyone. I understand some people may not agree but with the world we live in now which is a lot more open, these things should be spoken about at a younger age

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I cannot think of any reason why a child should not be taught about the LGBTQ+ community. It is not going to harm them by them having more information. If a child is taught things, they grow up with the tools necessary to deal with things in life. Surely that is much better for everyone than if they are left in the dark and have no idea about things. This could lead to them trying to find out information themselves on the internet which could lead to some inaccurate information. I just can't see why it would be a bad thing to teach children these things and prepare them for the future.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Every child should feel from an early age that the feelings they may have for other people throughout life are perfectly normal and natural. The beliefs we form at an early stage are easy to form, but become harder to change as our brains develop. This is on one of the main reasons why we should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school. It is really important that children know that those feelings are perfectly normal and natural and that there is absolutely no reason to feel ashamed of those types of feelings. This has a multitude of benefits. Firstly, it will improve the morale of children who may be LGBTQ+ themselves. In addition, it should reduce the likelihood that heterosexual children will bully LGBTQ+ for their feelings. This will hopefully continue throughout life and will improve the mental health of LGBTQ+ individuals and reduce suicide rates.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: It could help with teaching children about this topic and make them more understanding about this community. It can help them be more compassionate about the people in this community.  Would probably help those who think they may be part of the lgbtq+ community to understand what it is. I think it could be useful to teach to children so that they grow up understanding more about the people they are around. It could help reduce hate crimes against the people from this community in the UK  and improve society as a whole in the UK. Its a good idea.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I Don't think this is a subject matter to teach children as it will not lead to educational improvement. Children should be taught subjects that will help them achieve educational achievements and also skills that will benefit them in life. This is not a subject that will give them either. It is too divisive a subject and is not suitable for children. This is a matter for parents if they wish to teach them about this. Also, religions can be deeply offended by these sort of teachings and could lead to disruption in the classroom as well as in the community.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
I am fully in support of children being exposed to responsible and reasonable pubertal and sex related education, especially as they undergo puberty themselves. That being said, I do not think that cultural prevalences need to be taught to young and impressionable children. I think it should remain a choice of the parent as to whether they think it is best to explain subjective material. 

Sex education and puberty related education is factual and rooted in biological changes. Transgender identities are more subjective, are not rooted in fact, and as such should be left to the discretion of the parent. Children are extremely susceptible to influence, and an issue such as Transgender theory needs to be explained and informed in a moderated way.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Children have a right to an inocent childhood without being force fed information regarding heterosexual or homosexual life styles. They are unlikely to full understand either at a young age and may form opinions which  later lead to confusion. They should be allowed to come to their own conclusion over time snfd then whatever their choice they should by all means be given help and guidance as to how to follow these up and live a happy life content in their own skin and free from outside influence and prejudice. Only that way will they feel fullfilled and very content

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Children in primary schools are to yet developed both sexually and in their maturity. It could lead to having more negative impact by potentially causing misunderstanding. It could also lead to more bullying and misuse of the topic. The topic can be very divisive if not taught or explained correctly. As a result the topic is one which is better understood by a more mature mind and where sexual maturity is established. This I feel will lead to a better understanding by those being taught about the topic and would be more suitable for those that are learning about their sexual orientation to obtain support and guidance where needed by those tutoring.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I think primary school children are far too young to be thinking about such topics, it can be very confusing subject. By pointing out these differences at such a young age it could lead to isolation and bullying. Personally I think teaching children of that age about acceptance and inclusion without pointing out differences would be a better way to approach it. Let children be children, allow them to be care free in their younger years and all the information they need can be taught in high school when they are better equipped to handle such complex issues. I also believe that such a subject should be broached by a child's family, it is up to the parents/guardians of children how and when they want to introduce this issue.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I would feel very strongly if my child came home from school and told me all about their lesson in this subject. This way of life goes on, and has done for a long time. However would feel so wrong to teach this as a a normal subject. You see more and more of their shows  and presenters on tv, but the fact of the matter is, its not normal. The public are being forced to accept this type of community. Initially the claim culture began when people of this ilk were rejected from staying in a hotel, drinking at a pub etc and this made headline news. No doubt by the very same people. They seem to feel unjustly treated. Nowadays, the public 'have' to accept these people and their way of life as normal. It is not and I cannot accept it or would want children to.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Sexuality of any kind should not be taught at primary school; children should be allowed to be children. The transgender lobby is currently demanding a raft of privileges it is not entitled to, including being allowed to talk children into having life-changing (life-destroying) surgery at an age far too early for them to decide, and this behaviour is all too frequently supported by their parents. These children mostly turn out to be gay. The "+" part of the "community" encompasses any kind of deviancy, including some that are illegal, and people who follow these pursuits should not be allowed anywhere near a school.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Children who are at primary school are at an age where innocence prevails and life should be about fun, not delving into subjects such as this. I fail to see how it benefits either the LGBT+ community or children to be taught such a subject. They and their teachers time would be far more usefully allocated to learning to read, write and do basic arithmetic. Spare time should be filled with sport and other wholesome activities. Will they even understand what LGBT+ means in the context of their lives at that age? To me, this is political correctness gone mad.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
I believe that primary age children should be taught about ALL communities, with a concentration on accepting differences and choices - not teaching them what each means but a general understanding of families, and people that make up the world.
Teaching children that families can be made up of mums, dads, a mixture, an alternative - singles, adopted and so on. I feel this ties in with race, ability and religion also - the children should all be learning tolerance and understanding for all people regardless of all of the mentioned perceived differences.
I believe this builds a more understanding world.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I believe it's important for primary schools in the UK to acknowledge the LGTBQ+ community in the curriculum, but this should be done in a balanced and age appropriate way. Teaching children about the different types of people and families helps promote understanding and respect from a young age. It can help remove prejudice and help children from LGTBQ+ families feel included. However, the content should remain neutral and educational, aiming to raise awareness rather than encourage or promote any particular identity or lifestyle. The goal should be to foster inclusion without placing disproportionate focus on any one topic, ensuring that children are introduced to the concept as part of a broader understanding of diversity.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I am fully for teaching kids the importance of being inclusive and for being kind to everyone no matter what their preferences, however i think primary school is too young to be teaching sexual preferences and this could cause confusion in some young children's minds. I think it is ok to explain to children that some people have 2 dads/ 2 mums etc. but i think it is too young to go fully into the LGBTQ+ genre and explain in full detail what everyone means. so i'm not fully against the idea but i think if it is done it needs to be done in the most basic format.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
I believe that we are in a society that embraces diversity and is inclusive and this is something we should be proud of.   If we look around the children in a classroom,they will be form a range of families. Single parent, same sex, mixed sex,  adopted etc and we should ensure they all feel the same.  Eductaing children will ensure they do not face bullying or prejudice as they grow and move through school. 

We nned to make sure children are comfortable with all social settings and that we support this.  teachers are respected and children will absorb and follow their beliefs

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I feel if children were taught about the LGBTQ community during primary schooll than they are going to have a greater understanding of what this community is about and therefore less prejudiced against the community.  They would be less bullying and homophonic attacks.  Children will grow up knowing that some family situations are different than theirs and come in all shapes and sizes.  They will understand more about transgender dressing and that some men like to wear ladies clothes because they feel more comfortable in them than in clothing traditionally worn by men.  They would also get an opportunity to ask any questions they have always wondered about to do with the communty.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
-It is important that children know that regardless of background, religion, sexual orientation, colour, finance etc everyone has a place in our society. No one is more or less important. We all have a part to play. Maybe there would be less bullying in schools if this were the case, although it is also important to get the parents on board too.

-Children should feel safe and part of their school regardless of how they relate to society. If we can teach children to be more accepting of others from a young age and indeed talk about the LGBTQ+ community they would feel less alone and feel accepted. Also maybe we would have less hate crime and even less wars if people were taught to accept people who are not 100 percent the same as them.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: It should really be down to parents to decide if their children learn about contentious subjects like this. There will be a lot of parents that won't want their children to learn about this, as they may see it as encouraging children to consider being transgender, when it would be something they would not have considered otherwise. Some parents will disagree that a person can change gender and will see this as brainwashing their children at an age where they are accepting of anything they're told. However, learning more about this at a young age, could reduce bullying in later years, if children know more about this and as we celebrate equality, this could be seen to be part of that and essential. However, I do think overall that parents should get to voice their opinions on strong subjects like this and it would likely be too divisive to implement this at primary age.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Although I believe that education should be "rounded" I'm not sure for the need for this to be on the curriculum at school. I think that this may be better taught by parents of family members. There seems to be little learned in school to give children "life Skills" such as applying for jobs, how to budget for household expenses etc and I wonder if this would become less if the time is being taken up with LGBTQ lessons. The advantage would be that children would gain some knowledge in the circumstances where parents were not willing to teach this to children in the home

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Im a little on the fence about this as I think it needs to be very age appropriate. However that beinsaid the LGBTQ+ community is a way of life now just as normal as a hetrosexual relationship. There will be children in school whose parents have two mummies or two daddies so to stop bullying and questions being asked. The children will learn about this as they grow up so as long as its age appropriate it will help stop any stigma as they child grows, they may also feel they belong as part of that community so it will help them feel 'normal'.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text:
While a person is maturing through the different stages in life, they absorb information through different layers of understanding. It is important not to rush this development so no stages are missed. An example is to allow young children to believe in Father Christmas, even although you know later they will have to understand he is a fairly story. Done properly, they will be able to hold two conflicting ideas in the head without bothering them. In this example, Father Christmas does not actual exist, but it is fun to go along with the idea during the Christmas period.
    While their absorb many different things, including what are empirically provable facts and the various belief systems around. This includes wishful thinking, e.g. 1. You want you team to win this season. They have never won before, but it is OK to still cheer them on in the hope they will will. world view.  2. You have a bad feeling about a situation, place or person you have just met. You have no facts to back up that feeling, however, it is wise to acknowledge that feeling and perhaps remove yourself from that situation while you seek clarification.
    As a child moves through these stages, they can often feel inadequate, different from everyone else; especially if they have negative words spoken to them. In trying to reconcile these negative feelings, they often look around for a explanation of how they are different from others around them. It can be easy to decide you are LGBTQ+ to explain these feeling and be drawn into that life style where others will accept you, even though if you had not had those negative feelings towards yourself you would not have. This is not to say that this is the reason all people decide they are LGBTQ+ but some do and then get very confused later as their feeling about themselves and attractions change.
    It is important when teaching about LGBTQ+ or gender and sex in general, that it is not taught as just a mechanical or biological thing with no emotional consequences.  While It is good to explain that it is not wrong to be curious and have feelings, however, experimentation without a full understanding can be dangerous. Examples could be experimenting with different sexual acts (especially when a younger person is coerced by an older person into doing something) or drugs that they are offered.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: its just something i feel when you get older you can discover it for yourself school is already enough dont need that on top of it as it could just confuse kids more. I feel this is something parents can introduce to their kids if they want too, especially if they feel it would help their child find another community to help them discover themselves being around liked minded people but i feel at a young age kids are too impressionable so this wont help i feel just get in the way of their learning and just being a kid

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Schools have a responsibility to ready children for the real world. Although certain topics such as sexual relationships would not be appropriate for very young children I think it is important none the less that kids learn that relationships can occur between any two-or-more people. Parents should be the first people to teach their kids the ways of the world but they should not be their only source of information. If the parents are homophobic and they are the only ones talking to their children then their prejudices would be passed onto their kids. if the school are also teaching the kids then the kids can grow up and learn that their are more than just one opinion and can then go onto to make their own opinion on the subject

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I am not against making primary school aged children aware that there are many forms of relationships in our communities and that we should all be tolerant and supportive of one another. However, I am not sure that children of this age group would understand about sexuality: whether that's in heterosexual relationships or in any other types of relationships. I am not sure if teaching about sexuality and relationships is the responsibility of the school; I would suggest that parents should be the ones to decide how much information and what sort of concepts their children can handle. Let children be children!

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: As a Scottish person, I feel that we have little voice in Westminster. I appreciate we have devolved powers, as well as our own legislations however, they could all be taken away from us at any point. There are matters of other issues that due to our smaller population, our voice doesn't matter all that much such as in general elections. The whole of Scotland could vote for a certain party and that wouldn't matter. However, I respect democracy and we voted to remain and I think that it's fair to uphold that. A way around this could be another vote, now that some of the people who were too young to vote at the time have a voice.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Scotland should be allowed to leave the United Kingdom if enough people agree. I feel that splitting Scotland from England will give the country the autonomy it craves. A lot of the laws being passed in main parliament often does not apply to Scotland anyway. Why should Scotland have things different to UK citizens? For example Scots don't have to pay for their medical prescriptions and University students get free tuition. Unless everyone gets treated the same across the board, then Scotland should leave the Union. Look how they run their country and the level of help that they get. This may remind the prime minister that charity does begin at home and he should be doing what the Scots are doing - working for the general public and accounting for every single hard earned penny of the British public. Good luck to Scotland they deserve better.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I am not sure how entitled to an opinion on this issue I am, as I have never even been to Scotland and have only ever met one person who was Scots. I feel that the Scots people should have the main say as to if they want to stay part of the United Kingdom or not. On the other hand, I do like the idea that Scotland, England, Ireland and Wales should stay connected. I hope that this would be on friendly terms as much as possible, with all involved getting some benefit from the arrangement. I know that as it stands only some of Ireland is part of the UK and I also know that some in Cornwall want independence.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
It is up to the people of Scotland as to whether or not they feel they would like to remain in the United Kingdom or to leave. I recall that there have been one or two votes of independence and both times the majority of people have chosen to remain part of the United Kingdom. 

As an English native, I feel that this issues is entirely up to the people of Scotland to answer the vote and show Scottish parliament how they feel, that way a decision can be made fairly, based on the results of a given poll or survey answered directly by Scottish people.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I feel it is easier for everything to be managed when under the same government and royal family. Laws, rules and regulations can therefore carry all through scotland and other UK countries. However, if the scottish really did want to be separate, which to my knowledge is not a particularly pressing issue, then it likely would be able to happen efficiently as exemplified by Northern Ireland. I think that id Sotland was to gain independence, then all other countries in UK may be inclined to do the same until the UK no longer exists. It has its positives and negatives.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: From my understanding which admittedly isn't  a lot that the majority of people in Scotland are all for their independence so who am i to disagree with what they want.I'm not sure of the semantics of it so i can't really type a good argument for or against it other than give the people what they want and if being independent is their wish then i wholeheartedly agree as i can't think of a counter argument and i don't know if financially it will be beneficial for them to go their own way and whether it will help the rest of the UK

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: No taxation without representation! It is neither fair nor sustainable for the rest of the UK, principally England, to fund an independent country such as Scotland. Any independence conversation needs to emphasise the true costs of independence, such as Scotland joining the EU would entail withdrawal from Sterling; Scotland would need to pay for infrastructure, pensions, NHS, education policing and defence etc from its own taxation, the rest of the UK would not subsidise and Scotland as an independent country. Scotland cannot decide this issue in isolation - the rest of the UK needs to have an input on any independence issue.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
- I don't really know enough about Scottish independence to form an opinion on this - I have never lived in Scotland. I think some Scottish people don't like being part of the UK, they don't all like English people. I know this from hearing booing at football and rugby games. I don't know how long Scotland has been a part of the UK before and whether it was its own country in the past so I don't really understand the historical significance of this. Scottish people maybe think they'd be better off by themselves. They have lower levels of immigration and free prescriptions.

- I like the idea of the United Kingdom - surely there has to be some benefits to being part of something bigger - why else are Wales and Northern Ireland a part of the UK.  I like to think we are stronger together , rather than being individual - and thinking people can look out for each other . now we are sadly no longer part of the EU, I would rather still be a member of a group of countries, as a opposed to not being. I think Brexit was a mistake, and think Scottish independence might go a similar way if it were to happen.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
It is a well known fact that Scotland dont want to be part of the United Kingdom, they want to be a seperate country, but they still expect UK to pay and often their pensions and other welfare payments are higher than in UK.
They have their own laws and own parliament but we still have to finance them  this is not right.  I understand that last winter they scottish pensioners were paid the heating allowance ( I may be wrong ) where we here in England had to manage without. Pensioners in uk have become 2nd class people and treated as such.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: For me is very difficult to have a strong opinion either way on this matter. My knowledge is quite limited, despite having lived in the UK for 34 years but, not being an English citizen, I cannot have an opinion as it is something that historically doesn't effect me directly. But if there was a region of my country who would want to become independent it would not be a problem for me personally but I am sure that there would be some many complications and financial reasons why many many people would prefer that there was not the possibility of independence

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I believe that Scotland should stay part of the UK. Historically, it has always been part of the UK and I don't see a benefit to it being separate to the UK as a whole. People in Scotland get a lot more benefits than those living in England in terms of benefits of living there. I don't think they would get as many benefits if they were independent. I think that we should stay together to stay stronger but I also think that some of the arguments are increased by belonging in the EU and the prevelance of oil off the coast of Scotland.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: i dont think scotland have enough industry to be able to support themselves especially as the north sea gas starts decreasing. there is a high level of unemployment and not enough tax payers to support the system. apart from gas, scotland doesnt have much industry or ways of making money so i think scotland would end up deep in debt. scotland is stronger as part of the uk as the rest of the uk can strengthen the weaker areas while allowing the stronger parts of scottish economy, culture and history of to continue to shine. scotland is  an amazing country which should be helped to flourish

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: The united Kingdom is one of the great countrys of the world due to its unique make of the different nationalities and different regions, every country has its own identity and own strengths but when the countries align under the Union Jack we become a formidable nation its most things when partake in, whether that being in sport, the invention of a range of lengendary  discoveries, or fighting in conflicts all over the world the united kingdom proves time and again how great a nation we are together.  No other nation can demonstrate such a diverse range of people all representing the UK.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think Scotland being independent would have negative consequences, both for Scotland the rest of the UK. The main reason is economic instability. I am doubtful that Scotland could self support and it would lose the financial support it currently receive and it would result in higher costs for public services such as healthcare and education. In addition to this, establishing a new border would create barriers for trade and many scottish companies trade with English/Welsh companies so that would potentially result in a decrease/loss of business. And there's the issue of the movement of people, it would require border controls which would be costly and complex.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: The Scottish people had the opportunity to vote for independence and instead voted to remain part of the UK. The results of that referendum should be respected. It is wrong for governments just to keep people revoting repeatedly until they get the result they wanted. The United Kingdom has a long history. England and Scotland share a language, people are free to cross borders and move between Scotland, Wales and England. Although there may be some national rivalries these are mostly friendly and there is a genuine shared values and liking between the two nations. At a time of uncertainty where countries seem to be constantly at war with one another the nations which make up the United Kingdom benefit from presenting a united front. Separated they will appear smaller and more vulnerable to attack, with reduced world influence.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
The UK is a small nation that has historically punched above it's weight internationally on the diplomatic stage, financially and on the military stage.
Brexit has severely weakened the UKs standing in the world in these areas, and had a negative economic outcome for the country.
Scotland becoming independent would weaken the UKs standing even more. It would deprive the UK of energy sources at a time when energy independence is a growing vital security need.
Once the North Sea oil reserves run out, Scotland would need to refocus it's energies on other sources of income. They may have to become a low tax state like Ireland.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I would be very disappointed to see Scotland vote to leave the union, though it is their choice. All our institutions are bound together. The practicalities  of separation would cost billions on both side of the border and the ordinary people would not benefit at all. I'm sure there are deep forces within the UK establishment which are trying to ensure Scotland never becomes independent. It's up to them though. I'm very suspicious of the SNP and there has been a lot of publicity for scandals within that party, which may have been exaggerated by the forces I referred to.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: After seeing what happened when brexit went through, I hardly believe that separating Scotland from us can be a positive thing, as a citizen of England. This could mean more hassle at borders, it could mean more hassle/cost in importing and exporting, it could change everything yet again. One of the biggest things I think is that the Scottish may believe it's a good idea but as soon as people from England/Wales etc stop visiting because of the additional cost or hassle, their economy will suffer. Less people holidaying there will mean less money to them. I also think that separating people just causes a bigger divide and leads to more confrontation and violence with both the government and with the general public

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: As part of the United Kingdom, the people of Scotland should have the right to decide if they want independence from the United Kingdom. The United Kingdom is made up of four countries and it is my opinion that if the majority of any of those 4 countries wanted to leave after a democratic vote, then they should be allowed to leave. The ultimate decision about Scotland's position in the United Kingdom should only be in hands of the people of Scotland. Before any vote on independence is to happen both sides must be freely able to put their arguments for and against independence in front of the people of Scotland who can then make an informed decision on whether to vote for independence or not.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
with the NHS there is already separate set of guidleines and studies done between the England hospitals and the scotland ones, by further separating and making their own independant country, they will make it much harder for healthcare professionals to work in scotland. we as healthcare workers are sent to different hospitals for placements and training with so many niche training opportunities where we have to travel for whatever is available it would be harder for us to be better clinicians. 

the next point, the north south divide is more of a public impact. This would affect the general public in an economic, social and political aspect. firstly economically, there will be another currency surely (i think there already is and there was talk about them having the euro) which would make local travel a little harder. and export import trade will be something that the remainder of the UK will have to spend A LONG TIME trying to settle. we already struggle with relationships with places like europe and america and we have huge historyu with them, and someone like scotland would make it much much harder. the division between the two would discourage movement of people from the north to the south (or vice versa) given a new border, and a stigma about either scotland separating, or england abandoning kinda thing. (i think i wrote too much)

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I don't think Scotland should be separate to the UK because it allows more people to contribute to the UK government. Having them being an independant country will just cause more issues with people entering and existing scotland for holidays etc. A lot of people that live in scotland travel to and from for example london so them being another country would make this process hard and more time consuming which I think isn't a great idea. However, I'd be open to listening to points as to why it may be a benefit as I do not know much about this issue

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
Scotland has a large dependency on the UK, specifically England. Both in terms of defence and financially. I do not believe that Scotland would be able to sustain itself monetarily if it became independent. If Scotland chose to join the EU, it would impose a great many restrictions and rules which would impact trade, probably negatively. Even with the North Sea oil fields, and due to current net zero strangulation, I do not believe that it would offer effective income. Along the lines of the EU, it would cause greater friction between the block, the UK and Scotland due to politics. Scotland would also have to relinquish defence capabilities and loose the protection on the UK armed forces. 

Above all, I feel strongly that the UK is better together than being apart.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
If Scotland were to go independent, then as it stands, we would be spending about 10% more in terms of GDP than we collect in tax revenue, which is similar to Greece during their financial crisis. We would face a sovereign debt crisis right off the bat, as far as 2025, there are absolutely no measures in place to counter this, the whole independence movement is nothing more than a chest-beating, emotionally hate-filled campaign against the English, with very little financial, economic, and strategic blueprint to it.
Not to mention the currency issue, as far as I'm aware, the Scottish government's "idea" was to continue using the pound sterling and then transition to the Scottish pound; however, this would be far more challenging than they realise. The risks involved in this are astronomical and without a clear plan in place this is just far too risky.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I agree that Scotland should become independent because it could also bring some benefits to the rest of the UK. First of all, public spending might decrease. Scotland currently receives more money per person from the UK government compared to other parts of the country. If Scotland became independent, this financial responsibility would no longer fall on the UK. Secondly, the UK government could focus more on the economic needs of England, Wales and Northern Ireland without having to adjust policies to fit Scotland's different priorities. Finally, tax and budget decisions might become simpler and more efficient, as the UK would no longer need to balance four different nations' interests. I think in the long term, this could help the UK manage its economy more clearly and effectively.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: The political views in Scotland can be different from that in England and Wales. I therefore think that if they were independent from the rest of the UK, they would be able to explore these views further, without being constrained under the British government. This could potentially increase the satisfaction of living there due to citizens getting an increased say. This leads into my second point of more freedom. If the country was separate, they would be able to have more control over the countries future, potentially creating different allyships with other countries, expanding trade and connections. This may lead to a better economy, growth outside the country, and happier citizens.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think Scotland should become an independent country separate from the United Kingdom because they could decided on there own policies giving there own government more control on what happenes there such as how taxes are spent and how to run the health services more for the needs of the Scottish people they would be allowed to focus on there now strengths such as having more control over there finances were they spend there money and make there own economy better for the Scottish people also improve people when visiting there making it a better place to visit for tourists.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
No part of me feels like I should have a say in whether or not Scotland stays or goes. I was born in Northern Ireland, moved to England, my parents are from China. I could honestly not care less about all four countries of the United Kingdom staying as a United set.
If Scotland becomes independent, I would go "oh cool" and go about my day. Maybe visit it sometime. It really does not matter to me whatsoever what happens on that front. My "loyalty" to the UK only goes so far as the fact that I live here and I don't want to see it turning into a terrible place to live.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I feel the heritage we have is deeply bound together. We are 3 Nations who should celebrate our differences but be proud to stand together as a United Kingdom. We should respect our differences, but we are made stronger as a nation if we stick together. We have our own separate cultures but this just makes us a diverse nation of people. I think we are economically stronger standing together. We should be able to recognize and support the cultural differences without dividing us as a nation. I do not have the required knowledge of economics to make assumptions on the effect economically on England but feel it is not something I would be comfortable with. I would feel it was not a good thing.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: The sustainability issue is a complete con for our nation. The way all of these green incentives and taxes are currently delivered in the Uk is a complete disgrace. Sustainability in the UK means a couple of things... poor service, increased taxes and bullshit lies to make everything worse. Yes I agree in being kind to the environment and going through life with as little burden on the environment as possible but the insanity we have seen from successive governments on sustainability is insulting, boring and dam right stupid. I pray for a time when common sense and decency return to our country which has long fallen down.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: This is a waste of money which could be put into something much more worth the effort, we will never be a sustainable country not matter what. Whether something is mandated or not, there will always be people especially; like myself who simply do not care and do not see the point of mandating sustainability initiatives.  There is only a small amount of people who do care for the planet and do want to be somewhat sustainable. let the one's who want to be sustainable and save the already destroyed planet and leave the others to their own devices. It's a waste of time otherwise

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Firstly, the policy that has driven this ideology is not based on sustainability, but is just communism being pushed onto people by imposing rules and regulations that lead to disastrous outcomes. By imposing rules on local councils this does not reflect what people voted for and is just going to waste tax payers money as councils struggle to implement these rules. The rules just drive up costs such as we have seen in the energy markets where costs have been pushed onto the tax payer/ bill payers and have not led to any improved outcomes. Then their answer is to impose more rukes when these initial initiatives don't work.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Firstly, a lot of individuals do not know how to, or are not bothered to, live sustainably. Many people have far more pressing and obviously impacting factors to deal with in their lives. A push from local councils and guidance could help push people in the right direction. Not everyone, especially the more elderly population, is educated on how to look after the environment. It is a fairly new way of living, and people need information and education in order to help solve the climate crisis. The government also has the power and should bear the brunt of the responsibility to help us become a more sustainable society. The government has massively contributed to such issues by selling arms, buying oil, and looking to make money out of destroying the environment; they should be the ones to lead this kind of initiative.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: This country is overpopulated, and things are getting worse. More people means more consumption and more waste. We are constantly encouraged to consume and discard. We pay a lot to the council to dispose of waste, so they should take a lead in reducing it. Recycling has its role to play, but more important is to reuse packaging and not consume it in the first place. Food waste should be composted or fed to pigs, metal should be extracted and sold for scrap by the council, other stuff also has possible value. The council itself should not be wasting resources either.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: By councils collaborating they implement similar ideas so that normal people can do positive things towards sustainability without even thinking about it. If every homeowner in the country joins in, they do so without even thinking. Sustainability needs to come from the top, originally from the government, then passed down to local councils and then finally to the individual households. Councils could improve outside spaces so that people use cars, buses etc less therefore improving sustainability and improving peoples health. Solar panel grants are also a great way of reducing gas usage and encourage a more sustainable way of heating homes.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: We have been asked to consider whether councils should be mandated to implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives. On the surface of course it is a laudable aim, sustainability is a positive aim and one that everyone should be supporting. However my concerns as an ex local authority employee would be the lack of detail in the statement. We don't know whether the initiatives are meant to be ones for council services such as roads, schools, refuse services etc or whether the council is tasked with supporting citizens and businesses to be more sustainable. If this were the case, there would be additional legislation needed. We also don't know what area of sustainability this is aimed at; without better guidance it will be lost. The final point is funding for any scheme, local authorities are over stretched with statutory services, so how will this be funded and for how long?

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I mostly support and agree with this statement, however it would depend on how what comprehensive means for the council and how far they will go. It matters on what the local residents will get a benefit for their hard work. As already the council tax rates are extremely high. The council would need to do alot of work to prove themselves to inform that this initiative is worthwhile for everyone and is just not a trends. Providing some kind of incentive or discount for residents would be appreciated I think. This would ensure that residents needs are met appropriately.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
I believe that the Uk should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives. It could only help to have more information that is thoroughly researched and supported by the council available.
 I assume sustainability means being able to use something long term with minimal environmental impact and to look at more green ways of running day to day things. I couldn't imagine anyone would disagree with trying to do this as it's what every human should be trying to do. It's just I imagine making green changes could be costly and that may put people off. They may feel pressured if the council issues initiatives.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
-Enhanced collaboration: collaboration among stakeholders including local authorities and businesses to address sustainability challenges effectively. has to provide the information needed and the other necessary things to do in order to let the aim be achieved in a reasonably way which is wanted .
-Improved sustainability outcome: by making it clear up that sustainability goals and engaging stakeholders can leads to the creation of befitting action plans that can drive a well meaningful progress toward the environment ,social ,and economic well-being  so the goals will be reached easily and aquiredably for the purpose of the environment at large successfully .

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
Well I think that on balance, it is more probable tham not that if all local councils in the UK were to be ordered to implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives, it would help to boost up the probability that the same local councils would find ways to become more efficient, based on the initiatives that they would implement. 

Also, I think that if they were to implement such comprehensive sustainability initiatives, they would be more likely to use less resources, especiallty tangible ones, which would boost the chances that hey would save money, especially given they would be relying on tax payers money.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
I am not sure how wide the concept of sustainability stetches but environment is the first thing that comes to mind. On that score, we are all aware to at least some extent of the challanges we face in terms of climate change, but since the subject seems to be restricted to local councils, I assume factors such as sustaining wild/sea life is not relevant here and we are restricted to initiatives like recycling, using recycled containers etc. 

In that respect, given the challange undertaken by UK, it seems only right that the local councils should have infrastructure and processess in place to ensure compliance such as re-cycling facilities and encouragment of sustainable energy respources.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: If local councils begin to invest more in sustainability initiatives then it will reduce the negative and damaging impacts on the environment and help reduce carbon footprint levels. There will also be an increase in renewable energy sources which can mean that getting energy is cheaper and is more efficient. Possible initiatives include investing in public transport and expanding recycling programmes to reduce a communities overall carbon footprint. Not only would this benefit the plant but it would also benefit households with lower incomes as energy should be cheaper and an increased use of public transport should make the system more effective and cheaper

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: If local councils dont implement sustainability initiatives it would be bad for the environment. We need to do more to help the planet and local councils is a good start. We need to make sure our rubbish is properly recycled when possible. Local councils can control where new houses are built not on greenbelt. Local councils are responsible for green spaces and keeping parks clean and family friendly. Local councils can encourage using renewable energy and conserving water by making sure water pipes dont burst. They can encourage more use of public transport by putting on more bus routes and cheaper fares.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: By mandating all local councils to implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives this will create a good base to reduce the level of climate change and help significantly with reaching net zero goals.  Including all councils will also help with communication in all communities and help to engage and encourage them with sustainability measure.  There are also economic opportunities as sustainability measures can create new employment which would benefit all communities.  By incorporating sustainability measure in council operations this can also help greatly with their sustainability foot print and help with reducing climate change.  Transparency in the Councils actions can also encourage greater trust

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Sustainability initiatives are important within our society as they can help combat climate change and ensure our spaces are managed well without always thinking about profit. Studies have shown that sustainable places which utilise green and blue infrastructure can benefit mental health and can also help with physical health too. These are important places which should be safeguarded. Biodiversity also benefits from sustainable places. Another important aspect of sustainability is energy efficiency. Solar panels are good for the environment and sustainable. Thankfully they are now affordable and should be encouraged to decrease energy prices and limit the use of obsolete energy methods.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
The local councils should make it easier to improve the sustainability from its residents. For instance, people should be able to use general waste to put all the materials in and then the waste company can use automated systems to separate the rubbish to the correct corresponding material for recycling.

Furthermore, the local council can provide incentives for people that are making further efforts to recycle and use renewable energy methods such as providing discounts on council tax. The local council should make it easier and more rewarding for people to want to use sustainable methods since this is important for the future.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
Teh UK should mandate the all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.  We hjave a responsibility to protect our limited natural resources.  This are limited and cannot be replaced. Without coordinated, local action we risk depleting these assets, such as clean water, green spaces and natural environments.  these help support our way of life now and are needed for future generations.
Environmental protection cannot be left to chance or isolated efforts.  CLimate change, pollution and habitat loss are global problems but thier solutions often begin at home.  By requiring councils to take sustainable action the UK woudl be making a meaningful commitment to protecting the environment, while empwering communities to be part fo the solution

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: The country can ensure consistent progress toward environmental goals, reduce regional disparities and accelerate the transition to a greener future. These initiatives also bring practical benefits to communities from cleaner air and improved public health to new green jobs and more resilient local economies. This will make a great part of my argument on why i agree but also mandating sustainability initiatives for all local councils ensure that environmental action is not optional or inconsistent across the UK. it empowers local authorities to take meaningful steps in areas like waste reduction, green transport and energy efficiency which have a direct impact on residents daily lives

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
Education of the public on how to sustainability use and dispose of products can reduce the volume of littering and waste heading to landfills by providing extensive recycling services.
Conscious consumption reduces the wasteful use of natural resources and other materials.
A reduced reliance on non-renewable materials and energy sources will also reduce a population's carbon footprint and impact on their environment, reducing the risk of ecosystems being harmed due to pollution.
Upgrading recycling facilities to accept currently unrecyclable materials will also help in reducing the volume of rubbish heading to landfills. 
Finally, educating the public on eco-friendly alternative can help reduce our reliance on polluting chemicals and unrecyclable materials such as plastics.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
Implementing a mandate would ensure that this is abided to all across the country, driving towards the future goals of the future, especially with the climate change.

Councils may not introduce this mandate sufficient leading to it not being up-held, perhaps due to finances of that Council.

Introducing this Mandate would issue rules to the local councils, which would they would all have to adhere to, promoting them all to make an effort to the climate change for the future years, when this will be needed as the Climate Change needs to be looked at with immediate effect, for the future.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: we have to many body's in the UK that waste the funds of the people in foolish ideas and masses of paperwork and re tape, to many people involved in the higher areas of these councils have forgotten who they are actually meant to represent and what they should be trying to achieve, madness in the way money is spent on topics and areas that are not needed, trying to represent minority's when they should be representing the majority, by cutting back on waste in all areas, cutting funding for these pointless studies and stop employing people in roles that have no bearing on real life

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I fully agree with the statement that the UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability intitiatives, firstly because sustainability and climate action is still greatly important, and is being hugely overlooked by many, due to more pressing current events. I fully agree with the idea that this should be done on a local level, as it makes the task of making our country more sustainable actually feasible, as each local council could do their part and contribute to the country as a whole. I worry about the competency of many local councils, but that doesn't really affect my opinion.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives because local governments play a crucial role in addressing climate change and protecting the environment. Councils are closely connected to their communities and can implement practical solutions tailored to local needs, such as improving waste management, promoting green transport, or preserving green spaces. Their actions can significantly reduce pollution and environmental degradation at a grassroots level. Furthermore, requiring councils to create sustainability plans would encourage greater community involvement. When residents see their local leaders taking environmental issues seriously, they are more likely to adopt eco-friendly habits themselves. This engagement not only raises awareness but also builds a shared sense of responsibility for the environment. By making sustainability a consistent priority across all regions, the UK can ensure more unified and effective action in combating climate change.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I completely believe that Local Councils should implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives but am much less keen on them being mandated to do so. I also believe in local democracy and that local authorities (LAs) should have the power to manage sustainability in their own way. I would prefer to see central government making grants available to LAs so that they do what government wants but are not mandated to do so. A carrot rather than a stick approach. I do campaign in local and national debates and would seek to get a LA  elected which was doing comprehensive sustainability initiatives rather than one which is not that is local democracy.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Including feminist perspectives in the National literature curriculum would be a very positive thing to do, it would help to broaden students understanding of literature and also challenge the traditional male narratives. It would promote critical thinking which can only be positive, having the views of feminists along side the traditional male narrative would promote critical thinking about gender and power. The inclusion of feminist perspectives would have to be implemented thoughtfully, we would have to make sure that we ensure that diverse feminist voices and perspectives are represented in order to make sure that the approach is inclusive of all students. Feminist writers have had an important role in our society so it is only right that the national literature curriculum shows that.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Mandating the inclusion of feminine perspectives in the national literature curriculum  leads to a more inclusive, equitable and thoughtful educational experience which prepares students to engage with and contribute to a diverse society. It encourages the students to critically examine societal structures and promotes a more equitable understanding of gender roles. Also challenges the readers to question established narratives and consider multiple viewpoints, thereby enhancing their analytical abilities. I believe by integrating feminists perspectives into education, students are equipped with tools to challenge injustice and contribute to a more progressive society. It also offers representation to those who have been underrepresented in the mainstream narratives, giving them a sense of belonging.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Students should be exposed to a wide range and diverse opinions as it encourages them to think critically and analytically about complex and important issues which affect them and the wider society. It is important for them to study the struggles of discriminated groups, from women, LGBT groups, ethnic minorities etc. Studying literature from all these perspectives can show how stereotypes and prejudice are perpetuated and how they impact and effect society as a whole This can be achieved by analysing key literature text that look at sexism, gender discrimination and misogyny. It is important that we celebrate and encourage feminist literature in the curriculum.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Feminist perspectives on society, past and present, are essential to understanding both historical events and the current state of the world. Including this in the national curriculum is an essential part of education for this reason, and not including it would portray a dishonest view of society. With the current rise of sexism, particularly from the right but also increasingly from the left, including feminist teaching is critical to the fight to prevent it. Within this, intersectional feminism is crucial in order to include all perspectives. It is also important, however, within this literature, to include critiques of the material to give a rounded view. This is particularly important for extremes such as radical feminism which can often include trans-exclusionary and transmisogynist views, which are equally as important to fight against.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Including feminine perspectives in the national literature curriculum helps students understand how gender roles and inequalities have shaped both literature and society. Many classic texts reflect the values and limitations of their time, often sidelining or stereotyping women. By studying feminist viewpoints, students can explore how these portrayals came to be, how they have been challenged, and how literature can be a tool for social change. It also encourages students to think more critically about the stories they read and the world around them. Rather than accepting traditional narratives at face value, they learn to question whose voices are heard and whose are missing.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Making it mandatory to include feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum could cause several problems. One issue is that it might focus too much on one way of thinking and ignore other important viewpoints, which means students wouldn’t get to explore a wide range of ideas and opinions. Literature is diverse, and focusing heavily on just feminist perspectives could limit students’ understanding of different cultures, histories, and experiences. Additionally, this requirement might not truly represent what most people in society are interested in or concerned about, making the curriculum less relevant to some students. It could also limit the freedom of teachers to choose books and materials they believe are best suited for their students, and restrict students from discovering stories that speak to their own lives and interests. When education becomes too focused on promoting a single perspective, it can stop people from thinking critically and learning from many different voices. Overall, forcing only one viewpoint in the curriculum might do more harm than good by narrowing students’ learning opportunities.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: I don't believe that the UK should mandate the inclusion of any perspectives, including feminism,  socialism, or any other ism, in the national literature curriculum. 'Isms,' like feminism are political creatures, subject to redefinition by special interests. The term 'feminism' has been redefined during my lifetime. Once it meant the desire for women to have equal rights with men. Now it seems to have taken on negative connotations, fueled by gender ideology. While politics can be the subject of literature, one should not have to pass some kind of political litmus test to have your works studied for their literary qualities, and not their political ones.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: This is an important step towards a more inclusive education. Literature has historically been dominated by male voices, unless it's a love story. The female perspective is often pushed to the side, and rarely mentioned. But in real life, this is not the case. By educating children on the female perspective, they will have more understanding of it when they grow up. They will be able to understand the struggles of women more and respect them more because of this. It is also long overdue that the female voice has a stronger stage presence in life, they have been overlooked for too long

You men in particular would grow up knowing much more and understanding much more about feminism and, indeed, being a woman if they studied literature from that perspective. Early studies of feminist literature could be very revealing and quite exciting

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: While diverse perspectives are important, no particular one should be mandated. Requiring a feminist lens risks limiting academic neutrality and open enquiry. Students have always benefited from exposure to multiple viewpoints, which encourages critical thinking and allows them to form their own informed opinions. And because, based on the statement, no other perspective has been mandated in order to create a "balance",  even if such a thing was possible. Also, based on history, students are more likely to "rebel" against an idea if they perceive it as being forced on them, which would have the opposite effect  to the one the mandate intended.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: My opinion on mandating the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the UK's national literature curriculum is nuanced. On one hand, I recognize the importance of promoting feminist thought and its relevance to literary analysis. However, I am concerned that a blanket mandate might lead to an imbalance in the curriculum, prioritizing a single perspective over others. This could result in a narrow focus on specific themes and ideas, overlooking other critical works and perspectives. Moreover, I worry that such a mandate might oversimplify the complexity of feminist thought, reducing it to a simplistic narrative that does not accurately reflect the nuances of the subject matter. Instead, education should focus on creating an environment that encourages students to explore various perspectives and ideas in a nuanced and context dependent manner, allowing them to develop a richer understanding of literature and its diverse themes.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Including feminist perspectives within the national literature curriculum would be a good idea as it may help to empower young women and encourage them to speak up by highlighting the strength of a women's voice. However, it is equally important in the curriculum to demonstrate the necessity for equality within the curriculum. For this to be done, a feminist's perspective should not be singled out but, instead, should be equally mandated amongst other perspectives. Drawing from a number of perspectives within the classroom will allow for a broader range of understanding and will open the floor for wider discussion and debate.

rows.text: Transgender individuals are a minority which make up <1% of the UK population. Despite this, they are at a greater risk of discrimination and violence. It is important that the rights of trans people are codified into law to protect not only their right to self-identity, but also to protect their mental and physical health. It is possible to achieve this without "infringing" upon the rights of other vulnerable groups. The idea that cisgender women are more "at risk" when sharing spaces with transgender women is rooted in transphobia and a misunderstanding of how transgender identities are shaped and experienced.

rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.
rows.text:
Protecting vulnerable members of society: Transgender people often face high levels of discrimination. This is strongly interconnected with high rates of violence and poverty. Therefore the state has a real obligation to actively protect these groups within British society. There needs to be a comprehensive legislative framework to protect these people. 

Human rights: Every member of our society has a personal right to their own self gender identity and expression thereof. We all have the right to a basic level of human dignity. Therefore transgender rights need to be enshrined in law to cover key areas such as housing, healthcare and employment.

rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.
rows.text:
I don't have an arguments against protecting the rights and recognising transgender identity. My own take currently on this, is that rather than push forward with laws there are still gaps that need filling, some of those vital. Having a background in pharmacy and knowing about education and development, I have real and deep seated concerns about the ease at which young people can transition. From my experience, this transition and transgender identity go hand-in-hand quite often. Despite what people may think, we don't know the long term psychological effects and physical effects around the hormones and medications they are given at a young age. There are arguments about the ability of the young to make such heavy decisions (despite the arguments about having to go through a series of doctors over a time scale). They are many cancers driven by hormones and we appear to be willing to give these potent substances to our young. I don't agree with this aspect at all- they have have their identity, but should have to wait if that identity completeness means taking medication too. 
Women seemed to have come under the wrath or 'war' path of some in the transgender community and we appeared to go backwards in the rights of women being undermined over transgender rights and this concerns me about laws, which I feel would (not may) discriminate heavily against women having seen this in action until the recent landmark case.
Currently, transgender space does not to mean to me that they have a right to women's spaces, sports or other aspects of women's right as an automatic. Women absolutely have a right and a say in this and they have recently been denied this. Transgender individuals should have their own transgender spaces and sports and so on, because at the moment 'some want the cake and want to eat it too themselves' without concern for those it effects directly.

Encouraging and educating society and building a code of ethics seems to be a better way initially , until it is absolutely clear than other members of society, notably women , don't suffer further and move back with the rights they fought for and haven't really had the time to digest and taken these in (of course, women globally continue to suffer).
