rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.
rows.text: There are a number of positive implications of legally recognising all forms of transgender identity. Firstly, it would greatly improve the mental health of transgender individuals and would reduce depression and rates of suicide. Furthermore, it would help create a more equal and fairer society with everybody able to express their true selves openly. Moreover, it would help reduce discrimination in society which can affect individuals access to a number of areas such as healthcare and employment. However, there are a number of downsides to recognising transgender individuals such as the effect it can have on women's sports. Therefore, the law should be very clear on how to recognise transgender status without taking away from other people.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: I'm not so sure the legalisation of recreational marijuana is a good thing. Yes, it is less harmful than alcohol and said to have several health benefits, but it is still an addictive substance that can cause dependence and financial issues for users. A lot of people seem to claim that it isn't addictive, but from experience with users, I know full well that it is and that it dumbs people down completely, therefore it could end up being a stain on a society that is already stupid enough as it is. What the UK doesn't need is something that will make them stupider than they already are, and the population aren't trustworthy enough to use this privilege wisely.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text:
-Health risks involved;The use of marijuana for recreational use posses serious health concerns as it potentially affects and impacts on the mental health and cognitive health of individuals. This could potentially increase terminal illnesses as well as death rates of the nation. 
-Impact on youth and vulnerable groups;  This could potentially increase the access of marijuana for minors and in turn impact on the health of the vulnerable population or groups. This could also encourage erratic behaviour  which is commonly one of the side effects of the use of marijuana.
-Safety of the public; The use of marijuana could bring about serious public safety concerns. The risks of driving under the influence of marijuana could result in unwanted accidents and threats to live of the public, also concerns about workplace safety could arise as a result of the use of marijuana.

If legalised people might decide to use the drug more frequently and thus having a more prolonged impact on their mental health. rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: As far as I am aware, marijuana isn't as addictive or as bad for your health as smoking tobacco or drinking alcohol, therefore I cannot understand why it is still illegal.  If marijuana was legalised, perhaps tobacco and alcohol consumption would be reduced, resulting in less of a burden on our National Health Service.  Also the extra taxes that could be collected from the legal consumption of marijuana could be put to good use in supporting our health system.  At the moment, people who want to take this drug have to pay high prices that are set by drug dealers, and this in turn may cause users to become depressed, which is not good for the health of our society.   Legalising marijuana would ultimately enable the price to be controlled, and would prevent criminal gangs being able to make money on it.

If people have pain related conditions, or PTSD where they struggle to relax, marijuana will ensure this can be possible. 
It would need to be regulated, but it is possible so that people can have a choice of how they spend their time which does not always involve alcohol. 
Care would need to be taken to ensure strong strains are not introduced which can cause negative psychological effects - this is why we would need to police it properly.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: It is true that Marijuana has some stated health benefits for some health related issues, but overall, long-term use carries a net-negative impact on physical and mental health. Smoking Marijuana medicinally can have pain killing effects such as for people suffering from MS. Cancer patients have also used it to bring pain relief, post treatment. Marijuana used recreationally is a different story. Studies have shown that using Marijuana long term, can have a serious impact on health. Degradation in brain function as a consequence for excessive use over time has been observed and documented. The onset of mental health issues such as Paranoia, depression, anxiety and even psychosis are also ill-effects that can come about from smoking Marijuana. One could argue that casual use might be okay, but it is difficult to gauge what a safe limit is for most people, so it is safer to err on the side of caution and abstain completely from using it.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text:

It is highly addictive and once a user starts smoking it they can become totally dependant on the drug, it then can affect that persons health, mental wellbeing and their families in many ways.  In particular it is well known that marijuana can affect the users memory, increase the risk of psychosis and schizophrenia, it can affect young peoples brain formation causing them lifelong mental health problems.  It can cause issues with the lungs as it is inhaled and raises the risk of lung cancers similar to tobacco smoking.
Users who try to stop smoking  marijuana can suffer from anxiety, anger aggression, disturbed sleep and depression because of the potency of the drug.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: As a society we have legalised the purchase and consumption of alcohol for hundreds of years. I personally find that more injurious to society and the person than cannabis. Criminalising cannabis hasn't worked, and by legalising it we potentially create a tax income which could be used, and free up the authorities to concentrate on criminals selling class A drugs, but I personally think there is a case for being able to legally prescribe them. Bringing under the legal umbrella could provide better opportunities to study its use and the possible damage it causes to the human race. It would also provide the opportunity to provide a standardised medically tested product, taking away the risk of buying the unknown.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: I would say that we have comparable drugs that are legal. Alcohol, painkillers, anti-depressants and ADHD medicine can all be abused in the same way marijuana can, but we trust people to make their own choices for those drugs. Treating marijuana use as a public health/social issue can prevent young offenders from entering into a lifetime of crime. Having a criminal record for possessing a personal amount of marijuana can push young people into institutions, unemployment, drug abuse, self-harm that they otherwise may not have entered.  Treating marijuana use as a civil liberty in the way we do with alcohol consumption seems fairer and more modern.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: I have no personal experience with marijuana, but I know people who smoke it and believe it helps them cope with health and stress issues. I worked several times in the Netherlands with coffee bars openly selling the drug. The establishments always seemed to be very clean, quiet, and well-respected places to socialise. I do not recall hearing of a crime related to the use or sale of weed. It seemed to me that the police there were more focused on more dangerous substances and the protection of lives and property. Given the pressure on public funds, that seems a sensible saving for the UK. Many of the US states now sell gummies and weed in shops, and it appears that they have not experienced much in the way of adverse reactions.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: Although I do not use marijuana in any form I can several benefits of legalising it for recreational use. Firstly, it would mean that taxes could be raised on the sale of marijuana rather than the financial benefits going to criminals who currently sell it. The legalisation of marijuana could also mean that the authorities could focus on other illegal drugs and this would make sense from a law and order point of view. It would also acknowledge what many people already do. The current legislation does not dissuade people from using marijuana so it makes sense to remove it. It would also mean that the courts could free up more time for more serious issues when people currently are taken to court for possession.

Also think that the BBC is biased when it comes to political broadcasting as doesn't give other political parties enough air time and just concentrates on the party that happens to be in power at the moment. rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:
I believe the license fee is outdated and not in line with the current streaming landscape. The fact that it is an offence not to hold a license, is antiquated and draconian. 
However, in a democracy, I still favour the idea of having a state broadcaster where we can rely on factual and balanced content. Yes, I know the BBC work with the government of the day, something that was openly admitted when I interviewed a previous Director General, but that is not necessarily a bad thing, provided the broadcaster offers a wide and balanced coverage of views. I don't know how a restructuring of funding would work, but I feel the old system of public payment is outdated and needs to be overhauled.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The issue of funding for the BBC is multifaceted and does not have a simple answer. In light of the recent proliferation of platforms, many of which produce extremely high quality programming, the question for many is why the BBC is still being funded by taxpayer money. On the other hand, the BBC has become an institution and is synonymous with British culture for many abroad and within Britain. Defunding the BBC would lead either to its demise or to it having to look for other revenue streams such as advertising, which would for many cheapen the quality of its output and be a detriment to British culture and an established British institution.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC is a national broadcaster of great standing, and is relied upon internationally as a source of trusted, expert and impartial information, important in these days of misinformation and social media. It is a great ambassador for the UK, selling its popular shows and formats abroad and is a world class institution. The BBC is a public service broadcaster meaning it has a purpose and not just a commercial aim to make profits for shareholders. This means it makes programmes and resources for everybody to use and increases accessibility provision which no other broadcasters have a mandate to do. Young people have great choice now in their entertainment providers and argue that they should not pay the one amount licence fee because they do not use the BBC much, they should pay for what they need as with other providers. The UK is under a lot of financial pressure at the moment and funding the BBC is one area that maybe needs a review to check that it is still value for money.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I find this a hard one to answer, because on the one hand the BBC acts as a fantastic source of soft power for the UK - the world service is often referenced by people as something which either endeared them to the UK or kept them in touch and not homesick when away from the UK. However the BBC is increasingly becoming less neutral on issues and that makes it difficult as a taxpayer funded organization, I believe the BBC should operate like other TV operators in the UK on an advertising basis or it should advertise its programs outside of the UK to subsidize the UK element.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC supports charities and raises money for causes such as children in need, which has a positive impact on many people's lives and so without funding they may not be able to continue doing this which would cause some people to suffer. The BBC also has a website dedicated to education and learning such as BBC bitesize, which many students rely on. They offer content from Key stage 1 right up to high school meaning they offer a large range of content. This can be useful for children who are home schooled as well as others who wouldn't have access to other educational support should it not be for the BBC and so I think its important that their funding isn't cut.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: In light of the way most people in the UK watch television via streaming devices, I believe that charging a licence fee for one channel like the BBC is unfair, especially when their news and television content appears to be politically biased. The fee for the licence is now £15 per television. If we all paid that fee per channel, no one would be watching much television except the wealthy. I do not believe the BBC offer much value for their licence fee and feel that it should be voluntary. I also do not believe that the UK Government should be funding the BBC as this creates more opportunity for political bias.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: My thoughts on this are a little bit mixed.  The BBC hasn't had a great reputation in recent years but it is one of the remaining British organisations and I think should stay as a public service channel that is politically neutral.  There are many other channels that can be chosen if the viewer wants a different perspective or an alternative.  Although we as consumers have to pay for a licence, I would worry that it may end up a subscription model like many of the other streaming services, and that would be its downfall because it would not compete as well.  So I think it should retain its unique funding platform, even though it is a bit outdated but use it as a challenge to update and reinvent themselves and align with the British public again.  Perhaps lowering the cost of the licence will help bring the people back, and provide really good new solid programming instead of lots of repeated shows from the past. Maybe bring back some lost old favourites such as Top of the Pops?  That sort of thing?

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.

I know that the general population is not paying for their TV licenses, and I know that there are arguments for the scrapping of TV licenses. However, I truly don't know where the BBC would get their funding from if they were defunded. I would assume that more advertisements would be played, especially on iPlayer, but this would likely chase away more viewers too. I am unsure what the UK funding the BBC means in the sense of whether this is government funding or if it all comes from the TV licenses.

It is unnecessary  financial burden for every one, especially when there are more important and urgent areas like healthcare or education that could benefit from this funding. relocating these funds will lead to more direct and meaningful support for the nation.

TV advertising revenue is falling due to digital services so this would effectively defund all other services.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC continues to play a valuable role in our society and should not be defunded. As a public service broadcaster, the BBC provides trusted news, diverse cultural programming, and educational resources that are free from commercial influence. Its content reaches a broad audience and helps foster a sense of shared identity and community, especially during major national moments. While I do think the BBC must evolve, improving transparency, ensuring impartiality, and adapting to the digital age, scrapping public funding altogether risks losing something unique and important. Instead of defunding, thoughtful reform would be a better way to address valid criticisms while preserving the BBC's strengths.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.

The radio stations need the money from the UK TV Licences to keep maintaining the quality of programmes and being able to pay the top presenters. We need to keep funding the BBC to maintain these great TV and radio shows for future generations and to preserve the UK's reputation for brilliance. The BBC is part of Britain's heritage and should be cherished

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I disagree that defunding the BBC is a good thing, I think it is classist and a bad move from the government. Access to news is very important, I may even lean to saying that it is a right, and the way most people access news in the UK is through the BBC. It isn't fair that households with less income shouldn't have access to the news and the current events in the country/world, especially if it directly affects them. Whilst some argue that the BBC is bias (which it probably is), defunding it and making it a privatised business would make it even more bias! News should be unbiased but also universally accessible, and defunding it isn't the way to go about that.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.

Older people rely on the local radio services.  The shipping forecast is vital for those who live by the coast.  There are numerous educational and information outputs.  The world service provides important soft power in areas in the world in turmoil and it is broadly trusted.  There is no commercial provider who can do this, and despite some short comings it should continue to be funded as a national (and international) service

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC is part of British heritage and is something that we have all grown up with. It is a service that makes me proud to be British, is is provides media coverage that is current, topical and up to date. With the television programmes that are produced by the BBC, they are varied and of a high standard. The figure we pay each month for our license fee is great value for money, given the variety of TV, Radio and Online services it provides. There really is something for everyone when consuming the BBC, and it certainly would be the same is it became a commercial platform where there were continual ads.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:

Whilst the BBC does attract detractors from some for it's perceived political bias, mainly in comedy shows, time has shown that it is equally likely to lampoon any party. I don't agree that it has a left wing bias. 
also, the BBC has recently introduced it's BBC verify service and in an age of unreliable and misleading stories from Facebook/TikTok, etc, an unbiased source of information is much needed.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.

The main causes of accidents. Accidentaly cars with preemptive crash detection can decrease mortality by taking split second decision to take over and create a more favourable impact position, or preemptively deploy airbags etc. Aidttionaly most autonmous cars are electric, which means a lower center of gravity and therefore a better and mroe safe car, which is less likely to flip. Producivity as people will be able to work and take calls etc instead of dead time when driving.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text:
I feel autonomous driving will bring in more drivers and cars to the road, especially as several people are unwilling, unskilled or unable to drive cars today. I am not convinced that more cars on the road is a solution to the environmental and traffic issues we are facing today. I believe we should put in more effort to encourage public transport and mass means of transport instead. 

It can also cause issues with legal liability as we may not be able to apportion blame correctly (car versus driver) in case of an accident or an issue. If no liability is applied to the driver, it can cause a dangerous precedent with drivers misusing the privilege with speeding, for example. Tighter checks are needed on the system and appropriate legal frameworks before autonomous driving is launched. 

However I do accept there will be some benefits for people who have disabilities, enabling them to get on the roads.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I do not think the UK is ready to allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads. At the moment there are so many accidents on the road as it is, bringing autonomous vehicles it just an accident waiting to happen. It could become unreliable if there becomes a technical fault. I do not think this is a good idea. We would have to put a load of safety measures in place first which could become costly. There should be alot of checks and tests for this to become even a thought but again we are a long way from that.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Autonomous vehicles are being developed, largely in other countries, but as far as I know no country allows autonomous vehicles on all public roads.  The technology is still being developed and any vehicles allowed on the roads are part of tightly controlled trials - usually with a human in the vehicle in case of problems.  The technology is still in its infancy and there are lots of issues to be addressed before they are legal everywhere.  There would be a risk of these vehicles malfunctioning and a danger to other motorists, cyclists and pedestrians.  Besides the physical danger there also needs to be changes made to the law, to ascertain who is liable in law for any accidents arising from the use of these vehicles.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: The introduction of autonomous vehicles on UK roads is still far from being a reality. Public trust of such vehicles is at a low level and there just isn't the public demand for it. Demand is coming from tech companies who want to force this issue, and should not be allowed to put pressure on MPs as this is undemocratic . One of the key issues is guidance of these vehicles. Google maps and similar operating systems often lead human drivers into errors because of inaccuracies and i cannot foresee that the situation would be any different with ai operated machines.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: In my opinion it is quite dangerous for these autonomous vehicles to be on all public roads, they haven't been tested thoroughly and they might be unpredictable. No one can know how these vehicles would react in certain situations, maybe something like a sensor will break down in the middle of their journey and that can cause a potential dangerous situation. One can argue that human operated cars can also break down and this is true, but at least if this happens there is always a human behind the wheel that can figure out the problem and react properly to the situation before it gets risky.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Autonomous vehicles could significantly reduce accidents caused by human error, such as distracted driving, tiredness or generally bad driving. The autonomous vehicles can react faster and more predictably to traffice conditions which will reduce collisions, deaths and injuries. They will also increase efficiency by communicating with each other and help with traffic flow which will reduce traffic jams. They can follow the best routes available and reduce travel times and stop delays. They will also give greater independence for older people or those with disabilites, that are unable to drive. It would be much better and easier that using public transport.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Autonomous vehicles, although a relatively new technology and having a slow uptake, may in the future be the dominant vehicles on the road if we allow them and regulate them like any other vehicle. Autonomous vehicles would be quicker to respond to hazards than a human, and don't suffer from issues such as distraction or tiredness. They can keep driving for as long as they have fuel, without the driver having to take a break, so would be particularly suited for long distance journeys. If autonomous vehicles are used for public transport, it can provide cheaper fares for users since there won't be a need to pay the salaries of bus drivers

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I support allowing autonomous vehicles on all public roads in the UK. These vehicles have the potential to reduce accidents significantly, as most accidents are caused by human error. By relying on advanced sensors and AI, they can react faster and more accurately in many situations. Autonomous vehicles also offer greater independence to elderly people or those with disabilities who are unable to drive. Moreover, they can also be programmed for optimal fuel efficiency, helping reduce traffic congestion and emissions. With proper regulations and testing, this technology can improve road safety and modernise the UK's transport system for the future.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Having edited a book on autonomous vehicles, I'm in support of them. The programming and safety features mean that they are incredibly safe and unable to do things, such as break speed limits or use a mobile phone, that human drivers are not good with. I think the two main issues here are confidence, from both users of the AVs, other road users, and the public in general. I'd be hopeful that this could be overcome with data and demonstrations, plus some laws governing things like what passengers can and can't do while in an AV to optimise safety. The second issue is human error, mainly that they will be used incorrectly by their owners or, worse still, people will try to hack into the programming so that they can exceed speed limits or perform illegal manoeuvres. This seems a bigger threat to me.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I am too old to really embrace the idea of driverless cars, it is very worrying. I don't understand how a computer could respond accurately to every given situation. There have been so many accidents recently, would these have been prevented? I don't understand how. A human brain is much more complex and faster than any computer, and still accidents happen regularly. Aircraft have sophisticated computers and they still fail. I would be terrified in a driverless car. I have no idea what the rules would be. Is a qualified driver still required? Does the computer take a test? Too many unknowns for me.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.

To allow them on all public roads would be reckless whilst we still don’t know the full dangers and limitations of these cars. The public would also need full consultation and education around safety measures etc. Also what jobs would these be replacing and why? to what benefit? There would also be a loss of human connection and interaction if these type of cars were allowed anywhere and everywhere. It just seems like an unnecessary danger for little to no benefit in the short rterm

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: It's difficult to give an opinion on this subject, as I don't feel like I know enough about it, or am as informed as I could be on the subject of autonomous vehicles. However, I would not be comfortable with the statement that they should be on all public roads, as I have seen footage of autonomous vehicles in the United States, and I don't feel like they are fully safe, or that all the potential issues with them have been ironed out yet, for them to be distributed widely across the country, especially not on all public roads or highways.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: The autonomous vehicle technology is not fully and satisfactorily tested, therefore, it is a really bad idea to allow these vehicles on our public roads. The safety of pedestrians and other vehicles and infrastructure around the road could be severely compromised with the introduction of such vehicles. There are potholes everywhere which need a human to gauge and avoid. UK roads are really narrow especially in rural areas where even small cars can struggle. There are bushes and overgrowth in these areas that will confuse any sensor. To combat all that, there will be a significant amount of investment requires to bring up the road infrastructure in the UK. I don't know if the politicians are ready to invest that kind of amount.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I tend to lean against allowing autonomous vehicles on public roads as they might lead to human drivers becoming complacent, potentially increasing the risk of accidents. Additionally, autonomous vehicles could malfunction, especially in high risk areas such as schools and motorways, which could lead to very dangerous situations. However, I do recognise that the autonomous vehicles can be beneficial to some members of society, most notably those less able. The extension of the use of the vehicles can also give a new lease of life to them and allow them to travel more easily around. This benefit is one of the reasons why I am not totally against the idea of allowing the change.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I do not believe that AT THIS MOMENT autonomous vehicles should be allowed on our roads. I think that this technology has been tested thoroughly enough. It is being tested at the moment predominantly in the US so let them iron out the problems before we get involved. We do not need to be at the forefront in this as it could cause unnecessary deaths. That having been said it is obvious that the world is headed in this direction and this is the future of transport and as such it will need to be embraced at some point in the future, just not quite yet.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: A recent movie 'Leave the World Behind' showed autonomous electric vehicles crashing into each other with such force that the road became completely covered in them. I would not trust an autonomous vehicle to drive safely, unless there was an override included that would allow a human to take over if she/he felt that the car was in danger of crashing. There have been articles about autonomous taxis being considered in some cities, but I have not read any reports of them actually being introduced anywhere. I think that most people, apart from petrol heads, would need a lot of persuading to become a passenger in such a vehicle.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I've come across multiple instances of Waymo taxis in the US failing to navigate consistently. They seem to get stuck, especially when two encounter each other, travelling in opposite directions on a narrow road. That's just one of the issues. The problem with development is that, when you think you're close to the precipice, the road extends, by nature of diminishing returns. The journey from 90% to 99% isn't as hard as 99% to 100%. For autonomous vehicles to make sense, they would have to be significantly safer and more effective than having people drive, which, to many people, would be 100% safety and effectiveness. Until an AI model can dodge a crash as effectively as a human could, it shouldn't be deployed on public roads. The technology is nowhere near what it's being presented as. It can reason, but it can't reason as a human.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Self driving vehicles rely on a plethora of sensors and other high tech aids in order to be able to drive themselves. In particular they rely on cameras to identify and respond to road markings and warning signs etc. Given the appalling state of the UK road network I fear that it would be difficult for a car to actually "read" road markings in order to navigate itself and drive in a sensible and safe manner. Additionally I believe there are now concerns that the electronic systems in vehicles could be hijacked by terrorists and for the cars to be turned into "weapons".

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text:
I disagree with this policy as there are too many risks attached to this possibility. For starters, I don't believe the technology has been tested and proven enough to determine that this is safe. I don't believe this is the future, some people get enjoyment out of driving a car, so would feel forced into being in an autonomous vehicle. 
What happens when there is a mix of car drivers and autonomous vehicles? What happens in the event of an accident, who would take the accountability if the fault lies with the autonomous vehicle? How would insurance work in this case? I think there is a high risk of this, and the only positive would be the lessened risk of drivers who drive whilst tired.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
Kids and teenagers need to learn how to use the internet, but to be protected in it's current form until they reach a suitable age. They need to be taught how to develop healthy habits to maintain digital wellbeing and also how to stay secure online.

Social media apps are designed to take advantage of people at every turn. I'd argue that algorithms should be banned until the age of 25 when puberty really ends and the brain stops developing. These platforms can be far more harmful than helpful and enable bullying and all sorts at a young age too.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Banning children from internet usage could help to protect them from dangers that are present online, like cyberbullying and predators making their online usage safer. It could also encourage the children to spend more time outside playing and connecting face to face with other children, which can be important in developing social and emotional developement. But such a ban could also limit theiraccess to useful resources, information and learning opportunities, and the ability to gain useful knowledge o digital skils which they may need in the future. Keeping them safe is one thing completely restricting the internet can hamper their development and learning opportunites.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
I agree that stopping children using the internet would be an advantage to their development. It would give them a chance to learn, think and analyse the world around them. Without external pressure from influencers and social media, young people would be free to learn about themselves, what they enjoy and what makes them happy, rather than being swayed and pressured to follow the latest fashion. Yes, trying to fit in is part of growing up, but the conflicts would be localised and perhaps less encompassing.
Perhaps 14 is still too young to be let loose, as it is often at that age when teenagers become more self conscious and vulnerable. The internet might not be the best place for development. However, I do appreciate that computer use is very much part of life so it will be a fine balance between protecting children and preparing them for the world.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: This is really a silly one. How on earth will you enforce this? By probably wasting too much money which should be spent on housing or the elderly. So only so much time a day spent online and some of that has to be educational. I don't feel a law like this would work and would only cause problems in families and be a waste of government money when trying to put in place. Come on guys! Maybe make better places or schemes for young people to attend so they don't want to be online so much.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.

Thats my first point. My second point is that it could be limited to restrictive use with access to limited sites for school work, research or content that can be only viewed for under 14s such as travel information about countries/holidays, maths, english etc, educational and trustworthy, this could possibly work going forward.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: The internet is a place where kids who have access can accelerate learning at home much quicker than waiting on the days at school etc. Not only this but when used on safe online platforms the internet can be a great place to learn how to build structured conversations that set you up early to be able to go out into the world and be able to speak and hold conversations with people. There are also many apps online where your family and friends can have access to your in time location, This in turn can keep you safe by letting family or friends know where you are and when you are there.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I would be more in favour of this potential regulation of the internet if it was aimed at much younger aged children, such as the under 7s, but I think a ban of children aged up to 13 is unnecessary and probably highly unworkable. Parents are left to regulate a large proportion of a child's life so I don't think that their access to the internet should be any different - their internet usage can be managed much easier by their parents or guardians rather than a piece of legislation. It is probably a lot better to educate children around the safety of the internet and possibly denying them, access to certain websites rather than a blanket ban on the internet.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I think children are going to use the internet in their lifetime so it is much better that they are taught at a younger age about how to use it properly. They should be educated on the dangers of it and be protected from anything unsafe from them. It is however good for them to be able to use it for school work and for enjoyment without them being kept from it or scared of it. It is better to be shown how to use something properly than to just avoid it altogether and is better we as adults show them than them just stumble across it by themselves and potentially view something harmful.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Children under 14 should have monitored internet usage. The web is a scary, expansive place, children can so easily be influenced by the wrong sorts of people there. On the other hand, the internet, when used productively, is infinite knowledge at your fingertips. You can connect with people from anywhere and make friends from all over the world. It can also be a very dangerous hostile place for young, impressionable people. There are plenty of not so nice people just waiting to take advantage on the internet. So stricter guidance is needed, but banning would be taking so many points of connection from young people.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
Firstly, I believe that banning the internet for under 14 year olds would be unworkable. How would this be monitored and how would this be implemented? Adults would provide the internet for the children so it's essentially unenforceable. 

Banning things never works. It's best to teach good internet practise and limit the time spent on the internet but this will be down to good parenting. Unfortunately, bad parents would enable the child to break the rules if they saw fit.

It is best that children are taught to use the internet responsibly and also not allowed to become reliant on it,

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
I think that banning internet use for under 14s sounds like a knee jerk reaction to problems which are already under a form of parental control, namely children accidentally viewing harmful material. 
A blanket ban would be completely unworkable, how would the authorities know what each person is viewing online at any time? how do they know specifically the age of anyone looking at the screen? 
School age children need to use the internet daily for many legitimate reasons like doing homework or accessing help help. There are so many other things to learn about besides what is taught to them in schools, how are they supposed to learn about them, especially if they dont have a library nearby?

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: It is quite a blanket and firm statement to make, regarding the banning of access to the Internet, for under 14 year-olds. The Internet is an important resource and medium for people of all age groups, and nobody should be limited from accessing it. Though there should absolutely be some pressure placed on parents to monitor, control and restrict Internet use if needs be. The burden ultimately, should be placed on the parents and not the government. While Government should offer strong and clear advice to parents, it is up to the parent to act and protect their children, while they use the Internet.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I really dont think that the internet should be banned for under 14 year olds. Education for kids nowadays is more intense and the work is a lot harder. The internet is provided to all students in school for learning and even homework is set for children through an app or a website. The internet doesnt always mean social media or things like youtube where as you should be 16 to access social media anyway. I think in this generation, the internet has been made a vital part of young peoples lives and is easier way to access information for learning which i will back all the time.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: People need to be 'technology literate'; banning the Internet for young people would hinder this. The Internet is a valuable resource for learning and research. 14-year-olds are at a stage in their lives where they are learning. The Internet is the first and primary source for research and learning. Also, it would be impossible to enforce such a ban. The Internet is accessible everywhere, from computers to mobile phones to smart TVs. So even with agreement from parents, this couldn't be enforced. It is true that there is good and bad regarding the Internet, but it isn't going away. Perhaps the most negative area of Internet use is social media; perhaps this specific area could be better regulated.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I disagree that the UK should ban internet use for under 14s. The internet can be an incredibly valuable resource for studying and gaining an understanding of the world around us. In fact, the internet is such an integral part of day-to-day life now that banning under 14s from using it may damage their prospects in the long run. Given the amount of misinformation and disinformation online, schools should begin teaching students at a young age how to critically analyse information found online and be wary of hoaxes and scams. There should be open discussions around using the internet responsibly and protecting your mental health - I believe that under 14s are easily capable of engaging meaningfully with this. Finally, many careers require computer and internet literacy. Banning under 14s from opportunities that millenials and Gen z had free access to may disadvantage young people in the job market.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: There is absolutely no justification in the government wading in and overriding decisions made within individual families. A child's level of maturity and intellectual understanding does not correlate directly with age. Enacting such legislation would be totally pointless, since it would be impossible to police. Even if online IDs are imposed, parents that do not agree with their child being restricted in such a way will simply log on themselves and then vacate the keyboard for their child. Moreover, the concept is fundamentally flawed given how the Internet is an important source of information, all the more in a society where public libraries are being closed and schools often have insufficient funds to provide textbooks. Like it or not, people are also increasingly being forced to manage their lives through apps and websites that rely on an Internet connection. It is nonsensical to deprive a child from being able to use such tools as required.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I think it should be up to parents to decide, not the government. It takes away the freedom from parents to make these kind of decisions, parents should have the right to decide if their children should use internet or not.  The 14 age feels too high as well. Also, I understand that children need to have restrictions, but the government should only make restrictions/decisions for these kind of things only where there's a serious concern and has a significant affect on the children, but even then it would be best to give the right support to the parents instead so that they understand how to apply internet usage to their children.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Social media has its merits and benefits, many of which many people are enjoying today. However, the downside to this is that if care is not taken, it could take over peoples' lives especially children who are not matured enough and don't have any sense of responsibility for themselves. This creates a great room for addictive behaviour and aggression to anyone who wants to prevent them from using social media. Some become anti-social, learn and get involved in dangerous pranks and also fall for online 'predators' unknowingly. Since they are not mature enough to discern content that is bad or harmful for them, I strongly support that that under 14s should be banned from using social media

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
The internet is a vast lawless landscape that allows unchecked access to the worst of society. Teens and young children are particularly vulnerable to issues regarding their self esteem, self-identity, sexuality and body image.
A vast amount of the bullying, initimidation and harassment that young teens have to endure is online rather than face-to-face, reducing online access for the young and vulnerable in society will be a huge help. I forsee a reductioin in self harm, eating disorders and other adverse reactions to this harmful content.
If young people are not on their phones or staring at a tablet then they will have much more free time to take part in other healthier activities such as sport or outdoor games. This will help with childhood obesity and isolation too.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
The internet presents several risks for children under 14 including exposure to inappropriate content,cyberbullying and potential for addiction. It can also negatively impact social skills,mental health, and physical well-being in not managed properly.

Excessive internet use can lead to internet addiction, characterized by compulsive online behaviour and a disregard for real life responsibilities. This can result in social isolation difficulty in forming relationships,and mental health problems like anxiety and depression. 

The internet allows for anonymity, making it easier for bullies to target victims without fear of immediate consequences. Children are also vunerable to online predators who may try to groom them for sexual abuse or  exploitation.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: The idea of under 14s not having internet access (and smartphones) is very good, it would definitely be beneficial to the mental health and well being of children. However most of this is impractical. Banning smartphones for under 14s would be a start, but how do you enforce it, you definitely can not rely on parents. The same with general internet access, some access is required for school work and learning. So unless you can have limited internet access it is not viable. Even then I am sure many would get around this in some way. If it could be made to work it is ideal, but will never happen

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I think not all of the internet should be banned for under-14-year-olds because a lot of it can be useful, especially for research and educational skills at school. Certain aspects like social media should be banned because they can be harmful for children's mental health, encouraging more isolation and mental health problems like body issues. I think instead of outright banning the internet children should be encouraged to learn about it at school, utilising it during lessons when looking at research. Time on the internet should definately be limited, especially at home, and parent-locks should be in place to make sure material is appropriate.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
Although I believe that the internet can have a negative effect of younger persons, it is also a useful tool for learning. At present the responsibility falls to the parents to monitor usage, so it may be more useful to further inform parents of the pitfalls so they can decide how to monitor their children.  I think this was very my highlighted by the television program "adolescence" where the parents believed that their son was safe because the was in his bedroom. They had no idea of the damage that was being done online.
I'm not sure how we as a country could enforce such a ban.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: i don't think the Uk should ban the internet use for under 14s. the internet is really important for learning and keeping in touch with friends and families. banning it completely feels too harsh and could stop kids from gaining useful digital skills early on. Instead, it's better to teach kids how to use the internet safeky and responsibly. Many children under 14 can use the internet well and they can hvae guidance from parents at home, or teachers at school. with the right support and controls i believe kids can get the benefits of the internet without too many risks. so i believee teaching and supervising is better than just banning.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Although the internet is full of useful and informative information and many children are expected to use the internet to research homework topics, they also have access to very graphic, violent and sexual content which children under the age of 14 should not be exposed too. This content glamorises this and children become desensitised and in their naivety start to believe this to be normal and acceptable behaviour. At 14 you are still legally a child and some of this content is totally inappropriate. The overreliance on the internet is hampering the development of face to face communication skills, excessive texting and online interactions limits the opportunities to practice effective verbal communication, active listening and understanding social cues. Cyberbullying is also a big issue on the internet where unlike face to face bullying you can be subjected to a barrage of anonymous messages which cause great anguish and distress.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
1.to proctect the teenagers mental health and overall well - being, children under 14 years old are particularly vulnerable to the negative effects of internet use thereby exposing them to bully, rape, kidnapping, social media pressure and exposure to harmful content
it can also limit their health as a result of unhealthy consumption of internet

2. to encourage developing real life skills, excess screen time can affect sleep, studying, concentration physical activity and face to face interaction that can help to develop the brain, sensory motor skills, creative skills and all
banning internet use will help their communication skills and problem solving skills

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Today s children are obsessed with net and children playing in the fields have been disappeared, there is something in the net that is like the drug.  Also the net is full of dangers, violence, adult contents that are extremely harmful for the young minds. They can be victims of scammers, bad people who mislead children navigating them into wrong directions. Children have to play and socialize stay outside in the open air , move , jump, run, stay active it makes them occupied, result in healthier lifestyle, better appetite, happier childhood with the company of their friends that creates bond

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: In some areas a more socialist system would prevent price gouging and would improve efficiency of using resources. For example the transport industry (especially trains) would benefit. Currently these prices are extreme and staff have frequent strikes over working conditions and pay. Thus a government owned and operated system may be better for employees and consumers as prices will be fairer, working conditions and pay will rise, especially if the labour party is in power. However some capitalist ideas are good and are better than socialist ones such as  having a free market in alot of cases as it increasing competition and thus naturally decreases prices.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I believe that we should struggle to achieve a greater level of equality and fairness in our society; an objective which is not helped by capitalism which, by its very nature, creates divisions in our nation by leading to an unequal distribution of wealth which, in turn creates dissatisfaction amongst the large section of the populace who are not beneficiaries of the wealth it creates.
It also poses a risk to our democracy with the growth of extremely rich persons who are now tending to use their wealth to buy influence and power.   One has only to look to America at the present time to see what I mean and such a situation could very well lead to authority given to undesirable individuals.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
Comparing capitalist countries around the world to socialist ones I think there is a clear distinction in that capitalist countries are far more successful economically. Thus, it should follow that the only, or at least main, reason to switch would be socialist society would improve quality of life by removing/reducing poverty. 

However, I am entirely skeptical that this is the case, looking at examples around the world. In addition, the state has less money to spend on things like welfare, education, health etc, as a direct consequence of being less wealthy and earning less taxes. 

The knockon effect of all this should logically equate to a lower life expectancy too.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
If you do not have a person with overall responsibility then you can have conflict within companies or factories.  If different people think it should be done a different way, then who is right? Who will make that decision and stand by it?  At least if you have an Owner of the business or a Board of Directors then they have to make the decisions and be responsible for them.  They have to take the good with the bad and take the stress if problems arise.

If you are not profiting from your decisions then why would you bother?  You do not care whether it works or not.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: This is a hard topic to write about because I do not feel very knowledgeable about it, but I think that if the UK transitioned to a socialist economic system to replace capitalism then more social groups in the UK would have a more equal social standing and there would be less difference in the social status and wealth of people in the UK. This would mean that living in the UK would feel fairer, without such a wide rich /poor divide. It may also mean people place higher values on other things than money, such as family and arts.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I have little understanding of what "transitioning to the socialist system" would mean for the UK. I imagine this would be something similar to Sweden? But I'm also not sure Sweden is entirely socialist. I grew up in Soviet Union and I don't have a particularly high opinion of a socialist way of living, but Sweden does not remind me of the Soviet Union at all. 

I don't think capitalism should be completely abandoned as it has its benefits and can create a healthy competition and progress. I think it would be positive to have some sort of a hybrid scenario combining elements of different political philosophies.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I believe the statement above will divide the public in the UK. It can be seen has capitalism only benefits the rich in society through the labour of the poor and working class. This also relates to the cost of living crisis. Changing the system to socialist economic system could help the cost of living crisis but it will cause a decrease in those wanting to do better and get higher paid jobs and attending university. It will increase unemployment as well as this. Capitalism isn't very present currently in certain places of the UK but in others it is very strong. How does one change the system?

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: The UK is a national divided by beliefs in socialism and capitalism with Scotland and Wales generally being seen as more to the left than much of England which tends to have a more capitalist outlook. However, it can be argues there are pockets of England where socialism dominates (i.e. Yorkshire and North-East). A change from the existing system would be too much of a challenge and likely to provoke outrage - as can be seen by the current debate surrounding the abolition of the two-child benefit cap which would is seen by many as a regressive policy. Generally a socialist society has a higher tax burden and such a move would be deeply unpopular even if the quality and level of services provided increased. Unfortunately, there is a want everything for nothing attitude in the UK.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: socialism could reduce income inequality, promote fair wealth distribution, and ensure that essential services like healthcare and education remain focused on public good rather than profit. It could also strengthen the social safety net and protect vulnerable populations. However, critics warn that socialism may reduce innovation, weaken economic incentives, and lead to inefficiency through government overreach. Capitalism, they argue, drives growth and individual freedom. Rather than a complete shift, many propose a mixed economy--combining capitalist markets with strong social protections---to ensure both economic efficiency and social fairness in a balanced and sustainable way. The debate pertaining that matter is so complex.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I am ambivalent not because I haven't thought about this issue, but because I have thought about it a  lot andjust don't have a solution. Capitalism has many bad points, and it is contributing towards the destruction of the planet, It also just doesn't care about people, the weakest do just go to the wall and trickle-down economics is, in most cases, nonsense.
However, the only socialist societies that I am historically aware of (the USSR, China) have not really worked for the majority of people either, although perhaps Cuba had some success. I visited Cuba a long while ago and it seemed to be improving people's lives. I think maybe capitalism-socialism is a spectrum, and the UK should work towards being more socialist than it is now. I actually think the planet is the overriding issue.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: There is more chance of a fair distribution of wealth as the government would try to equalise wealth between the rich and poorer. Social needs would be more of a priority than profits and so individuals would be better looked after than the government just focusing on profits. Wages and wealth would be more equal rather than rich getting more profits and money. Having private ownership of companies/land etc would mean industry would favour the wealthiest that are the owners rather than the whole economy gaining from any profits. There is more chance of decisions about any resources being allocated to the needy and not just the wealthy or those who have invested the most.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: A socialist government would mean the output and prices of goods and services would be more controlled meaning no free market, the goal of socialism is the public to have more control and reduce big corporations setting   tariffs and rates which are unaffordable. There are several people in the UK who struggle financially and poverty is higher than ever, giving the public more control would help sort this issue due to realistic payable rates being set.  Everyone knows already the NHS is struggling throughout our country, with socialism there is unlimited access to healthcare services so citizens don't need to worry if they get ill, there should be no private healthcare within our country especially dentists in which there is a crisis at the moment.  The economy will be more controlled with chances of boom and bust being limited due to citizens being more equal in terms of wealth.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: A socialist system prioritizes redistribution of resources, ensuring that everyone has access to basic need like healthcare, housing and education . By shifting focus from corporate profit to public well being , essential services can be run in the public interest rather than for shareholder gain . Also, centralized planning can help avoid the boom and bust cycles of capitalism and allocate resources more efficiently for long term societal benefit . Shared ownership and democratic control promote solidarity and reduce class division . For these reasons, I think the uk should transition to a socialist economic system , replacing capitalism

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I find it heartbreaking that so many people are living in poverty when in reality there are enough resources for everyone if people were permitted to have what they needed (not necessarily what they wanted). Any society is only as strong as its weakest members. If we could somehow transition to a desire for more compassion rather than more money, where the most compassionate people have the highest social regard and the richest are somehow looked down upon, people would be more motivated to help each other and collaborate towards shared goals. There are many forms of socialism, and great care would need to be taken that the solution does not become even worse than the initial problem.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I do not believe that the UK should transition to a socialist economic system, for a number of reasons. Firstly, whilst the capitalist system might be flawed, especially with regard to the growing levels of inequality, I don't believe that there is any system that is better. Further, there is no country in the world were there has been a true socialist economic system, so why would we think that a country without a history of socialism would be suited to this. The current system is so entrenched that I don't believe that it could be upended without a revolution or coup.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
Whilst Capitalism has very many downsides and pitfalls it is the only economic system that is seen to be able to function in the world in which we live. Without financial motivation many systems within society would fail completely having a bigger impact on the wider economy and society in general.

Socialism is a very fine principle however in reality it can be open to abuse as an economic system. There are those who would choose not to contribute believing they would still be taken care of. The more that happens the quicker the economy would fail as not enough people would be helping.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I think that there is likely to be more innovation in a capitalist society. People will feel free to innovate, it will lead to invention and efficiency and potentially a better society. It could lead to prices being cheaper given greater competition.

In a purely socialist economy there is less incentive to do this.  There are some positives: for example healthcare for all which is a good idea but there is also the risk of ineficiencies in this system.

Socialism potentially could lead to economic problems and authoritarian societies. Examples of this are the Soviet Union which is not a great example of a succesful and free society.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: A socialist system would not work in the UK as it is too different to what we have in existence at present. Capitalism encourages hard work and rewards those that try the hardest to succeed in the world of work. A socialist system would help those less well off but could easily suppress economic growth. Socialist ideals do help level all members of society so that no one group is better off than any other. The benefits of capitalism, however, can be seen to raise the aspirations of the population in general and the growth in the economy that it brings filters down to all citizens.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: A full transition from capitalism to socialism would require a complete overhaul of economic policies and regulatory systems, which would be disruptive and politically challenging / socially unsettling. Market competition fuels creativity which surely drives industries forward. A socialist system with state controlled resources might reduce incentives for new business ventures and suffocate innovation. In a socialist model, citizens rely more on government provided services which could lead to dependency rather than self motivation, restricting individual initiative. In a nutshell, the benefits might be an easier life but a life without the need to push boundaries or feel driven by competition.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I don't believe in an economy where people who don't work get free handouts. It creates an issue where there is no incentive for people to work. 
I understand that some people who hit upon hard times temporarily, and who have pain in and contributed to the system should get help, but we are now in a position where people who do work hard are worse off than those who choose not to. Many people who are working for minimal wage are no better off than those on benefits who get help with council tax, housing, cost of living school dinners and uniforms child benefit, dentistry and opticians, the list goes on.
I hate the term social exclusion where people who won't work feel that they should not be socially excluded by not having access to the latest mobile phone or a car or holidays. If you don't work, in my opinion, you don't get those things.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: Capitalism isn't perfect, but it is the best system we have. It has allowed for previously unseen levels of scientific innovation over recent centuries, with the profit incentive motivating people to innovate in the fields of technology, medicine, and beyond. This innovation has seen incredible rises in living standards for all, with our lives becoming more convenient, and things previously considered impossible, like instant long-distance communication, flying, and having access to all the information in the world at our fingertips at all time becoming accessible to so many. Such progress would not be possible in a socialist system where there is no concept of profit to incentivise the production of ideas.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: Transitioning to a socialist economic system in the UK could create a fairer and more equitable society. By reducing the wealth gap, socialism ensures that resources are distributed more justly, mimproving quality of life for all. Basic needs such as healthcare, education, and housing would be gueranteed, removing financial barriers that exist under capitalism, Additionally, workers would gain more contraol and voice in the economy, decreasing expkloitation by large corperations. With profit no longer beeing the main driver, policies could focus more on social welfare, sustainability, and community well-being. This shift could lead to a more just and inclusive society.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: There shouldn't be a system in place where everyone gets treated the same and salaries are the same. This stops people from making the effort to better themselves and strive for more. The United Kingdom already have enough people who sit around doing nothing and expect hardworking people to foot the bill for it. Capitalism rewards those who are prepared to work hard and do what it takes to get ahead. They should be rewarded for their efforts and it wouldn't be fair if everyone received the same as them. Society as it is should stay and those less "fortunate" need to take more responsibility for their place in life.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: Socialist governments always fail by running out of other peoples money to spend.   In a socialist economy there are always people that bail out of working and live on the state because the benefits are too comfortable.  In the end socialists always tax and spend too much - strangling businesses and running economies down because they have taken the incentive to make money away from hard working people.   Look around the World to see a successful socialist country.  I cannot name one.   China is probably claiming to be socialist but it runs many capitalistic policies and look at the people control they have to implement!  - No thanks!!

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: At the moment even though we live in a supposedly democratic country, once in parliament the people in power do not have an understanding of the majority of people and how they survive. If there was a fairer system where people from all walks of life could make decisions to benefit the wider part of society then it would be far more fair than the current system where people who are in power make decisions which do not include being fair to the citizens.  I would imagine that if everyone could make the important decisions on helping people to live a decent life then the country would be a much nicer place in which to live.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
A degree of capitalism is healthy for ensuring people are driven by ambition and their desire to be entrepreneurs. These qualities are necessary to improve the world we live in. For example, climate change is a serious issue and today's youth should be financially rewarded for coming up with schemes to solve the problem.

At the same time, an unbalance of wealth is not a good thing. There needs to be a way of ensuring that the government does not need to make cuts such as winter fuel cuts the hit the poor the most. Many of the rich have more money than they need to the extent that they do not know what to do with it. Much of this money can be redirected to the poor.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
market socialism reduces inefficiencies and unpredictability and allows long term strategic investment that a capitalism system can not match.

One just has to look at the many things that were privatised in the United Kingdom during the 1980s to see that capitalism in monopolies just does not work. Take water for example, an item every human needs to survive, handed over to private companies without debt and now, companies like Thames Water are asking for bail-outs, an unintended pun if ever there was one, as it's previous owners devised a way to load the company with debt to increase short term profits.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
We have never been a capitalist society.  This has happened in other countries and it does not work. We should keep our democracy as it is, this is what works best in U>K> 
Socialism is another that does not work. It ends up with the poor being poorer and the rich getting richer. This is at the expense of opportunities being fairly distributed to the lower end of society.
The total upheaval regarding transitioning would be monumental. It would take many years to put the framework in place for this, and people would soon loose any patience waiting for this, and indeed, probably would not vote for it in the first place.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: You only have to look at the state of the US now to see how capitalism has failed. Power in the hands of a few ultra-wealthy autocrats who control the media, destroy opponents, and only work for themselves and their cronies. It was the inevitable conclusion of years of a self-serving rat race society. I say conclusion, but of course, it's not over yet and things will only get worse.By the end, we can expect 3 or 4 major corporations to own everything. Meanwhile, everyday folk will become pawns with little freedom of will and only the delusion of happiness. It's probably not possible now, but I would rather a society where people shared resources fairly and cared for one another.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: This topic is super controversial and honestly unpleasant as I don't think ending someone else's life is a power anyone should legally yield. I think it would be a slippery slope to the punishment being abused and misused. It would also lead to massive issues if someone was falsely accused or proven innocent after sentencing, as does happen with prisoners mid sentence, you can't undo the death penalty. I also think bringing back such an inhumane approach to repercussions would set us back 50 years and I would worry what other aspects of the early 1900s would return with it. I just don't think its the solution to our crime problem, harsher sentences sure, but something so final and aggressive as the death penalty isnt a good idea to me.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: In my opinion, it is a better punishment to keep a perpetrator of a serious crime locked away for 30 years than to kill them  instantly - in this way, they know that the majority of the remainder of their lives will be spent without freedom. This also highlights the possibility of rehabilitation, wherein they may change their ways after a lenghty stint in prison. I also think  that it is morally wrong for a human to take a life without a just reason, i.e. self defence. One could argue that the human who is taking the life is doing so for justice; but I feel that this still constitutes as murder.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
- It is inhumane - the death penalty feels like an archaic aggressive response to a crime. Yes the person deserves to be punished but that means that we are condoning violence. It also means that we believe that people cannot be rehabilitated. That is true in some circumstances but there are many others that can be rehabilitated and join society again. That is what we should be trying to do.

- What about if the person is innocent? There have been countless incidences where people have been sentenced to life in prison then for the case to be overturned and for them to be found innocent or problems with the evidence.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Most important for me is the risk that we could convict and sentence to death somebody who is innocent of their crime. We know that there are miscarriages in the judicial system now again, and we just cannot risk killing an innocent person.
