Furthermore, there is the moral standard that our country upholds and projects to the rest of the world. I do not believe that the death penalty is in line with British democratic values. For the person responsible for administering the lethal injection, there is a high risk of psychological trauma as they are directly ending another person's life. There is also no totally 'safe' way of ending somebody's life without the risk of causing them pain. When administered incorrectly, the lethal injection can be excruciatingly painful, tantamount to torture. Torture is never acceptable.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
I am strongly against the death penalty on ethical grounds. I believe the criminal justice system is not there to enact vengeance on behalf of society. It is my understanding that all civilised countries have banned the death penalty. The state should not murder people.

One argument in favour is that the death penalty is seen as a stronger deterrent for some crimes. My understanding is this is not true. People commit capital offenses mainly in the heat of the moment and not with the thought they will get caught. The death penalty is no greater deterrent than imprisonment.

A secondary issue is that miscarriages of justice are often made. We cannot secure convictions with enough certainty that the death penalty will be acceptable.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I don't believe in the death penalty - from looking at the American states that use it there is still a lot of crime. And the whole process is so drawn out in legal battles it would be interesting how expensive it is compared to life imprisonment. It's so final - if there has been a miscarriage of justice there is no overturning a death sentence. And where would you imprison death-row inmates? Away from the majority of the prison population? That needs new prisons being built. I hope that our justice system would never return to the death penalty. There are major problems with the penal system but bringing back the death penalty won't solve them.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I feel very strongly that the death penalty is very wrong. I don't believe people have the right to decide who loves or dies. The death penalty would likely be used for the most extreme crimes such as murder, people often say people who murder should face the same punishment but i believe this makes the people deciding this just as bad as the initial perpetrator. There are also incidences where people have been given the death penalty and evidence has come to light later that has proven them innocent  but this can obviously not be undone. I find the idea very inhumane

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: The main reason I think the dealth penalty is a good idea in really serious crime is to act as a deterrant. Another idea is to make criminals think 'is it worth it' when thinking of deliberately killing or causing someone great harm. Another eason I think is to save having to pay huge amounts of money to keep someone  in prison for the rest of their life. As I understand it, really bad 'lifers' are inclined to be disruptive & often cause problems in the prisons & 'they are never going to get out, so why not'. THe prisons in the UK are so full, it would make room & cells hopefuly be less crowded.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I think that the death penalty should we brought back depending on the type of crime an individual has committed, such as: Murder, Rape, Terrorism, etc. These things drastically harm the public and its victims so it is only fair if the perpetrator themselves is punished for what they have done to other individuals. As part of this, if an individual is also showing absolutely no remorse towards the crime they have committed then they should be punished as they will never show remorse or any kind of regret for harming any other individuals. As part of this, it deters future perpetrators from committing any of these crimes as they will see what the potential outcome will be and decide not to commit such acts.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
it seems that prison alone is not sufficient deterrent for those that choose to kill. A life sentence does not mean that and sentences are often curtailed. In countries where the death penalty is rigorously enforced, (Singapore), crime rates are significantly reduced. The cost of keeping a criminal in prison is a huge burden for the state and public funds should not be spent on keeping someone incarcerated forever.
Justice for the relatives of the victims is very important and there can be a huge sense of closure that justice has been obtained for their relative and they have no need to continue to be anxious / angry that the murderer continues to live on.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: This is a tough one and I can see both sides to the argument. There are miscarriages of justice, such as the Lucy Letby case, she might have been hanged back in the day. But there are clear cases, such as the Southport killer, who I'd be happy to see hanged, electrocuted or poisoned. People like that will never change, have no remorse and are of no use to society. So why keep them in prison for 50 years or more? I therefore think it would be appropriate it some cases. Pointless discussion though - never gonna happen in the UK as liberal bed wetters have taken over.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I think it is a different subject and I'm not sure either way. On one hand, it would save money which the taxpayer could perhaps save in taxes or could be spent on other things, rather than housing criminals in prison for years. Some victims and their families may also prefer the death penalty as a punishment, especially for serious crimes. However, there could be cases of mistaken identity and people could be murdered when they are innocent and there's no going back from this action once it's done. I do think having it in place, would deter some criminals from committing crime as they would be frightened to be executed.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Imagine if the wrong person is convicted of a crime, as has happened a few times in the UK, if that person was killed in the name of the law, then what recourse happens then? Are the persons who killed them then responsible for unlawfully killing them? You cant be 100% sure anyone has certainly committed a crime unless there is evidence showing them committing a crime. Who is the death penalty for? The victim's family? Sometimes they do welcome death to theprisoner, but other times, people don't want to see someone else die, its already traumatic for the family and long process of the death penalty would drag out their grief. Sometimes sitting in a cell everyday for years is a worse punishment than death, death is quick and in most cases painless during the execution. If its because its seen as a bigger deterrent than sitting in a cell for years then this clearly isn't working.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I disagree that the UK should reintroduce the death penalty. There is always a risk that the wrong person is convicted, and that an innocent person may be sentenced to death for no reason. In countries that have the death penalty (such as the USA), people sentenced to death may be on death row waiting for the punishment for years, which can be an expensive and costly process. Finally, the fact that death is an irreversible punishment needs to be considered - there can be no reversal ad brining back to life. If there were new evidence found etc, then nothing could be changed.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
My greatest concern would be that an innocent person was executed if the legal and jury system got it wrong. There is no going back after execution and it is then too late to rectify the error.
I would also not be convinced that this line of punishment would be enough to deter some criminals for carrying out their crimes. I am not sure that there is sufficient real evidence that this kind of punishment does actually work as a deterrent.
The cost factor may be very expensive given legal costs and the many levels of appeal routes that would presumably have to be gone through. It may work out to be more financially expensive than the cost of jail time over a number of years.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
When the UK had the death penalty before, mistakes were made and innocent people lost their lives. This cannot be allowed to happen again. There are still instances of people who have been jailed for many years, for crimes that they did not commit coming to light quite regularly. 
Also, you cannot kill somebody when it is illegal to kill another person - that is just wrong, morally and ethically, in my opinion. If somebody has been accused of murder, how can you then punish that person by murdering them, too? It just doesn't make sense. Nobody has the right to take another person's life, in any circumstance.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Criminals are devloped through envirnonments, and can be rehabilitated and turned into productive members of society, the brain is a plastic and moldable object. Even if the extent of the crime, involves not being integraged into society ever again, their labour could be used productively, and thus to benefit socieyt more, rather than drain it through the money spent incarcerating people. Meaning disposing of people should never be allowed. No government or power should have the power or support to take the life of one of its own citizens. Irrespeive of the crime or its evience. Adittionaly, by truly reahbiliting and supportig and upskilling inmates, we reduce the future chacne of crime comitted, as we teach them empathy and compassion

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
My primary issue with the death penalty is that society would not be able to correct its mistakes. I accept some crimes and criminals are so awful that it is a reasonable, emotional reaction to demand that some criminals deserve to die, but there will always be mistakes and miscarriages of justice and we would inevitably execute innocent people, and to me that makes the system worse than the criminals.
I also think the death penalty for certain serious crimes brings a risk of a populist move to more and more crimes being punishable by death, and that seems a dangerous power to give politicians, especially given the woeful standard of politicians we currently have.
If we want to really punish the worst criminals then prison sentences should be longer ( life means life ) and prison should perhaps be less comfortable - this might, however, create challenges with prisoners who have no expectation of freedom and no realistic threat/consequence to bad/violent behaviour in prison

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I can certainly understand the desire to reintroduce the death penalty, given some of the terrible, heinous crimes that you see in the news. Some people, I believe, are indeed beyond redemption. Never the less, I feel that if the UK were to reintroduce the death penalty, that that would ultimately make all of us as citizens, complicit in the same types of crimes that we otherwise despise. As a state we would be asking someone to perfom an execution on our behalves; surely that would make us the worst kind of hypocrites. Our society often criticises others for whom the death penalty remains; with this we would be no better than them.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Bringing back the death penalty in the uk is a tough decision and the major worry is that tha innocent people might be executed mistake and there type of mistake can not be undone and evidence show that  having the  death penalty doesnot make any effect on crime rate and and its also against the human right becaue every life is important and and impact on the role of the state  and we can use the resources of death penalty for stopping crime before it happens and helping people who have done wrong in the past to change there life and be a good citizen these are  the reason for disagreeing with bringing back the death penalty

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I am pretty sure that there are many people who would say we should bring back the death penalty as crimes get more serious and more common.  If there was a way of proving 100% that someone was guilty, then maybe I would consider the death penalty, dependant on the crime committed, but I believe that it should be a decision that the whole country need to be behind, not just the 50.2% Brexit margin either, maybe 75% of the population. I always think of the what if scenario.  What if they were innocent.  What if the evidence was flawed.  There are still too many variables for me to get behind the death penalty.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: There have been many miscarriages of justice in the past.  A couple of recent ones include someone who was incarcerated for 20 years following a guilty verdict for rape and another one where someone had been jailed for life for murder. Both were jailed without positive DNA because that particular sort of testing was not available when the crime was committed. Technology evolves and can always be refined. The police service too, has been guilty in the past of trying to find someone to fit the crime and not necessarily using great detective skills.  I have been a detective and am not impressed with the record and forensic storage.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I believe that no civil authority has the right to end the life of someone regardless of the crime that they have committed. There are plenty of other options. The exception to this would be in case of war when the military have the option to kill to defend the nation. There is plenty of historical evidence that innocent people have been executed through faulty evidence. Even today there are people in prison, and have been in priso,n who are not guilty of the crime of which they have been sentenced. If a person has been executed, there is no comeback from this. Execution is a barbarous treatment of a human being whether it be by hanging, poisoning, execution by firing squad, the electric chair or any  other method.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: There are certain cases where this would be appropriate, for example the Hindleys, and other mass murderers who no longer deserve to be alive. These are sick individuals and the crimes are the worst crimes imaginable and such people do not deserve the luxury of prison when the families of the poor children have a daily living hell to endure. They are costing the tax payers money and this would be one solution. This would only be appropriate for a few of the worst criminals, however and could not just be agreed haphazardly. There would need to be strictest governance and oversight to ensure that everything was appropriate otherwise there is the risk of innocent people coming to harm.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
If a person has such wicked intent, they do not deserve any place or conditions in the British legal system. Putting someone in a cell and giving them ample food, entertainment and a place to rest or sleep is not good enough. Even a full life sentence will never be an ample punishment. They are allowed to continue existing, when the victim may have never had that chance. Then we think of the victims loved ones. They now have a life of constant suffering without ever being able to have made a difference. The only possible danger in my mind would be the case of possible miscarriage of justice.
But in todays modern world with the tools now at our disposal (like DNA or satellite tracking) this should become a much rarer event.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
This is a difficult subject as I have no personal experience of witnessing someone who longs for death. I think that if I had been I would be able to make a better argument. 

I know that there is a concern from the disabled community that some people might be pressured into this process to prevent them from becoming a burden to their families or society. And there are concerns that people suffering from mental health problems may ask for the procedure, whereas they may have been able to make a recovery. 
It is a hard ask for the medical professionals who will be involved in the process.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I rated my agreement at 53 because I slightly lean toward agreeing with the statement, though not with full confidence. The topic seems to have both strengths and limitations, which makes it difficult to form a firm stance. Some of my experiences support the idea, while others contradict it, leading to a more nuanced view. I'm open to being persuaded further, depending on the evidence or examples presented. Overall, I feel that the statement holds some truth, but it doesn’t fully align with my experiences or beliefs, which is why I remain somewhat ambivalent and open to changing my view with new insights.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I am in the middle when it comes to euthanasia as I feel like it isnt my place to say whether or not it should be legalised. There are people in the country that feel like they have lost all hope when it comes to life mostly when it comes to a medical condition. Having that option available to them and not have it be so taboo - it could be a good thing and open up conversation with people. Someone really wanting this option can just travel to another country anyways. On the other hand, this could provide some downfalls when people abuse the systems and controls put in place.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I am of no view on this .I have heard debates and if people want to vote on euthanasia so be it . I personally wouldnt want to end my life but if people want to so be it, It must be awful to live an unbearable life in pain .I do worry that people may be coersed as they may be a burden .I wouldnt vote for it I would hope everyone who wants it would turn up to vote . I think it should be a doctors choice not the governments and more than one doctor should make he choice

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
There is considerable support in some circles for euthanasia to be legalised, in order that people can decide what kind of death they wish to have.  I have sympathy for this point of view, although equally some people with religious convictions particularly do not agree with this.  

I am split on this topic, on the one hand, having witness my mother's lingering weeks, there is an argument for a clean and painless exit, on the other hand - despite not being particularly religious, I have a strong feeling that working against the laws of nature is a bad thing - and I have concerns about individuals manipulating the system for the wrong reasons.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I do agree in part that it should be legalised as it offers the right to a choice that is granted to everyone else if they wish to commit suicide in other ways, but in a way that is safer and more comfortable. It would allow people who are terminally ill or otherwise chronically suffering to end their pain with dignity and comfortably. However, I can also recognise that legalising this would raise several ethical issues and risk further complications. For example, it risks being a 'slippery slope' where people may feel coerced into euthanasia by others, or could even be used as a method to kill someone against their will with an 'excuse'.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: This is a tough issue with valid arguments on both sides, so if it were to be made legal, then there would have to be rock-solid safeguards in place to ensure that it is not used inappropriately. Euthanasia can be seen to be a good way for people to end suffering if they have a terminal illness and are in a lot of pain. However, if it was legalised, then there is the potential for people to be coerced into taking it without their consent. I heard a story of a Canadian woman, ex-service person who was disabled and applying for benefits and it was suggested to them that they might consider euthanasia as a solution. If this is true, then I find it to be wholey inappropriate and we would need to ensure that such a scenario never happened in this country.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: The decision to end your own life should be a personal one, when this is possible. Obviously, for a lot of people, this is a non-decision - you live until you are unable to continue your life. However, some people find life too difficult to carry on. I have known several people who suffered from mental and physical illnesses and did not necessarily want to live. I would never blame them for making their own decision to end their life. However, state involvement leads to the possibility that people will feel pressured to make decisions that go against their personal preferences. I have no idea how to prevent this, but I have known people who felt pressure to continue living, despite major illnesses and overwhelming pain. I am sure that if euthanasia is legalized, there will be pressure on people to die to suit other people's agendas, and not their own.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I know that if I was facing a painful death in a few months I would like to end my life peacefully at home with loved ones beside me and so I would wish it to be legal. it is easy to think of situations where people may be persuaded to do this, whether for good or bad reasons, by those close to them and such a decision should be only in the hands of person. of course only people who are able to assess the options, so those with cognitive decline for example would not be able to make such a choice.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I do believe that the UK should legalise euthanasia. Although this is a very complex issue which has a lot of opinions on both sides, I believe that people who are suffering with no end in sight should be able to take control of their situation and choose to die. I saw another angle of this recently, when my grandma was suddenly very ill in hospital and was not going to recover. As her family, we had no choice but to watch her deteriorate over a month until she passed away. Family members should be able to end a loved one's suffering when it is certain they are going to pass away anyway. I also understand the other side of the argument, as we don't want people who are ill and struggling financially to feel like ending their life is the only way out.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: people should be allowed to choose if they want to end their life in a pain free way. I understand that there are ways that this can be abused and laws will need to be in place to prevent any of these issues but overall if someone is in a lot of pain and they have no chance of getting better then who is anyone else to decide that this person should continue living through the pain just because they are unable to physically do anything or wish to go silently and painlessly. Allowing people to choose should definitely be an option.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Everyone would agree that an animal in a state of constant pain or distress that cannot be cured should be put to sleep humanely. How can we not extend the same kindness to a fellow human being? If an adult choses to be euthanised at a point in time of their choosing because of constant severe pain or a terminal or degenerative illness, why should their wishes be ignored? With robust safeguards in place to ensure that the person so choosing isn't being pressured into ending their life, we would progress towards a more humane society. In the case of children with severe degenerative and terminal illnesses it should be decided by a panel of doctors whether the patient is a suitable for euthanasia only after the parents or guardians have made specific enquiries. The quality of life of the patient should be main factor in determining suitability.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I am in a strong support that the uk should allow euthanasia as it is a compassionate choice for people with terminal illness or incurable health conditions. A long term suffering of such condition comes with unmanageable pain which my comes with no recovery expectation and if they chose to end their life peacefully, it should be accepted with great respect. At the same time, this decisions can be looked into and be determined if this is what best for each individual but above all, I believe it  is about respecting personal decision. No one enjoys suffering and there is a point where everyone believes it is enough

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
- People in the UK - some of them are suffering from horrible , life-limiting illnesses that cause them a considerable amount of pain every single day - some of them have absolutely no hope of any recovery. We enthuanise animals as it is the kinder thing to do. I don't understand why anyone has to be kept alive if they are in so much pain. Obviously there needs to be strict rules so people can't abuse the system. But in clear cut cases, I think If people can prove how much they want and deserve to be able to die, they should be able to. 
-People who want to commit suicide often have to do so in horrible ways, which can traumatise people who find them - for example family members, or members of the emergency services - or even say a train driver who unfortunately runs someone over - this can have a lasting effect that is extremely harmful on the person who witnessed it. if the person really wants to kill themself, they will find a way - so may as well give them a safe secure environment to do it in - like the suicide pods in foreign countries . I think that's much better than finding someone.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: People should be able to have dignity at the end of their lives. For some people, being able to choose to die is more preferable than staying alive and they should be allowed to make this decision. At the moment, only rich people can as they can afford to travel abroad where euthanasia is legal. Particularly where people have had life changing experiences such as an accident or similar, they should be able to choose if they do not want to continue living. Living with depression over something like this is not living. We should be able to trust our medial professionals to work with people and assess when they are in correct mind to make such a decision if necessary. I also think just trust people, sometimes people know what is best for them.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I believe that the UK should legalise euthanasia as it can reduce the suffering of the old or terminally ill, who have little prospect of their situation improving and often very little quality of life.  As well as giving them control over when exactly they will die, it also allows them to plan for this moment and have some dignity in their last days.  Philosophically, I feel that we should have autonomy over our bodies and the right to ask for our lives to be ended in such circumstances, especially as no one asked to be born.  A final argument is that euthanasia is currently only available to those wealthy enough to travel somewhere like Dignitas in Switzerland.  This seems unfair as your right to die in dignity and without excessive pain shouldn't be dependent on your material circumstances.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
I am strongly of the opinion that the UK should legalise euthanasia because this will offer terminally ill patients, patients in palliative care who experience unending pain and distress, the great option of a way out when their health has worsened beyond recovery. I see it as a way to achieve peace from this painful world.

Also, all individuals should have the right to make decisions they feel are best for them. So individuals should have the right to make the choice of choosing death to respect their dignity. Not allowing for this to me is an infringement of their rights which should not be so.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I feel extremely strongly about this. If you want to end your life you should 100% be able to choose to. I know this is primarily aimed at people who have terminal illness and for those who are suffering. If I could sign something right now and agree to be euthanised if I had a terminal illness or something where I wasnt able to communicate, I would do it. People suffer everyday with conditions such as depression etc, they should be allowed to kill themselves. We need more control over our lives. It is surely better for people to choose whether they suffer or not.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I believe euthanasia should be legalised because it gives people the choice to end their suffering in a peaceful and dignified way when they are facing serious pain or a terminal illness. Sometimes, no matter what doctors do, the pain and distress can be too much and people should have the option to decide when enough is enough. Legalising euthanasia also respects personal freedom and lets individuals have control over their own bodies and their own lives, including how and when they want to die. While its a difficult and sensitive issue, allowing euthanasia can provide comfort and relief to those who are suffering rather than forcing them to continie living in unbearable conditions.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Legalizing euthanasia can be dangerous because some people might feel forced to choose death even if they don't want to. They might think they are a burden to their family due to financial or other reasons. This raises serious ethical concerns about consent and whether their decision is truly voluntary and driven by pressure. Also, with the advancement in the field of palliative care, modern medicine now has ways to help people feel less pain and suffer at the end of their lives. Instead of allowing people to end their lives, we should focus on making this better so patients can live comfortably with dignity.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I personally don't believe its our right to end a life under any circumstances. I have watched a loved one die under the old Liverpool Pathway and it was barbaric and amounts to euthanasia under the guise of something else. I obviously wouldn't want anyone to suffer from illness etc but that's where palliative care comes in to make the end more comfortable, ending their life with Euthanasia is a step too far and leaves the door wide open to crimes taking place of taking a life for inheritance or some other such example. We are playing god in this instance.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I don't agree that the UK should legalise euthanasia. I believe that life is very important, and allowing euthanasia could make people think that some lives are less valuable than others. There's also a risk that some people, like the elderly or disabled, might feel pressure to choose euthanasia even if they don't really want to. They may feel like a burden to their family or society. Also, many people have strong moral or religious beliefs thay say it's wrong to end a life, own life, on purpose. Instead of legalising euthanasia, we should help and support people who are suffering and make sure they don't feel alone or helpless. Every life deserves care and respect, no matter what they're going through.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I have a great deal of sympathy for those who want to see euthanasia legalised in the UK. Nobody want to see someone's last days and months to be painful ones.  And it seems only fair that those suffering be given the freedom to choose.  But this choice is a false one whilst we do not have high-quality end-of-life care for everyone. The choice is also a moot one if patients feel under pressure to end their life prematurely to avoid the financial burden of caring for them. Until palliative care is massively improved, I do not think euthanasia should be legalised.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: For certain conditions such as locked in syndrome or advanced motor neurone disease or where people are in severe pain that is not abated by drugs,  I can understand the attraction for such a policy but i would be worried that ill frail people would feel pressured into ending their own lives. People who are extremely vulnerable and perhaps unable to make the best decision. I don't find the current safegaurds very reassuring. In addition at a practical level it will take a great deal of resources at a time when the NHS is very stretched for cash. Another point is that it opens the door to unscrupulous family members who may wish to obtain an inheritance

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I think under the right set of circumstances people should have the ability to end their own life, though this should be governed and closely monitored by health professionals. I think the ethics of it are very very challenging as someone choosing to end their own life has wider implications, the family, friends, and health professionals involved all need to be factored into the conversation as they will feel the emotional pressure of such a big decision. If someone is terminally ill and under increasing pain then I can understand, and I'm sure the other parties I've listed would do too, but for things like mental health challenges I'd be slightly more cautious. It's a tough one, for sure!

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: i think that that is a personal choice and that if someone really does want it then i shouldnt be able to stop them. i dont know what someone who is considering that could have been through and maybe its all to much for them and if thats the case then isnt it more humane to let them choose for themselves. if someone really wants to die isnt it better for everyone that its done in a humane and safe way instead. alot of people are suprised when someone they know ends theyre life but if they were to be euthanised it would give them time to explain why and reassure people

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
-People should be able to chose how to end their own lives for valid reason of course, instead to have to suffer months of pain, uncertainty and fear. IF there is no hope where is the point of carry on living. Of course this is a personal choice and we must be sure that the patient knows exactly what they want and are not influenced by someone near them.
- Why die slowly and lose our self esteem when we could go earlier, voluntarily, without destroying the ones near us? 
As long as it is our decision what right has a government to decide when we go? After all suicide it is not a crime so no one should have the right to stop us choosing

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Immigration levels are a very difficult and emotive topic.  On the one hand I can see arguments to limit especially with the resulting burden on both the benefit and health systems on the country.   However, immigrants also provide vital contributions to the workforce and help create a more diverse society allowing different views.  Further with a decreasing birth rate and ageing population do we have enough people in the country to fill jobs that we need people to do in the country.  Overall perhaps there should be a targeted approach to limit immigration to those individuals who can add to the economic envelopment of the country.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: This is a tough subject purely because I generally believe that immigrants should be more than welcome to travel into ANY country. I think the Western world are so privileged with the way we look at immigration - a lot of my friends very casually share their dreams of moving to Australia or Canada, completely forgetting THAT IS immigration. But when someone from another country, maybe a poorer country with a different skin colour to us, wants to move their life, often to make their lives better, the Western world see a real problem with that. Suddenly it's 'our' country. Suddenly it's 'our' culture. We stole it all, and not to mention our ancestors literally stealing other countries' land and forcing them to be part of a 'commonwealth'. The one down side to immigration is ensuring that people are coming to the country with pure intentions - to live peaceful, to provide, to enjoy the country. Not to ruin it.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The statement is very broad.  The country needs some levels of immigration to help with services such as NHS which relies heavily on overseas staff.  However illegal immigration does need to be reduced.  The question is how to do this.  Recent governments of both parties have tried to do this and have failed.  It needs a Europe wide approach and taskforce to stop this at source - it it too late by the time they get to either side of teh English Channel.  Illegal immigration is costly in pure financial terms and in terms of other resources needed to help the immigrants on their arrival

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Immigration is a tricky issue.  The UK certainly needs to prevent illegal immigration, particularly where people are being brought into the country for the wrong reasons - eg modern slavery.   The UK does however need to attract people with skills to fill the jobs that we can't fill ourselves.  Unplanned immigration causes issues with the provision of services such as hospitals and schools and has to have a negative impact on housing provision.  The current levels of immigration are causing increasing concern amongst some UK residents about the negative impact uncontrolled immigration can have.  I wonder what the impact of that will be and fear it will lead to increasing levels of resentment and unrest.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The majority of people in this country lead a comfortable life. This is not the case for others around the globe. I believe it is our responsibility to take on a certain load as fellow human beings therefore the UK needs to allow a certain level of immigrants to enter the country. I believe at present we are accepting a reasonable share, I am unsure the country could take many more without impacting current residents detrimentally. I therefor believe the current level is about correct. I do not think we should reduce the level we allow in as we need to take some responsibility.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The issue in this country is that the majority of the time, the media or social media outputs images of places like immigrant camps and crimes committed by immigrants that sways the populations opinion to 'all immigrants are criminals and don't contribute to this country'. Unfortunately though, this country has 1000s of citizens who are also non contributors. Our services such as the NHS are at such a severe level that it often feels like it'll implode. My issue is not with immigrants, it is more along the lines of the population is increasing at an alarming rate and our government seem to do little to help in supporting this incredible rise. We also have a vast amount of people who struggle daily, who try to work, pay their taxes etc, just to live in complete poverty. It is not fair.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: whilst legal immigration can bring lots of benefits to the country, in respect of filling skilled job vacancies and making the country more diverse. Surely given the number of people unemployed we should be ensuring that children are well educated and unemployed people trained to take on vacancies. We are a small country and it feels increasingly difficult to access health care and for people who were born here to get housing. Lots of the traditions and customs of our country have been diluted - such as companies avoiding saying Easter or Christmas etc. As for illegal immigration we have to get tough, far too many single men who have different understanding and beliefs in respect of women are allowed to stay. The cost to the country is too much and any person who arrives illegally should never be given the full benefits of a person born here or a legal immigrant

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Whilst I fully support people who are fleeing war and awful living conditions who come to the UK with the intention of building a better life for themselves, I don't agree with people who are simply wanting to come to the UK because they know that there is a welfare system to support them. The NHS is struggling massively and has been for a long time and the people who are suffering because of that are the people who were either born here, or who have been here for many years. It is at risk of complete collapse as it is.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The population increase in England over the last three decades is destroying our land as towns and connecting infrastructure have been forced to expand, housing cannot keep pace with demand, population density increases and we lose our once cherished green spaces and arable land. Immigration creates long-term financial obligations on the state in housing, infrastructure, welfare, healthcare and pensions that are simply unpayable under the current system for people who paid nothing in before arrival. The mass influx of people from incompatible cultures who do not integrate has ghettoised our cities, destroyed any sense of shared social and civic values and is now undermining our political and social framework to the point that sectarianism is now growing.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Hard working English people are paying high taxes, and a proportion of this is going to house, feed, clothe immigrants. They are being provided with mobile telephones and get free prescriptions - things that i (as a hard working English person) do not get for free. We do not have enough housing in this country for our own residents, and we should be focussing on them before we look after the immigrants. Immigrants are coming over to the UK as we have the wonderful NHS and the NHS will not turn anyone away - they also get benefits and housing when they come over. Why would they not come to the UK, we need to make it less appealing for immigrants.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: There is insufficient council/social housing or affordable private housing to rent already without the home office or local councils taking homes for immigrants, especially while being processed. Also the vast cost of hotels that are still being used. if there are people fleeing conflict that maybe that is acceptable, but people coming for economic reasons is wrong. Some of these people do not come with job skills that are required. And in some cases there people are criminals fleeing from their own countries. A lot of people from non British backgrounds do not have adequate values as we have seen with the child grooming scandals.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: I completely agree with this statement. While I do agree that some forms of migration are not only acceptable but necessary (e.g., skilled workers to fill domestic skill gaps), most migration is completely inappropriate. The first issue which arises is that of public safety, namely that immigrants who come to the UK today tend not to assimilate into British society, and thus perpetrate which, in their culture, is regarded as acceptable (notably, the ongoing grooming gangs scandal). Secondly, there is an economic argument against migration, in that the government seems to over-spending on providing welfare to illegal migrants, such as rooms in luxury hotels, which ended up costing around a total of £18 million per day in 2023. This money could have been used to improve domestic public services, such as the NHS, rather than funding accommodation for illegal migrants.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The country is in obvious decline. A controlled demolition of the British way of life. One of the reasons for pointless wars and regime change operations seems to be to generate migration. This migration is profited on and the leads to division and chaos. People no longer trust their neighbours and our cities become no go zones. Hard working people are expected to pay for declining services and home ownership becomes unaffordable. The new arrivals who work reduce wages and put pressure on services and housing. This is an experiment that has failed. People are labelled as bad people for telling the truth

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text:
Each case of immigration should be treated on its own merit. There are people who are fleeing prosecution in their own countries, running away from war torn areas or under regimes that stifle them to do things in a certain way, I feel these people have the right to seek sanctuary in a country where they feel safe. I do understand we cannot take everyone but we can do our part.

People also need to be treated with humanity. When Ukraine invaded Russia we all rushed to help and support them and England was not their first safe country but other immigrants are judged because of the colour of their skin first before we know their story and what has brought them to another part of the world.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The UK's replacement rate is not sufficient to maintain the current population which means that as time goes by the native population will skew older with a worse worker to pensioner ratio. Therefore we need to import people to ensure that there are workers to carry out necessary tasks such as care which do not currently pay salaries that support a good standard of living for UK residents but which could be attractive to younger short term immigrants who are in a better position to share accomodation, save money and return to their country of origin with an accumulated nest egg. We also need to attract people with skills that are needed and in short supply here to remain economically competitive

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: There are so may positives that come about as a result of immigration. Think about food, cultural dress and expression, beautiful 'mixed' relationships and children. These are certainly positives. Yes I agree that people immigrate because they are seeking better opportunities for themselves and their families but that is not a negative thing. In fact, if these people are helped sufficiently and properly trained, they can work and become an asset to the country in so many ways including health. There are many non immigrants here who are quite capable of working but who refuse to work. Choose people to help who will certainly make a contribution.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Reducing immigration significantly could have serious negative effects on the UK economy, as many industries depend on migrant workers to function effectively. Sectors like healthcare, hospitality and construction often face labour shortages that are filled by immigrants, who take on roles that are difficult to staff with domestic workers alone. Without this workforce, services could slow down or become more expensive, affecting everyone. Beyond economics, immigration also brings cultural richness and fresh perspectives that help communities grow and evolve. From food and music to business and education, immigrants contribute to a more vibrant, innovative and globally connected society. Embracing diversity reflects the UK's values as an open and inclusive nation.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text:
I don't think that this is the right approach in a global community, immigration is needed to fuel industries such as medicine, manufacturing, agriculture & hospitality.
Immigration should be targeted to fulfil jobs in those industries rather than just a blanket reduction. 
It is also importantant that student schemes like Erasmus can continue bringing foreign students into the country so as they can train and study in those fields to fulfil those roles in the future. Cutting immigration would also reduce student numbers thereby putting jobs and industries at risk. In short, immigration should continue but with a purpose in mind.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: It is racist and hateful to seek to prevent people coming to this country when they are escaping war or oppression, or simply want to work and achieve a better life.    Brexit was based on a similar mentality and that has been a disaster.  Many industries here, such as hospitality and health care, will struggle to cope with the lack of workers from overseas.  I have never seen any evidence of immigrants being given luxury housing, cars and money which some people claim.   The notion of "Britain First" is distasteful to me as it is selfish and lacking in any kind of empathy for our fellow man.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text:
Immigration helps people from different countries to come into one country. It helps coexistence.  It helps people to live with different people and understand each other's perspectives.  It makes a person evolve. When people of different cultures live together a lot can be learned. A lot of knowledge canbe gained. Different types of food can be tasted.
There are many families who go abroad for work and hence their families may want to live with each other. If immigration is stopped it can create less jobs for people. It can cause families to stay isolated and not with each other.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The uk should not reduce immigration levels, instead we should help immigrants the UK become productive members of society. They can contribute to the UK economy by working and paying taxes, which means we can invest the money elsewhere like into the NHS and defense. I think a lot of [people have misinformed opinions on immigration and just assume people are coming to claim benefits and take up public housing, but people are coming to make a better life for themselves and their families. we should protect them, especially those who are fleeing because their countries are dangerous and those people should be given resources to help get off their feet so they are able to work.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: I do not think it is right to turn people away, especially based on claims by right-wing newspaper articles. The idea that immigrants come to the UK to cheat the system is inaccurate, many immigrants work immeasurably hard for little pay, showcasing that they came here to escape an even worse quality of life in their home country. I have not seen any data supporting the idea that immigration is a crisis or that it requires any action by the UK government. Many immigrants have a high level of education and sacrifice that to work lower entry jobs when they arrive here.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: Provided they apply to come into the country through the proper channels and provided that they coming to work, I do no think that it is a major problem.  However, if they gain illegal entry into the country then, they should be deported immediately by to their country of origin.  Equally, if they come to work they should be tagged for the first six months and also have to register weekly or monthly during the duration of their stay.  Anyone seeking asylum should have to apply as they do now, but, whilst their application is being considered they should also have to work.  This could be in the private sector or in the form of national service, but not military.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text:
Immigration is a very emotive subject. However, I believe that on the whole it is more good than bad. As someone who has family members in the NHS, I know from them how important immigration is to allow the smooth functioning of our healthcare system.
I also believe that we have a responsibility as a developed country with relative political and social stability to provide a safe haven for genuine asylum seekers. We can often be a preferable destination over other "safe" countries for some asylum seekers for a variety of reasons. It could be that they have a reasonable grasp of English as opposed to French or German so it is easier for them to integrate in our society.
However, I also understand that unregulated immigration can lead to the rise of internal right-wing groups which we are seeing on some scale in the USA. It is a difficult situation.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: While I am far from anti-immigration, I do accept that current levels are not sustainable in a failing country. Public services are not up to scratch in many areas and the NHS is crumbling. Education standards are under pressure and social care is at breaking point. This is not the fault of immigrants at all — however, it needs to be fixed before the population rises further. I welcome refugees and people who can and will contribute to the country. But the way it is handled now needs revising. Too many new arrivals are unable to work due to red tape and poor induction systems. Let's sort that and then welcome many more.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.
rows.text: The amount of work undertaken by immigrants in this country is huge.  I have not been to a hospital when I have not been treated by or heavily staffed by people overseas.  In general they pay taxes and give way more then they take.  Not only the NHS but the care sector in general could not function without them.  They are often used as scapegoats by parties to detract from issues such as billionaire not paying taxes or the climate.  They are an an easy target with no comeback available to them unlike the billionaires who target them.   They also make for amore varied and interesting society.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: The hunger problem facing third word countries is a significant issue which has improved in recent years, but still has a long way to go. I believe the government needs to do more regarding this, but personally I believe the UK needs to first focus on it's own nationals who are living in poverty and having to use food banks to survive. As a child of poverty and hunger, it's clear that there is not enough help and resources out there, including limited jobs in the market and lack of benefits for people who actually need them. Perhaps a nationwide survey is required.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: At the moment, UK is battling many areas that needs to be tackled first before foreign aid for global hunger should be taken up. Since COVID and BREXIT, UK has been struggling economically which has affected its infrastructure as well as employment opportunities within UK. This has resulted in many individuals affected. If these priorities are not tackled first, there is a good chance, UK will struggle to achieve aid in the first place to be provided for global hunger. Even though, UK needs to reestablish its global position and providing aid is one such way, now is not the right time for it.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: How can the UK guarantee that the money given to other countries is going to aid in the production of food?. Many countries have corrupt governments who do not care about their own people. I do not agree with giving food aid as it stop-gaps a current problem but does not stop the root cause that often in foreign countries the land cannot sustain large numbers of inhabitants. I do not believe in people creating new life knowing their land cannot sustain themselves never mind another mouth to feed. I agree to helping other countries to engineer the land to enable water to be stored, to improve land quality so it can sustain life and to help countries to help themselves.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: currently the British people give more then enough to foreign aid. we have given foreign aid for decades and decades. The amount the uk has given to combat hunger could of actually abolished world over 5 times over. out of all of the money that we have given year after year for foreign aid to combat world hunger only a small fraction of this actually goes to feeding people. Foreign aid is nothing more then a simple tactic implied by the government to extract more money out of its people. foreign aid is nothing more then a cleverly constructed scam.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text:
whilst I think it is important to help other countries with problems such as hunger, we have to realise that there are many people going hungry in our own country.  We have many homeless people and many who whilst not homeless, do use food banks and often the parents have to go hungry in order to fee the children.  
The UK economy is not in a good position to be constantly helping other countries, and it is a problem that will go on and on. It is not just a case of sending a few million pounds and being done with it.  They will want more and more as the years go by.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I think this would be a bad idea. We already give one of the highest aid budgets of any country. I this us giving any aid budget at all is contentious. If we are to do this then the people should get a say on where this aid goes. At the moment it seems that it is used for largely political reasons to gain favour. Increasing it would be pouring good money down the drain. I don't believe any of this money ever reaches anyone truly in need of aid. We are also in a precarious financial situation in the UK and we need all the mney we have to help ourselves.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Time and time again, foreign aid has been proven to be consistently redirected and stolen. It seems like, once they get the money overseas, it's much easier to steal it, which is probably why they started doing this in the first place. I understand this probably sounds cynical, but you can only hear the same story so many times. People in the UK are going hungry, sleeping rough, and are in desperate need of help. All of the money that's being used in foreign aid should be used to fund programs to help those who are struggling here. It should be put towards researching how to help people who claim not to want it, in our own country. If we want to help others, we should be at maximum efficiency as a country first.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Britain's public services and infrastructure are terrible and the more we spend on foreign aid the less can be spent on alleviating these problems. We need to prioritise our own wellbeing. Never ending aid to Africa has been a disaster. It has created a never ending dependency and it would be better to leave them to help them selves to end this. The fact that we have spent decades sending huge sums of money abroad to the detriment of ourselves is suicidal altruism. Nobody voted for it. The whole industry is a top down imposition, decided by those at the top.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text:
- In recent years there has been so much corruption discovered around charities and organisations set up to help those in need that personally i have a really hard time trusting some of them to do the job they were supposedly set up to do.
I also feel there is a bigger issue at hand here one of control in some cases control over the foreign lands that were once colonised by countries like England and especially France for example Burkina Faso and their gold mines and precious metals etc its in the colonisers best interest to keep these countries poor so that they are reliant on help from these governments etc.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: If we think about food in general, it is our common ground for all. Hunger is a pressing issue that demands immediate action. While some suffer from obesity, many children are tragically dying due to malnutrition, highlighting the severe disparities in food access and distribution. Addressing global hunger is crucial not only for humanitarian reasons but also for improving overall well-being, fostering economic stability, and promoting better health outcomes for everyone. Currently government increasing on defence lately, where important things are being cut off, and there is a chance that it has a negative impact on public services which are crucial and vital for public.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: This is a bit like vaccines and medical treatment for children in developing countries. Western countries massively reduced child mortality in these countries from the 1960s onwards, however the outcome is a massive increase in the number of human beings living on the planet and an increase in immigration (especially the illegal kind) to the west. Before, when a woman had a dozen children, only 2 or 3 might survive to adulthood (I have checked records of my relatives in the 17th, 18th and 19th century, epidemics would wipe out almost all the children in an area). Rather than feeding people, the priority would be to encourage them not to have so many children, or not to have children at all. You often get the impression, when talking to people from developing countries (including supposedly middle class immigrants from India) and even people from Eastern Europe (Poland, Romania), that for them, a woman's worth (and respectability) is positively correlated to the number of children she has had. Quantity, not quality.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I do think that the UK should contribute to foreign aid and it is very important to combat world hunger. We live in an unequal world whereby those in less developed countries do not have the same access to the resources we do and that is not fair. Just because you are born in a particular country does not mean you shouldn't have the same opportunities as those born in other countries. At the same time, I believe that more money needs to be put into tackling poverty and hunger in our country. So if you were to increase the foreign aid budget, you should also increase the domestic aid budget to tackle hunger here,

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: In an ideal world, the eradication of global hunger would appear near the top of every nations list of priorities.  But we do not live in an ideal world.  The demands on purse strings are many and varied, both national and international, and scrutinised from every angle.  Foreign aid is a noble cause, but the global north may now consider the route that this takes to reach the world's hungry - largely based in the global south - is inefficient and ineffective.  Until such concerns have been addressed, there is a need to achieve maximum return from budgets and combatting global hunger may suffer.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I think increasing foreign aid is very important, i do believe that we as a country should be doing are part in helping combat global hunger. I do believe that there should be a split between both helping foreign aid, and helping ourselves. We have a bad problem here in the UK with people not being able to afford food, and has seen a huge increase in people using food banks etc. Overall, i think its a good idea to increase the budget for foreign aid, as long as it doesnt effect us too much as a country that our own people are starving.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Whilst I completely agree that global hunger should not be an issue, no one should need to go hungry wherever they are in the world, I feel I do not know enough on the subject to speculate where the UK money should be spent. I would like that as a 'richer' country that we could help as much as possible, but am aware there are always lots of differing things pulling on the pursestrings. I do tend to feel in areas like this that its really a joint topic that needs to resolve an on-going issue by finding solutions to solve these issues once and for all so they don't arise in the future rather than just it always being knee-jerk aid payments

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: First world countries, for example the UK, should offer assistance through food, infrastructure and money to other countries that have trouble with famine and other ecological problems. With the threat of global warming disrupting the ability of countries to feed and care for its citizens. Areas of the world that have more extreme weather conditions need assistance from everyone that can help. We are in danger of countries becoming inhabitable. The UK has been a strong country dealing with this in the past and we need to continue it. Global warming will affect everyone eventually, so if we can devise and implement workable strategies to combat global hunger and allow countries to work and prosper beyond it then it will have a long term benefit for al the planet.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Global hunger not only affects the UK but other ally and non allied countries. People die every second of hunger and specially in underdeveloped countries. I believe this is a major humanitarian issue that the UK should put a lot of resources into. Even if it doesn’t affect the UK in a major level it is good to practice empathy and give to others just in case one day we are in the same situation as those today. Overall I think we should increase the funding given to those causes as it is currently not enough to combat world hunger.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I strongly agree that the UK should significantly increase it's foreign aid budget to combat global hunger. As one of the world's wealthy nations there has to be a responsibility there given. Beyond a humanitarian goal there is an onus to ensure global food security which contributes international stability, which reduces the drivers of conflict between third world nations  Stuff like forced migration or political unrest can be lessened by foreign aid even if it isn't immediate relief, it supports sustainable development by strengthening education and healthcare services in the respective nation. The UK's standing as a global leader is also upheld.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I completely agree that the UK could do much more to help combat global hunger.  We should increase our budget for this and offer more targeted help to other countries - particularly in third world and war-torn areas.  In this day and age, there is no reason any person should be going hungry, and so helping others is the right thing to do.  That said, we also do need to tackle the ongoing challenges around food poverty in the UK, and I feel we should prioritise this before increasing our budget for foreign aid, as it is not right that individuals are having to rely on food banks for basic provisions.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Irrespective of who we are helping with foreign aid, we have a moral obligation to help fellow human beings. This is further exacerbated by the fact that many of the countries that formed part of the British Empire need our help. Further to this, we also have a moral obligation to help because we have the luxury of being a developed nation. With that comes certain obligations to distribute our wealth across poorer nations, particularly those that we have previously benefitted from when they were part of the British Empire. It is the right thing to do to help your fellow man.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Whilst being a worthy cause, a 'significant' increase would place strain on UK's own struggling economy, impacting service levels and social care services and so forth. A measured response, comprising a more affordable increase, matched by all other 'first world' countries would appear to provide grounds for believing that inroads could be made to addressing world hunger without causing suffering at home. Political leverage should be applied to all countries who could afford to contribute aid and, ideally, those countries most wealthy would contribute the significant amounts necessary to provide a notable boost to the organisations dealing with delivering aid.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text:
Our priority should be to help those around us first before reaching further afield. Despite being a developed country, there are many people here who are doing almost as badly as those suffering abroad. We need to address local hunger issues first especially amongst children, some of whom are sent to school hungry. Adult hunger in this country is not uncommon in people on benefits and these people must be helped too.
As a rich nation we should help others by providing food or assistance for them to grow their own food. In times or crises such as war or natural disasters we should provide more help. 
But our budget must not be misused and aid must not end up in the hands of the corrupt. The needy must benefit from out aid.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: there is no point in sending funds and food to needy countries while the threat of war remains so high. We need to teach the hungry how to feed themselves, while trying to maintain global security. Simply sending aid is a very short term fix, we need to send solutions, ie farming experts, irrigation specialists, and contraception information. It is very distressing to imagine how many lives could be saved, how many people fed, and how many economies boosted if it were not for war. One nuclear missile could simply wipe out decades of foreign aid and help in one strike.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: While helping other countries can be beneficial, significant increase in foreign aid budget seems unnecessary. Many countries are surviving and should attempt to look after themselves rather than significantly relying on the UK. At the current moment there seems to be many issues in the UK far more important, and we should pay attention to these issues before considering giving out money that we could otherwise use to improve the country. "Significant" increases would require unnecessary sacrifices and possible increase in tax. An increase in foreign aid may be ok but care must be taken and perhaps something should be offered in return for foreign aid to other countries.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I think all countries should give aid to help combat hunger globally but at the moment the UK is in a real mess with no budget for anything. I do agree the UK should give some aid but can't see how it can increase the foreign aid budget when it can't look after its own citizens. The services to citizens in the UK are all underfunded or non existent right now so I don't think there is enough to significantly increase foreign aid. All countries should give what they can to help globally and the budget could be increased when a country is in a good financial situation.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: I have mixed feelings on this. On one hand, I believe it is a wealthy nation's responsibility to help those in need, and provide aid, resources and financial support to those who need it. However, we do have issues in our own country needing to be addressed such as poverty and homelessness, both leading to hunger, and maybe the money would be better spent trying to resolve this before helping others. There is also the aspect of global hunger being caused by underlying issues such as climate change, inequality and war. The UK contributes to these factors in many ways, so reducing our contribution and funding other things such as climate change controlling measures may be more impactful.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: It is such a tricky issue because of course we should be contributing to combat global hunger, in 2025 no person, anywhere on the planet should be starving to death, especially when we live in a country with such gross excess. Having a foreign aid budget is important for the UK's standing in the world and to be perceived as a strong global contributor and power. However, there is also an incredibly alarming, and rising, number of people in the UK who are struggling to afford to eat so we must not allow for our taxes to increase. I do genuinely believe there is so much waste in budgets funding the government personnel etc. that cuts could be made elsewhere in order for the UK to continue or grow its foreign aid budget.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: UK always help those who are in need. UK know that we always help the less fortunate and thus increasing foreign aid will paint a good picture of our country among other countries in the world. It can significantly improve our diplomatic relations with other countries and thus we can trade with other countries easily. Those people need our help for them to survive and thus increasing aid will be saving lives. As a developed country we are capable of increasing foreign aid without adversely stressing our resources. We should know that sharing is caring and we are caring country.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text:
Reducing poverty and increasing inequality; the increase in foreign aid can help alleviate poverty, reduce inequality and promote economic growth. It also helps in creating a more stable and prosperous world.

Improve global health and well-being; hunger and malnutrition are associated with many health issues, specifically when it comes to children vulnerable population. By increasing this aid, the UK will help fix these health issues.

Enhance food security and stability; investing in hunger relief and sustainable agriculture would help ensure food security for the people. reduce the risk of famine and promote stability in regions vulnerable to crisis and conflict.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text: To even consider going backwards to a time where same-sex marriage was not legal doesn't make sense. Same-sex marriage was a logical step, clearing up an inequality based on ancient groundless prejudices. It was a progressive move and one which (I believe) the majority of the population supports. It renders the institution of marriage equal. Its reason for existence is to recognise a longterm loving partnership and the sexuality of the people in that partnership doesn't seem relevant. Making same-sex legal makes the financial and legal support of marriage available to all in a fair and equal way. It seems wholly beneficial.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text:
