If only evolution is taught in schools, this means that people of some religions that don't believe in this may not be as willing to learn in school as the things they are being taught are directly contradicting what they are learning at home and what their religion or faith teaches them, meaning they may be less motivated
Some atheists who would have been validated into believing into evolution only as school only teaches this ,and if school, mosts teaches you right things this means that they may be force fed into thinking evolution is the correct way to think, whereas if they were showed an alternative interpretation they may be able to come up with their own opinion

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Due to how busy and understaffed the public sector is, private firms may be more efficient and have more staff in ensuring prisons are not overcrowded and building more sites in order to account for higher numbers of prisoners. This would also relieve pressure on the public sector so that the budget and staff could be placed elsewhere and the focus would be on areas such as the NHS. The key issue is handing over the welfare of human beings to the private sector where the priority might be money and profit and efficiency over the welfare of the prisoners.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: The government of UK when privatise prison systems means that it should be run by private companies. Supporters argue that it can cut costs, boost efficiency, and improve conditions through competition. Critics warn that profit motives may lead to poor staffing, unsafe conditions, and less focus on rehabilitation. While some UK prisons are already privately run, full privatisation would expand this model.  The debate centres on balancing cost-effectiveness with accountability, safety, and purpose of prisons -punishment or reform. Ultimately, privatisation raises questions about whether justice services should be proft-driven or remain fully under government or public control to ensure transparency and fairness.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: If the prisons are privatised, the owners of the prisons can choose to do whatever they want with little to no consequence leading to corruption as they can favour some people and be worse to some other people which the government will not have as much control over. Also owners will try to find loopholes to get away with things they can whereas government owned prisons are easier to regulate. I wasn't completely with disagree because this could mean that the government won't need as much focus and money on prisons and can focus on other problems like inflation, education and climate change.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
I'm not completely sure if it would be better if prisons would be better if they were privatised. I believe some prisons are owned by companies such as G4. I think if they were all run and owned by HMPS there would be continuity and I believe it would be run properly and not just for profits. However if it was run privately it might mean that those particular prisons may not be over crowded.
Like I said before it is not something I know an awful lot about so that is why I have put the marker in the middle

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Any thing that becomes privatised turns in to being all about money and profits. What will happen is the opposite of what should as the government needs to take control and be at the helm directly rather than enabling other companies to do their job. They will loose the ability to be nimble and enforce new policies for the prison service a quickly as they could do. If the prison system was to actively rehabilitate prisoners too, if it wasn't some how incentivized then private companies would just not provide it as it would cost more. I just think we should look at what other countries have done and evaluate.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: The prison service cannot afford to be privatised. The security consequences of a private companies becoming involved in the prison sector could be dire. Privatisation has not worked in other industries, the railways being one, where the government has now taken it back into their control, if private companies become involved who will prioritise profits over security then it could pose serious threats to national security. It is one sector that needs serious funding from government level and not different companies that will try to get involved without realising or being overly concerned about the consequences of underfunding and the dangers that could be posed.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
I feel privatisation of the prison system would likely see a huge drop in welfare standards for prisoners in the UK. Government funded prisons means there is a standard they must adhere to in order for the public to be satisfied. If this became privatised there is no guarantee this standard would be kept. It also incentivises incarceration for private gain which is morally reprehensible. 

Due to the reasons above I believe that privatisation would not benefit from actually rehabilitating prisoners, which in turn would lead to higher reoffending rates which is ultimately more crime on the streets. The incentive to keep more people in prisons and for longer, will also result in an even lower quality of living and could possibly impact prison suicide rates.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Inmates are still people and their punishment is living in a prison for their given time, not neglect. Prioritising profit will result in neglect of prisoners, and will result in worse living conditions, through lower quality of food, overcrowding, unhygienic living conditions, etc, all in the purpose of maximising profit. To maximise profit, less staff would be employed, and consequently those working there will be overworked, reducing the quality of their work, therefore cleanliness will decrease, creating risk of disease spreading, and violence will increase as less staff are able to manage increasing inmate numbers. This will also put the staff at risk, resulting in a higher risk of violence against staff, as they are less able to manage the inmates. Overcrowding will increase, as building more space for inmates is costly. We should not be neglecting our inmates. They have human rights too, regardless of their crimes.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text:
When this happened with Schools and nurseries, making a profit became the most important factor. Once it becomes money making, unscrupulous shareholders can demand more and this could affect prisoner's lives and chances for education. It could lead to abuse as each institution would have their own rules on things like punishment and restraint.
Some prisons would be run fairly, with rehabiliation important, others may concentrate on keeping people locked up with no opportunities to improve.
This would put prison owners in charge of their own staff recruitment and this is one area they may try to save money with unqualified staff and recruitment agencies. This could be dangerous to prisoners and guards.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Private companies may aim to profit off of the prisoners and reduce funding for staff, healthcare and rehabilitation, and this should not be what the prison system is about. Profiting off prisoners is unethical and the prison system is not a business and should not be seen as such. The government is more likely to focus on the rehabilitation of prisoners and to be more transparent with the practices it undertakes, meaning they are more likely to be subject to laws regarding the prison system. Government ownership also ensures that the treatment across prisons is consistent, while private prisons may vary greatly in their treatment and practices.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: I would be opposing to the idea of a prison system being run by private companies, because I would fear for the inmantes welfare, who might be subjected to depravation of reabilitation programmes to favour profit. I am also unsure how well a private company can be monitored and scrutanised as opposed to a public one, so I would be very weary of any kind of information being disclosed if the system becomes private. I feel that private company's focuses are not in lines with the welfare of the inmantes, making it quite contraversial and possibly dangerous for the inmates themselves..

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: I disagree with the idea that the UK should privatize its prison system. When prisons are operated by private companies, the is a risk that profit becomes more important than the welfare  and rehabilitation of inmates. This can lead to cost-cutting measures that compromise safety, heath services, and educational programs, ultimately reducing the chances of successful reintegration in to society. Furthermore, private prisons often lack transparency and may not be held to the same standards of accountability as public institutions. The justice system is a fundamental responsibility of the state, and prison management should reflect public values such as fairness, human rights, and rehabilitation.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Privatising the prison system will mean that prisons are put into the control of people who want to maximise profit first and foremost, rather than actually reduce reoffending and protect society. Profit-maximisation measures will likely lead to overcrowding, worse living conditions for prisoners, and more prison labour with little pay. This will likely have damaging effects on prisoners' mental and physical health. It will inhibit rehabilitation and exacerbate existing problems. Private owners of prisons have little incentive to actually rehabilitiate prisoners and offer them good mental health and literacy programs for example. All this will harm prisoners themselves and make them more likely to reoffend when they are released, putting society at large at risk.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: In my opinion prisons should not be privatised. I have several reasons for this. Firstly, services that are privatised become a business rather than a service; owners are focused on how much profit they can make to line their own pockets, rather than providing a service that meets the requirements of the consumers. We only have to look at the poor state of our water supply and rail services across the country since privatisation. Not enough money is invested in services to keep them fit for purpose. Prisons, in particular, need to be secure but also take into account the wellbeing of the inmates. I feel privatisation will result in cutting costs thereby putting prisoners health and well being at risk. Also that of the prison staff.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Privatisation could mean that we save money on  prisons but it could also mean that we spend more as private companies need to make a profit and indeed could charge exorbitantly.  If they do not get what they want, private companies would probably cut costs on buildings and manpower, etc., which would compromise security and put the public at risk. Government would have little or no control over what happens in prisons and this too would put public safety at risk. It would also be more difficult to implement changes and improvements to the prison system, even though these might be good for society.

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: In my opinion prisoners in the UK have a very easy life.  The prison system is no deterrent for many of them.  They are often given free access the television, 3 paid meals a day, a bed, free healthcare along with many other services that many  free law abiding citizens struggle to access or pay for.  Therefore, privatising the system could make money to put into the system if prisoners are made the work for their 'keep' just as people on the outside do.  The government, as a result, could save the money normally spent, to use for something else such as funding the councils to reduce council tax bills

rows.proposition: The UK should privatise its prison system.
rows.text: Firstly, I think that it would help the government to save money as they can use it for other areas like education, crime or healthcare which are highly important. By privatising the prison system, they would give make private companies take ownership and handle the day-to-day running costs which would save money in the long-term. This would also make it a competitive industry because they can give out contracts to different private companies and let them provide the services. The idea is that these companies would compete with each other to provide better service. It would also put them under pressure and makes them held responsible for running the prison system. The burden would be taken away from the government so they would be able to focus on other important issues.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I believe that the UK should not Monday Covid vaccinations for all eligible citizens because in my opinion this takes away human rights and choices. Mandating Covid vaccinations for all people in the UK would be an unfair policy because people should have choice and be able to make their own opinions in this matter and their own choices. I believe if the UK was to mandate vaccinations this would cause people in the UK to cause riots and things like that because they would not agree with being forced into doing something that was against their own choice of their own will.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
Mandating the Covid 19 vaccine is a misstep because the vaccines were rolled out under extreme time pressure , leading concerns about their long time efficacy and safety

There is data out there suggesting a concerning number of adverse events, including deaths and long term illness among people who have received the vaccine.

This raises serious questions of the risk-ratio factor, particularly for a universal mandate. 

This would be an overreach by government but also disregard the potential harm to certain sections of the population underlying public trust in healthcare agencies and scientific institutions and could possibly evoke an uprising by the population.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I think the UK should make COVID-19 vaccines mandatory for all eligible people because it keeps everyone safe. Vaccines help stop the virus from spreading, which is important for protecting people who are older or have health problems. When more people get the vaccine, the virus spreads less, and everyone stays safer. Getting vaccinated is not just about helping yourself, but also helping others. If fewer people get sick, hospitals will not be as full, and businesses can stay open. This helps the country get back to normal faster. In short I think vaccines protect everyone and help the country recover.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I am in too minds with statement. As someone who received all COVID shot, and someone who got each of the boosters offered to them, I think this should be mandated. I never thought I needed all the boosters I was offered, I have also had COVID and didn't get particularly sick from it, pre vaccine. But for me, I knew that wouldn't be the case for everyone. So, I got all the vaccines as I knew it would be the best way of protecting myself and other people I would come into contact with. I also think the more people that receive this, the less strain this would put on the NHS, the less people would be admitting themselves into hospital due to being unwell. However, I also am a firm believer in bodily autonomy and people having free will to do what they want with their bodies.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
Catching COVID can be an unpleasant experience for most people who catch it and can be deadly or leave people with long term conditions that are life limiting.  The cost both to to the country and the individual of COVID can be extremely high compared to the low cost of vaccination. Even if the vaccine does not stop infection it can reduce the severity.

Herd immunity is conferred on those who cannot be vaccinated and it is selfish to expose others to avoidable risks for no benefit to yourself.  There are no significant risks behind vaccines  and conspiracy theories are damaging national and global health.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I'm divided on this issue. On the one hand, it makes sense to mandate vaccinations for all eligible citizens but on the other hand, I'm not sure that this is the best approach from the government. Certainly, it would make our country safer but we would still be at risk from others who are carrying the virus. I imagine there would be less chance of a major outbreak and having to quarantine and lock down again. I don't think it should be a government mandated scheme as people need to be able to make their own choices and not forced to do something that might be against their will.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
There is a firm belief amongst people who support he vaccine who say that getting the vaccine will not only help you but it will help the people who have severe medical issues....these people include scientists,nurses and doctors who i believe it is in their best interests that people of all ages take the vaccination for reasons of health.
There  is also a huge body of people who believe the vaccine does not prevent you contarcting covid and it comes with many side efffects sometimes these are very serious.
Unless a person is subject to serious medical pre existing conditions it is not thought that taking the vaccine is a positive move in general.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I have assumed the Covid outbreak is not as serious as the original pandemic. For me personal choice is the (almost) overriding consideration. There is a choice to take the risk dependant on your own assessment. But clearly is the NHS was under severe pressure in may be injections were mandatory. But worry how this would be enforced by a government. People have many reasons or religious beliefs about the injections. There are also concerns that the vaccinations are safe. It would be disastrous if a type of vaccine was found to be harmful. On balance I believe personal choice is the only practical way.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I am pro vaccines but with the Covid vaccine I do feel that individuals should be able to make an informed decision, weighing up the benefits of receiving it.  I think the threat of Covid is still very present and it does make people very unwell, but I am not convinced that mandating vaccines is necessarily ethical for it.  The vast majority of people recover, and this is a huge cost to the government.  I think people also do have concerns regarding how quickly it was developed (whether or not it was created using existing vaccine methods), and some people have become unwell from having it.  It would be a difficult initiative to enforce and would probably lead to tensions.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
First, and most importantly, everyone should have freedom of choice over the vaccinations they receive and if they should receive them. No one should be forced to take a vaccine if they do not wish to, regardless of how effective it may or not be. 
Secondly, not everyone will be able to receive a vaccine. Some people may be allergic to the vaccine. What happens then? In that scenario, it's not possible to mandate vaccinations when not everyone can take one.
Finally, the vaccine may not fully protect everyone from the infection, which isn't as severe as initially thought and so manadatory vaccinations for COVID-19 seems overkill.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I absolutely disagree with this , there is no way I would ever have the vaccination , I didn't when covid was happening and will never have it ever , I know lots of people I am sure will feel the same . Why would the government even think of considering it ? People I know have been ill since the vaccination , and people have also died because of the vaccination , I do not trust the government at all , there was something more to that vaccine than they were telling us , basically no one person should be able to tell someone else to do something

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: No government should be able to force or coerce people into getting covid vaccinations. Adults have every right to refuse vaccinations, especially since there have been some lethal side effects such as blood clots etc. It is only a few small steps from taking away peoples personal freedoms in healthcare to taking away their freedoms of speech (already gone in Scotland), freedom to vote, freedom to travel etc. What's next? Implementation of Sharia law? Forcing children to be trans? it is all downhill from here and people just don't seem to understand what is at stake. The future does not look bright at all for those who value their personal freedom.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text:
I'm not in agreement with government mandating any medical procedure - that is totalitarianism and a dictator state. All vaccines have risks and we should be told those and them left to decide ourselves. We are able to come to our own decisions if we are left to them. In the past many, many government decisions in the field of health have proved ineffective and downright dangerous (thalidomide, factor 8, HIV, cot death advice, now voluntary euthanasia). They should not make us do anything.

Present the facts and let independently minded people come to their own conclusions rather than be dictated to.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Although something like this would be good for protecting public health, this kind of law would be ethically and morally wrong. There are members of the public who cannot, for health reasons, receive such vaccines and thus this would be a risk to their health and life. People in the UK have a freedom of choice and should not be forced by the government to do such things against their will.  This kind of law would cause lots of backlash and might even fuel some of the conspiracy theories already going around such as the COVID-19 vaccine being used to track people, etc.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: Vaccinations play a crucial role in limiting the spread of infectious diseases. The government has an important role to playing advising its citizens on public health matters and providing opportunities for people, particularly the most vulnerable, to avail of a vaccination programme. However, vaccinations should be a personal decision and any government that mandates them is over stepping its political authority. As we saw with the Covid 19 pandemic, there are many people who do not want to take vaccinations for a variety of reasons and they should not be coerced into taking them against their will. The governments role is to provide reliable advice and information and easy access to available vaccination services.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I can see why such is policy is something that people would be interested in. Mandating covid vaccines could save many lives, ease the burden on the NHS and social care having to support people with long covid. However, such a policy would be totally unprecedented - no other vaccine is mandatory, and it would be hard to justify why just the covid one should be. Such a policy wouldn't pass a vote in either houses of government, and would be against public opinion. Even if it did pass it would probably be repealed by the next government, defeating the point of the policy. Finally, there are more constructive nudges the government could make, such as requiring it for travel abroad.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I believe there is a case for compulsary covid vaccination as with past deseases such as measles which was almost wiped out until scare stories about mmr reduced the uptake and it made a comeback . Our N.H.S. is already over loaded especially in the winter months when covid is more likely to emerge along with other seasonal illnesses . I also think that the more people who are protected the less the virus is likely to spread among large groups thereby reducing the need to close pubs and restaurants to help  curb the spread which would also not have such a huge impact on the economy .

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: We saw so much delay during Covid and who knows how many lives were lost due to indecision, conspiracies and so on. I remember a fitness instructor who didn't want the vaccine because he had believed some false information about the vaccine and he subsequently died. He almost certainly would have lived if he had had the vaccine. There is a responsibility on ourselves to act in the best interests of the whole public, and we saw too many people acting selfishly. Therefore let's take away the indecision and move more quickly by making it mandatory to be vaccinated. It would save lives.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate COVID-19 vaccinations for all eligible citizens.
rows.text: I think that mandating a Covid-19 vaccine for eligible citizen is justified and an effective way to protect public health. The vaccines help to significantly increase general immunity in the public which in turn helps to reduce transmission of the virus. This is essential to preventing large outbreaks which strain our healthcare system. Beyond individual protect, I think it is a societal responsibility to have a vaccine if you are eligible. We should feel like it is the right thing to do to protect each other, especially those who are more vulnerable and may not be able to take the vaccine themselves. Also, vaccine mandates have been historically successful for diseases like smallpox and polio. The results of the past prove to me that it is a worthwhile mandate that gives long term public health benefits

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: The U.K should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions in order to ensure that the health and safety standards of workplaces are regulated through enforcements of codes such as proper PPE. Furthermore, labour unions keep companies in line when it comes to paying minimum wage and not setting unlawful hours for workers. Not only this but also allowing adequate sick leave and holidays throughout the year. Additionally, with limited powers labour unions are not able to enforce rights for workers when it comes to discrimination either within the workplace or upon recruitment or redundancy. Overall it is clear that labour unions are vital to the workforce and strengthening their rights and powers would serve to aid in the safety and security for workers and their families.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Labour unions are the core of advocacy and human rights for many workers, especially those who do manual labour/factory work. This ensures that workers have access to healthy and safe working environments. By increasing the rights, of labour unions, it may facilitate workers and employers into coming to better agreements in terms of pay, environment, and working hours. It may also facilitate better working contracts immediately, and less likelihood of situations becoming extreme, as employers would be more afraid to have to deal with strengthened labour unions. When circumstances do then become extreme, workers will not have to fight for their livelihood with worry, but can rely on labour unions wholeheartedly.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Labour unions play a vital role in maintaining a balanced and fair free-market system by acting as a crucial check on the power of capital. Strengthening their rights helps preserve the markets proper function and ensures that the social benefits it can generate are more evenly distributed. When governments provide legislative support to labour unions, they help create a more level playing field between labour and capital. This not only protects worker's interests but also promotes economic fairness, equity and stability. Empowering unions through law reinforces democratic values and encourages a more equitable and just economic environment for all participants.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: During my working life the positions and rights of workers in this country have been systematically and aggressively eroded. Each successive generation of workers see their pay stagnate and their contracts get less favourable compared with those who have come before. Anyone who has worked in a unionised vs a non-unionised work place can see that the only way to protect the conditions of workers is through the power of collective bargaining. The right to representation and the right to strike are essential to this. Furthermore rather than being reflexively anti-union employers should realise that having a capable, organised body to negotiate and communicate with is an asset to an effective business.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I always vote Labour, and so I suppose I should be a supporter of the unions over the management.  But I also own shares, and don't see big business/capitalism as necessarily being a bad thing.  I believe I am autistic, and believe that this might explain why I dislike the idea of my ever going on strike - I have never been a member of a union (I haven't worked in such an environment where it was ever a possibility), but I like to do what I am told, so if my employer wanted me to work when there was a strike I think I would feel compelled to work, even though this would put me under severe pressure from union colleagues.  I did dislike some of the Thatcher anti-union laws in the early 1980s, but these have seem to become accepted: the idea of ballots before strike action is taken, and a ban on secondary/flying pickets.  I think that there are still sufficient safeguards for unions to call strikes when they have genuine grievances against the management, so don't see that there needs to be strengthening of the rights and powers of the unions.  I do also dislike keyworkers going on strike (e.g. doctors and nurses, train operators, emergency services), and would be in favour of a ban for those workers.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Being a part of a trade union means that the worker, new or old, has access to knowledge of what the current situation of the trade is and can contribute to valuable and relevant discussions. Moreover, whenever policies related to the trade are announced, trade unions evaluate those and find what the impact of those policies might be. Then, they can voice their opinions and demand what would be in their favour. This means that a worker's rights are preserved by this representative entity that is separate from the government, employers, and policy makers. As a result, decisions are discussed and improved through suggestions and demands from trade unions and the rights of the workers are preserved.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Perhaps it is the press coverage they receive or perhaps I read the wrong newspapers but the monies paid to some of these union leaders and the perks they receive are totally disproportionate to their abilities to negotiate "reasonable"  deals or to accept in may cases what appear to be more than reasonable pay and condition settlements. There is quite often a disconnect between ordinary union members and union leaders. The closed shop would appear to still be in place in certain areas and the present voting systems perhaps should be looked at. The days of "Red Robbo" may have gone but there is still an element of intimidation and coercion present. On the other side of the coin employers are quite often not blameless.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: The unions were once a powerful group, but they have become toothless in recent years. I put that down to corruption and backhanders to the egoists at the top. They tend to stand up for woke causes now and not British workers rights. They were formed to protect the worker but tend to attach themselves to the wrong causes, a bit like the modern Labour Party who aren't really Labour at all.  I don't think it would make any difference if they are strengthend or not. They are what they are. Their best days are behind them and that is very sad.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: The labour unions that are still active in the UK have been dealing with a huge decrease in membership over the past few decades, which means they have less power than they did during the industrial revolution. In addition, most unions only represent public service workers, like train drivers, who disrupt essential services when they strike and anger the general public with their actions. There's also a perception in the general public that the union leaders exploit their members to hold the government to ransom. These union leaders are also generally paid very well, which angers private sector workers even more when they are inconvenienced by strike disruptions.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Unions can represent and fight for the employee’s rights more effectively, so in this respect they are vital for having a checked balance between rights and obligations from both sides -employees and employers.   Labour unions also can advocate for different social justice causes .  However, having too much powers can be a way to cause more disruptions like strikes that can not only disrupt companies but the economy in general.  It is very common to see how a very powerful union can stop changes in the conditions or in the market that will have an impact in the ability to compete in a world that needs constant changes to be competitive in the market.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I believe in the idea of unions, if there work for the good or there members, employers and the country. Most unions are sensible and do not make out landise demands of the companies or governments, it allows them to speak on behalf of the great numbers of union members when wanting a decision on an issue which will either short term or long term effect there member. It is very important that there is clear transparency of how they conduct themselves for both there members and the great public for us to have a belief and understanding of there process and how they reach an decision.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: One of the greatest challenges facing everyday working people is a job market and employment prospects that are sliding in the wrong direction in terms of respect and support for employees. Under the previous government fire and rehire became commonplace which lead to an erosion of worker's rights. I also feel that, whilst wages have actually grown comparatively well in recent years they are still well short of where they should be when adjusted for inflation over the last 15-20 years. This I believe is down to poor worker representation over recent years which has prioritised a low wage and low regulation market in favour of employers and capital.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I disagree that labour unions should have more power. There seems to be an endless stream of people who are already well paid, campaigning for more pay. They go on strike and it is the general public who suffer when their bins are not emptied or their medical treatment is postponed. I have no sympathy as they all earn more than me because I am self-employed! When wages increase due to union intervention, that extra cash has to come from somewhere and it usually from raising the costs of the output of the business, so products and services cost more, and we all suffer so the workers can earn more. When a group gets a pay rise, other groups want one too, and so the situation can escalate.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: Although I believe that business across the country should have their employees' representation in bodies like labour unions, which are often far better equipped in terms of legal knowledge that could protect workers' right while in the job, giving the unions too much power could inadvertently lead to business slowdowns due to more monitored and effectively more expensive work labour. This is especially visible in high production low wages settings like factories and warehouses where business models are based on cheap labour, higher that sector average turnover and minimum salary. By increasing unions' powers, we would be passing the final costs over the end-consumers which could lead to economical slowdown with lesser buying powers.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I think labour unions are important because they protect workers and ensure they have rights and are entitled to protections, so giving more rights and powers to labour unions ensures workers are being fought for and are able to get help/are protected when they need to be. I think the uk strenghtening the rights and powers of labour unions also sends a message to other countries that the uk values it's people and workers, and wants them to be protected. I don't see why anyone would disagree with this as all workers deserve to be protected at work and have good working conditions.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text: I believe the UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions. These unions are good for workers and have powers than normal workers do not have. The have the best interests of workers in their hearts and make decisions solely for workers. It's great that workers have people who look out for them and support them when needed. They also make decisions for the workers that maybe some wouldn't understand. The unions can help arrange collective action that can make employers sit up and take notice. This is hard to do when you are an individual. Action is always better when a group takes it.

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
The employees should be better protected in terms of benefits, redundancies, bullying, harassament etc
Unions play a crucial role in helping with getting better wages for the employees, better work conditions.Empowering unions helps balance the power between employers and employees, prometes workplace justice and reduces exploitation. This also encourages dialogue and resolution in some cases. A strong union encourages open dialogue  and more equitable and productive workplace. This can benefit the workers, the employers and the economy. 
At the moment people are strugling with wages because of the increase of the bills, if we have stronger unions that fight with the employees this can help to get better salarys

rows.proposition: The UK should strengthen the rights and powers of labour unions.
rows.text:
I am not a believer in giving labour unions greater rights and powers. I think this would give them more freedom to be more productive in their actions if they don't get what they 'believe' their members want.  It would give labour unions an easier way to hold the government and country to ransom for bigger pay rises and more disruptive strike action.

Labour Unions would normally call strike action if they don't get what they want. This seems to be an easy option and is in industries which can have a dramatic effect on the economy of the country or has an affect on the service sector where the government is held to a form of ransom. The outcome being that an increase in earnings is more than the country can afford and in this regard unions can have too much power.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I am a bit on the fence on this one, they always used to call nuclear weapons the nuclear deterrent on the basis that, they said we are less likely to be attacked with nuclear weapons is a potential attacker knows that to do so will unleash a deadly revenge in kind. I don't like it, I don't like the potential implications of using nuclear weapons but I can see that maybe you can never say never because if we say that we would never use nuclear bombs under any circumstances they what does anyone have to fear from using them on us. Also what about NATO and our allies they may no longer be our allies if we aren't prepared to use the means necessary to defend ourselves and others and that would leave us very vulnerable.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I agree with a policy to avoid usage of nuclear bombs, since as far as I'm aware, nuclear war is disastrous for all parties involved, and no one 'wins' such a war. Likewise, nuclear bombs injure, kill, and damage the property of innocent civilians to a great extent, so avoiding their usage makes sense. However, I am personally less knowledgeable about nuclear bombs, nuclear war, and wars in general, so cannot strongly agree with the statement. Additionally, 'under any circumstances' is a huge commitment, since there could be a situation where using nuclear bombs is the best/only option available to the UK, and so such a policy would not be optimal during such a situation.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: This is a complex argument. Nuclear bombs work as a deterrent but we should never use them because of the terrible consequences of their use but because this would lead to nuclear retaliation and mutually assured destruction. I also have a fundamental problem with them in that if we have them and the ability to use them, then how can we prevent others obtaining them and potentially use them. I find it difficult to argue why we allow a country such as israel to have them but not Iran. It may seem logical and in our own interests but seems prejudicial especially if I was Iranian.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
I think nuclear bombs are a force of destruction that should not be used on another nation but they can act as a deterrent, preventing large scale wars. Having a policy that says the UK will never use nuclear bombs under any circumstances might mean other nations would feel like they could attack without nuclear retaliation. This policy would leave the UK vulnerable. 

There would be not point in having nuclear weapon without the threat of using them as this leads to other countries becoming hesitant to attack. Since the invention of nuclear weapons there has not been any world wars, no nation wants a nuclear war because of the amount of devastation that would cause

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
- The development of nuclear weapons is one of the human race's most foul, and appalling missteps.  Instead of spending millennia coming up with new ways to eradicate ourselves, why are we not focused on making the planet better for ourselves?   

- Nuclear weaponry has only been used twice in warfare (which is already twice too much), and it remains one of the darkest stains on the history of the human race.    Hiroshima and Nagasaki were deliberately chosen to demonstrate the raw, destructive power of nuclear weaponry, and it came at an appalling loss to civilian life.  I view it as a war crime, and indeed, it's been argued that this was illegal (under international law at the time), as "indiscriminate bombardment of undefended cities".  Of course this also set a precedent for where warfare would go next and we now have nine major nations with known nuclear capabilities.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
I believe that nuclear weapons should never be deployed. If there were the results would be utterly devastating. The level of human suffering that it would cause is cruel and unthinkable. I believe only insanity could ever prompt the deployment of such weapons and whatever the use of such a weaponry the  intended outcome would not serve any positive purpose. 
Retaliation with the same type of weapon from others and before we know it a huge proportion of the human race no longer exist and the survivors  I suspect would not survive for long, nor I suspect would many of them wish to. The thought of nuclear weapon deployment and the results of doing so are almost to devastating to consider.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
I strongly believe that the UK should adopt a policy of never using nuclear weapons under any circumstances. The destructive power of these weapons inflicts indiscriminate suffering on civilians, causes long-term environmental damage, and results in intergenerational harm—all of which are ethically unacceptable. A clear no-use policy would reinforce the UK's commitment to peace and global nuclear disarmament, encouraging other nations to follow suit. 

Maintaining a nuclear arsenal does not guarantee safety; in fact, it increases the risk of accidents, miscalculations, or escalations that could lead to catastrophic outcomes. True national and global security should be built on diplomacy, mutual understanding, and international cooperation—not the threat of annihilation. By taking a firm stance, the UK can lead by example in shaping a safer, more humane world.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I don't believe any country should ever use nuclear weapons and I believe that the use of them causes more destruction and suffering than any good that could come from using them. They have long lasting negative effects in the areas they are dropped and can cause retaliation which only causes more destruction. I believe that by adopting a policy to never use nuclear bombs, more countries would follow the UK example and the world would be a much more stable place with far less chance of war breaking out. I do not believe that anyone should have the power to wipe out life at the touch of a button. It is never justifiable.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: the immediate reason is the amount of lives that a nuclear bomb would destroy. Countless lives would be lost and the main victim would be innocent civilians. The ling term effects would be severe as the damage that would be done to the environment would mean the place would be inhabitable for years. It would severely impact diplomatic relations and would hinder all trust that was once there between countries. It would set us all back years and years if a nuclear weapon was ever used. Most countries would retaliate in some way which would lead to a global war potentially wiping out humanity

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I believe it is a good thing because we should stay away from causing wars especially where people die without reason and even if they're not bad they are in the danger of dying so I think it is important to not harm people especially knowing what is going on in the world in terms of countries and conflicts. If we can see such outcomes occurring already then there is a respectful lesson to learn on not repeating such mistakes. I think that is the fairest way we can do such is to stay away from bombing anyone at all.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
Innocent civillians do not deserve to lose their homes and lives due to political disagreements. Innocent people are just trying to live their lives. They should not have to live in fear. Life is already hard for a lot of people living in the UK. We pay a lot of tax and the average civilian has difficulty making ends meet due to the economy being on its knees. If the UK government could commit to never using nuclear bombs, it would give individuals much needed peace of mind. 

The cost of nuclear bomb attacks is huge on our economy and the economy around the world. We are already paying off debts from the previous world wars. We cannot cope with further austerity. Families are already living in poverty in the UK. Lets not make it harder for the poorest members of our society.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text:
The creation and maintenance of a nuclear arsenal is extremely expensive.  To commit to never using it is to admit we are spending money on a thing that has absolutely no utility, like ploughing our sandy coast with a fleet of tractors every day.

The utility of a nuclear arsenal is to prevent a potential enemy from harming us.  It is only credible as a deterrent if we are committed to using it under undisclosed conditions of our own choosing.  Even restricting our use of it by setting out explicitly those conditions will severely limit our ability to defend ourselves, as external circumstances change and geo-political concerns and technology create new dangers not foreseen when we enunciated those conditions.  A potential enemy must be kept in doubt as to our intentions.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I feel quite conflicted over this, but mostly lean towards this being a bad idea. I believe the use of nuclear bombs is never justified, and that privately UK Prime Ministers should commit to never consenting to the use of them. However, announcing such a policy publicly would mean that the nuclear deterrent goes away, which puts the country in a more dangerous and threatening situation. To my understanding, the only reason the UK has nuclear bombs anyway is to act as a deterrent, and I believe publicly committing to this policy would undermine this deterrent. However, nuclear bombs should never be used under any circumstances.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: It is ideal for the UK to never use nuclear bombs under any circumstances and their use would be disastrous to other populations in the world and would cause immense environmental damage which would pollute land and water for years to come. The humanitarian effects would be catastrophic and nuclear bombs must be there as a threat only. But in the current climate, other nations have nucleaer weapons and it is considered a credible threat that they might be used. This is every citizens worse nightmare. I would wish that humanity could move beyond this darkest path and commit to peaceful means of finding solutions through co-operation. Sadly, there are some very ugly aspects to human nature and certain world leaders can become focussed on domination and may seek to invade and exploit other countries in the most horrific ways as have been seen in previous wars. Therefore, at present, it seems necessary to have nuclear bombs but not have a policy of never using them under any circumstances as this would defeat the purpose of them existing at all.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: although i understand the need for the uk to have a nuclear weapon i believe this should only be as a deterrent. using such a weapon will only ead to mutual destruction. once the first country fires one of these on another country , the destruction of the planet will have started. even the threat of these being used should be sufficient to bring every world leader to the negotiating table to do whatever if is necessary to avoid such a catastrophe. it is extremely worrying that any responsible world leader is considering the use of a nuclear weapon. the world should be striving to ensure these weapons are destroyed forever

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: If a country like Iran knew we were not going to use nuclear weapons there would be less of a deterrent for them. They would feel more emboldened to attack or take advantage economically of the uk should they ever wish. This is more so for nuclear weaponised countries like Russia. Our defences would be severely weekend and we would be regarded as a laughing stock if we were one of the only countries to agree to it. If we were attacked by nuclear weapons we would have no way to respond and would be sitting ducks enhancing the likelihood of nuclear weapons to be used against us in time of war.

rows.proposition: The UK should commit to a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances.
rows.text: I don't believe the UK should ever use nuclear weapons, however the nuclear deterrent effect greatly increases our national security. While a government may even agree with a policy of never using nuclear bombs under any circumstances, they should never commit to, or acknowledge this to the public. This would mean we no longer have a nuclear deterrent, as our adversaries would know that we would never use it, rendering it useless. This would greatly diminish our national security, as hostile nations would know that they now have greater freedom in pursuing hostile acts against the UK, as they no longer have to worry about a nuclear response. This position would also put our position in NATO into question

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: Our distant ancestors did do terrible things but the world has now accepted and acknowledged this.  It is not the fault of MY generation that these things happened so it should not be expected that the current generation are somehow financially liable to pay compensation.  We should instead concentrate on creating a fair and just society with all sections of society being treated equally and with respect.  A formal apology might be a good thing but that is really as far as it should go.  Schools should teach all children what happened to further emphasise that these things did happen.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I believe the call for reparations is largely a grift by people in ex-colonies who sense the smell of money. There is absolutely no need for any monies to be paid after all this time. It would amount to people who were never involved in colonisation paying money to other people who were also never subjected to it and as such as vacuous virtue signalling. Even if people think colonisation was wrong my modern standards, you cannot transpose the standards of today to centuries ago with a view to extracting compensation of erstwhile "victims". The past was a very different place and that needs to be recognised. It's also interesting that there are no calls for reparations from Arab nations who also practised colonisation and who, arguably, still utilise slave labour from developing countries.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I have concerns over what harms are to be considered in relation to colonisation and what extent the benefits balance this out. Are we considering the impact of slavery several hundred years ago, or the effects of colonisation closer to home both pre and post this period, such as in Ireland, Scotland and N Ireland as recently as 30 years ago. Who decides what qualifies for payments. Also does the current generation who did not benefit from colonisation have a duty to pay for historical figurers who did?  Why am I responsible for something that happened hundreds of years ago in countries that have been independent for considerable periods before my birth and who is entitled to these payments? the ancestors of people who lived all that time ago or anyone who is living in that country now?

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I understand that this may still be painful for the countries affected by colonisation but it is time to move forward. We need to learn from mistakes made and make changes where necessary to prevent such things happening again in the future.  We cant change our history we can only learn from it.  Many countries as well as the UK colonised countries, will they do the same, provide reparations and apologize.  We cant keep raking up the past and we cant change what happened we need to work together to improve and integrate, working and living together in a peaceful way.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: This is a very difficult question. There is no doubt that the UK caused damage to the former Empire countries and that many British people made their fortunes at the expense of those countries. I do think that there should be an acknowledgement that the behaviour of the UKs  has contributed to the poverty and the lack of development in some of these counties. However no one alive today is responsible for the colonisation and I am not sure how meaningful an apology from a Government in the twenty first century could mean when the events took place in the nineteenth century and even earlier,

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: While I do accept that many horrid things happened in our history, particularly in regards to the impact of colonisation, we can pay reparations to these countries for things that happened hundreds of years ago. The question would be how far back do you go, what impact did it have on these people and countries, how were people affected, and what is the purpose of providing money for something that can never change because it's already happened.  If we have to pay, can we claim back against other countries that affected us, ie, the Scandinavian countries whose Vikings invaded and forced people to become slaves, or the Romans who also did the same. It's opening a huge can of worms that is endless and futile.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I would say that we should but I only moderately agree as I don't know too much about the history of the UK colonising. I know that we have done this and it is part of our history but am not super aware. My main point as I've already said is that it is morally the right thing to do and I wasn't aware that we haven't already done this so I would say yes to it. My other point is that it is part of our past and I think it is important to address the past rather than just ignore it.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The Uk should in my believes apologise as a nation to colonisation that happened many years ago. They should point out that the behaviour was despicable and will never happen again. Although if they apologise then they will be expected to pay for the mistakes of others and provide reparations to the countries that were enslaved, this could be a very costly thing to the UK. But also the reparations would certainly help the countries that were involved in the slavery giving them a more stable economy and enriching the lives of their people. We should also still maintain that this slavery was done by the people who lived many years ago and it is not how we think the world should be today.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I am strongly opposed to the UK formally apologising and provide reparations for colonisations. As with every country we have evolved since the times of colonisations, we are no longer the same country and this happens hundreds of years ago. There is therefore no one alive who went through this period of time. This is simply asking people who did not make these decisions to apologise for something they did not do and provide reparations to people who are no longer alive anyway. I personally do not see the point in this. The country has moved on and everyone else should also.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: There are few nations which haven't oppressed somebody else fairly recently.  Apologising for whatever harm we did is reasonable, and so is returning any treasures which we stole rather than bought.  Paying reparations, however, takes us into complicated territory.  The UK already spent a considerably fortune in ships and lives to end slavery, and working out how much we might owe in reparations would be impossibly complicated.  We used some predatory trade practices in India - but would India have been richer without us, or would it have been mired in local wars between petty kingdoms?  If we pay reparations to the Empire, should the Netherlands pay reparations to us for William of Orange, who was effectively an invader?  It would be better in my opinion to strengthen our ties with the Commonwealth, and give Commonwealth countries preferential trade agreements and aid and easier immigration, rather than trying to calculate blame.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text:
I do not believe there is an advantage to apologising. That may seem uncaring and harsh but I cannot see the value for anyone; apologising for something that happened in a different time, under different circumstances, for decisions that were made under those conditions.
Again, offering monetary compensation for things that happened in different times and circumstances seems futile. I do concede that there would be no harm in agreeing that we would never engage in the same behaviours in future though.
I also agree that it is important to acknowledge that certain behaviours happened, why they happened and to ensure that this never happens again.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: I believe that the UK should formally apologise and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation because it has caused damage that has created a lot of disadvantages for a big portion of the world. Colonized countries have been exploited by the UK from their resources and have not been able to use them to develop. Furthermore, the impacts of colonisation continue to this day and the UK is still benefiting from the impact it has had in the world. Former colonies are undeveloped and the best people from these countries are immigrating to countries like the UK. This puts those countries at a disadvantage again and the UK benefits from the brain and talent that moves here.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The way in which people have settled in other countries over the years and taken power away from the indigenous people where they are made to be lesser citizens than those who have taken over and become part of the country without a thought for the people who are originally from the place. I find it terrible to think that people have taken over whole communities and made the people who already live there work for them and give them no power when they were there in the first place and the country belongs to them and not the invaders.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text:
With the UK refusing to apologize and provide reparations in the conservative government it comes as no surprise that Labour are doing the same.
At the end of the day it comes down to money.  If they could apologize without financial repercussions then they would but it's like Pandora's box... apologize and a whole host of other questions and expectations will come with it including remuneration.
Slavery still goes on today in the form of trafficking so focus, time and money should now be put on this and move forward with the times. Sad to say it but i can't see an apology on the horizon

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: With British colonisation, Judeo-Christian values were also brought over and forced upon the people of numerous nations we colonised and is in large part why we see anti-LGBTQ+ laws in numerous countries across the globe, including India, where we also forced our prudish culture onto and why the modern Indian cultural wear is significantly more conservative/modest as opposed to the traditional wear seen pre-colonisation. We did not have the right to do this and whilst an apology will not fix the decades these countries have been impacted by us, it is the least we can do to fix this. We also exploited the resources of numerous nations, hence why we see such a big wealth gap in India and other countries, and picked out our own leaders for various nations, resulting in corruption and political instability. We should work towards helping these nations, and reparations is one way to do so.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: The UK should certainly apologise for the crimes committed during its building of an empire.  These crimes involved the subjugation and exploitation of the populations of countries in the Americas, Asia and Africa. In the 16th through to the 20th  century England benefitted not just the products of the countries it controlled, but was also one of the leading slave trading nations, transporting hundreds of thousands of Africans via the triangular trade to work the colonies in the Americas.  When Britain ended its slave trade it compensated the slave owners but not the slaves and their descendants.  Many of the countries of the former empire are extremely poor and Britain has a moral duty to try to narrow this povert gap by sharing its wealth.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text:
It reflects poorly on the UK that this is part of our history. Colonisation is not a positive thing and in now modern society we can understand this. Taking land that is not your own is terrible so the UK should definitely apologise for their history in their participation of this.

This can only benefit all involved with this. For the countries colonised, they deserve an apology as they did not deserve this to happen to them. For the UK, it will make the country look better that they are taking responsibility for past actions that were negative. Taking accountability is important.

rows.proposition: The UK should formally apologize and provide reparations for the impacts of colonisation.
rows.text: It is shocking how entire countries and governments, not just individuals, could ever think that colonisation was morally defensible. It is not. Serious crimes were committed, human beings were treated worse than animals, and raw materials were extracted from countries that desperately needed them for their own development. Apologising is the very least we should be doing. Reparations is more complicated though - how would it work, how would we determine which decendants of slaves receive what, or would we compensate whole countries at government level? We also couldn't afford a just settlement at this stage, so it seems unlikely that financial compensation would every happen. But an apology would be a good start.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text:
* Moral Obligation: Ukraine is defending not just its sovereignity but the fundamental principles  of democracy and international law against a brutal invasion marked by atrocities. By abandoning Ukraine, the UK would tactily endorse a world where dictators can violently redraw borders and commit war crime without consequence

* Deter future conflicts: Robust military aid to Ukraine send a clear message to agressors linke Iran, North Korean that the west will impose crippling costs on unprovoked invasions. If Russian succeds, it would embolden authoritarian regimes to pursue expansionist war e.g China against Taiwan, Iran against Gulf states. A Ukrainian victory would reinforce detterence, proving that alliances like NATO and democracies like the UK will defend the rules-based order decisively

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: Russia invaded Ukraine in an unprovoked attack. Ukraine needs our help and the help of other countries to overcome this invasion. If Russia is not stopped, it may well seek to invade other countries including our own. There is evidence of an increase in Russian military resources near the borders of other countries such as Finland. Ukraine is not a member of NATO so it needs to rely on the help of other countries such as ours. So far, sanctions do not appear to have had much impact and the Russian onslaught continues daily. I think that the UK must increase its military support to Ukraine.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: Russia is the aggressor in this war, with Ukranians the innocent victims of Putin's mercenary and callous desire to further his dominance and control. Whilst Ukranians continue to fight with courage and determination, they require much outside support in order to actually win the war. It is not only in their interests to provide support but necessary to protect Europe and elsewhere. Putin would not stop with his invasion of Ukraine but continue to expand his territory. The UK has shown support and provided weapons but not to the extent necessary to really help Ukraine. Putin is a bully and needs to be stopped.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: I wholeheartedly support this statement. Ukraine suffered a completely unprovoked assault by Russia, and has been left single-handedly fighting a war against an aggressor with wider ambitions to take territory in Europe - rebuilding the Russian Empire. Ukraine doesn't have the resources to continue to fend off the Russian assault indefinitely and relies on support from other countries, and it's vital that the UK does all it can to help. We've already provided funding, but this will be for nothing if we don't provide the fullest possible bacing and Ukraine ultimately falls. If it were to fall, the offensive would likely progress across other parts of Europe and we'd be required to commit even greater resources in order to see off this increasing threat. It would be shameful if the heroic people of Ukraine were to fall to Russia and we hadn't done all that we could do as a country to support them.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: Ukraine haven't done anything wrong to my knowledge that would give Russia the right to do what they are doing, and so I think it is right that we as well as other countries, support them and increase military aid. Especially as they are a smaller country and so do not have the same level of military as we do, further more they do not have a military alliance in terms of NATO and so they do not have the same level of protection as we do. However I would be cautious to increase military aid as this would open up the UK to a possible attack/war with Russia which could put our country and its people in danger and so it may not be worth putting ourselves in danger.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: The world is a far more dangerous place since the invasion in 2022 and the UK military is not enough to defend against adversaries such as Russia as it stands. Increased spending will bring better capabilities and more forces which are an effective deterrent against states that are a threat to UK security. Not increasing spending would set a bad example for allies in NATO and would jeopardise the joint commitment to increase spending in order to preserve peace. With Trump coming to power again it is more important than ever that Europe can stand up for itself if the need came.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: I definitely think that something needs to be done to aid the current situation as it feels as though not much has changed in the last 18 months let alone since the 2022. But with the United States' recent involvement, leaning their support towards Russia, I do not think it is a good idea for the UK to increase their military support as it would lead to increased tensions with the US Government, which is one of the last things we want at the moment. I worry that if we were to increase military support, further than already done, this could lead to a big shift in global relationships and possibly create a huge fallout.

However we do not have a lot of resources left to give and we do not want to be short if we end up at war ourselves. we also do not have a lot of money and our country is struggling financially. the more we give the more it is going to cost us. but can we really put a price on saving human lives and helping humanity?

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

We should look after the interests of our own people and focus more on defending our own borders. The money would be better spent on housing and such.

Working class people fighting for obscure reasons and never for the reasons that are said to be the reasons for the war. If banks could no longer charge interest on loans from money printed out of thin air how long these wars would continue is an interesting question. There are no winners in war.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.

This is not our war to win and for goodness’s sake we have enough trouble with issues within our country to deal with. Let's do that before we offer help else where.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: I strongly agree that the UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 invasion. Russia's unprovoked aggression represents a serious breach of international law and threatens the sovereignty of a democratic nation. By increasing aid - including defensive weapons, training, and intelligence sharing, the UK can help Ukraine resist occupation and deter further Russian expansionism in Europe. This support also strengthnes NATOs collective security posture and reaffirms the UK's commitment to international norms and democratic values. While military assistance is crucial, it should be complemented by continued humanatarian aid and diplomatic engagmenet to work towars a just and lasting peace.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: Following the invasion of Crimea, Russia aggression has only increased and leaves Ukraine in a vunerable position, it has little in the way of miltary alliances, so by supporting Ukraine with military aid, it is likely to slow Russian advances, give western military tech an opportnity to be battle tested by the Ukrainians and hopefully slow down Russian aggression towards western nations. At the fall of the USSR, Ukraine gave up its nuclear arsenal with gurantees of protection from wetsern powers, we should hold to our word or this suggests we do not honour military gurantees for other ally nations. Finally, the border for NATO, which has since expanded, but at this time sat in this region, we have military gurantees for Baltic nations and by allowing Ukraine to fall places more strain on these nations.

rows.proposition: The UK should increase military support to Ukraine in response to the 2022 Russian invasion.
rows.text: We have not looked after our homeless, our people who live in poverty, those who have to use food banks to eat. You cannot save others when your own ship is sinking, its very stupid and causes the rest of the ship to want to commit mutiny. Also we risk being nuked by Russia, as a very small island, it would be pretty stupid to carry on giving aid for a never ending conflict.
