rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text: The monarchy is an unfair system and should be replaced with an elected president. It continues an unfair system of hereditary succession, which is out-dated and expensive to maintain. The number of members of the monarchy receiving tax payers money is too many and the royal family should work to earn their keep. By working they can relate to ordinary working people and be respected as such. The cost to tax payers at a time of recession is unacceptable and should be changed. By opening up their residences to the public more money would come into the country from foreign visitors and make a big difference to the finances of the country.

rows.proposition: The UK should abolish the monarchy.
rows.text:
The royal family are a rather outdated institution that props up a hierarchy that does not benefit the majority. They belong to the upper-elites who only serve their own self-interests. This is an institution that should be abolished as it does not serve the rest of the country.

The members of the royal family are often engaged in charity work, yet they own larges fortunes and large spaces of land. This could be used to serve the rest of the country. It's hypercritical to engage in such charity work when they could use their resources to help others.

They have a history of very poor conduct. They are involved with problematic figures and do not properly represent the interests of the British people. They should be more accountable to the people and behave in way that represents the UK in a better light.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: Climate change is a severe threat to human existence and we have to take steps to tackle it as best we can. Limiting carbon emissions will help the fight against this and I don't trust profit-driven industry to spontaneously introduce reasonable limits. I believe it falls to the legislature to introduce and enforce strict limits on carbon emissions, and I believe that by doing this the UK can provide a useful example to other countries. Hopefully they will then follow suit and introduce their own limits, not just so that UK industry remains competitive but so that the international community can use this method to combat climate change in concert.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: By capping carbon emissions, the UK could improve pollution levels as the amount of greenhouse gas being produced would be far less. I believe carbon emission caps allow an element of trading amongst companies i.e. you can trade the allowance that has been set to you. This can help encourage an environment where companies are all focused on low levels of carbon and may lead to more innovation. The Government could potentially make money through capping ,which would mean they could allocate this in other areas of the country where more funds are required. Companies may also need to create new technology that can help cut emissions which is good for the UK.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
-Immediate action is needed to turn the tide on climate change.  For too long the human race has operated unchecked, and now we're starting to see the consequences of our actions.  It's imperative we turn this around, otherwise we face extinction.

- The richest and most powerful people are driven only by greed and the acquisition of absurd levels of wealth; as such they propagate lies and distractions to keep the general populace in a state of compliance and denial.  Is it not farcical that we unite in uproar when soup is splashed over the Perspex screen protecting a work of art , yet we happily enable acts of far, far greater damage on a daily basis, to the planet that houses these works of art and, is pivotal to not only our continued enjoyment of said works, but our ability to live at all!

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: The over riding positive to be taken from this initiative would be the overall benefit of reduce emissions for the environment/planet. There is clear benefits presently and for the future to reduce global warming which is made worse by carbon emissions. Companies that produce the most carbon emissions should be accountable for the potential damage they are doing and try to make improvements but having consequences for exceeding a target/cap. Air pollution in general would improved if there was a limit on carbon emissions and all communities would benefit now from cleaner air and it would go a way to positively impact environment.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
Again, I understand only a little about this subject.  As I understand it, the government have already set a cap within a set timeframe to some companies, for example, the motor industry.  In this example, I believe that all new cars will be hybrid or fully electric in the future, and carbon emissions cap has been set for 10 years time in the UK.  I do not know the rules internationally.  

Although this will help the environment and climate change, ultimately the consumer will have to spend more and learn how to use hybrid or electric cars.  This can be daunting for many drivers.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: This is one of the most difficult dilemas that the world currently faces.  To cap or not to cap emissions.  There is evidence from some quarters that this must be done to help prevent global warming and rising tides, whereas other emminent scientific leaders say that it is not necessary and that the planet goes through periods of high and lower temperatures.  Whatever the final consideration is based on,  the lives and living standards of people that this may affect and thier own personal wellbeing must be taken into account as by closing high carbon producing industries, it could affect thier future unless more 'green' employment is available.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: This a complex and divisive matter. It is yet another subject where there seems to be a split of completely different views. I believe the fossil fuel lobby are driving negative reports on the subject of climate change, promulgating stories which suggest that the cost of energy will increase dramatically if net-zero policies are introduced. The UK could be in a position to introduce legislation which will show the world that carbon emissions could be reduced without a negative effect on the economy. We owe it to our children to ensure there is a habitable planet left when we are gone.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: I understand the reasoning behind the pros and cons of this statement, but in my opinion I am not sure if this would solve the world's emission problems as many big countries around the world do not shre the same concern over rising climate change. Pushing companies towards adopting more greener technologies is a part of the aim to promote cleaner energy and sutainable practices. However, this can lead to higher operational costs which would then negatively impact the consumer with higher purchasing costs. Not to mention the negative effect it would have on people's livelihoods as it would create major job losses.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text:
I will assume that the primary goal would be cleaner air, water and pollution free transportation also cleaner feed for the growth of crops and meats for human consumption. This will benefit future generations more than those of us living now.
In the long run the costs to industry would reduce but the public may suffer in the way of increased bills, possible power cuts and shortages during the transition, and they may even suffer job insecurity as they are replaced by a more economic system of deliverance over various industries.  
Ultimately it would protect ecosystems, public health, and future generations from the worst impacts of climate change

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: I think the UK should implement a carbon emissions cap for all industries, but it needs to be done carefully. Climate change is a serious issue, and some kind of mandatory limit is necessary to reduce environmental damage and push companies toward greener practices. A cap would also encourage investment in cleaner technologies, which could benefit the economy in the long run. However, its important that the cap isn't too strict too quickly. Otherwise, it could damage small businesses, raise costs, or lead to job losses. The approach should be flexible, with different targets based on industry size and capacity, so the environmental goals are met without creating unnecessary economic harm.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: Climate change is a worldwide crisis and it is vital that the UK leads the way in finding ways that we can work to halt and even reverse what was is happening. Further, carbon caps mean that companies will be encouraged to develop greener ways to produce energy. This will create jobs in research and development and other areas. Patents could be developed that would be very valuable. Overall, it could be a way for the UK to become a world leader in an area that will become very important as the century progresses. This is why I believe this.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: Implementing a mandatory carbon emission cap for all industries in the UK would support national climate targets. A cap would help the UK keep its promise and protect the environment. Businesses would be encouraged to adapt to cleaner practices and sustainability. The government should help those industries with clear rules and limits, and check to make sure companies follow them. A cap would help reduce pollution, support green jobs, encourage innovation and improve public health. Lower emissions means cleaner air, cooler temperatures and reduced climate disruptions. Without a cap some industries may continue high emissions  while other sacrifice, creating unfairness.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: For the sake of our descendants, it's essential to avoid a complete collapse of the global ecology. Their very lives depend on this! Climate change is inevitable, but the current rate of climate change is threatening global agriculture, which depends on a level of predictability that is already beginning to be in serious doubt. The main driver of climate change is the rising level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, due mainly to the oxidation of geologically stored carbon - fossil fuels - but also to release of methane from permafrost and submarine clathrates, as a secondary effect of global warming. Drastically reducing carbon emissions is the main action industry can take to mitigate this, but whether a cap is the best way to achieve this may be questionable.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.
rows.text: The idea itself is great. We all know carbon emissions are mostly caused big companies and industries. However, the statement is a little too blanket and needs to be thought through more carefully. Reducing carbon emissions will come at a cost for companies, cost that the billionaires behind can most definitely afford but will try to make the consumer pay anyway, potentially lowering the wages of their workers. This needs to be prevented. Second, it wouldn't be a bad idea to evaluate each industry separately instead of having a blanket carbon emissions cap for all of them. Essentially, it's about making sure the policy doesn't negatively affect the majority of the population financially.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.

It is just unnecessary regulation to me.

My second concern and this is probably my greatest concern is the having mandatory carbon emission regulations put in place for UK business it will immediately negatively impact these UK business compared to foreign businesses.  The cost being born by the UK businesses will not be the same for foreign business and in a tough international global market place this is not acceptable.

rows.proposition: The UK should implement a mandatory carbon emissions cap for all industries.

There should be joint responsibility and support rather than mandatory targets. The state should impose as little as possible, individuals need to be accountable for what can realistically be achieved at certain time periods.
The economy and getting people back in work should be the priority over capping emissions. There is over-reliance on being told what to do rather than making sound decisions for oneself. This is not the right time to enforce limits and companies mood towards this is unlikely to positive.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I do strongly agree that there should be an introcduction to learning about the LGBTQ+ community at primary school level. Children should understand, from an early age, that not all people are the same with regards their sexuality (in addition to ethnicity, religious beliefs etc). My experience of children of this age group id that they are more accepting of people who are different to them, and by not talking about the LGBTQ+ community there is a risk of stigmatisation. In a class of children, it is likely that a significant number are likely to either have friends or family who could be classed as LGBTQ+. I would, however, be wary of introducing more of the sexualised nature of the subject at an early age, in the same way I would be wary of introducing straight sexualised content,

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Teaching children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school can help build a kinder, more inclusive society. Kids are naturally curious and open minded, so introducing ideas about different types of families and people at a young age can help prevent stereotypes and bullying before they take root. It also means that children with LGBTQ+ parents or those questioning their own identity feel seen and valued, rather than isolated. Of course, this teaching needs to be age appropriate, no one's suggesting complex discussions, but simple messages about respect, diversity, and kindness. It's about helping children understand that everyone deserves to be treated fairly, no matter who they are or who they love. Ultimately, it's a small step that can make a big difference in shaping a more accepting future.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.

One being it helps promote respect meaning it teaches them about how they need to understand families and individuals come in different forms and helps to reduce prejudice and bullying from a younger age. Also, it has a big reflect of real life and modern society meaning children may already live or know someone in the LGBTQ+ community and it helps children to diverse the society that we live in. It massively helps prevent bullying and bad mental health which is a huge aspect.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I think that children should be taught about the LBTQ society early in life and in schools.  LBTQs are part of society these days and are subject to prejudice in society.  IF we teach children about these issues early in life then hopefully they will grow up without such prejudices.  This would benefit all of society.  Prejudice is a cause of great harm in our society and anything we can do to stop this in the future will have great benefits.  Of course there's the parents of children like this with prejudice already that won't like their children learning about this in schools.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I would hope that the UK would be able to find a way to positively teach children about the LGBTQ+ in primary school. Teaching them about adults live, in all the many different variations, in an honest and open manner, is a good thing. Hopefully by teaching them early, they will become used to it, and as they grow up they will not see it as anything other than normal. With that in mind, hopefull, it will also help lessen the stigma around the LGBTQ+ community too. Another benefit of teaching kids about the community is that, in time some of the children may realise that they are part of the community too, it may help them as well.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Teaching children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school is important because it promotes inclusion and respect from an early age. When children learn about different types of people and families, they are more likely to treat others with kindness and understanding, which helps prevent bullying and discrimination. It also reflects the real diversity of society, as many children have LGBTQ+ relatives, or may one day identify as LGBTQ+ themselves, and seeing their lives represented in the classroom can make them feel valued and accepted. Early education also builds empathy and social awareness, helping children grow into open-minded and respectful individuals. Finally, learning about LGBTQ+ identities supports mental health and well-being by showing that everyone deserves love, respect, and the freedom to be themselves. By teaching these values early, schools can help create a more inclusive and supportive environment for all students.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I don't know what the current age is for children to learn sex education in school, but it seems more appropriate to do this early on in secondary school, and include LGBTQ+ within that. I question why children need to learn about sexual preference when it is not something they would naturally be interested in. Given that there are sensitivities within different communities about this, whether they are very traditional, religious or are not in favour of it for personal reasons, it would seem wise not to include this. It feels more appropriate to allow parents to discuss with their children at home if they feel it's appropriate or necessary.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Many people feel that they need to be part of a community. When it is a niche community they feel a sense of belonging. In this case there are in my opinion some very damaging ideas in this community which can effect someone's whole life in a negative way. Its not right to expose young children to this risk. Besides this, children of primary school age are too young to make such decisions as many have not hit puberty yet and there ideas have not been developed. They need to experience more of life before we force this ideology down their throats in my opinion.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I dont think children in primary school should be taught about the LGBTQ community as i feel they are too young to grasp and understand what its really about. I think they should be taught it later in life, when they reach high school, along with when they get taught sex education. I believe some parents will also disagree with the teachings of this and could cause conflict between parents and the school if they are forcing children to learn this topic. It should be left to the parents to teach this to their children if they wish to do so.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: Children should be able to enjoy childhood without being lectured about sex at an early age. The subject should be introduced gradually and sensitively, only with the parent's express wishes. It should primarily be about sexual reproduction. It should not be taught without their express consent. Teaching about LGBTQ+ is putting the cart before the horse. These are complex and difficult subjects which a child does not need to know about in any detail until they are at least ten years old (and even then, only at a surface level). There have been many examples of four year olds being taught (indoctrinated) about masturbation, gay sex, read to by transvestite men dressed in women's clothes, coached that they may be be trans, without their parents knowledge or consent. This is disgraceful.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: I believe hat primary school kids are too young to actually understand a complex topic like this. There are too many other things that can be promoted like having good manners, respect and kindness. I believe this discussion should be handled by parents and when the kid grows up, they can choose their own path. If you introduce these type of topics too early in schools, especially in primary schools. It can create mass confusion for kids when they are growing up as this is the last thing we want as parents that are kids stressing up this topic. When they can be learning about much more important topics, which will help them in the future. Overall, i don't agree with this topic taught in primary school as it can lead to the kid stressing out due to the topic being so complex and personal.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: There should be no distinction between different communities when it comes to the rights and equalities before the law, if one person is being discriminated against for an unjust reason then the entirety of society is too. Children should learn that basic fundamental principle and then get a better understanding of the background of our society which makes it so difficult for some people to follow that principle. Hopefully this will not be an issue come the future generations once they have developed as the homophobic/bigoted individuals will have died out or not had as much influence in our political discourse

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: It is fairly important to teach children about this topic as it is increasingly relevant in today's society. It raises awareness of the topic and helps with their understanding of this community. it will give them a broader perspective on different types of sexuality and how we treat individuals who may seem a little different to ourselves. It will hopefully make them see things from different perspectives and challenge some of the prejudices which they may have been bought up with. It would be more helpful if the children were older primary school children rather than younger ones who may find it a little confusing.

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: The UK is an open and democratic society, and therefore, in the first instance it should not be the case that a certain issue or topic "of the day" is excluded from teaching curriculums, based on the whims of the government. Education, as a public service, should be there to support and engage all members of society. To that extent, any attempt to block or otherwise obfuscate the teaching about this group is inappropriate and goes against the moral fabric of the UK - that being an open and accepting society. It should also be considered that the exclusion of teaching about one group may prejudice other groups (both positively and negatively).

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.
rows.text: personally when i was at school we was not taught about LGBTQ+ community as i believe sometimes when you are taught things at a very young age you can be very heavily influenced , however i do not believe that they should not not be taught about it i just feel there should be an option for if children if they would like to learn about it therefore it is up to them if they would like to learn more about the community and topic , however i feel all subjects and teaching at school should be this way . optional

rows.proposition: The UK should teach children about the LGBTQ+ community in primary school.

This helps make it more comfortable for people who are in this community. However, they should teach it because it promotes love, kindness and inclusivity which are important things it also makes everyone feel seen and it is very inclusive. helps people understand why people are part of the community and also the history of this community and group with is again inclusive and part of history.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Scotland should become an independent country because it deserve the right to self determination. Decision about the economy, healthcare and education should be made by those directly accountable to the scottish people, not by a UK government often led my parties with limited scottish support. The brexit vote highlighted a key division. Scotland overwhelming wanted to remain in the EU, yet was pulled out by the broader UK vote. This disconnect underscores why independence is essential to protect scotland's democratic vote. Moreover, independence would empower scotland to manage its own vast natural resources and social systems in ways that reflects its unique values and priorities.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think that Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom. We have seen that Scotland is not allowed to have its own voice while under the United Kingdom government and while there has been devolution, it hasn't provided enough ability for Scotland to make its own decisions. Scotland has most of the UK's green energy but still pays the highest prices especially in the north. We have the ability to be a self sustaining country but the UK government demands its share rather than making their own improvements and unfairly distributing finances collected from the Scottish Government.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
Scotland already as different rules already so I do not see any objection to it becoming an independant country seperate from the UK.
Scottish people pay their taxes at different levels to people in England plus council tax is different as well, together with rules for prescriptions being different.
The Scottish public I feel would want another referendum since I believe that the majority of Scots would welcome a seperate Scotland. When ever I have spoken to friends in Scotland or holidayed there the Scots people would like to be seperate from England and do not want to be part of the UK.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I can understand the intent behind this request as it gives Scotland a chance to move forward independently.  The country has always maintained a nationalist stance and its residents have edged towards independence from the UK albeit not as a majority.  The country has a long history of conflict with England in particular and this has given even more support towards devolution from the United Kingdom.  With the recent vote to leave the EU many will think that this is now the next logical step for the Scottish electorate and will provide further strength and support of the voters within the country.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Potential economic benefit: proponents argue that an independent Scotland could lead to greater self-determination, allowing the nation to tailor its economic and social policies to its unique needs and cultural identity. this could potentially lead to economic benefits by focusing on specific industries and resources. However, independence also presents significant challenges. An independent Scotland would need to establish its own international relations, currency, and economic systems, which could lead to economic instability and uncertainty in the short term. the potential benefit of greater self-governance must be carefully weighed against the risks of economic and political disruption. Scotland will benefit but also suffer some consequences.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: The right to self determination is a basic concept. It was promoted and applied extensively after the First World War and the defeat of the empires that constituted the Central Powers. But interestingly, only in a manner that suited the victorious powers. Nationhood for the Czechoslovaks and Latvians but not for Mesopotamia, where there was oil. Which brings us to Scotland. Not so long ago the rest of the UK would have been acutely aware of the loss of North Sea oil revenues. In a post-carbon fuel world the impact of North Sea oil is becoming increasingly less relevant to the debate.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
I maintain a neutral stance regarding Scotland's potential independence from the UK as a separate nation. 
The matter presents multiple complex elements that stem from political aspects and historical background and cultural factors. The available information does not provide sufficient understanding of how Scotland and the rest of the UK would be affected of by independence. The debate between self-determination and local control coexists alongside concerns about economic stability and international relations. I choose to remain neutral because both sides present convincing arguments until I conduct additional research. Finally, I think it would be good if the bout side of this statement decides on the final decision.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
I understand that the people in Scotland can feel far removed geographically from the British government and in much the same way that those people who chose to support Brexit, they probably feel they will have more agency  if governing themselves. I have no objection to them choosing to be independent if that is what the majority want.

I would however like to know more about how this would work in reality. They currently receive a lot of funding from the current government, how will it work when they are independent will the countries taxes be enough for them to be independent? I also wonder how long it will take for them to be independent will it need to be a gradual process?

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I believe if Scotland becomes an independent country  from the United Kingdom they will be highly affected because Scotland benefits from the UK  and a few examples are lower taxes, higher public spending having access to the UK markets they receive more public spending per capita. part of the UK levelling up initiative as they receive support on projects and infrastructure its will also affecting there economy as it will possibly face potential challenges in the areas like the currency and establishing new risks to public finances and challenges to make strategic decisions about in the future which can be a major risk to them

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
Scotland has been part of the United Kingdom since the Act of Union in 1707 which merged both parliaments. Whilst devolution has provided a Scottish parliament, it does not have the power over defence and foreign relations.

For over 300 years having Scotland be part of the United Kingdom has resulted in such things as the nuclear submarine  fleet based in Scotland, as well as vital detection systems monitoring the North Sea. As Brexit has shown, having a border causes delays in logistics and increase in paperwork does not help businesses, in fact it hinders it.

What does independence achieve? In today's world, nothing.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think it would create more of a divide, us and them. We are the UNITED kingdom and I think we should stay united. Look at all the issues since Brexit. This would be like another small scale version of that. It could also cause issues when visiting and travelling. Different policies, different laws etc. They would probably start charging toll fees to enter Scotland and vice versa. I do think that the Government HQ being in London seems out of touch to the Scottish citizens and more consideration should be given to up North! That is the North of England and Scotland.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Most people, in Scotland and England, want to keep the United Kingdom together.  There was a referendum not long ago and people voted to keep the status quo.  The current set-up, with a Scottish parliament and leader, keeps most people happy and ensures that the people of Scotland can (up to a point) make their own decisions.  It is hard to see who would benefit from a separate Scotland, other than lawyers drawing up endless new agreements for the independent country's relationships and trade agreements with the rest of the world.  Many people from Scotland relocate to England and if the countries were separate they would lose that option.  The people of England and Scotland have more in common than the devolutionists suggest.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: If Scotland leaves the Union it will mean we will not be able to use the same currency as the UK and we'll need to start our own new currency or use the Euro.  I'm not sure how the Euro would work if we're not part of the EU now and would the EU allow us to rejoin as a smaller country.  On another note, is Scotland going to be able to afford being independent?  At the moment we are devolved and are given money from the UK government but this would stop if we left.  In my opinion I think we are better sticking being part of the UK and I have always thought that.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text:
It's a difficult one.  I understand why many Scottish people want independence but it may be very problematic. For example, what would be their currency.  Would international financial markets accept Scottish sovereignty?  Would they not be greatly overshadowed by the much larger English economy?
We live in an international world where there is very significant interdependence of nations.  Is there really a fure for nationalism and in particular Scottish nationalism?
The SNP has been in power for over a decade and its track record has been quite poor.  Corruption, higher taxation and poor educational achievement are just some of its failings.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Scotland is rather reliant on the rest of the UK for funding, I believe this is known as a "block grant" which is a ring-fenced amount of money given by Westminster. The impact of no longer receiving this might be challenging for the Scottish people, who could see price increases and a lower quality of life as a result.  The United Kingdom is better when we are just that - United - we share a long cultural history with Scotland, and whilst some of it may be less than pleasant, it does exist and is totally relevant today. As an independent nation, Scotland would lose much of its voice - when combined with the rest of the United Kingdom, it has its views heard much more clearly.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: Scotland like England and Wales should not become an independent country. All nation states of the union benefit from the current status quo and the UK citizens support the current situatiuon. This was put to a vote of the Scottish people back in 2014, I think, and the idea was rejected. If we look across the world there are a number of nationalist parties who appear to have one central theme, that be to split from their neighbours and friends for no real reason and no obvious benefits. The benefits of the union include tourism, joint defence, foreign aid and a united front to the world.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think Scotland should become independent because it would mean that a lot of decisions that are made by the UK government now wouldn't focus on Scotland.  If Scotland were independent, they could make fairer policies to support workers, improve healthcare, and invest in education for children and uni students. This would help reduce inequality and make life better for more people in Scotland. Also being independent would mean Scotland would be able to push harder on green policies without interference from UK politics that might prioritise other interests. Climate change is a huge issue, and they have a lot of potential to lead in renewable energy I think.

rows.proposition: Scotland should become an independent country, separate from the United Kingdom.
rows.text: I think that by becoming seperate and independent from the UK, we could face challenges in economic growth, and this could lead to economic decline, as we would have to rely on funding services such as the NHS as well as increasing salaries across Scotland. This would be difficult, because salaries in England are generally higher than in Scotland. We would need to increase job opportunities. Moreover, we would have lack of support from the EU due to brexit as well as, now the rest of the UK, if this were to happen. Similarly, the export and import of goods and products such as food would become more expensive and costly as we wouldn't be part of the UK anymore, and would have to consider things such as import tax or more expensive flight tickets across the country. However, it can be noted that becoming a country could help us focus and priotize ourselves and Scotland more.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
'Sustainability' is to a large extent a con. Councils mandate recycling and sorting of waste, then councils throw much of it into a landfill anyway. A lot of other waste is shipped off to the third world and burnt. It hasn't helped the environment one jot yet it costs a lot of money for the British taxpayer, and funds a whole industry that have built up around sustainability, and that is one of the main purposes. The other being to meet artificial targets that, once, again, don't make any difference to the world environment.

We need to bring taxes down and ease the cost of living burden on people, not pursue madcap schemes that make no difference to the environment.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
If you take the statement at face value, it's something I would agree with. Anything that reduces waste should be seen as a positive.

However, the majority of councils in the UK seem to have debt issues. We're currently seeing council tax rises of between 5-10% per annum being rolled out across the country. My worry with the statement stems from where the money is going to come from to plan and then implement these comprehensive sustainability initiatives.

Realistically, the money isn't there to be carry out such a plan. Therefore, to afford such an initiative, it's likely that councils would have to pass on the extra costs to the tax players. That would drive up the cost of council bills at a time when households budgets are already stretched.

Therefore, overall, I probably wouldn't be in favour of such a mandate at the present time.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I can see that this statement has positive and negative drawbacks.  By mandating all councils ensure consistent and effective environmental action across the country, ensures everyone is driving the progress towards the national sustainability goals. It helps all to foster the innovation and then sharing best practices amongst councils, however all councils to all align with the same initiatives and protocols may have a negative impact for peoples needs and priorities at a local level. Councils are already on a tight budget, so this could lead to inefficient resource allocation and have negative consequences on local communities that may rely on certain council programs, that could be taken away with a mandate like this.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I believe that any major initiatives should be done at a national level. I don't think councils have the resources or the knowledge to effectively implement anything. I can see this as a reason why councils will put up council tax bills but in reality the money will be used elsewhere. There is the potential for green washing in that they say the problem is worse than it is to try to extort more money from the tax payer. I think in today's cost of living crisis the tax payer's money could be better used for other purposes rather than individual local projects which tick the right the box but don't actually do anything.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Sustainability initiatives always sound that they are a good idea.  In a lot of cases measures such as these come with an increased cost to the local taxpayer. It should also be considered if these initiatives are beneficial to the local area and indeed, if they are even needed.  there is so much that can be read on initiatives such as these that there is always evidence both for and against them.  Finally, funding if agreed upon by all parties should be considered.  there may be some people that would find it difficult to help finance such measures and these considerations should always be taken into account before implementation.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I think it seems a good idea for all local councils to implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives and I would support this, if it were affordable.  I worry about the feasibility of councils doing this and I worry about the affordability of such action.  I wish that central government could afford to fund sustainability strategies throughout the UK and I could not support this action if it meant a big rise in council tax, because the cost of living is already a struggle for many families.  I support increased recycling initiatives and councils sourcing as many recycled consumables as they can afford.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
it is all very well the government setting out a nationwide mandate but its just not that simple.  most councils are reducing spending at the moment due to government cuts.  If the cost could be paid by central government im sure all councils would like to improve their sustainability.  It probably something that everyone wants.  But the cost of implementation would more than likely exceed any savings that could be made.
if there could possibly be an investigation to prove that implementing safety inititive would be value for money it would be an easier challeng for the councils to convert their people.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Councils are short of funding to employ staff and run existing services, which are essential to residents. Many residents will not want other services cut, to focus on sustainable initiatives and won't want their taxes to increase to pay for it. However, we all need to do more to be sustainable and to look after the planet, so Council's should do their bit as well. Council's are supposed to do things to benefit residents and this would provide benefits. They are also seen as role models and their actions may affect how residents also do things at home. I think there is a balance and Council's should do more, but it needs to be balanced with cost.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: It is down to all of us on an individual level to be environmentally conscious and be aware. The councils should be aware pf environmentally friendly schemes and environmentally friendly initiatives. The council should lead the way, so have recycling centres, and some information about environmentally friendly transport schemes and any incentives available such as a reduction in fee for solar panels or heat pumps? They should partner with other schemes to help the public be more environmentally friendly. Thry should also educate the public as to how to save the environment and make people aware, if people are aware they can change there behaviour

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Local councils are in a better position than the national govt. to roll out effective sustainability policies that match local needs. They know the area, they know the the people and thus they can act faster. Sustainability isn't just about emissions targets - it directly affects things like public health, transport and cost of living. So it needs to be taken seriously at all levels. Getting councils involved helps hit national and global goals and if people see positive changes happening locally - cleaner air, better recycling, more green spaces - they're more likely to support and take part in it too. A nationwide mandate just makes sure that councils don't ignore it or keep pushing it down the list.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: To save our planet we all need to do our best to use sustainable actions. The world needs help to cut our pollution which is destroying seas, nature and our air. There is no reason why we should not use 'cleaner' fuels. Everyone should help clean up our mess for future generations. Although there would be an initial outlay to change things to more sustainable transport local councils could mandate that new  vehicles must be 'green' rather than renewing like for like. Recycling can actually make a profit for the council rather than costing more in taxes. It can also create jobs for the local area.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I believe this is important as councils are looked to as an example of what other stakeholders view as acceptable practice themselves and can then be judged by or argue against. Sustainability plans such as these are also essential for the stewardship of the borough the council resides in, improving the area and being seen to do so demistifies the council and may allow for the community locally to get involved and begin to care more about the environment themselves and gain a better understanding of why things are done the way they are to make more informed decisions in the future both in voting and personally.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I definitely agree with this statement as i think it is a really important issue that is affecting everybody globally. The uk should be the ones to make the change sooner rather than later. If local councils implement this, it will improve closeness to communities and therefore use that as an advantage to use the sustainability plans for more suited local needs. On top of this, by using local councils, they can manage things that mean sustainability will have an quicker impact on the public. For example, energy uses and public transport. It means that every council will have the opportunity to act and therefore have a better affect on the climate change crisis.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: We all have lots of vague ideas about sustainabilility but if it was done through local councils it would be very intentional. I think this is a great idea. It would be more measurable for research. You could compare places for where is doing well and why and then adjust it to do better. The local council can also have ideas on what it's people will do well and what is realistic for their ages and dynamics.  it could be more indivildualised and specific. Like some ideas for eg London it could be plastic bottles and pollution levels. Others could be farming and energy.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: every single person in every single council authority across the country is affected by climate change, which is potentially worsened by poor sustainability practices. why should somebody in the northernmost city in the country be affected by worse climate change just because the southernmost city has poor sustainability practices? in essence, the northerner is also paying for this, which brings me to my next point. To my understanding, local authority funding is set by the government. central government sets the funding so they should set the agenda too. were currently in a situation where tax money could be spent on something not voted for because the local authority has different policies yet enact these policies with central money.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: It should be able to be maintained without causing harm or depletion, A lot of people do not understand these things so a a workshop where people understand what sustainability is. It should be catorigised, social sustainability and enviroment protection, by protecting the diversity of different people. Looking at issues such as pollution and biodiversity.  Ensuring a fair and equitable access to resources and opportunites and a healthy enviroment to all people. Making people aware of the benefits of recycling, reducing pollution by walking or cycling to work and not driving.  or using public transport. Ethical consumption of foods and bevarages.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I don't agree with this policy as it is quite wishy washy.  It opens councils up to greater use of resources which could be more appropriately used elsewhere, such as housing.  I think it would also be difficult to really see the benefit of this tangibly.  It would prove difficult to justify as it would obviously require quite a lot of expense and may prove challenging to justify to residents.  Further, the term 'comprehensive' in this context would be highly subjective and open to a waste of bureaucracy resources.  I don't think it is a good initiative when there are issues of high importance.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text:
Environmental Protection: Implementing comprehensive sustainability initiatives at the local council level plays a vital role in safeguarding the environment. These efforts help preserve the local ecosystem, prevent the degradation of natural habitats and enhance air and water quality. By reducing pollution levels, such initiatives contribute significantly to public health and promote biodiversity. Cleaner environments lead to healthier communities and more resilient natural systems.

Economic Efficiency: Sustainable infrastructure and energy-efficient practices lead to significant long-term cost savings for both the council and residents. By reducing energy consumption, minimising waste, and optimising resource use, councils can lower their operational expenses. Additionally, these initiatives drive local green innovation, create employment opportunities and attract investments – stimulating the economy whilst protecting the environment.

Social Equity – Comprehensive sustainability policies support fair access to clean air, enery and green spaces, improving quality of life for all residents.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: Mandating comprehensive sustainability initiatives across all UK councils is essential for meeting national climate goals and securing a liveable future. Local councils are uniquely positioned to drive meaningful change in areas like housing, transport, waste management and green infrastructure. A national mandate would ensure cinsistent progress, preventing disparities between regions and reinforcing the UK's commitment to net zero targets. Moreover, many sustainability measures such as improving energy efficiency and promoting active travel offer long term financial savings and health benefits. While some councils already lead on sustainability, others lack the directive on incentive to act. A government backed requirement, paired with appropriate funding and support, would empower every local authority to contribute effectively. This approach ensures that environment action is not optional, but a shared national responsibility . By embedding sustainability into local governance the UK can build more resilient, equitable communities while tackling the urgent climate crisis.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate that all local councils implement comprehensive sustainability initiatives.
rows.text: I usually agree that the UK should mandate all councils to implement comprehensive sustainable initiatives. Climate change is a national and global issue, and local councils play a vital role in tackling it through waste management, transport planning, and green space protection. By requiring sustainability efforts across the board, the government can ensure a consistent, coordinated approach to reducing emissions and protecting the environment. Local authorities also understand their communities' needs and they can adapt national goals to local realities. However not all councils have equal resources or expertise so central government funding and guidance would be necessary to make this fair and achievable

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text:
When students analyse how women are portrayed in literature, they can learn about gender stereotypes and the impact that has on society as well as individuals... such as social structures and power dynamics in the workplace. Expanding the range of voices represented in the curriculum, which is predominantly male dominated, gives students a different view point in which to learn from.
By being exposed to information in this way, students will become more empowered to challenge the way things are in society and work towards a more equal world where women and girls no longer face historical and ongoing inequalities.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.

To learn about the feminist movement, its purpose, and the good that can come of it is important, but it should not be an opportunity for feminists to further a polarising agenda to young, impressionable minds.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.

I think the national curriculum should include literature from all eras which reflects how literature, culture and social issues have changed over the centuries.  While I accept it would difficult to do this I don't think there would ever be total agreement as to what should and shouldn't be included. rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: Mandating the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum is important for promoting gender equality and encouraging deeper critical thinking. Literature has been long dominated by male voices, and by studying texts through a feminist lens, students can better understand the often-overlooked contributions and experiences of women. This approach helps challenge traditional narratives and reveals how gender roles and expectations have shaped, and continue to shape, society. Including feminist perspectives also makes the curriculum more diverse and relatable by reflecting a wider range of human experiences. Furthermore, it prepares students to engage thoughtfully with contemporary issues around gender, identity, and equality. By examining how literature reflects and influences these themes, students gain a more well-rounded and socially aware education, equipping them with the tools to question injustice and promote fairness in their own lives

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.

rows.proposition: The UK should mandate the inclusion of feminist perspectives in the national literature curriculum.
rows.text: The heart of this is understanding what a curriculum is for - it is to inform and educate in a way that allows individuals to be aware of different perspectives, be able to weigh the views and formulate their own views. Steps that aim to control a curriculum beyond by mandating are a slippery slope to a situation in which particular ideologies are seen as preferred while others are excluded. It then becomes a short step to controlling what people are allowed to think. Curricula are about education and allowing people to experience information, some of which they may not like or be aware of, but in a way that does not suggest that there is a preferred aspect.

rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.
rows.text: An enlightened and free society recognises and protects all its citizens, especially those who are most vulnerable. Members of the transgender community are often targeted by intolerant and ignorant people, sometimes suffering physical harm and often ostracised and made to feel that they do not belong. It is important that the law recognises and offers protection to all forms of transgender identity, so that people can feel valued for who they are. Many cultures around the world shun the transgender community and the UK could set a good example, showing how tolerance to all can enrich our lives and make everyone feel safe.

rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.

rows.proposition: The UK should legally recognize and protect all forms of transgender identity.

There is no reason why not - it harms no-one and causes no actual problems (other than the ones that are caused by the opinion of other people). If a person wants to say that they are one thing or another, this should be their decision, not that of a distant authority.

They would also be subject to the same laws that govern the country they live in - therefore should they use their trans identity as an opportunity to commit crimes, they would be treated like any other citizen. rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: Marijuana is already an easily-accessible drug, and to legalise it's use will mean that supply of the drug can be regulated and controlled. It should be considered in the same way that alcohol is: those who sell alcohol for consumption on premises must have a license for both the individual and the premises, as well as delegated authority to those also authorised to sell. This will mean that sale can only be approved to adults on prove of age. Regulations can also be put in place to standardise the product, therefore creating a safer product to consume for the user.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: The Government could raise millions if marijuana was legalised, regulated and taxed, similar to alcohol and tobacco. This could fund public services, as it has done in many American states since it has been legalised there. Currently, all this money goes to lining the pockets of criminal gangs and funding serious organised crime. Police would no longer have to spend time on minor drug offences and could concentrate on more serious crimes and Class A drug dealers. People who smoke cannabis would no longer be fearful of breaking the law and receiving a criminal record for possession of small amounts of it.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: The stigma that marijuana use is for low-life, good-for-nothing people in society would be removed and its social status would be elevated. Also, it could bring revenue to the government, create jobs and save money that is meant to be used to prosecute users could go towards improving public services. It also helps to treat and alleviate some chronic illnesses and diseases. As a natural product, this treatment would come under traditional/natural healing and would be effective. It will no longer be associated with drug addicts and the less desirable people in society, but would be used by the elite as well.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text:
By legalising marijuana for recreational use the government could have more of a control on the quality and quantity of marijuana and therefore this would free up a burden of the legal justice system in dealing with those illegally selling it. If the market was controlled by government then it could be taxed and make money for the government. Legalisation would also give people freedom to choose much in the same way as alcohol and tobacco is used. 

However there are many negatives, such as medical side effects from using marijuana that are not fully known which would lead to higher use of NHS services if it was more freely available.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.

Would it be sold in shops alongside cigarettes and if so would the state be collecting ting the tax and become the new drug dealer. rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: Legalising marijuana for recreational use could cause an increase in people struggling with addiction, as marijuana is known to be a gateway drug. It is likely that more people would feel comfortable trying marijuana if it was legal, causing more people to experiment with drugs. This can lead to trialling additional substances that are known to be highly addictive and illegal in individuals pre-disposed to addiction. There is also a body of scientific research that links marijuana consumption to damaged brain cells, which can have a negative impact on a person's cognition, particularly if marijuana consumption starts when the brain is still developing (e.g., in adolescence). Marijuana being more widely accessible could also have implications for drug related injuries relating to drug driving, but also as a consequence of unpredictable mental health impacts (e.g., paranoia).

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.

I think that alcohol consumption can be just as harmful and its too late to make any changes there but I don't feel like we need to cause more issues by introducing legal Marijuana use too.  There are not enough studies to show long-term effects.
