You never see a lairy stoner! 

I do feel it should be illegal to smoke and drive, reactions will not be fast.

I understand it can be considered a gateway drug to other things, and I get that is bad - but the drug itself is harmless if used safely (ie not in charge of children, or vehicles etc etc)

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: It should be legalized because it is being widely used by a large part of the UK population anyway, if it was legalized then the Government could get taxes off it and they could be able to more regulate what people are smoking and the amount that people could buy could be limited to prevent overdosing. The health benefits are also important as it has been shown to help people who suffer from mental health problems such as anxiety and stress, even though it can be prescribed the NHS can be slow in getting results so people being able to buy it would result in lower mental health cases

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise marijuana for recreational use.
rows.text: I believe that it is something that should be explored by the UK government and other countries should be looked at beforehand to understand the pros and cons of such a law. I think that it is something that could be implemented with guidance and advice on how to use the drug in ways which are safe. Alcohol is a legal drug which can have similar effects to marijuana and some say is more dangerous and addictive. It is definitely a viable law which I could see being taken into action in the next ten years of so in the United Kingdom.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:
I have mixed feelings on this topic as I love BBC programmes on TV and particularly on radio, and this institution has been part of my life from early childhood so hold a lot of nostalgia for me. I am still a devotee of Radio 4 and never miss an episode of The Archers. I like the values that The BBC stands for, it's longevity , the lack of disruptive advertising and it's international standing and appeal.
 However, in a changing world with so many people now accessing and paying for different streaming options and with so many other channels available on TV and radio, I can understand the resentment felt at this increasingly expensive charge being levied and the calls for it to be funded differently.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC has been infamous for the protection of very prolific criminals, whose acts were known by executives and brushes aside for the sake of making money. Also the spread of certain propaganda, one of which being the demonization of the working class population for other working class citizens to gawp, laugh or scold at. One example being the Miner's Strike during Margaret Thatcher's time as Prime Minister, in which miners who were trying to afford a decent wage in order to feed their families were made out to be hooligans who attacked the police as well as violent protestors.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC remains a prestigious and highly respected brand and though it may need financial support the last thing that it needs is further governmental involvement. As an independent it wields considerable influence and it is trusted by governments elsewhere as well as by individuals who look to it for unbiased reporting. It may be inevitable that individual producers, presenters and other key figures have their own political or ideological viewpoints but there are checks and balances in place to ensure that accountability is high. Even those with strong philosophical leanings also hold ethical principles and in the main this is fairly reflected in the BBC's output. Other media outlets would do well to learn much from it.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I feel sometimes the BBC does not report accurately news from all over the world and sometimes it feels biased in terms of who they are supporting at the given time, and when they get a bit of backlash they will start reporting unbiasedly. I have noticed that sometimes they do not even cover a certain topic. I think the BBC should represent what the people in UK believe in and not just the majority but also the minorities to give different aspects. It feels as if they only cover certain aspects of news or documentaries depending on what the producers believe in or who fund them.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I don't know much about this issue and am uncertain as to the meaning of the statement.  The BBC has generally been held in high regard and held an important place in UK society. In recent years it has been involved in scandals (ie. Jimmy Saville) and is likely less respected than it once was. It is also needed and watched less, due to streaming services and a revolution in the way people access entertainment. I really don't know if tax-payers' money would be better spent elsewhere, but am inclined to believe it still provides good value for money and holds an important place in the lives of many.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:
If the funding is appropriate then there is no reason to defund it as we do need media to understand what's going on. 
But if funding can be reduced and use it in other departments which is much mire needed like health, housing, education, etc then they should definitely take action. That said defunding and reducing funding are different things. There should be clear reason and evidence to support why they are considering or trying to defund or reduce funding which should be clearly stated and voted with public. As BBC has its name for the longest and oldest standing media and do have reputation and trust of people.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I really don'y know how I feel about this one. When I used to watch television back in the 'olden days' when  there were only a few channels, I would have definitely said - No to the BBC being defunded, because it was good that we had two channels that didn't show adverts. And the money they got was put to good use, making good tv content. But these days, I don't have SKY anymore, and always watch streaming services. If the BBC didn't get funded and had to have adverts, it might be a good thing because they might have more money to make better programmes.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: We are all forced into buying a T.V licence. That seems crazy to me in this day and age. The BBC should start having adverts and self fund through that, the same as the other mainstream channels. I pay for Netflix etc and when I go abroad I can still watch it. However, even though I pay for a licence, I cannot watch the BBC channels when I am abroad. How do they get away with that. I should be able to watch something that I pay for wherever and whenever I like. Also I think it's disgusting that people are criminalised for not getting a licence.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:
I don't feel any media entity should be government funded. It removes too much choice from the public and means we are being pushed to consume something we're not necessarily interested in.
I feel the TV licence should be opt in as opposed to having to declare. I personally don't watch any bbc channels or catchup and I like to get my news updates from a variety of sources rather than just one that has been thrust upon me.
I also find the BBC to feel very dated, strict, tiresome and feel there are better alternatives we could be using

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: Commercial TV is driven by the requirements of the funders and will not nessecarily align with the requirements of the viewers. Also there is a degree of infrastructure that a national broadcast organisation is required to support and this also needs to have a predictable and assured source. Defunding the BBC would result is a pure commercial focus on broadcast and so creativity is likely to be subsumed in favour of populist desires - I for one have no idea for 24/7 reality tv shows and the like. There is also the danger of political interference is news and media.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: In the great scheme of things, given the state of the country and the problems we are dealing with (e.g. unemployment, social care crisis, NHS, policing), this issue is irrelevant. However, I would say that, at least by funding the BBC through the licence system, it means that at least we have a couple of channels on the TV that are not a constant bombardment of adverts for alcohol and gambling (both of which surely place additional pressures on our fragile NHS). Energy should be put towards stricter controls on advertising. However, when it comes to the licence, it should be free for everyone over the age of 75, regardless of income.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC is not good value for money.  It spends far too much on inflated salaries for people - e.g. Gary Linnekar.  There are too many unneccessary jobs/positions for example diversity, quality and inclusion when other countries are able to access this for free without having to pay a licence fee like ourselves.  Whilst I do not like having to watch all the adverts etc on other Channels why should we be forced to pay a licence fee if we don't watch it.  We do not watch BBC without our household for these very reasons and we see the current licence fee basically as an unneccessary tax.  It simply is not acceptable.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text:
Why should people to have to continue to pay the licence fee when they do not watch it.
 I think some of their reporting of the troubles in Gazza has been appalling. They have clearly been in support of Hamas who are a terrorist group.
 And licence payers have been paying the wages of some of the most depraved humans for many many years while the BBC has tried it's best to cover up the actions of it's employees. The BBC doesn't have a place in the would we live in right now. So funding should end as soon as possible.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: yes the BBC should not be funded by the tv licence, we don't get any say in what is put on live TV, we should not have to pay for a tv channel that is simply just to be allowed to watch live tv, there are so many other streaming platforms now that the BBC does not seem relevant. The bbc should run like other channels and have advertising, literally the only benefit of the tv licence is the privelege of not watching ad breaks during shows. I don't mind watching adds if it meant not paying a TV licence.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: BBC broadcast content that is loved by the people. It is not a profit driven media but produce content that serves the interest of people. It has high reputation and is recognized internationally. It provide content that are accurate reliable and in depth something that will be lost should UK decide to defund it. BBC provide equal access to information. They provide content that cuts across from educational politics documentaries and thus promoting culture and diversity. Their covering is wide and the commercial broadcasters cannot meet the requirement of their audience. It has a high number of audience and therefore defunding it will be unfair.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: Personally I feel that the TV licence is a suitable way for funding the BBC. It is something that is 'uniquely british' and by having government involvement it ensures that the BBC remains neutral and unbiased. While some believe this isnt the case, I personally can see how the BBC takes steps to ensure they remain unbiased. Departments like 'BBC verify' ensure that while the BBC News may not be the first to report breaking news, they ensure that they do not publish misinformation. I do not feel that the BBC would be the same if it was funded by advertising or subscriptions.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: The BBC is internationally respected for producing content that adheres to high journalistic standards, which is made possible partly by its public funding. This journalistic integrity might be lost if it were defunded and acquired by, say, a private company with no interest in maintaining journalistic standards. Public funding also allows the BBC to invest in local talent and arts that might not be otherwise considered commercially viable (e.g., by big corporations like Amazon and Netflix, which are interested primarily in making money, unlike the BBC in its current, publicly funded incarnation). This investment might not be possible if it were defunded.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: I think that while the BBC has faults, it is overall something to protect. It is a British institution and has produced some of the finest drama and comedy entertainment that the UK is known for across the world. If the BBC did not exist it would further concentrate all media being owned by a small group of American companies. The BBC also produces documentaries and niche content that others do not because there would be no commercial interest to. I feel there is some issues with the BBC's news coverage and how it so strongly favours the establishment and government, and if this was addressed the BBC as an institution would once again be very popular in the UK.

rows.proposition: The UK should defund the BBC.
rows.text: If it goes along the lines of all other platforms it will turn into a streaming platform the same as everything else. Yet another subscription service that I would not want to sign up to. I like the BBC as it is and am happy to pay the licence fee. I like having access to the podcasts, news, TV etc all under one roof in a simple format. There is no other place that I know of that offers a learning service, TV, radio etc - I think it is a valuable service that needs to stay as it is - simple and easy to access for all.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Autonomous vehicles have already been implemented in some large cities around the world, and seem to generally have a positive impact on their users. At their current level, the technology is largely there to ensure appropriate safety levels, although this still could stand to be improved. They are also not particularly accessible to the average driver, as the cost is a prohibiting factor for most people. If the affordability improves and more autonomous vehicles enter the road system then the benefits - largely safer, easier on drivers, more eco-friendly - may appear apparent. For the time being, this is not a scenario that is due to become reality any time soon.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: The use of autonomous vehicles on public roads has equal advantages and disadvantages. It would mean access to transport and vehicles 24/7 whereas sites like Uber only offer as many cars as they have people working and if people are not working there are no cars. Also, it would mean that the vehicle can opperate regardless of human input. It is therefore not affected by human illness, leave from work, or staff shortages. This means that it is more accessible overall. On the other hand, it may be difficult for autonoumous vehicles to account for human errors as other drivers can be unpredictable and this may cause increased road traffic incidents. Also, if there is an error, the vehicle may become stuck and not know how to react in certain situations and just stop and therefore cause traffic issues. For example, if there is a stuck red light that is not changing or it may cause dangerous situations like if traffic lights are broken it would use the road as normal but not account for that people are being cautious and need to give way to traffic.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Innovation is incredibly important to retain future proofing for the UK across all future industry. Thus ignoring or disapproving of innovation isn't relevant (to me). I would encourage the safety aspects and look to test MVPs, have Innovate UK offer sufficient grants to enable first to market vehicles be part of a British answer. Love China, but it would be great if the UK could support this future innovation. The BSI could ensure safety standards for future roll out. Global standards should also be included and a Code of Practice global group share (as appropriate to IP such as trade secrets and patents), leading to licencing deals and incoming money back into the UK. Its coming - lets make it British :)

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: By allowing autonomous cars on all public roads we as the uk will be the first nation to have self driving cars in abundance, this will improve our perception to the rest of the world and if it goes right we will be more respected and seen as successful in the artificial intelience field which is the  future of this generation, the issue i see is actually applying this to real life, there are many risks and it will increase danger on the road as we are not too familiar with how autonomous cars work and this could lead to more accidents.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I am generally in favour of autonomous vehicles but am not convinced that this is safe for other road users and pedestrians as yet.If there was a person able to override the vehicle I would feel more confident as some driving conditions are very confusing and I wonder how an autonomous vehicle would cope .It is difficult enough for an experienced driver like me to know the right thing to do on occasions so how would an autonomous vehicle manage?You need split second judgements to prevent accidents on occasions as people in other cars do foolish things on a daily basis.However If all the cars were autonomous it may work.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I'm not sure that the UK is ready at the moment for autonomous vehicles on all public roads. There is an autonomous vehicle that carries people around a business park close to where I live and as far as I'm aware, this has been littered with issues. Other car users have tried to overtake them or try to drive next to them and the vehicle has stopped or stalled. I also believe that the vehicles have to have a member of the safety team onboard in case of an emergency, which kind of defeats the object of having an autonomous vehicle in the first place. This will probably change in the future. I don't feel that the UK roads are set up for these types of vehicle

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I am reasonably sure that autonomous vehicles will eventually become reliable enough to be safe on the roads: very probably within the next five to ten years.  At the moment, however, I do not believe that they can be trusted, and this is especially true of Teslas which rely on visual information without using other senses such as sonar.  There have been too many cases of self-driving vehicles not being able to identify hazards in test runs.  Admittedly human-operated vehicles aren't 100% safe either, but I know too much about artificial intelligence to be willing to trust it at this point.  We should wait and see how China's experiment with self-driving commercial lorries turns out.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: As long as self-driving vehicles have been demonstrated to be safe through rigorous testing then I believe they should be allowed to drive on public roads. I believe they can be safer than cars with human drivers. Humans are prone to errors and not following the law. Self-driving cars will follow all the laws of the road and may even drive more efficiently and therefore may be better for the environment. I think there may be some trepidation from the public but like all new technologies once the public get used to it and more comfortable then they will become more accepting.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text:
Autonomous vehicles provide cleaner, safer, more spacious roadways and cities/towns.
People will not need to park their vehicles or even own vehicles as costs will come down massively and their abundance will remove the need for personal ownership.
Computers do not become tired or distracted. They will make fewer mistakes than humans currently do. Less mistakes means less injuries, deaths. etc. Insurance costs will reduce as there will be fewer accidents making everything much cheaper.
Removal of drivers removes the most expensive part of travel for most people, the driver. Doing this will allow all people in society to travel and their will be no limits.
This is an exciting time.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: Autonomous vehicles, or self-driving autos have the potential to both revolutionise transport and to become safer and less accident--prone than human operated vehicles.  The future of transport, whether goods or people, will be autonomous and the UK must play a leading role in development of such without unduly blocking progress on spurious safety grounds.  Not all autonmous vehicles will operate without incident and there will be, especially initially, a few accidents.  However, we cannot block progress, rather, we must take te opportunity to learn from such and prove ourselves a land open to new ideas and new technologies.  To block self-driving cars would be to demand the tide of progress roll itself backwards.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: While autonomous cars will become common in the future, I'm sure - right now, I don't think the technology is sophisticated enough to make split second decisions, or make sensible, balanced judgements as a person would. Because of this, it could lead to accidents and deaths. On a less awful level, this lack of human judgement means that there could be hold ups, delays, etc. because a car doesn't have the judgement to just drive round a, let's say, box, in the middle of the road. I don't know any facts on this, but would the car companies perhaps be tracking our movements as well - I wouldn't want that.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: As far as I am aware, the only vehicles that this refers to are small delivery vehicles, designed to deliver shopping to customers' doors. I haven't seen or heard about any further developments regarding these vehicles which leads me to believe that there haven't been enough trials completed yet. I certainly haven't seen any being used. If this proposal was to go ahead then it would lead to job losses for people who currently do deliveries, many of which do so as a secondary income because they are struggling to live on their earnings. It also would set a precedent that it is okay for us to dispose of humans as they are no longer an integral part of a job.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I am somewhat in favour of autonomous vehicles on all public roads, but with cautions. These vehicles have a big potential to reduce accidents caused by human errors, and improve traffic efficiency. Their ability to follow the rules precisely and communicate with each other can lead to smoother travel and reduce congestion. However, there are still concerns about the safety of current technology, the risk of hacking, and who is responsible in the event of an accident. Additionally, the UK's infrastructure and regulations may need further development to fully support widespread use. A gradual well well-regulated rollout would be preferable.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I think the UK should allow autonomous vehicles on public roads, but only when they are fully tested and safe. These cars can help make driving safer by reducing human mistakes, which cause many accidents. They could also make traffic flow better by working together with other cars, which might reduce traffic jams. Autonomous vehicles can also be better for the environment because they can drive more efficiently, saving fuel and lowering pollution. However, before allowing them everywhere, there should be strict rules and tests to make sure they are safe. Once these are in place, I think they could be a good idea.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text:
Passengers in vehicles that are autonomous are at risk as they are not in control of the vehicle. They are ate risk not just of physical danger in the event of an accident but also the mental anguish that is likely to happen in the likely even of an acident. Being the owner or occupier of the vehicle that crashes as you watch the incident unfold could have severe damage to that person's mental health even if they were to be lucky enough to avoid physical harm.

Road users who have to share the roads with vehicles that are not being driven by responsible individuals are at risk of harm. Additionally, the vehicle itself won't be held responsible for its actions meaning no responsibility will be taken.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I do not have a great deal of enthusiasm for autonomous vehicles in this country as some of the stories i read there are still huge safety issues that need to be addressed. I have seen footage on tv of cars not stopping at traffic light or when there is a stationary vehicle in front of it. I feel that more testing has to take place to eradicate these problems. Our towns and cities are so busy now that i would not be comfortable with the present levels of safety. My third issue is the price of these vehicles .We already have to pay more for electric cars so goodness knows what the price of an autonomous car would be.So overall i am not a big fan.

rows.proposition: The UK should allow the use of autonomous vehicles on all public roads.
rows.text: I think there is enough danger on our roads with the vehicles we already have. Tesla cars for example, already have a form of autonomous driving on a smaller scale, which has resulted in numerous accidents where the driver has not been paying attention. When a fully autonomous vehicle has an accident, it is going to be a nightmare at Court for the other party who is actually driving their car. I can see the arguments, the computerised car will have its 'experts' fighting their corner and stating it can not make mistakes while the car with a person driving, will always be blamed for human error.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I understand the reasoning behind the discussions on the matter of young people using the internet, However I believe a blanket ban would be the incorrect way to go about this. I used the internet safely prior to being 14 years of age. I believe that we should focus more on firstly educating young people around the dangers of the internet and making them more savvy with relevance to this. Secondly I believe it is around parental education and increasing parents awareness of current dangers and issues but also teaching them how to use parental controls on certain websites and apps rto mitigate any risk of danger.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: The UK should ban internet use for under 14 years to a certain degree. This needs to be managed better for family and caregivers and how they supervise young children on the use of devices. The UK Government need to ensure they take advice from specialists that work on the issue of online impact on young people and discuss legislation around this. At the moment the current laws are not effective enough and need to be reviewed. The legislation needs to clearly ensure that Social Platforms and content producers adhere to what they put online. Parents and Caregivers also need to be educated on how to protect their young families.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: An unregulated system where extreme views can flourish needs to be carefully monitored for immature minds. Children (and many adults) are easily manipulated by what initially sounds to be a reasonable view which has a hidden agenda to exploit and manipulate. Moderate views are descried and ignored - the more extreme the view the louder the clamour which can be both seductive and dangerous. There is little fact checking and once a false idea is expounded it is almost impossible to eradicate or moderate. What should be viewed as extreme becomes viewed as the norm - this often escalates as extreme 'norms' are replaced by ever more extreme views.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: My first reason is that the internet is a key part of everyday life and has become the norm even for young children, whilst there may be some advantages to restricting it's use it is too late it is everywhere and it permeates every part of daily human life.  Further there are lots of benefits from children using the internet not least as an education and a communication tools, young people are far better informed these days because of the internet.   Finally I think even if there were convincing arguments to introducing a ban it would be near impossible to effectively enforce given the variety of devices that can  be used to access the internet and how  widespread the use is.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: The internet is a brilliant tool, which it is almost impossible to live in a modern world without interacting with it at least once during the day. The risk vs reward of using the internet is just too weighted towards good to blanket ban children under 14 from accessing it. Many things that children use such as online gaming and chatting with each other needs use of the internet, and it would be almost prohibitive for learning for children not to be able to access the internet for research using videos or online tests etc. There are downsides of course to letting them have access to the internet such as online bullying and seeing things that they shouldnt, but to blanket ban it would be a knee-jerk reaction that is not needed

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
The uk should not ban the internet for many compelling reasons,banning the internet would have widespread negative consequences for individuals,,the economy and society as a whole .Economic devatstation would be caused from this.
An impact on learning and education would occur as schools ,colleges use the internet to teach and help learning.
Blocking the internet  would mean information about healthcare and emergencies.Banning the internet would be catastrophic and socially harmful as well as harming it fundamentally.
Skill development would be harmed as literacy is a critical skill and reading happens often on a phone, as a result this would leave them under educated.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: The internet can be a horror show, there are vile predators online who pose a risk to children. It should be the groomers who are hunted and punished not the children. Banning things is not the answer, it is a quick fix by cowards to stamp their pitiful authority. The internet can be a wonderful tool for learning and it should be available for all ages. There is obviously a problem that needs be fixed and there has to be think tanks who can find out ways to terminate the predators while allowing children to use the internet in constructive educational ways. Banning anything is just pathetic.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
-No ban ever really works certainly banning under 14 year olds would be very difficult giving the large number of smart devices young people have access to today, what is required is proper regulation of the content and internet service providers should be robustly policed and if required face heavy penalties for any breaches. The internet has numerous benefits especially in educating / seeking knowledge and skills.
- Banning will if anything make it far worse and drive under 14 year olds to go underground seeking illegal and potentially far worse and less regulated internet access, bans only make something appear far more enticing and to be honest determined young people will always find a way round.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
I believe that the potential benefits of internet use for young people under 14 far outweigh the harms. It would be draconian and foolish to try to stop young people from accessing the internet because of the potential harms.

Of course the internet provides access to harmful materials and material that young people should not be accessing. But it is also potentially the most incredible educational resource in human history, and many young people derive huge educational and social benefits from the internet. The internet is also a place where young people can develop and showcase their creativity. It is ridiculous to think we should simply close off these resources from young people, and would be a great injustice to young people if we tried.

We definitely need to get better at making the internet safe for young people - we are far having solved this problem yet. But this should be about the responsibility of service providers on the internet, parents and children themselves. Our focus should be on making the internet a force for good for young people, not trying to turn the clock back.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: There is literally no point in banning the internet for under 14 year olds as they need to learn how to use the internet safely as they'll be coming across and using the internet at home, at school and in their future employment. They need to learn from a young age about the internet and the pros and cons it brings. They should be taught internet safety and how to be careful when using it. Another reason on why I don't think such a ban will work is because many parents already don't monitor their children using the internet at home. Many children from age of 11 can be given a smartphone with the internet freely available on (which in my opinion is a bad thing to do). I don't think a ban would be strictly followed in the homes of these children. Parents won't be monitoring them after work when they are finally getting some peace from their kids. Children are also very clever and will obviously find interest in things that are banned such as the internet when they see their parents on it. They will sneak on their phones. They will go on their laptops and tablets. We shouldn't be putting more stress on parents. We also don't want a nanny state where we have rules for everything that take away responsibility - parental responsibility in this case should be applied and we allow families to create their own rules within their private homes without the state getting involved.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I strongly disagree with the banning of internet use for children under 14.  The internet is an essential part of modern education and development, offering access to learning tools. creative platforms and opportunities for communication.  While online risks are real banning access completely would hinder digital literacy and isolate children from a key part of today's world.  A more balanced approach involves educating children about online safety, encouraging parental supervision, and using content filters to create age appropriate experiences.  Rather than implementing a blanket ban, we should aim to teach responsible and safe internet use from an early age to prepare children for the digital world they're growing up in.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: i think that the internet should be banned for under 14 year old children because it can be very unsafe for them to use. they can be easily manipulated by things that they see online and there is a lot of content that is not suitable for children. it can be very dangerous if they are exposed to harmful content and inappropriate content. there is also a lot of misinformation online that could lead to them believing things that are not true and that could have a massive impact on their way of thinking when the are growing up into adults.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: Children spend far too much time on the internet and social media and it is causing so much damage to young minds. A huge number of children are suffering mental health issues and harming themselves, or even committing suicide because of all the hate and misinformation online. Everything is about how you look these days are you wearing the right clothes and going to the right places? So called influencers and celebrities are like gods, they must be obeyed or you will suffer. young children should be playing games with friends, creating their own ideas and not worrying about adults.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
Banning the internet for kids under14 would cause several issues regarding school. My kids are now adults but they had to use the internet to access study material. I would imagine that access even more relied now 10 years later. Those studying for exams will be 14 and over so it wouldnt effect them. Maybe schools need to bring kids study away from the internet.
However I do think a ban would be good regarding access to social media. Its not fun for a kid to have playground hassle in front of them at home via the internet. They can never get away from it. They are exposed to adult stuff that they cannot understand as being fake or lies.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text: I think that it is valid to be concerned about Internet usage for young children and teens, given the harmful material and content that is available and so easily accessible online. However, I don't think the right approach is to completely ban it, as there are some beneficial elements of the Internet for young people. There are lots of useful learning platforms and resources available, and it is also a good way to communicate with friends and family who may live far away. I do think there should be tighter restrictions on what under-14-year-olds can access online, and parent controls should increase. There are certain platforms they definitely shouldn't be able to access, but a complete ban is not the right apprpoach.

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
The Internet is growing part of learning and development for younger people. Taking this away could hinder and not progress their education like it should. It could cause more stress leading to higher levels of school drop out and under achievement.

There is a lot of reliance on technology and it is a big part of what people do and how people communicate. Taking this away would make males feel isolated and unaware of what others may be going on about. It could end up almost like a giant social click of boys against girls.

If the concern is due to what can be found online then the ban would need to be implemented across the board not just aimed at males. There are resources which make it easy to monitor and restrict what can and cannot be accessed which may be more beneficial

rows.proposition: The UK should ban internet use for under-14-year-olds.
rows.text:
I feel that a ban on internet for kids under the age of 14 will have fundamentally positive impacts on their childhood with very little downside. Firstly, without social media, kids will be forced to use other avenues to socialise with their peers, reducing their reliance on instagram, and other social media platforms that allow them to skirt away from face to face interactions. In the digital age, and the rise in social media usage by the younger generations, has started having an impact on their social skills when face to face, and this is most likley in part due to their over reliance to use text based, emoji based communications rather than speak face to face or even via voice calls. reducing text based messaging and 'tiktok/reel' based communication could have a positive impact on their ability to express themselves and carry themselves in social situations. 

Secondly, removing socialmedia will prevent addiction cycles of doom scrolling and endless dopamine releases from just watching reels continuously. Which will be good for their brains and development. 
Lastly, no social internet access will prevent them from having exposure to harmful and radicalising websites and influencers, for exaple 4 chan, certain reddit threads, twitter and the sort. Or even bad infleuncing youtubers/streamers. This could allow the kids to grow up without a significant amount of influence which we now have today.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: This one is a bit more difficult for me to get my head round as I'm not one who knows infinite details behind political parties policy workings having only a general grasp of basics. However I think we should be somewhere between socialist and capitalist systems to get the best of both worlds. Leaning towards socialist away from capitalist will lead to a better distribution of wealth so better for the poorer off. We could lose more of our individual freedom going this route but may address sustainability quicker by better practices. As I said I think there needs to be a balance between the two but a very complex problem which is not easy to address.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: The UK and most of the Western World uses a capitalist system, some of the world lives in a communist system and some countries are run by dictators or martial law. Capitalism relies on the generation of profits to fund society on the basis that those who run companies or have ideas that generate profits benefit most and the rest of society experiences the trickle down effect that our incomes and experiences are elevated. However this does not work very well so that most of the country's money is in the hands of a few who keep it for themselves and do not use the same healthcare or education provision as the masses so they have little interest in improving matters. A socialist system would be more equal giving more opportunity and care to everyone and evening out the chance to succeed. However I am unsure in this case how the income is generated in the first place and how you encourage businesses to share most of their income

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: In a perfect world every one would earn the same no matter what level they worked at if they where in the same company. As those at the top in business carry a lot of stress and possibly the weight of many peoples jobs on their shoulders they would not do this for the same money as the person who was cleaning the toilets or making sure that the coffee machines where full. every job within a company needs to be done for that company to run at its optimum level so in one way every job is as important as the other but to get the right people in right place you have to pay.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I see myself as left-of-centre, working towards a social democracy. I think Keynesian economics where the state intervenes in times of crisis, but some use of private sector to bring growth and innovation is key. Why should we ignore a history of capitalism growing economies and bringing increased living standards? Progressive taxation and sensible government subsidies for health, education etc can certainly exist within a capitalist market system. Also, some of our socialist politicians in the UK (e.g Corbyn,) are, frankly, self-hating, seeking to ally with authoritarian regimes and withdraw from NATO which will weaken our place in the world.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
It has been a whiles since I was at school and studied socialism, so forgive me if my arguments are weak and my knowledge poor. I belive that socialism is about everbody having a say and a vote, a little like democracy.  There are no  class barriers and the opportunities the country has are open to all, wherever you come from.  This then leads to people in skilled jobs being from all backgrounds and starts to spread the countries wealth across more people etc.

The workforce is therfore mixed, communities become mixed and everyone is working for one thing which is the good of the country .

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I am uncertain in my answer, as I am unclear as to what difference it would make. A socialist economic system would be prefered by some, while others are all in favour of capitalism. I would need to know more on what it would actually do to the common man on the street and the ones with lots of money. Also how would it affect businesses? These are questions i would need answers to before I could give my honest oppinion on any vote of this sort. If it would benefit more people, then yes let's do it. If it makes the richer, richer, then no... Let's not vote for it.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
The current iteration of capitalism ceased to be a realistic option for the UK when in 2008 the banks were deemed too big to fail; this version of turbo neo liberalism that has facilitated the largest transfer of wealth in the UK over the last 6 or 7 years in history.  
real wages of CEO's has increased by 000's of percent in a genration whilst the average working person is hardly any better off over the same timescale.  The UK has been asset stripped by the neo liberal right wing of the country and under the guise if trickle down ecomonics has handed huge swathes of the UK infrastrucutre in the hands of share holders who are only interested in extracting profit from them.  Water being a case in point; all key infrastructure should be returned to public ownership ASAP

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I think that transitioning to a more socialist society could help create a fairer society by reducing inequality and ensuring everybody has access to essential services like healthcare, education and housing - at the same equal levels e.g. no private schools or private healthcare. Capitalism often leads to concentrated wealth in the hands of a few, whilst there are many people left in poverty who struggle to meet basic needs. A socialist model would help to distribute the wealth of an economy more equitably, reducing systemic inequality. It would promote social cohesion and a general stronger economic safety net for all citizens.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: What do we even mean by "socialism"? If it means handing over more of the economy to be controlled by the government then this why would anybody think this would be a good idea? All of our political parties have had an awful record over the past few decades, so why would they suddenly improve if they had more control of the economy? The history of the Soviet Union is not very encouraging for socialists. Whenever socialism has been tried, it has failed to live up to its promises. Usually this begins right at the root: "replacing capitalism" inevitably means "stealing peoples' property", and it doesn't get any better from there on in.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text:
I can't think of 1 socialist country that can be seen as being successful.
They all seem to:
1. Determine what news can be consumed and how it's consumed. They are scared that their people will object to and be envious of the successes and freedoms being seen from capitalist countries
2. Limit the success that can be achieved by their inhabitants. They aim for economic equality but in reality they are just keeping everyone poor
3. Avoid public ownership ensuring that the state controls everything
4. Be aggressive in nature. They struggle with accepting the fair competition of capitalist countries

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I am against a purely socialist economic system, while recognising that the current system is unfair and needs to be improved. However, I do not feel this is the way to do it. A socialist economic system is likely to stifle individual freedoms. Socialist and more particularly communist economic systems really require central planning and control. This will mean that the state exerts more direct control on the citizen. It is also frequently observed that in extremely socialist economies jobs are created solely so people have jobs, not because are needed to be done, 'to each according to their need' is in effect the subsidisation of those with a poor work ethic or laziness by those who are creative and willing to put the effort in. The market is also distorted so that what is produced is what the state wants and not what they people need.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I don't the Uk should switch to socialism and replace capitalism. Capitalism encourages people to work hard and come up with new ideas, which helps the economy grow. Of course, inequality is a real issue, but there are ways to fix that without changing the whole system. Socialism often lowers the motivation for people to push themselves and take risk, and that could slow down progress and cause economic problems, Instead, I think it's better to keep capitalism but make it fairer with good regulations and support for those who need it. That way, we can balance growth with fairness without risking instability.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I think that a socialist economic system would benefit us in so far as it would provide a safety net for everyone, reduce the gap between rich and poor and make the playing field more level for young people and children. It would also lead to better mental health, because people would not spend so much desiring consumer goods or a lifestyle (holidays etc.) that they cannot afford, get in debt to buy stuff that they think they cannot live without, and there would also be less of an incentive for people to turn to drug dealing, prostitution etc. to earn money. If it was easier for women to work and the gender gap salary wise was reduced, men would have to earn women's respect and love instead of 'buying' them by offering them accommodation and money in exchange for sex. I haven't visited Scandinavian countries yet and only know them through novels and TV series (which do tend to be 'dark', focusing on crime etc.) and what people who have moved there have told me, but these countries do seem to be better places to live than the UK, or France (where I've lived). For one thing, you don't see a lot of people emigrating from Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Iceland to our countries...

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: There is no way that the UK should transition to a socialist economic system. History shows that such systems just do not work and lead to dictaorships, inequality, famine, death and many other negative things. Capitalism is not perfect, that is for sure, but it is the best economic system devised by man to date. No other systems offer the stability, the opportunity or the innovation that capitalism brings. Capitalism fosters innovation and vast technological gains, all of which gain society as a whole. Just look at the vast living standards between those in North and South Korea for proof.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I don't think the UK should fully switch to socialism. If you take away competition and make everything government run, people have less reason to innovate or work hard. Plus, too much state control usually ends up being wasteful or corrupt. A better option is a mixed system where we keep capitalism but also support people properly through public services. Though capitalism has its flaws, this way we get both worlds progress and fairness. That way, we can support the vulnerable without killing off ambition or progress. It is more flexible, more practical, and avoids the extremes of either side.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to a socialist economic system, replacing capitalism.
rows.text: I think socialism is a nice idea and in an ideal world it would be great but unfortunately its not how the world works. The countries that do have a socialist/communist party ruling reads like the who is who of places I wouldn't want to live. To make the shift to being socialist from a capitalist country would be a disaster and would never happen. The only way it could happen is if certain elements of socialism were brought in but the system as a whole would not be able to change as the bedrock of Britain is build on us being a capitalist country

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I understand that for the very worst crimes it's easy to say that the perpetrator should not be allowed to live because what they did was so horrific. However, it just isn't right to kill someone. It doesn't undo what has already been done. There obviously needs to be punishment, but a whole life sentence should be sufficient. I understand that this is actually a costly option which some people may disagree with and it may seem that the perpetrator is then given an easy life being kept and looked after in prison. I also understand that the death penalty can be seen as a deterrent but it's still not right to kill another person.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
People can be wrongly judged.
It is not a given that on reaching a verdict that a court will get it right, falsification of evidence, wrongly  interpreted evidence, personal bias can all happen within a hearing. People can be 'set up' or framed.
Against the taking of human life. 
I am not religious in any way whatsoever but I am morally against the taking of human life. I am completely against the idea that one life is more important than another. Unless in a war or combat situation where my own life will be taken unless I take my adversary's life, that is the only situation where I see life taking as justified.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Modern society should not use the death penalty but instead an individual should be tried in a court of law and convicted and imprisoned for their crimes, if necessary in a high security facility. The state should not take the life of its citizens but instead set a high moral standard in its commitment to look after the welfare of all. An eye for an eye does not resolve societies problems. A modern society can rise above this brutality and demonstrate that reasonable and just process can deal with the crimes of the individual. In other societies, if the death penalty was acceptable, it is possible the wrong person might be convicted and a life taken wrongly or the state might abuse its power and use the death penalty for political crimes. It is important that the highest moral standard is maintained.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
The UK should not reintroduce the death penalty because we have seen several miscarriages of justice where the accused has been hanged in the past and then later found to be innocent.   
Looking back to when the UK had the death penalty, murder rates did not increase after the repeal of the death penalty and so you could argue that it served no purpose.  If someone is intent on murder or committing some other awful crime they will do it whatever the risk.  
It is also a very cruel system in that there is often a very long wait between being caught and having the penalty carried out.  It isn't a very humane way to treat people.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I do not agree with bringing back the death penalty in the UK. One main reason is that courts can make mistakes, and if someone innocent is killed, we can never fix that. Life in prison is already a very serious punishment and keeps dangerous people away from the public. Also, research shows the death penalty does not always stop others from committing crimes, so it may not help lower crime rates. I believe the justice system should try to change people for the better and not just punish them in the harshest way. Killing someone as punishment can lead to more pain and does not always bring true justice.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Since in UK a lot of criminals get very minimal sentences or even go unpunished by the government or court it is best if we put criminals down. The criminals that undoubtedly murder or rape people should be held accountable. Death is less worse than the actions they do to others like cause lifelong traumas to children, women, men or even change their entire physical selves. It would mean  we would also save a lot of money in taxes to spend on better things like healthcare and so on. It would be a good thing for every citizen to save money and also have less crime in cities.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
I think the government should reintroduce the  death penalty.  Some crimes are so wrong and so bad that the only reasonable thing to do is to put them to death.  Child murderers, paedophiles that reoffend, rapists, terrorists, mass murderers all deserve the death penalty.
There are great benefits to society in putting these people to death and not letting them reoffend again.  The cost of inprisoning somebody for life is huge and these kind of people don't deserve to live.
The guilt of the person must be absolute with no doubt whatoever before this decision is made though.  Some of the cases I can think of the guilt is definite.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
I believe that some of the most serious criminals deserve the death sentence. Especially violent criminals. I don't believe they deserve to live and it shouldn't cost the tax payer money for their place in prison. I find it hard to believe that some violent criminals can be rehabilitated so I think it is a waste of time them spending the rest of their lives in prison costing the tax payer. 
I think their are many in the UK that would support re-introducing the death penalty. I also think that it would help with prison overcrowding. So reducing costs and pressure.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
The implementation of death plenty can lead to increased vigilance among people and a greater respect for the law among criminals. While it may seem extreme in some cases, evidence shows that crime rates tend to decrease in countries where capital punishment is enforced. For acts of severe brutality against human beings, the death plenty can be seen as justified and proportional response.  
However, death plenty remains a deeply controversial issue. Critics argue that it involves the risk of wrong conviction. Others argue that life imprisonment can be a more humane and equally effective measure. So the debate centres on finding a balance between justice and value of human life.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I think the death penalty is an easy way out and is a very extreme punishment. If someone has murdered, for example, I do believe they do not deserve to be here on this planet. However, I think they should suffer and rot in prison, supporting the aim of retribution. Also, innocent people could wrongly get accused and receive the death penalty as seen in cases in America. Therefore, if the death penalty were to be ever reintroduced solid factual evidence would have to be provided. On the other hand, the death penalty is a good deterrence to have in society, and I would expect offending and re-offending would reduce as a result.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Reintroducing the death penalty would challenge the UK's ethical approach to justice and it's focus on human rights, shifting the focus from justice and rehabilitation, to retribution and "an eye for an eye" mentality. More importantly, no justice system is perfect. There are instances of wrongful convictions even now, and having the death penalty would leave no room for correction. The police force, justicial system, etc., are all fallible. Even one innocent person getting the death penalty destroys the faith we place in the system. Finally, there is minimal evidence that capital punishment is an effective deterrent. One only has to look at the United States

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I believe that the death penalty is old-fashioned and doesn't disincentive crime - the idea a jury can decide that someone deserves to live or die or not is not fair as there are always biases involved. people argue it would reduce overcrowding, but as can be seen from the US prison system which is still overcrowded this doesn't work that way and fails to put people off violent crimes. Stats from the US also show that minorities are much more likely to get the death penalty than other groups, and there are so many examples of people being killed and later found not guilty that it can't work in practice without being unfair

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Even with the advances in forensic science, people have been wrongly convicted of crimes that carried the death sentence. Many more have had life sentences over turned as new evidence or witnesses have come to light. The death sentence does not give a chance to be over turned once the act has been carried out. The death does not always work as planned and can be protracted and painful. We should be civilised and not treat people in that manner even if they have carried out terrible crimes. I believe a more appropriate punishment would be to give them a whole life sentence in prison.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I don't think any country needs to have the death penalty. A life in a cell is harder than death which is an escape. For those that would be deemed deserving of the death penalty, give them a life in a cell with no luxuries as that is much more difficult for them than dying. Killing someone isn't right in any scenario, we shouldn't lower ourselves to the despicable level of these criminals. A life in a cell, with no luxuries, no contact with family or other people in jail would be much harder. We shouldn't give these criminals an easy out.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I think it depends on the situation and the crime that has been committed; the death penalty is not relevant for all crimes. For instance, I think paedophilia or any child abuse cases should be punishable by death - especially where there is a chance of the offender recommitting such a crime. There are cases, such as murder cases, where the lines may be greyer. For example, if someone murders an innocent person in cold blood, it would be morally right to punish them by death. On the other hand, if someone murders someone who has wronged them, e.g. their rapist, it could be considered morally wrong to punish them by death.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
As mentioned, it does feel that there's a growing tendency towards brutal and violent crimes, not just towards women although many of the ones we hear most about are against women, so it does feel that there should be greater deterrents. The current prison system (and the even more recent system of having to let criminals out part-way through their sentences because of overcrowding) really does not appear to be working as a deterrent. Having the death penalty available to judges, for certain crimes may help to provide more of a deterrent, and more of an appropriate punishment for some crimes.

As an teacher, I used to teach a unit of work based around the Derek Bentley and Christopher Craig story, so I completely understand how / why any reintroduction of the death penalty should not be done lightly and it's why I'm hesitant to be more 'for' the introduction of it.

However, with the growth of forensic science, technology providing evidence and greater access than ever before to DNA evidence and databases, it's hopeful that if the death penalty were reintroduced, there would  be strict requirements to use types of evidence which are highly reliable and irrefutable.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
First of all, I'm against death penalty, human life is simply too valuable to just take it away. I would love to see prisoners being instead involved in some wider country improvement programs; US system - although flawed - involves prisoners in contributing to the country - be it by small scale manufacturing ar social work. UK is not utilising those opportunities.
On top of that there is always a doubt when it comes to the sentence, and although I believe most of sentences are correct, mistakes can happen - that is not defining factor against death penalty for me though.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: Some crimes occur that are poorly understood, For example, an abused woman might kill her attacker in self defense, with seemingly no other option. Some people are wrongly aquitted of a crime that they have not committed. Some judges might have no understanding or compassion for a person who has commited a crime, and make a biased decision. Who has the right to put another man to death ? Some sentences are already totally unfair, judgements have been made abouta person, or examples been made of someone, and a very unjust prison sentence been given. THis could easily extend to the decison to put a man/woman to death

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text: I do not think that the UK should reintroduce the death penalty, no matter what the crime is. I feel that executing a criminal is a barbaric and uncivilised punishment. What crimes would warrant this? Who would decide how and when someone is executed for a crime? What if the execution went wrong and the criminal was not humanely put to death? What safeguards would be in place to ensure that the right person was found guilty of the crime? It's impossible to bring someone back from the dead if it is later found that the criminal was, in fact, completely innocent. There are too many variables to even consider bringing back the death penalty.

rows.proposition: The UK should reintroduce the death penalty.
rows.text:
- cost saving - rather than pay to keep people in prison for a long time then if they were given the death penalty instead this would save money. The cost of keeping someone in prison for life must be more plus then it would free up prison spaces.
- maybe the death penalty would deter people from crime. If they killed someone then it would be a suitable punishment rather than being let out in a few years. 
- this should only be given if you are certain the person is guilty. if there is any doubt then maybe not give the death penalty.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: In general, I am in favour of euthanasia if it is the person's choice. I can see no reason why, if someone is very or terminally ill, they should be made to suffer until they eventually die. However, if euthanasia is legalised, there is the danger that it would be used unethically; without a patient's consent. It is already becoming popular for people to travel abroad to do this and, again, if it is their choice I see no issues with this. If it was legalised there would have to always be consent from the patient but I do not know how this would be enforced.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: i am a bit confused if this is right or not as i know it can bring comfort to people with a limited way of life and also termally ill people who are looking at a painful death or other reasons. but also i can understand how it could be hard for familes who have to see someone the love choose this. it would have to be for very important reasons and not just giving as an option to anyone. i think it would bring comfort to people who are dying or not living a life in a nice way if they could choose thid though

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I know that there are some very convincing moral arguments to legalise euthanasia, e.g. that it allows people the choice to die with dignity and not have to suffer for their last days of living. I also recognise though that it will be difficult to legislate for this - e.g. who should be able to make this choice? how long should they have left to live? how severe do their symptoms have to be? These are difficult questions that I don't really trust legislators to make the 'right' decision on. There's also the concern that people may feel like a burden and feel they must end their lives so they do not cause financial and emotional strain on their loved ones and the state. In this sense, this legislation may have significantly negative effects on vulnerable people, people with disabilities.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: So I think this probably should be legal but is very much down to individual choice. It seems that if someone is hell bent on this course of action that they should have to travel to Switzerland with all the added stress and expense that must entail. Plus surely it is better that someone who might contemplate suicide to be helped to make it as easy as possible instead of suicide which could fail or lead to an even worse situation if the attempt is survived. I have no religious opinion concerning this although I can't imagine ever wanting to take advantage of it myself but who knows. Circumstances can change. I think there would have to be the most watertight protections possible against legalised euthanasia being abused and people being talked into it for selfish reasons.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: My position on the subject is unclear. While nobody wants to die a painful death with a prolonged process of suffering, legalising euthanasia may offer individuals greater autonomy in deciding how and when they die and under what circumstances. As such it feels like a something a humane society might chose to do. On the other hand,  the choice to die  could, if open to all result in a significant rise in the suicide rate. Many people who have survived a suicide attempt are glad that they did so because their feelings were transient. Legalising euthanasia could essentially validate such short-term feelings and lead to a nihilistic embracement of death especially in the young.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: on one hand i think yes it should be legalised that they should end a life and my it legal if an individual in a lot of pain or there is no cure to disease or they suffering for very long time , but then i think if they help to have a life alive and keep try they might recover , who knows the future ? may be later on there is some possibility or cure to disease or may be body heals it self and pain and discomfort goes away , i think its entirely depends on case to case circumstances hard to make a statement about it

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Other countries of the world people are allowed to pick euthanasia if they are terminally ill. If a pet that we love and care about is terminally ill we are allowed to take them to the vet and have them put to sleep and out of their suffering. I think it is personally awful that in this country we have to allow our loved ones to suffer right until the end of their life. How anyone can think that is normal in this day in age is really disturbing. I do believe that there needs to be a certain criteria for it to be allowed though.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: If someone is terminally ill and medical assessments find that there is nothing that can be done to save them, then by ending the life this will be kinder to the person and also better for the family, as they wont have to see their loved one suffer any more.  This would need to be assessed by at least two healthcare professionals to ensure that it is being done for the correct reasons, as it could possibly be done for the wrong reasons, so it needs to be carefully monitored to ensure it is being carried out correctly and for the right circumstances

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I believe that legalising euthanasia will have overwhelming positive impacts. If a person is planning to end their life anyway, doing so by other means (such as suicide) would have severely more devastating effects. Assisted dying via euthanasia will help families to prepare for a person's death and may help to lower the blow. It may also help put the person at ease who is going to die, knowing that they won't be suffering any longer. People are capable of ending their own lives whether euthanasia is in place or not, so legalising euthanasia (a safer, more controlled method of achieving death) would be more positive for all involved parties.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
I think it is about time we legalised euthanasia in this country.  Whilst I understand it is a difficult decision for family members and professionals alike to make, we have made such advances in medicine that we are keeping people alive for longer and they sometimes have an awful quality of life. I think if it is a properly managed system, then people could have the change for a pain free and dignified end to their lives - benefiting both the individual and the family. It may also save the NHS money in not keeping people alive and feeding them drugs to enable them to do so.
There is a counter argument of course - is it morally right? Who gets to make the decision - so robust legislation must be in place of course, but I am wholly in favour.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Why is it that if a beloved family pet has an incurable and painful disease, we can choose to have them put down, but if a person is endlessly suffering they cannot choose to end their own life? We are putting more of an emphasis on the feelings of the loved ones around the sufferer, rather than the person with the incurable illness themselves. Allowing someone you love to suffer indefinitely through their pain because the idea of death is too emotionally painful for those around them is unfair and cruel. There should be easier access to end-of-life care for those who have to go through it.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Some people have to live with pain at the end of their life as they suffer from an ailment which is going to kill them anyway. It can be the kindest thing for them not to have to endure this pain and to make the decision to end their own life on their own terms. If someone wants to end their life early, it should be their choice and they shouldn't be prevented doing that due to a law. People deserve a large amount of freedom in how they live their life and they should also have the right to end it early  if that is their ultimate wish.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text:
elimination of pointless suffering. Having witnessed on two occasions people who were going to die, everything that could have been done had, being forced to live on until their bodies could not sustain life, made me pro euthanasia.
On both of these occasions, it was the dying person's wish to get it over with and escape the suffering, that was their choice to end their life simply to end their suffering. sometimes it seems that people don't understand that there can be a sentient being in there whose feelings and thoughts are still active and are very concerned about their loved ones seeing them in such a state of distress. why do we finish off a deer with a heavy object that has been run over? its not because we are evil it's quite the opposite.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: I don't support the legalisation of euthanasia in the UK. Whilst the intention may be to offer relief from suffering, it could put vulnerable poeple such as the elderly, disabling or those with mental health challenges at the risk of feeling pressured to end their lives. Legalising it might also shift focus away from improving palliative care and suporting people through difficult times. Even with safeguards in place, there's always a risk of mistakes or abuse. Over time, the boundaries could blur, leading to broader and potentially dangerous applications of euthanasia that could go beyond the original intent of the law.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
