With proper legislation and oevrsight we can clamp down on any dodgy practices and coercion so vulnerable people are protected. All in all it seems a humane and sensible step forward that balances individual rights with societal responsibility without opening a slippery slope. I hope it will help a lot of people and provide support for the faimily and friedns as well.

rows.proposition: The UK should legalise euthanasia.
rows.text: Euthenasia is illegal in this country ans so people who wish to have their life terminated have to travel abroad to places such as Switzerland or Belgium to be euthenised. I think that if a person is suffering from an incurable illness and has come to the decision that life is no longer worth living then they should be allowed to have their life legally ended. I have some experience of this : my brother-in-law had Guillome-Barre syndrome which meant that he could not breathe without a respirator and would spend the rest of his life in that state. The doctors suggested in blunt language that "we are flogging a dead horse here" - and suggested turning off the respirator. He died. Was that euthenasia? He wanted to go and the alternative was years in hospital at huge cost to the country.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly reduce immigration levels.

Immigration is not equal, it comes in many forms.  Immigration for the sake of asylum is perfectly acceptable and is a basic human right.

Illegal immigration for the purposes of crime is a totally different matter.  This should be discouraged via tough sanctions and deterrents.  If our justice system is seen to go soft on this type of immigration, this has to be addressed.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: Recently cuts have been made to the overseas aid budget, with funding for military spending seemingly prioritised in these turbulent times. However as one the richest nations, it's morally incumbent on the uk and its partners to contribute to the alleviation of global hunger. In addition many of the nations now affected by poverty were formerly British colonies, whose present poverty is at least in part due to the previous exploitation of their people and resources by the British Empire. This has since been exacerbated by sometimes venal leadership in those countries and organisations such as the IMF and World Bank, which have further entrenched disparity by perpetuating unfair lending.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.
rows.text: As a wealthy nation, the UK has a moral obligation to support countries facing extreme poverty and hunger. With its resources and influence, the UK can make a meaningful impact by increasing its foreign aid budget. Combating hunger helps reduce the risk of conflict and political instability, which benefits not only affected regions but also contributes to global security. Additionally, providing this kind of aid reflects the UK’s core values of compassion and international solidarity. It demonstrates a commitment to justice and humanity, showing that we care about improving lives beyond our borders and supporting vulnerable populations in times of urgent need.

rows.proposition: The UK should significantly increase its foreign aid budget for combating global hunger.

Our support from other nations in global politics is undermined both by our cuts to foreign aid and by other nations filling any gaps. Hunger itself is a terrible, unnecessary condition. As a rich nation we should be proud to help less fortunate people. Enabling people to survive short term, as well as thrive long term, would be both morally the right thing to do and, moreover, create a more stable environment that people could build their lives on.

It is also concerning that if this was to happen, other rights could be rolled back too - women's rights, racial equality, etc. If the UK government was to make same-sex marriage illegal, they should make marriage illegal for all (I wouldn't be against that - it's outdated!).

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text: I don't believe there is a logical reason for making same-sex marriage illegal. To do so would be to actively discriminate against many couples who deserve the same rights as anyone else, and it would also show a disturbing swing towards the right-wing politics that seems to have taken over the United States. Making same-sex marriage illegal seems like it would be a slippery slope: one could quite easily say that if a marriage is defined as a union between one man and one woman for the purposes of childbearing, then those who do not intend to have children (or simply can't have children) shouldn't be able to marry. In short, the only time legality should enter the discussion of marriage is in terms of age (must be adults), consent (must not be forced), incest (must not be related), and to ensure there's no bigamy.

Banning same sex marriage would be a form of discrimination against gay people and a denial of their rights.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text: I completely disagree with this statement as I believe it stems from a religious belief, and I do not think that incorporating religion into the law is democratic, especially within the UK which is a very multicultural nation. It also marginalises minority groups from the LGBTQ+ community by excluding them from a sacrament that is supposed to represent love and union. This statement represents a harmful ideology that normalises heterosexual relationships and criminalises anything else, which is homophobic and not progressive at all. It would also send a message to young people, who may already be struggling with their sexuality, that homosexuality is wrong and therefore may cause mental harm to people who may struggle to accept themselves.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text:
I feel that making same-sex marriage illegal is discriminatory to same-sex couples. Sexual preference is a protected characteristic when it comes to discrimination law (Equality Act, I think), so to start preventing people from taking part in socially-recognised and legally-binding practices such as marriage would be the discriminatory according to the current law. It would also present something of a very unwelcome precedent when it comes to just 'removing' groups from the rights and protections of the Equality Act 2010).

As the act of marriage is also one which brings certain legal protections (such as next-of-kin etc) it feels discriminatory to exclude same-sex couples from their rights, protection and status of the legal side of marriage. We should all be equal in the eyes of the law so we shouldn't exclude any people from taking part in this legal ceremony, if they wish to make the commitment of marriage.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text: The statement could have very serious consequences for individuals and the UK as a whole. The statement itself is quite bigoted, which, if acted upon, would be frowned upon by the international community and fundamentally alter perceptions of the UK. Individuals could be subject to violence, hate speech and bullying, with consequences ranging from poor mental health to physical violence and riots. Finally, and perhaps actually most importantly, I think this is a violation of human rights for same-sex couples : their right to love and marry who they choose as well as a loss of dignity. The UK would be flying in the face of its own policies and the will of the people if it acted on this statement.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.

We learned that slavery was wrong, that women should have equal rights, that animal suffering caused by us is wrong. We also learned that same-sex relationships offer happiness and love to those within them, in the same way that different-sex relationships do for those within those. We learned that suffering, by denying same-sex relationships is cruel to those involved. We also learned that such relationships offer no threat, neither to us nor our children. To ban them would be to persecute a segment of our community for no reason.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text: I feel conflicted about this topic. On one hand, I fully support the right of individuals to marry whomever they choose, including same-sex couples. Personal freedom and equality are core values in a democratic society. However, I also recognise that some religious or cultural communities feel deeply uncomfortable with same-sex marriage due to longstanding beliefs. While I don't agree with making it illegal, that would be an infringement on personal rights, I do think there needs to be room for respectful dialogue and protection for differing views. Striking a balance between civil rights and freedom of belief is a sensitive but important task for society.

rows.proposition: The UK should make same-sex marriage illegal.
rows.text:
This could be supported depending on a person's religious views. An evangelical Christian would support this- arguing it is against the will of God for there to be same sex marriages.
On the other hand, in modern society this proposal looks outdated and very traditional. It is not in alignment with the principles of inclusivity and diversity- which modern society aspires to achieve generally. It could reinforce prejudices against same sex couples. Further, human beings have legal rights- thinking of the European Convention On Human Rights- and this policy would infringe such rights, and break international law that the UK is a treaty signatory to.
Therefore, overall I oppose the proposal- seeing the disadvantages much outweigh possible contrary opinions.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to 100% renewable energy by 2030.
rows.text: I think to attempt to change to 100 percent renewable energy in the next 5 years or less is totally unachievable and we will end up importing energy from other countries and that defeats the object. The weather conditions are a big factor and they are taking up greenbelt land with their hideous solar panels and wind farms, not to mention the destruction of wildlife in the process. It will require high initial costs and will also greatly affect delicate ecosystems. It will mean we cannot store energy. The carbon footprint can also be high when first producing the installations

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to 100% renewable energy by 2030.
rows.text:
I fully endorse the renewable energy transition, yet I believe the UK faces challenges to reach 100% renewable energy by 2030. 
The UK needs to prioritize renewable energy investments in wind power and solar power and either renewable sources because they fight climate change and enhance public health. The transition to complete renewable energy systems faces substantial challenges because of the massive requirements for infrastructure development and technological advancements and economic system modifications during this short timeframe. A sustainable energy transition requires a realistic phased approach which should receive strong policy support and investment and innovation to maintain energy security. The target needs to be forceful and realistic.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to 100% renewable energy by 2030.
rows.text: The idea is a good one but it is too ambitious and would require to use a heavily significant proportion of our GDP which is not possible, a more attainable target is between 2035 and 2040 which would allow for sufficient planning and efficient implementation of the overall idea. Financial resources are pretty limited in the Uk at the minute and we can invest too much upfront costs into major projects at this time. Additionally the timescale is just not possible with current technology limits and we need to wait for more efficient and cost efficient sustainable energy sources to successfully implement this.

rows.proposition: The UK should transition to 100% renewable energy by 2030.
rows.text: Renewable energy is important because we are polluting the planet by burning fossil fuels and causing Carbon Monoxide Emissions from transport. It has a very detrimental effect on our health and nature and the planet as a whole. This has to be something we aim for, but not at the expense of people losing their livelihoods. The goal has to be realistic and I'm not sure that we have sufficient alternatives to fossil fuels to achieve it. There are also problems with wind power at this time; it ruins the countryside and kills birds, and it creates a lot of waste.
